Same parser gap: a buggy implementation could accept `ralph` as the target, consume `--` as the `-e` operand, and proceed. SPEC 4.1's "wherever it appears" applies symmetrically regardless of whether `--` shows up before or after the target. Same fixture as T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10: pre-create a file named `--` in the project root containing `MARKER=should-not-be-loaded\n1BAD=warning-if-loaded\n` (so the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key line is present to make env-file-loading observable independently of any child spawn), plus `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (writes a marker file). Run `loopx run ralph -e --` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage error (broad wording, same pattern as T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10), (c) `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` did not run, and (d) **stderr does not contain the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning for `1BAD`** (does not mention `1BAD` and does not surface the env-file invalid-key warning text) — independently observable from (c) per the rationale in T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10. Together with T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10, this closes the option-operand-position branch of SPEC 4.1's `--` rejection — neither order ("flag-then-`--`-then-target" nor "target-then-flag-then-`--`") allows `--` to slip through as an operand. *(Spec 4.1, 4.2, 8.1, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-12**: `loopx run -n -- ralph` → usage error, exit code 1, with the rejection specifically targeting `--` as an unsupported / unrecognized end-of-options token (not as an "invalid `-n` operand"). Closes the **other** option-operand position covered by ADR-0004's "`--` is rejected wherever it appears" rule: T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10 / T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-11 cover the `-e` operand slot; this test covers the `-n` operand slot with `--` immediately following `-n`. SPEC 4.1 says `--` is rejected "wherever it appears" outside the `-h` / `--help` short-circuit — including the position where `--` could be misread as the `-n` operand. The SPEC-conformant rejection treats `--` as the offending token (an unrecognized flag / unsupported end-of-options marker), it does not accept `--` as an operand and then reject it as a non-numeric value. A parser that consumed `--` as the `-n` operand and then surfaced an "invalid `-n` operand" / non-integer-value error would still exit 1 but is **not** SPEC-conformant: that path treats `--` as having been validly accepted as an operand (just one with a bad value), exactly the parser shape SPEC 4.1 forbids. **Setup.** Create a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (writes a marker file on execution and emits `stop: true`). Run `loopx run -n -- ralph` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error referring to `--` as the offending token — the test accepts flexible wording for the rejection category (e.g., "unrecognized flag `--`", "`--` not supported as end-of-options marker", "unexpected token `--`", or any synonymous phrasing that **identifies `--` itself as the unsupported / unrecognized token**), but **must not** accept stderr text that frames the failure as "invalid value for `-n`", "non-integer `-n` operand", "`-n` requires a non-negative integer", or any wording that treats `--` as having been consumed as `-n`'s operand and then rejected on operand-value grounds. The test asserts on this discriminating axis (the offending-token surface) and not on exact phrasing of the `--`-rejection wording, (c) `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` did not run (no marker file was written), and (d) stderr does not mention an integer / numeric / operand-value validation failure for `-n`. The (b) / (d) pair is the load-bearing assertion: a buggy parser that consumed `--` as the `-n` operand and then surfaced an integer-validation failure would pass (a) and (c) but fail (b) / (d), surfacing the parser shape SPEC 4.1 forbids. Unlike T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10 / T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-11, this test does **not** need a pre-created file named `--` or a SPEC 8.1 invalid-key discriminator: `-n` accepts a numeric operand, not a path, so env-file loading is not a side-channel — the offending-token assertion on stderr provides the discriminative power. *(Spec 4.1, 4.2, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-13**: `loopx run ralph -n --` → usage error, exit code 1, with the rejection specifically targeting `--` as the offending token (not as an "invalid `-n` operand"). Counterpart to T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-12 with the target preceding the `-n --` sequence — symmetric to the T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10 / T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-11 pair on the `-e` axis. Same parser-shape contract: SPEC 4.1's "wherever it appears" applies symmetrically regardless of whether `--` shows up before or after the target, and the rejection must treat `--` as the unsupported / unrecognized token, not as a successfully-consumed `-n` operand with a bad value. **Setup.** Create a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (writes a marker file on execution and emits `stop: true`). Run `loopx run ralph -n --` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage error referring to `--` as the offending token (same flexible-but-discriminating wording requirement as T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-12 — accepts any "unrecognized flag `--`" / "`--` not supported" / "unexpected token `--`" phrasing that identifies `--` itself; does **not** accept "invalid `-n` operand" / "non-integer value" / "`-n` requires a non-negative integer" / any wording that treats `--` as having been consumed as `-n`'s operand), (c) `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` did not run, and (d) stderr does not mention an integer / numeric / operand-value validation failure for `-n`. Together with T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-12, this closes the `-n` operand-position branch of SPEC 4.1's `--` rejection across both orderings (flag-then-`--`-then-target and target-then-flag-then-`--`); together with T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-10 / T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-11 (the `-e` operand-position pair), the four tests close the full "`--` cannot slip through as an option operand" axis for the two `run` options that consume operands (`-n` and `-e`). *(Spec 4.1, 4.2, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-04**: `loopx run adr=0003 -h` → run-help short-circuit, exit 0. Per SPEC 11.2 / ADR-0004 §5, the `run -h` / `--help` short-circuit ignores `name=value` tokens "in any position" — including positions where the `name=value` token appears **before** the help flag. T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-05 covers `-h ralph adr=0003` (help precedes `name=value` after a target); this test covers the symmetric case where `name=value` (here as a sole positional that would otherwise be parsed as an invalid target by T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-01) precedes `-h`. A buggy parser that validated the `name=value` positional as a target before scanning argv for help would surface the invalid-target error instead of help. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) run-help is printed on stdout, (c) stderr does **not** surface the invalid-target / name-restriction-violation error (the would-be T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-01 outcome must not appear), and (d) no `.loopx/` script execution occurs. *(Spec 4.2, 11.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-05**: `loopx run ralph adr=0003 --help` → run-help short-circuit, exit 0. Per SPEC 11.2 / ADR-0004 §5, the help short-circuit ignores `name=value` tokens and extra positionals in any position. Combines two would-be usage errors: (i) extra positional after target (T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-03 surface), and (ii) `name=value` token in argv (T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-01 / 03 surface). T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-05 covers the same combination with `-h`; this test exercises the long-form `--help` for parity, ensuring the short-circuit accepts both spellings symmetrically across the "name=value before help" position. Same assertions as T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-04: exit 0, run-help on stdout, no usage / target error on stderr, no script execution. *(Spec 4.2, 11.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-01**: `loopx run adr=0003` with a valid `.loopx/` (containing a `ralph` workflow but no workflow or script named `adr` or `adr=0003`) → **invalid target error**, exit code 1. Per Spec 12 "Invalid target strings ... `loopx run adr=0003` parsed as a target" — loopx does not interpret `adr=0003` as a named argument. It is a single positional, parsed as a target string, which fails validation because `=` violates the name-restriction pattern `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`. Assert stderr mentions the invalid target / name-restriction violation. *(Spec 4.1, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-02**: `loopx run adr=0003` with **no** `.loopx/` directory → the missing `.loopx/` discovery error surfaces before target validation. Assert: exit code 1, and stderr mentions the missing `.loopx/` directory (not the invalid-target error as the surfaced failure). Per SPEC 7.1, CLI discovery runs before target resolution/validation. *(Spec 4.1, 5.1, 7.1, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-03**: `loopx run ralph adr=0003` → usage error (extra positional), exit code 1. The grammar accepts exactly one positional (the target). T-CLI-27 covers two bare positional words; this test pins down that a positional of the form `name=value` is also rejected as an extra positional, not silently consumed as a named-argument tail. *(Spec 4.1, 4.2, 12)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-INHERIT-01**: Per-run CLI parameterization via the **shell env prefix** (`adr=0003 loopx run ralph`) reaches the spawned script unchanged through the inherited-environment tier. Per SPEC 11.2 / 12 (and ADR-0004's "no CLI surface for `RunOptions.env`" rationale), the **shell env prefix** is the sole CLI surface for per-run parameterization — `adr=0003 loopx run review-adr` continues to work at the inherited-env tier. T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-* and T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-* pin down the negative parser-rejection axis (no `--` separator, no `name=value` tail); this test pins down the **positive** counterpart by asserting that the shell-env-prefix mechanism is observable end-to-end on the spawned-script env. The harness does not literally invoke a shell to apply the prefix — instead it sets the named variable in the CLI process's inherited environment (which is what `key=value loopx run …` produces in any POSIX shell), making the assertion shell-implementation-independent. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `adr` via `observe-env` and emits `stop:true`. Spawn `loopx run -n 1 ralph` with `adr=0003` injected into loopx's inherited environment (the test driver's child-spawn `env` option, equivalent to running `adr=0003 loopx run ralph` from any POSIX shell). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the `observe-env` marker for `adr` records `{ "present": true, "value": "0003" }` — the inherited shell-prefix value reached the spawned script via the inherited-env tier (SPEC 8.3 tier 5 — inherited system environment), (c) no `name=value` token appears in the loopx argv (the test injects `adr=0003` via the env option, **not** as an argv token — proving the shell env prefix is the only mechanism exercised, with no argv-tail path involved). T-ENV-24 covers the four-tier precedence chain for an arbitrary non-protocol variable (asserting `local` wins over `global` over `system`); this test isolates the **inherited-env tier alone** with the canonical `adr=0003` example from ADR-0004, providing direct traceability to the ADR-0004 "CLI per-run parameterization uses existing shell idioms" rationale. A buggy implementation that consumed `adr=0003` from argv as a named-argument tail (a misreading explicitly forbidden by SPEC 12 / T-CLI-RUN-NAMEVAL-03) would not be exercised by this test (the harness does not pass an argv token); but a buggy implementation that scrubbed `adr` from the inherited env before spawning the script (e.g., as a misguided "non-`LOOPX_*` variable doesn't belong in the script env" filter) would surface here as an absent or empty marker, failing (b). *(Spec 4.1, 4.2, 8.3, 11.2, 13)*

#### Missing Flag Operands

- **T-CLI-97**: `loopx run -n` (no operand for `-n`, no target) exits with code 1 (usage error). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-98**: `loopx run -e` (no operand for `-e`, no target) exits with code 1 (usage error). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-99**: `loopx run ralph -n` (missing `-n` operand after target name) exits with code 1 (usage error). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-100**: `loopx run ralph -e` (missing `-e` operand after target name) exits with code 1 (usage error). *(Spec 4.2)*

#### CLI `-e` Option

- **T-CLI-21**: `loopx run -e .env -n 1 ralph` with a valid `.env` file makes its variables available in the script. Use `write-env-to-file` fixture in the workflow's `index.sh`. Assert the marker file contains the expected value. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-22**: `loopx run -e nonexistent.env ralph` exits with code 1. Stderr mentions the missing file. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-22a**: `loopx run -n 0 -e nonexistent.env ralph` exits with code 1 (env file validation happens before `-n 0` short-circuit). *(Spec 4.2, 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-22c**: `loopx run -n 0 -e malformed.env ralph` where `malformed.env` exists and is readable but contains an invalid line (e.g., `1BAD=val`) → exits with code 0, stderr contains a parser warning for the invalid key, and the script never runs (assert counter file does not exist). Proves env files are actually parsed under `-n 0`, not just existence/readability checked. *(Spec 4.2, 7.1, 8.1)*
- **T-CLI-22b**: `loopx run -n 0 ralph` with `.loopx/` containing a name collision in a workflow → exits with code 1 (validation occurs before `-n 0` short-circuit). *(Spec 4.2, 5.2, 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-22d**: `loopx run -n 0 ralph` with `.loopx/` containing an invalid script name (e.g., `-bad.sh` in any workflow) → exits with code 1. *(Spec 4.2, 5.3, 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-22e**: `loopx run -n 0 ralph` with `.loopx/` containing a sibling with an invalid workflow name (e.g., `.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh`) → exits with code 1 (global validation catches the invalid workflow name before the `-n 0` short-circuit). This is the `-n 0` counterpart to T-DISC-47b (invalid workflow name in sibling, normal run mode). *(Spec 4.2, 5.3, 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-22f**: `loopx run -n 0 -e unreadable.env ralph` where `unreadable.env` exists but is unreadable (e.g., mode 000) → exits with code 1. Env file readability validation fires before the `-n 0` short-circuit. This completes the local `-e` file failure coverage under `-n 0`: T-CLI-22a covers missing, T-CLI-22c covers malformed, and this test covers unreadable. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 4.2, 7.1, 8.2)*

#### CLI Pre-iteration Ordering (discovery before env before target resolution)

SPEC 7.1 defines a concrete CLI pre-iteration order: (1) discovery/validation, (2) env loading, (3) target resolution. When failures from different steps are both present, the earlier step's failure surfaces first. These tests pin down that ordering across all three step boundaries. Programmatic-API pre-iteration ordering is mostly implementation-defined (SPEC 4.1 / 9.3), so this block is CLI-only.

- **T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-01**: Env-file error beats missing-workflow error. Create a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (so discovery and validation pass). Run `loopx run -e missing.env nonexistent`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the missing env file (e.g., `missing.env`), and (c) stderr does **not** mention the missing workflow (`nonexistent`) as the fatal error — target resolution was never reached. Per SPEC 7.1 step 2 runs before step 3. *(Spec 7.1, 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-02**: Env-file error beats invalid target. Create a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Run `loopx run -e missing.env ":script"` (invalid target format). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the missing env file, and (c) stderr does **not** mention the invalid-target validation error as the fatal error. Invalid targets are rejected at the target-resolution point (SPEC 4.1: "at the same point as a missing workflow (after discovery)"), so an env-file failure must beat them too. *(Spec 7.1, 4.1, 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-03**: Discovery / global-validation error beats env-file error. Create a valid target workflow `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and a **broken** sibling workflow `.loopx/broken/check.sh` + `.loopx/broken/check.ts` (same-base-name collision per SPEC 5.2 — fatal across all discovered workflows per SPEC 5.4). Run `loopx run -e missing.env ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the sibling collision (e.g., references `broken` and the conflicting `check.sh` / `check.ts` entries), and (c) stderr does **not** mention the missing env file as the fatal-exit reason — discovery failed first (SPEC 7.1 step 1 before step 2), so env-file loading was never attempted. This closes the discovery-before-env ordering gap that T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-01 / T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-02 do not cover; together the three tests pin down the full SPEC 7.1 pre-iteration ordering `step 1 → step 2 → step 3`. *(Spec 7.1, 5.2, 5.4, 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-04**: Missing `.loopx/` beats env-file error. Set the fixture cwd to a directory with no `.loopx/` subdirectory. Run `loopx run -e missing.env ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the missing `.loopx/` directory (per SPEC 7.2's "Missing `.loopx/` directory" clause and T-CLI-42 / T-DISC-10a coverage), and (c) stderr does **not** mention the missing env file as the fatal-exit reason — the missing-`.loopx/` check is part of discovery (step 1) and fails before env-file loading (step 2). This is the existence-branch counterpart to T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-03 (validation-branch of discovery): together they cover both sub-paths of SPEC 7.1's step 1 — missing-`.loopx/` and sibling-validation — each beating env-file loading. *(Spec 7.1, 5.1, 7.2, 4.2)*
- **T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-05**: Env-file error beats workflow `package.json` / version-check warnings. SPEC 7.1 orders pre-iteration as discovery (step 1) → env loading (step 2) → target resolution (step 3) → workflow-entry version check (step 5). T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-01 / 02 prove env loading beats target resolution; T-CLI-RUN-ORDER-03 / 04 prove discovery beats env loading. This test pins down the *next* boundary: env-file failure (step 2) prevents the workflow `package.json` warning paths from running at all (step 5 never executes). Two parallel sub-cases that share the same fixture shape, run independently:
    - **(05-bad-json)**: Create `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (a runnable script that writes a marker file on entry) and `.loopx/ralph/package.json` containing `{{{INVALID` (broken JSON — would normally trigger the SPEC 3.2 "invalid JSON" warning on workflow entry). Run `loopx run -e missing.env ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the missing env file `missing.env` as the fatal-exit reason, (c) stderr contains **no** `package.json` parse warning for `ralph` (the workflow-entry version check at SPEC 7.1 step 5 was never reached), and (d) the marker file does **not** exist (the script never ran).
    - **(05-bad-range)**: Same fixture as (05-bad-json) but `.loopx/ralph/package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": ">=999.0.0" } }` (valid JSON with an unsatisfied loopx range — would normally trigger the SPEC 3.2 version-mismatch warning on workflow entry). Run `loopx run -e missing.env ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr mentions the missing env file as the fatal-exit reason, (c) stderr contains **no** version-mismatch warning for `ralph` (the workflow-entry version check was never reached), and (d) the marker file does **not** exist.
  
  The two sub-cases together prove the SPEC 7.1 ordering rule across both `package.json`-failure branches that produce stderr output (invalid JSON, unsatisfied range). The unreadable-`package.json` and invalid-semver-range branches are not separately exercised because they share the same step-5 trigger point: the test's contract is that step 5 is never reached, and the bad-JSON / bad-range pair already excludes the bulk of the warning surface. CLI-only — programmatic-API non-abort pre-iteration ordering is intentionally implementation-defined per SPEC 4.1 / 9.3. *(Spec 7.1, 3.2, 8.2, 4.2)*

#### CLI Stdout Silence

- **T-CLI-23**: `loopx run -n 1 ralph` where `ralph:index` outputs `{"result":"hello"}` and writes a marker file — the CLI's own stdout is empty (result is not printed), AND the marker file exists (proving the script actually ran). *(Spec 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-23a**: CLI stdout silence on **raw (non-JSON) successful** script output. SPEC 7.1 normatively says "The CLI does not print `result` to its own stdout at any point" and SPEC 2.3 says "If stdout is not valid JSON, is not an object, or is a valid JSON object but contains none of the known fields, the entire stdout content is treated as `{ result: <raw output> }`." T-CLI-23 covers structured JSON success (`{"result":"hello"}`); T-LOOP-24a covers the failure path (script writes structured-looking content, exits non-zero, the CLI must not pass it through). Neither covers the raw-stdout success path: a script that writes plain text to stdout and exits 0. A buggy implementation could route raw-stdout success through to the CLI's own stdout while correctly suppressing the structured-JSON case (e.g., "if I parsed it as structured output, suppress; otherwise pass through") — that bug would pass T-CLI-23 and T-LOOP-24a but break the SPEC 7.1 contract. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that writes a raw non-JSON string to stdout (e.g., `printf '%s\n' 'hello raw'` or `echo "hello raw"`) and writes a marker file to confirm execution, then `exit 0`. Run via `runCLI(["run", "-n", "1", "ralph"])`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 (clean exit — the loop reset and `-n 1` capped iterations, not a script failure), (b) the CLI's captured **stdout is byte-empty** — the literal string `hello raw` does not appear on stdout, (c) the marker file exists (proving the script actually ran), and (d) the script's stderr (if any) passes through normally — but the test does not exercise stderr beyond confirming nothing leaked to stdout. Together with T-CLI-23 (structured success) and T-LOOP-24a (failure with structured-looking content), this closes the three success-or-failure × structured-or-raw quadrants of the CLI-stdout-silence contract. *(Spec 2.3, 7.1)*
- **T-CLI-27**: `loopx run ralph beta` (two positional arguments within `run`) exits with code 1. `run` accepts exactly one positional argument. *(Spec 4.1)*
- **T-CLI-96**: `loopx run ralph -n 1 beta` exits with code 1 (extra positional argument interleaved with options). *(Spec 4.1)*

### 4.2 Subcommands

**Spec refs:** 4.3, 5.4

#### `loopx output`

- **T-SUB-01**: `loopx output --result "hello"` prints valid JSON to stdout, exits 0. Parse stdout as JSON and assert `result === "hello"`. Do not assert exact byte-for-byte text or field order. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02**: `loopx output --goto "next"` prints valid JSON to stdout, exits 0. Parse stdout as JSON and assert `goto === "next"`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02a**: `loopx output --goto "review-adr:request-feedback"` prints valid JSON with a qualified goto target. Parse stdout as JSON and assert `goto === "review-adr:request-feedback"`. The output helper does not validate the goto target format — it only serializes. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02b**: `loopx output --goto "check-ready"` prints valid JSON with a bare goto target (intra-workflow). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02c**: `loopx output --goto ""` prints valid JSON with `goto === ""`. The output helper does not validate — empty string is serialized as-is. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02d**: `loopx output --goto "a:b:c"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "a:b:c"`. The output helper does not validate malformed targets; it only serializes them. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02e**: `loopx output --goto ":script"` prints valid JSON with `goto === ":script"`. The output helper does not validate leading-colon targets; it only serializes. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02f**: `loopx output --goto "workflow:"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "workflow:"`. The output helper does not validate trailing-colon targets; it only serializes. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02g**: `loopx output --goto ":"` prints valid JSON with `goto === ":"`. The output helper does not validate bare-colon targets; it only serializes. This completes the invalid-shape matrix from Spec 4.1 (empty string, bare colon, leading colon, trailing colon, multiple colons) for the CLI helper. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02h**: `loopx output --goto "missing-workflow:missing-script"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "missing-workflow:missing-script"` and exits 0, even without a `.loopx/` directory existing. The output helper does not validate target existence — a well-formed but nonexistent target is serialized identically to a real one. The existing non-validation tests (T-SUB-02c–02g) cover syntactically invalid targets; this test covers a syntactically valid but nonexistent target, proving the helper performs no existence check at all. *(Spec 4.3, 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-02i**: `loopx output --goto "bad.name"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "bad.name"` and exits 0. The output helper does not validate name-pattern violations in bare targets. The dot is not allowed by the name restriction pattern `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`, but the helper only serializes — target validation is deferred to loop execution time. T-SUB-02c–02g cover invalid delimiter shapes (empty, bare colon, leading colon, trailing colon, multiple colons) and T-SUB-02h covers a syntactically valid but nonexistent target; this test covers a third category — a bare target that is not colon-malformed and not merely nonexistent, but violates the name-restriction regex itself. A helper that incorrectly regex-validates names would pass T-SUB-02c–02h and fail here. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02j**: `loopx output --goto "bad.name:index"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "bad.name:index"` and exits 0. The workflow portion of the qualified target violates the name restriction pattern (the dot is not allowed), but the output helper does not validate either portion of the target — it only serializes. This is the qualified-target counterpart to T-SUB-02i (bare invalid name), proving that name-pattern validation is deferred for the workflow portion specifically. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-02k**: `loopx output --goto "ralph:bad.name"` prints valid JSON with `goto === "ralph:bad.name"` and exits 0. The script portion of the qualified target violates the name restriction pattern (the dot is not allowed), but the output helper does not validate either portion of the target. Combined with T-SUB-02j, this proves that name-pattern validation is deferred for both the workflow and script portions of qualified targets. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-03**: `loopx output --stop` prints valid JSON to stdout, exits 0. Parse stdout as JSON and assert `stop === true`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-04**: `loopx output --result "x" --goto "y" --stop` prints valid JSON to stdout. Parse as JSON and assert all three fields present with correct values. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-05**: `loopx output` with no flags exits with code 1 (error). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-05a**: `loopx output --result ""` succeeds — an empty-string value still counts as a provided flag. SPEC 4.3 requires "at least one flag must be provided" and calling `loopx output` with no flags is an error (T-SUB-05). This test pins down that "provided" means the flag is on the command line, regardless of its value — `--result ""` is a provided flag with an empty-string value, not an absent flag. Run `loopx output --result ""`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, and (b) stdout is valid JSON with `result === ""` (the empty-string value is serialized as-is). An implementation that gated the "at least one flag" check on truthy/non-empty values would wrongly reject this call and match the T-SUB-05 path; this test catches that regression. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-06**: `loopx output --result "x"` works without `.loopx/` directory existing. *(Spec 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-06a**: `loopx output --result 'value with "quotes" and \\backslashes'` → stdout is valid JSON with the value correctly escaped. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-06b**: `loopx output --result $'line1\nline2'` → stdout is valid JSON with the newline correctly escaped. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-06c**: `loopx output --result` (missing operand for `--result`) → usage error, exit code 1. The SPEC 4.3 grammar `loopx output [--result <value>] [--goto <target>] [--stop]` requires an operand for `--result`; existing tests cover no-flags (T-SUB-05) and valid-flags but not the missing-operand sub-path. Run `loopx output --result`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category (missing-operand or "expected value after `--result`" — exact phrasing implementation-defined; the test does not pin a specific phrase), and (c) stdout does not contain JSON output. A buggy implementation that consumed nothing for `--result` and emitted a JSON object missing the `result` field would pass T-SUB-05 (which is no-flags) but fail this missing-operand check. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-06d**: `loopx output --goto` (missing operand for `--goto`) → usage error, exit code 1. Same shape as T-SUB-06c applied to the `--goto <target>` operand requirement. Same assertions: exit 1, stderr usage-error category, no JSON stdout. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-06e**: `loopx output --unknown` → usage error, exit code 1. SPEC 4.3 enumerates only `--result`, `--goto`, and `--stop`; an unrecognized flag is not a recognized output-helper token. Existing tests cover no-flags and valid-flags but not the unrecognized-flag sub-path. Run `loopx output --unknown`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the unrecognized `--unknown` flag, and (c) no JSON stdout. A buggy implementation that silently ignored unrecognized flags and required only "at least one recognized flag" could pass T-SUB-05 (no-flags) and T-SUB-01 (valid `--result`) while accepting `loopx output --unknown --result foo` — the explicit unrecognized-flag check pins down rejection in the bare-unknown case. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-06f**: `loopx output --unknown --result x` → usage error, exit code 1. Strengthens T-SUB-06e by combining an unrecognized flag with a recognized one. T-SUB-06e covers the bare-unknown case (`--unknown` alone); a buggy implementation could pass T-SUB-06e by failing on "no recognized flag was provided" (the T-SUB-05 path) rather than on "unknown flag was rejected." This test pins down that the `--unknown` flag itself is rejected even when a recognized flag (`--result x`) is also present and would otherwise satisfy the "at least one flag must be provided" rule. Run `loopx output --unknown --result x`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the unrecognized `--unknown` flag, and (c) **no JSON stdout** — the recognized `--result x` does not slip through despite the unknown flag's presence. A buggy implementation that silently ignored unrecognized flags would emit `{"result":"x"}` and exit 0, which this test catches. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*

#### `loopx env set`

- **T-SUB-07**: `loopx env set FOO bar` then `loopx env list` shows `FOO=bar`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-08**: `loopx env set _UNDER score` succeeds (underscore-prefixed name valid). Follow with `loopx env list` and assert `_UNDER=score` is present. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-09**: `loopx env set A1 val` succeeds (alphanumeric name). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-10**: `loopx env set 1INVALID val` exits with code 1 (starts with digit). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-11**: `loopx env set -DASH val` exits with code 1 (invalid name). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-11b**: `loopx env set FOO-BAR val` exits with code 1. SPEC 4.3 specifies the env-set name pattern `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`. T-SUB-10 covers a digit-first invalid name and T-SUB-11 covers a dash-first invalid name (both reject at the first-character class), but neither exercises a name that starts with a valid first character and then contains a violating **interior** character. A buggy implementation that validated only the first character would accept this input and write an unparseable `FOO-BAR="val"\n` line into the global env file (which the loader would then reject per SPEC 8.1's env-file key-name rule). Run `loopx env set FOO-BAR val`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (validation rejects the interior `-`), (b) stderr surfaces a name-validation error (exact phrasing implementation-defined), and (c) the global env file is not written / not modified — `loopx env list` after the failure does not include `FOO-BAR`. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-11c**: `loopx env set FOO.BAR val` exits with code 1. The interior `.` violates the pattern `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`. Same assertions as T-SUB-11b applied to the dot-interior case. Together with T-SUB-11b (dash interior), this pins down that interior-character validation rejects symbol classes that the first-character check passed unrelated. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-11d**: `loopx env set "FOO BAR" val` exits with code 1. The interior space violates the pattern. The argument is shell-quoted to ensure `argv` carries `FOO BAR` (the single 7-byte string `0x46 0x4f 0x4f 0x20 0x42 0x41 0x52`), not two separate `argv` tokens. Same assertions as T-SUB-11b / T-SUB-11c applied to the space-interior case. Together with T-SUB-11b (dash) and T-SUB-11c (dot), this closes the interior-character rejection coverage for the three most likely accidental inputs (hyphenated names from kebab-case, dotted names from dotted env-var schemes, and quoted-multi-word names typed by mistake). *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-11e**: `loopx env set "" val` exits with code 1. The empty-string name has zero length and therefore cannot match the SPEC 4.3 pattern `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`, which requires at least one character. The argument is shell-quoted to ensure `argv` carries an empty-string token (not absent — that case is covered by T-SUB-25 / T-SUB-26 missing-operand grammar). Existing T-SUB-10 (digit-first), T-SUB-11 (dash-first), T-SUB-11b (dash interior), T-SUB-11c (dot interior), and T-SUB-11d (space interior) cover the non-empty invalid-name shapes; this test closes the zero-length branch. Run `loopx env set "" val` against an isolated global config (via `withGlobalEnv`). Assert: (a) exit code 1 (validation rejects the empty name), (b) stderr surfaces a name-validation error (exact phrasing implementation-defined), and (c) the global env file is not written / not modified — `loopx env list` after the failure does not include any zero-length-keyed entry, and any pre-existing variables remain untouched. A buggy implementation that accepted the empty name and committed `="val"` (or otherwise serialized a malformed line) would fail this test. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-11a**: `loopx env set lower_case_key val` succeeds. The SPEC 8.1 env-file key pattern `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*` explicitly allows lowercase letters; `loopx env set` writes to the same env file and must accept the same key pattern. Existing `loopx env set` tests (T-SUB-08 underscore, T-SUB-09 alphanumeric `A1`) use names that begin with `_` or uppercase; none exercise a lowercase-first key. Run `loopx env set mykey myval`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `loopx env list` subsequently includes `mykey=myval`. An implementation that tightened the key-validation regex to `[A-Z_][A-Z0-9_]*` (an "uppercase convention" buglet) would fail this test. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-12**: `loopx env set FOO bar` then `loopx env set FOO baz` then `loopx env list` shows `FOO=baz` (overwrite). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-13**: `loopx env set FOO bar` in a directory with no `.loopx/` → exits 0 AND `loopx env list` subsequently shows `FOO=bar`. *(Spec 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-14**: `loopx env set` creates the config directory (`$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/`) if it doesn't exist. *(Spec 8.1)*
- **T-SUB-14a**: `loopx env set KEY "value with spaces"` → `loopx env list` shows `KEY=value with spaces`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14b**: `loopx env set KEY "value#hash"` → value preserved including `#`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14c**: `loopx env set KEY "val=ue"` → value with `=` round-trips correctly. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14d**: `loopx env set KEY <value containing an actual newline byte>` → rejected. Exit code 1. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14e**: `loopx env set KEY 'val"ue'` → `loopx env list` shows `KEY=val"ue`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14f**: `loopx env set KEY "value  "` → `loopx env list` shows `KEY=value  `. Trailing spaces preserved. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14g**: `loopx env set KEY <value containing an actual CR byte>` → rejected. Exit code 1. *(Spec 4.3)*

#### `loopx env set` On-Disk Serialization

- **T-SUB-14h**: `loopx env set FOO bar` → read the global env file. Assert it contains the line `FOO="bar"\n`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14i**: `loopx env set FOO "value with spaces"` → file contains `FOO="value with spaces"\n`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14j**: `loopx env set FOO 'val"ue'` → file contains `FOO="val"ue"\n` (no escaping). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14k**: `loopx env set FOO ""` (empty value) → file contains `FOO=""\n`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-14l**: `loopx env set` backslash round-trip — literal backslashes are stored and read back unchanged. Per SPEC 4.3 "the value is written literally within double quotes" (no escape sequences) and SPEC 8.1 "No escape sequence interpretation". The value under test is the exact 18-byte sequence `0x76 0x61 0x6c 0x5c 0x77 0x69 0x74 0x68 0x5c 0x62 0x61 0x63 0x6b 0x73 0x6c 0x61 0x73 0x68` (i.e., the string `val\with\backslash` containing exactly two literal `0x5c` backslash bytes, one between `val` and `with` and one between `with` and `backslash`). Pass this byte sequence to the CLI by any means that reaches `argv[n]` unchanged (e.g., shell-literal single-quoted argument, or `execa(..., ["env", "set", "KEY", "val\\with\\backslash"])` — where the JS source-level `\\` escape produces a single `0x5c` byte per pair, for a total of two backslash bytes). Run `loopx env set KEY <value>`. Assert: (a) the global env file on disk contains the exact byte sequence `KEY="val\with\backslash"\n` — specifically, `0x4b 0x45 0x59 0x3d 0x22 0x76 0x61 0x6c 0x5c 0x77 0x69 0x74 0x68 0x5c 0x62 0x61 0x63 0x6b 0x73 0x6c 0x61 0x73 0x68 0x22 0x0a` (key, `=`, opening quote, value bytes with the two literal `0x5c` bytes preserved, closing quote, newline; no escape-doubling applied at write time), and (b) a subsequent `loopx run` of a workflow that observes `$KEY` (via `write-env-to-file`) records the identical 18-byte value sequence in the marker file (no escape interpretation at read time). This is the combined write-side (SPEC 4.3) and read-side (SPEC 8.1) "no escape interpretation" assertion for the single value type most likely to reveal an accidental escape layer — a backslash-containing string. T-SUB-06a covers the *output* side's JSON-escaping of backslashes (CLI stdout); this test covers env-file serialization and round-trip through script spawn. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1)*

#### `loopx env remove`

- **T-SUB-15**: `loopx env set FOO bar` then `loopx env remove FOO` then `loopx env list` — `FOO` is absent. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-16**: `loopx env remove NONEXISTENT` exits with code 0 (silent no-op). *(Spec 4.3)*

#### `loopx env list`

- **T-SUB-17**: With no variables set, `loopx env list` produces no stdout output, exits 0. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-18**: With variables `ZEBRA=z`, `ALPHA=a`, `MIDDLE=m` set, `loopx env list` outputs them sorted: `ALPHA=a`, `MIDDLE=m`, `ZEBRA=z`. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-19**: `loopx env list` in a directory with no `.loopx/` → exits 0 and produces no stdout. *(Spec 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-19a**: `loopx env list` parses a malformed global env file and reports only well-formed entries with a parser warning. SPEC 8.1 says env-file parsing applies to the global env file (loaded for runs and for `env list`); existing env-parser tests (T-ENV-15c and similar) drive parser behavior through `loopx run`, but do not cover the `env list` code path specifically. A buggy implementation could parse the file correctly for `run` but use a different code path for `env list` — e.g., reading raw lines without the parser — that skips the warning or includes malformed keys. Set up a global env file (via `withGlobalEnv`) containing raw lines `1BAD=val\nGOOD=ok\n` (first line has an invalid key per SPEC 8.1 `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`, second line is well-formed). Run `loopx env list`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — malformed lines do not fail `env list`, only produce warnings, (b) stdout lists `GOOD=ok` exactly once (sorted output per T-SUB-18), (c) stdout does **not** contain `1BAD` (the malformed line was dropped), (d) stderr contains exactly one parser warning for the `1BAD=val` line (same warning class as T-ENV-15c when the same file is read under `loopx run`). This closes the gap in env-file parsing coverage between `run` and `env list` — a buggy implementation that maintained two separate parse paths would fail this test. *(Spec 4.3, 8.1, 5.4)*

#### `loopx env` Grammar Coverage

SPEC 4.3 defines exactly three `env` subcommands — `loopx env set <name> <value>`, `loopx env remove <name>`, and `loopx env list` — and no other forms. The tests in this block cover the grammar's missing-subcommand, missing-operand, and unrecognized-subcommand paths. Existing tests (T-SUB-07–19a) cover the success and validation paths for each subcommand individually, but do not pin down rejection of the malformed-grammar inputs that would result from typos or partial commands. The rejections follow from SPEC 4.3's grammar (no other forms accepted) and SPEC 12's usage-error contract for parser-level surface errors.

- **T-SUB-24**: `loopx env` (no subcommand) → usage error, exit code 1. The bare `env` subcommand without any further token is not one of the three enumerated forms. Run `loopx env`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category (e.g., listing the valid subcommands `set` / `remove` / `list`, or a missing-subcommand error — exact phrasing implementation-defined), and (c) no env-file mutation occurred. A buggy implementation that defaulted to `list` (or any other subcommand) when none was specified would pass T-SUB-17 but fail this test. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-25**: `loopx env set` (no name, no value) → usage error, exit code 1. The grammar requires `<name> <value>` after `set`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the missing operands, and (c) no env-file mutation occurred (no key was written). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-26**: `loopx env set FOO` (name only, missing value) → usage error, exit code 1. The grammar requires both `<name>` and `<value>` after `set`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the missing value operand, and (c) no env-file mutation occurred — `loopx env list` after the failure does not include `FOO` (with any value, including empty-string — a buggy implementation that defaulted the missing value to `""` and committed `FOO=""` would fail this assertion). *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-27**: `loopx env remove` (no name) → usage error, exit code 1. The grammar requires `<name>` after `remove`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the missing name operand, and (c) no env-file mutation occurred. A buggy implementation that defaulted the missing name to `""` and silently no-op'd (matching T-SUB-16's nonexistent-name silence) would pass T-SUB-16 but should fail this test — the nonexistent-name silence applies only when a name is supplied. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-28**: `loopx env unknown` (unrecognized subcommand) → usage error, exit code 1. SPEC 4.3 enumerates only `set`, `remove`, and `list` under `env`; any other subcommand token is unrecognized. Run `loopx env unknown`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the unrecognized subcommand `unknown` (or listing the valid subcommands — exact phrasing implementation-defined), and (c) no env-file mutation occurred. *(Spec 4.3)*
- **T-SUB-29**: `loopx env list extra` (extra operand to `list`) → usage error, exit code 1. The `list` subcommand takes no operands per SPEC 4.3's grammar. Run `loopx env list extra`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the extra positional, and (c) no stdout listing of variables (the failure short-circuits before `list`'s normal output path). A buggy implementation that ignored extra operands and ran `list` regardless would emit a sorted list and exit 0, which this test catches. The rejection follows directly from SPEC 4.3's `list` grammar (no operands) and SPEC 12's usage-error contract for parser-level surface errors. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-29a**: `loopx env set FOO bar extra` (extra operand after `set <name> <value>`) → usage error, exit code 1. The `set` subcommand grammar is `set <name> <value>` per SPEC 4.3; a third positional is outside the grammar. Run `loopx env set FOO bar extra` against an isolated global config (via `withGlobalEnv`). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the extra positional (exact phrasing implementation-defined), and (c) **no env-file mutation occurred** — the isolated global env file does not contain `FOO=bar` (or `FOO` at all) after the failure. A buggy implementation that accepted the extra operand and committed `FOO=bar` regardless would write the variable and fail this test. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-29b**: `loopx env remove FOO extra` (extra operand after `remove <name>`) → usage error, exit code 1. The `remove` subcommand grammar is `remove <name>` per SPEC 4.3. Pre-seed the isolated global config with `FOO=bar` (via `withGlobalEnv`). Run `loopx env remove FOO extra`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the extra positional, and (c) **no env-file mutation occurred** — `FOO=bar` is still present after the failure (a buggy implementation that ignored the extra operand and removed `FOO` anyway would wipe the entry). *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-29c**: `loopx output --result x extra` (extra positional after `--result` value) → usage error, exit code 1. The `output` subcommand accepts only the named flag-value pairs (`--result`, `--goto`, `--stop`) per SPEC 4.3 — no positional arguments are specified. Run `loopx output --result x extra`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the extra positional `extra`, and (c) stdout is empty (no structured JSON was serialized — the usage error fired before the output-serialization path). A buggy implementation that ignored the extra operand and emitted `{"result":"x"}` would fail this test. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*
- **T-SUB-29d**: `loopx output --stop extra` (extra positional after `--stop`) → usage error, exit code 1. The `output` subcommand's `--stop` is a boolean flag with no operand (unlike `--result` / `--goto`); a following positional is outside the grammar. Run `loopx output --stop extra`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error category mentioning the extra positional, and (c) stdout is empty (no structured JSON serialized). Together T-SUB-29 / 29a / 29b / 29c / 29d pin down extra-positional rejection across `env set / remove / list` and `output --result / --stop`, following from SPEC 4.3's grammar + SPEC 12's usage-error contract. *(Spec 4.3, 12)*

#### Broken `.loopx/` Tree is Ignored by Non-Validating Commands

SPEC 5.4 specifies that `loopx env *` and `loopx output` do **not** validate `.loopx/`. Existing tests cover the "no `.loopx/` exists" case (T-SUB-06, T-SUB-13, T-SUB-19) but not the "broken `.loopx/` exists" case — a buggy implementation could ignore a missing `.loopx/` but still validate a broken one.

All tests in this block share a fixture where `.loopx/` exists and contains a broken tree: an invalid workflow name (`.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh`), a same-base-name collision in another workflow (`.loopx/broken/check.sh` and `.loopx/broken/check.ts`), and an invalid script name (`.loopx/other/-bad.sh`). This is the same fixture shape used by T-INST-42c.

- **T-SUB-20**: `loopx output --result "x"` with broken `.loopx/` tree → exits 0, prints valid JSON (same as T-SUB-01 in a clean fixture), and stderr contains no discovery or validation warnings. Per SPEC 5.4, `loopx output` does not validate `.loopx/`. A buggy implementation that validates when `.loopx/` is present would emit warnings here; this test catches that. *(Spec 4.3, 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-21**: `loopx env list` with broken `.loopx/` tree and no env variables set → exits 0, produces no stdout, and stderr contains no discovery or validation warnings. Same assertion shape as T-SUB-20 applied to `env list`. *(Spec 4.3, 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-22**: `loopx env set FOO bar` with broken `.loopx/` tree → exits 0, and stderr contains no discovery or validation warnings. Then `loopx env list` shows `FOO=bar`. Per SPEC 5.4, `env set` does not validate `.loopx/`. *(Spec 4.3, 5.4)*
- **T-SUB-23**: `loopx env remove FOO` with broken `.loopx/` tree — after `loopx env set FOO bar` — exits 0, and stderr contains no discovery or validation warnings. Then `loopx env list` does not include `FOO`. *(Spec 4.3, 5.4)*

### 4.3 Workflow & Script Discovery

**Spec refs:** 2.1, 5.1–5.4

#### Workflow Discovery

- **T-DISC-01**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing `index.sh` is a valid workflow. `loopx run -n 1 ralph` runs the `index.sh` script. Assert via marker file. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-02**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing `index.ts` is a valid workflow. `loopx run -n 1 ralph` runs it. Assert via marker file. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-03**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing `index.js` is a valid workflow. Assert via marker file. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-04**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing `index.jsx` is a valid workflow. Assert via marker file. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-05**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing `index.tsx` is a valid workflow. Assert via marker file. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-06**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing only `index.mjs` → not recognized as a workflow (`.mjs` is unsupported). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` fails with "not found." *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-07**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing only `index.cjs` → not recognized as a workflow. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-07a**: `.mjs` and `.cjs` files are ignored during discovery when they coexist with valid scripts. Create `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (valid workflow entry) and `.loopx/ralph/helper.mjs` (unsupported extension, coexisting with the valid script). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — the `ralph:index` script runs (marker file written), (b) stderr contains no warning about `helper.mjs` being an unsupported extension (the `.mjs`/`.cjs` exclusion is silent, not a warning), (c) `loopx run -n 1 ralph:helper` fails with exit 1 and a "script not found" error — the `.mjs` file was not discovered as a `helper` script. T-DISC-06 / T-DISC-07 cover the "only-file unsupported" case where the workflow would not exist without the `.mjs`/`.cjs`; this test covers the coexistence case and is necessary because an implementation could plausibly discover `.mjs`/`.cjs` as scripts when a valid sibling script makes the directory a workflow. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-07b**: Same coexistence fixture as T-DISC-07a with `.cjs` instead of `.mjs`. `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` + `.loopx/ralph/helper.cjs`. Assert identical outcomes: `ralph:index` runs, no warning about `helper.cjs`, `ralph:helper` is not a discovered script. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-07c**: `loopx run -h` does not warn about `.mjs` / `.cjs` siblings of valid scripts. Same fixtures as T-DISC-07a and T-DISC-07b. Run `loopx run -h`. Assert: (a) exit 0, (b) the discovered-workflows section lists `ralph` with `index` as its default entry point — `helper` does not appear in `ralph`'s script list, (c) stderr contains no warning that mentions `helper.mjs` or `helper.cjs`. This is the help-mode counterpart to T-DISC-07a/07b and mirrors T-DISC-10d / T-DISC-10e (invalid-name loose files ignored under `-h`). *(Spec 2.1, 5.1, 11.2)*
- **T-INST-42m**: Install-time — source workflow with valid `index.sh` plus top-level `.mjs`/`.cjs` non-script files. Unsupported extensions are copied as non-script workflow content, not treated as scripts or validated. Create a single-workflow source containing `index.sh` (valid script) and a top-level `helper.mjs` alongside a top-level `tool.cjs`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/` and contains `index.sh`, `helper.mjs`, and `tool.cjs` byte-for-byte (`.mjs`/`.cjs` are workflow content, not script-discovery candidates), (c) stderr contains no validation warning about `helper.mjs` or `tool.cjs` being unsupported extensions (install-time validation per Spec 10.4 only checks **discovered** scripts; unsupported extensions are not discovered and therefore not validated against the name pattern), and (d) a subsequent `loopx run -n 1 <name>` runs `<name>:index` successfully, and `loopx run -n 1 <name>:helper` and `loopx run -n 1 <name>:tool` both fail with "script not found" (the install-time treatment of unsupported extensions matches the runtime-discovery treatment from T-DISC-07a/07b). This pins down that install-time workflow self-containment preserves non-script content alongside scripts without validating or flagging supported-extension-adjacent extensions. *(Spec 10.3, 10.4, 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-INST-42n**: Install-time — same-base-name collision immunity for unsupported `.mjs` / `.cjs` extensions. Install-time counterpart to T-DISC-24a / T-DISC-24b. SPEC 10.4's install-time validation reuses SPEC 5.2's collision rule, which applies only to discovered scripts; unsupported extensions are not discovery candidates and therefore cannot participate in same-base collision detection. A buggy installer that extension-fold-matched on base names (e.g., flagging `check.ts` + `check.mjs` as a `check` collision) would wrongly refuse install for sources whose contributors happened to ship companion `.mjs` / `.cjs` artifacts. Create a single-workflow source containing `check.ts` (valid script) and a top-level `check.mjs` and `check.cjs` (same base name, unsupported extensions — conceptually the kind of files a build pipeline might emit alongside the canonical `.ts` source). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/` and contains all three files (`check.ts`, `check.mjs`, `check.cjs`) byte-for-byte (the unsupported-extension siblings are copied as non-script content per T-INST-42m), (c) stderr contains no collision warning or error referencing `check.mjs` or `check.cjs` (install-time validation only flags collisions among **discovered** scripts), and (d) a subsequent `loopx run -n 1 <name>:check` runs `check.ts` successfully (marker file written) — proving that runtime discovery also resolves `check` to `check.ts` without same-base ambiguity, mirroring T-DISC-24a / T-DISC-24b. Together with T-INST-42m (mixed unsupported-extension content alongside a different-base-name script) and T-DISC-24a / T-DISC-24b (runtime same-base immunity), this closes the install + runtime same-base-collision-immunity coverage for `.mjs` / `.cjs`. *(Spec 10.3, 10.4, 2.1, 5.1, 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-08**: `.loopx/ralph/` containing only `readme.txt` and `config.json` → not a workflow (no supported script extensions). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` fails. No warning. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-09**: Empty subdirectory `.loopx/ralph/` → not a workflow (no script files). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` fails. No warning. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-09a**: A top-level entry inside a workflow whose **name** has a supported script extension but whose entry **type** is a directory does not count as a script — workflow detection requires top-level **files** with supported extensions. SPEC 5.1 / 2.1 say "A subdirectory is a workflow if it contains at least one **top-level** file (directly inside the subdirectory, not in nested subdirectories) with a supported extension"; the entry must be a **file**, not a directory whose name happens to end in `.sh`/`.ts`/`.js`/`.jsx`/`.tsx`. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh/` (a directory, not a file — created with `mkdir -p .loopx/ralph/index.sh`) alongside a valid sibling workflow `.loopx/other/index.sh` (so `.loopx/` discovers at least one workflow). The directory `.loopx/ralph/index.sh/` has no top-level files of any kind inside it (or is empty). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a missing-workflow / workflow-not-found error referring to `ralph` — `ralph` is not a discovered workflow because the only top-level entry inside it is a directory (which fails the "at least one top-level file with a supported extension" rule), not a missing-default-entry-point error (`ralph` does not exist as a workflow at all), (c) `.loopx/ralph/index.sh/` is silently ignored — no validation warning or error about its presence, (d) the sibling `.loopx/other/index.sh` is still discoverable, and `loopx run -n 1 other` succeeds. A buggy implementation that enumerated `.loopx/<dir>/` entries by name pattern (matching `*.sh` etc.) without checking whether the entry resolves to a regular file would wrongly classify `ralph/` as a workflow and surface a different error (e.g., "cannot execute `index.sh`: is a directory" at spawn time, or a discovery-level error confusingly reporting `index.sh` as a script). *(Spec 5.1, 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-09b**: A top-level directory inside a workflow whose name shapes as `script.<supported-ext>` (e.g., `check.ts`) is not discovered as a script alongside a real `index.sh` file. SPEC 5.1 says scripts are top-level **files** with supported extensions; a directory whose name happens to end in a supported extension is not a script. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (a real file, valid script) plus `.loopx/ralph/check.ts/` (a top-level directory whose name shape matches `*.ts` but is not a file — created with `mkdir -p .loopx/ralph/check.ts`). The directory may be empty or contain unrelated content (e.g., `.loopx/ralph/check.ts/notes.md`). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` (bare workflow, default entry). Assert: (a) exit code 0 — `ralph:index` runs (marker file written), (b) `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check` fails with exit code 1 and a "script not found" error — `check.ts` was a directory, not a file, so no `check` script was discovered. Additionally run `loopx run -h` and assert: (c) `ralph` is listed with `index` as a discovered script, (d) `check` does **not** appear in `ralph`'s script list, and (e) stderr contains no warning about `check.ts` being a directory — directory-shaped entries are silently ignored during script discovery, the same way nested subdirectories are ignored (T-DISC-14). A buggy implementation that lists names without checking entry types would wrongly add `check` to `ralph`'s script list and either fail to spawn it (a directory cannot be executed) or surface a confusing "script not found at spawn time" error. *(Spec 5.1, 2.1, 11.2)*
- **T-DISC-10**: Files placed directly in `.loopx/` are never discovered. `.loopx/loose-script.sh` exists alongside `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. `loopx run -n 1 loose-script` fails (not found). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` succeeds. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-11**: `.loopx/loose-script.ts` directly in `.loopx/` is not discovered even if it has a supported extension. Assert `loopx run -n 1 loose-script` fails. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-10a**: Flat-script explicit no-migration-warning test. `.loopx/loose-script.sh` exists alongside a valid workflow (`.loopx/ralph/index.sh`). `loopx run loose-script` fails with exit code 1 (not found). Additionally assert — **structurally** rather than by prose blacklisting — that stderr does not contain a migration-guidance or deprecation warning as a separate output category alongside the not-found error. Concretely: the stderr output is limited to the standard target-resolution error (and any unrelated warnings that would be emitted regardless of the flat-script sibling). There is no additional warning/notice/advisory line whose subject is the loose flat-script layout (the `.loopx/loose-script.sh` file itself is not mentioned in any extra category of output beyond, at most, the routine "target not found" message). Prose-level phrase matching is intentionally avoided — the check targets the presence of a distinct migration-warning output category, not specific wording. This is the flat-script counterpart to T-DISC-20c (legacy directory-script no-migration-warning), explicitly locking down the ADR-0003 consequence that no migration warnings are provided for any legacy layout. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-10b**: An invalid loose file directly under `.loopx/` is ignored, not just valid ones. `.loopx/-bad-name.sh` exists (a name that would fail name restriction validation if it were inside a workflow) alongside a valid workflow (`.loopx/ralph/index.sh`). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` succeeds with no errors or warnings about the invalid file. This proves flat-root files are completely outside discovery and validation — they are not scanned, named, or validated at all. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-10c**: A non-workflow subdirectory with an invalid name is ignored entirely — it is never validated. `.loopx/-bad-dir/` exists containing only `README.md` (no script files) alongside a valid workflow (`.loopx/ralph/index.sh`). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` succeeds with no errors or warnings about `-bad-dir`. Because `-bad-dir` has no top-level script files, it is not a workflow and its name is never checked against the naming restriction pattern. This is distinct from T-DISC-47b, where the invalid-named directory IS a workflow (contains scripts) and IS fatal. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-10d**: Same fixture as T-DISC-10c under `loopx run -h`. The non-workflow directory `-bad-dir` is neither listed in the discovered-workflows output nor warned about on stderr. Only `ralph` appears. This is the `-h` counterpart to T-DISC-10c, and the invalid-name counterpart to T-CLI-104a/104b (which use valid names for non-workflow subdirectories). *(Spec 5.1, 11.2)*
- **T-DISC-10e**: An invalid-named loose file at `.loopx/` root is ignored under `loopx run -h`. `.loopx/-bad-root-file.sh` exists alongside a valid workflow (`.loopx/ralph/index.sh`). `loopx run -h` lists `ralph` with no warnings about `-bad-root-file.sh` on stderr. This is the `-h` counterpart to T-DISC-10b (which covers run mode). *(Spec 5.1, 11.2)*
- **T-DISC-10f**: Same-name loose root file and workflow coexist. `.loopx/foo.sh` and `.loopx/foo/index.sh` both exist. `loopx run -n 1 foo` runs `foo:index` successfully (asserted via marker file). No collision or error is raised — `foo.sh` is a loose root file, ignored by discovery. This is the same-name variant of T-DISC-10 (which uses different names for the loose file and workflow). A leftover flat-script namespace implementation could pass T-DISC-10/T-DISC-11 and fail only here, where the loose file shares the workflow's basename. *(Spec 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-10g**: Same fixture as T-DISC-10f under `loopx run -h`. Assert: (a) `foo` appears as a discovered workflow, (b) `foo.sh` is neither listed nor warned about on stderr, and (c) no collision warning is raised. This is the help-mode counterpart to T-DISC-10f and the same-name variant of T-CLI-104 (which uses a different name for the loose file). *(Spec 5.1, 11.2)*

#### Script Discovery Within Workflows

- **T-DISC-12**: All top-level files with supported extensions inside a workflow are discovered as scripts. `.loopx/ralph/` contains `index.sh`, `check-ready.sh`, `setup.ts`. `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check-ready` runs `check-ready.sh`. `loopx run -n 1 ralph:setup` runs `setup.ts`. Assert via marker files. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-13**: Script name is the base name without extension. `.loopx/ralph/my-check.ts` → script name is `my-check`. `loopx run -n 1 ralph:my-check` runs it. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-14**: Files in subdirectories within a workflow are NOT discovered. `.loopx/ralph/lib/helpers.ts` exists. `loopx run -n 1 ralph:helpers` fails. The `lib/` subdirectory is ignored during script discovery. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-14b**: Files in workflow subdirectories are usable internally by workflow scripts, even though they are invisible to loopx discovery. Create `.loopx/ralph/index.ts` that imports `./lib/helpers.ts` (e.g., `import { greet } from "./lib/helpers.ts"`) and calls `output({ result: greet() })` with the imported value. Create `.loopx/ralph/lib/helpers.ts` exporting a function (e.g., `export function greet() { return "hello from helpers"; }`). Observe via `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1, cwd: project.dir })` — SPEC 7.1 specifies that the CLI never prints `result` to stdout, so the programmatic API is the correct observation surface for yielded `Output.result`. Assert: (a) the returned array's single `Output` has `result === "hello from helpers"` (proving the helper was successfully imported and its return value reached the yielded output), and (b) additionally, a direct `runCLI(["run", "-n", "1", "ralph:helpers"])` invocation exits non-zero (the subdirectory file is not a discovered script). This tests both halves of the Spec 2.1 statement that subdirectory files are "invisible to loopx and available for internal use by the workflow's scripts" — T-DISC-14 and T-DISC-14a cover "invisible," but not the positive "available for internal use" half. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1, 7.1)*
- **T-DISC-14a**: Nested invalid files inside a workflow do not trigger fatal validation. `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` plus `.loopx/ralph/lib/-bad.ts` (invalid script name) and `.loopx/ralph/lib/check.sh` + `.loopx/ralph/lib/check.ts` (same-base-name collision). `loopx run -n 1 ralph` succeeds (exit code 0). The nested files in `lib/` are not discovered and therefore not validated — invalid names and collisions in subdirectories do not trigger fatal validation errors. This is the runtime counterpart to T-INST-64a (which covers the same scenario at install time). *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-15**: Non-script files inside a workflow are allowed and ignored. `.loopx/ralph/` contains `index.ts`, `schema.json`, `README.md`. The non-script files do not cause warnings. `loopx run -n 1 ralph` works. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-15a**: Config-style file with a supported extension is discovered as a script (no exclusion mechanism). `.loopx/ralph/` contains `index.ts` and `eslint.config.js`. Discovery treats `eslint.config.js` as a script with base name `eslint.config`. The dot in `eslint.config` violates the name restriction pattern `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`. `loopx run ralph` fails (exit code 1) due to global validation catching the invalid script name. *(Spec 2.1, 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-15b**: Same setup as T-DISC-15a but via `loopx run -h`. The invalid script name `eslint.config` is reported as a non-fatal warning on stderr. Assert that the warning text contains the offending script name. Help still exits 0. This locks down the ADR-0003 rule that there is no exclusion mechanism — every top-level file with a supported extension is a discovered script, even config-style files. *(Spec 2.1, 5.3, 11.2)*
- **T-DISC-15c**: Uppercase script extensions (`.SH`, `.TS`, `.JS`) are not supported — they do not match the lowercase-only supported-extension set (`.sh`, `.ts`, `.js`) and are therefore not discovered as scripts, and a subdirectory whose only supported-extension-shaped files are uppercase is **not a discovered workflow at all** (SPEC 5.1: "A subdirectory is a workflow if it contains at least one top-level file ... with a supported extension"). SPEC 2.1 / 5.1 enumerate the supported script extensions in lowercase form; the rule is case-sensitive (extensions are filename-suffix matches under POSIX, where filenames are case-sensitive — `index.SH` and `index.sh` are distinct files). Set up `.loopx/ralph/` containing a single file `index.SH` (uppercase extension only, no lowercase counterpart) alongside a valid sibling workflow `.loopx/other/index.sh` (so `.loopx/` itself discovers at least one workflow and the run does not short-circuit on "missing `.loopx/`"). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 1 — not because `ralph` has no `index` script, but because `ralph` is not a discovered workflow at all (the only file in `.loopx/ralph/` has an unsupported extension, so the subdirectory fails the "contains at least one top-level file with a supported extension" workflow-detection rule and is silently ignored during discovery), (b) stderr surfaces a **missing-workflow / workflow-not-found** error referring to `ralph` (the exact wording is implementation-defined; the test does not pin a specific phrase but does require the failure category to be missing-workflow — **not** a missing-default-entry-point / missing-`index` error, and **not** a script-execution error), and (c) `index.SH` did not run (no marker file). Companion case: also run a variant with `.loopx/ralph/index.SH` **plus** `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (both files present) — in this case `ralph` **is** a discovered workflow (because `index.sh` has a supported extension), only `index.sh` is discovered and runs, and `index.SH` is silently ignored (no warning, no name-collision warning, since collisions are checked across the *recognized* extension set only). This pins down case-sensitive extension matching, which a buggy implementation that lowercased filenames before extension matching (a common bug under macOS where the underlying filesystem is sometimes case-insensitive but the extension-matching logic should not be) would fail. **Conditional on a case-sensitive filesystem** — on case-insensitive filesystems (e.g., default macOS HFS+ / APFS) the two filenames cannot coexist, so the companion case is skipped; the single-`.SH` case is runnable on either filesystem because the file simply does not match the supported extension set. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-DISC-16**: Workflow directory with no top-level supported script files — only supported-extension files in subdirectories (e.g., `.loopx/ralph/lib/helpers.ts` exists but no `.loopx/ralph/*.{sh,js,jsx,ts,tsx}`) — is not recognized as a valid workflow. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*

#### Default Entry Point

- **T-DISC-17**: `loopx run ralph` runs `ralph:index`. Create `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and `.loopx/ralph/check.sh`. Assert `loopx run -n 1 ralph` runs `index.sh` (via marker file), not `check.sh`. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-18**: `loopx run ralph:index` is equivalent to `loopx run ralph`. Assert both produce the same marker file content. *(Spec 2.1, 4.1)*
- **T-DISC-19**: Workflow with no `index` script: `loopx run ralph` → error (exit code 1). `loopx run ralph:check` with `check.sh` present → succeeds. *(Spec 2.1, 4.1)*
- **T-DISC-20**: `index` is not otherwise special. It can be the target of `goto`, follows collision rules, can `goto` other scripts. Create a chain: `ralph:index` → goto `check` → `ralph:check` produces stop. Assert both scripts execute. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-20a**: `package.json` `main` field is ignored for entry point resolution. Create `.loopx/ralph/` with `package.json` containing `{ "main": "check.ts" }`, `index.ts`, and `check.ts`. `loopx run -n 1 ralph` runs `index.ts`, not `check.ts`. Assert via marker files that `index.ts` executed and `check.ts` did not. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-20b**: `package.json` `main` field does not provide a fallback entry point. Create `.loopx/ralph/` with `package.json` containing `{ "main": "check.ts" }` and `check.ts` but NO `index` script. `loopx run ralph` → error (exit code 1, missing default entry point). The `main` field is ignored. *(Spec 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-20c**: A legacy-style directory-script layout is not discovered as a workflow, and loopx does not emit migration warnings. Create `.loopx/mypipeline/` with `package.json` containing `{ "main": "src/run.js" }` and no top-level files with supported script extensions (place `run.js` inside a `src/` subdirectory so no top-level supported-extension files exist). The subdirectory is not a workflow (no top-level supported-extension files). `loopx run mypipeline` fails with exit code 1. Additionally assert — **structurally** rather than by prose blacklisting — that stderr does not contain a migration-guidance or deprecation warning as a separate output category alongside the not-found error. Concretely: the stderr output is limited to the standard target-resolution error (and any unrelated warnings that would be emitted regardless of the directory-script sibling). There is no additional warning/notice/advisory line whose subject is the legacy directory-script layout at `.loopx/mypipeline/` (the `main` field, the nested `src/run.js` file, and the presence of `package.json` without top-level script files are not flagged in any extra output category beyond, at most, the routine "target not found" message). Prose-level phrase matching is intentionally avoided — the check targets the presence of a distinct migration-warning output category, not specific wording. The spec states legacy layouts are simply not recognized — no migration tooling or warnings are provided. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1)*

#### Name Collisions Within Workflows

- **T-DISC-21**: `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` and `.loopx/ralph/check.ts` both exist → `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check` refuses to start with error listing the conflicting entries. Exit code 1. *(Spec 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-21a**: `index` follows the same collision rules as any other script. `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and `.loopx/ralph/index.ts` both exist → `loopx run -n 1 ralph` refuses to start with error listing the conflicting `index` entries. Exit code 1. A bad implementation could special-case `index` and still pass collision tests that only use non-index names (T-DISC-21). *(Spec 2.1, 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-22**: Same-base-name collision in one workflow is fatal for any target in any workflow. `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` and `.loopx/ralph/check.ts` exist, `.loopx/other/index.sh` is valid. `loopx run -n 1 other` still fails because global validation catches ralph's collision. *(Spec 5.2, 5.4)*
- **T-DISC-23**: Same base names in **different** workflows are allowed. `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` and `.loopx/other/check.ts` coexist without collision. Both `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check` and `loopx run -n 1 other:check` succeed. *(Spec 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-24**: Non-conflicting scripts within the same workflow → no error. `.loopx/ralph/alpha.sh` and `.loopx/ralph/beta.ts` coexist. *(Spec 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-24a**: Same-base-name collision immunity for unsupported `.mjs` extension. SPEC 5.2's name-collision rule applies to **discovered** scripts only, and SPEC 2.1 / T-DISC-06 / T-DISC-07a establish that `.mjs` files are not discovered as scripts. A workflow containing `.loopx/ralph/check.ts` (a discovered script) plus `.loopx/ralph/check.mjs` (an unsupported-extension sibling sharing the same base name) must therefore **not** be flagged as a collision — `check.mjs` was never a discovery candidate, so there is no second `check` script to collide with. T-DISC-21 / T-DISC-21a cover the genuine same-base collision (two **discovered** extensions); T-DISC-07a covers `.mjs` non-discovery in the absence of a same-base sibling. This test pins down the intersection: same-base sibling + unsupported extension ≠ collision. Set up `.loopx/ralph/check.ts` (a discovered script that writes a marker file and emits `{"stop":true}`) and `.loopx/ralph/check.mjs` (any content — its content is irrelevant since it is never discovered). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the marker file exists (`check.ts` ran — not blocked by a spurious collision), (c) stderr contains no collision warning or error referencing `check.mjs` (the `.mjs` file is silently ignored, not flagged as a same-base conflict per the SPEC 5.2 collision rule's "discovered scripts" scope), and (d) `loopx run -n 1 ralph:check` resolves to `check.ts` deterministically — a buggy implementation that extension-fold-matched on base name (so `check.mjs` and `check.ts` were both treated as `check`) would either reject install with a same-base-collision error or pick one extension non-deterministically, both of which fail this test. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1, 5.2)*
- **T-DISC-24b**: Same-base-name collision immunity for unsupported `.cjs` extension. `.cjs` counterpart to T-DISC-24a — same fixture pattern (`.loopx/ralph/check.ts` + `.loopx/ralph/check.cjs`), same assertions: `ralph:check` runs `check.ts`, no collision flag for `check.cjs`. T-DISC-07b covers `.cjs` non-discovery in isolation; this test pins down the same-base-sibling case for `.cjs` to mirror T-DISC-24a's `.mjs` coverage. Together with T-DISC-24a, this closes the unsupported-extension same-base-collision-immunity coverage for both `.mjs` and `.cjs`. *(Spec 2.1, 5.1, 5.2)*

#### Workflow and Script Naming

- **T-DISC-25**: Workflow name restrictions follow `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`. `.loopx/my-workflow/index.sh` → valid. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-26**: `.loopx/_underscore/index.sh` → valid workflow name. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-26a**: `.loopx/1flow/index.sh` → valid workflow name (digit first). `loopx run -n 1 1flow` succeeds and runs the script. Assert via marker file. This mirrors T-DISC-30a (digit-start script name) but covers the workflow-name validation path — an implementation could wrongly require workflows to start with a letter or underscore. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-26b**: `.loopx/42/index.sh` → valid workflow name (all digits). `loopx run -n 1 42` succeeds and runs the script. Assert via marker file. This mirrors T-DISC-30b (all-digit script name) but covers the workflow-name validation path. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-27**: `.loopx/-startswithdash/index.sh` → invalid workflow name, error. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-28**: `.loopx/has space/index.sh` → invalid workflow name (space not allowed). *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-29**: `.loopx/has.dot/index.sh` → invalid workflow name (dot not allowed). *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-30**: Script name `check-ready` (hyphen in middle) → valid. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-30a**: Script name `1start` (digit first) → valid per `[a-zA-Z0-9_]`. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-30b**: Script name `42` (all digits) → valid. *(Spec 5.3)*
- **T-DISC-30c**: Uppercase workflow name is valid and runnable. SPEC 5.3's name pattern `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*` explicitly allows uppercase ASCII letters — the existing T-DISC-25 / T-DISC-26 / T-DISC-26a / T-DISC-26b coverage exercises hyphenated, underscore, and digit-first forms, but not uppercase positives. Set up `.loopx/MyWorkflow/index.sh` (mixed-case workflow name, starting with an uppercase letter) that writes a marker file on execution. Run `loopx run -n 1 MyWorkflow` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the marker file exists (proving the workflow was discovered and `index.sh` ran), and (c) stderr contains no validation warnings for the workflow name. An implementation that lowercased workflow names during validation — or that treated the name pattern as case-insensitive and folded before matching — would fail to find `MyWorkflow` when the user typed it as `MyWorkflow` (under a case-sensitive filesystem). *(Spec 5.3, 2.1)*
- **T-DISC-30d**: Uppercase script base name is valid and targetable via a qualified `workflow:Script` target. Set up `.loopx/ralph/CheckReady.sh` (a non-`index` script with a mixed-case base name) alongside `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (the default entry point, present so the workflow is discovered without ambiguity and the target is explicitly the uppercase one). `CheckReady.sh` writes a marker file on execution and then emits `{ "stop": true }` via `$LOOPX_BIN output --stop`. Run `loopx run -n 1 "ralph:CheckReady"` via the CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the marker file exists (proving `ralph:CheckReady` was the resolved target and the script ran — not `ralph:index`), and (c) stderr contains no validation warnings for the script name. This is the script-name counterpart to T-DISC-30c, pinning down that uppercase script base names are accepted by both the name-pattern validator and the qualified-target parser. *(Spec 5.3, 2.1, 4.1)*
