Together with T-TMP-24a (`.next()`) and T-TMP-24c (`.return()`), this completes tmpdir-cleanup-before-settlement coverage across all three settlement interactions enumerated in SPEC 9.3's abort-after-final-yield clause for the **`maxIterations`-reached** final-yield trigger. *(Spec 7.4, 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-24e**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield runs tmpdir cleanup before `.next()` surfaces the abort. Cleanup-order counterpart to T-API-66c, mirroring T-TMP-24a's relationship to T-API-66. SPEC 9.3 enumerates `stop: true` and `maxIterations` reached symmetrically as final-yield triggers, and SPEC 7.4 pairs each abort-after-final-yield settlement with tmpdir cleanup before the abort surfaces. T-TMP-24a / T-TMP-24c / T-TMP-24d cover the `maxIterations`-reached trigger across the three settlement interactions; this test closes the `stop: true` trigger × `.next()` settlement combination. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker file (path external to the tmpdir, so it survives cleanup for the post-throw read), then emits `{"stop":true}` on its first iteration. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next(); /* first.value.stop === true */ c.abort(); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` After the rejection, read the tmpdir path from the marker and assert it no longer exists on disk — cleanup ran before the abort surfaced through `.next()`, matching SPEC 7.4's "before the generator throws" rule. *(Spec 7.4, 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-24f**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield runs tmpdir cleanup before `.return()` surfaces the abort. Cleanup-order counterpart to T-API-66d. Same fixture as T-TMP-24e. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next(); c.abort(); await expect(gen.return(undefined)).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` After the rejection, assert the tmpdir path no longer exists on disk. Together with T-TMP-24e (`.next()`) on the same final-yield trigger, this pins down the cleanup-before-settlement contract for the `.return()` settlement on the `stop: true`-driven path. *(Spec 7.4, 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-24g**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield runs tmpdir cleanup before `.throw()` surfaces the abort. Cleanup-order counterpart to T-API-66e. Same fixture as T-TMP-24e. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next(); c.abort(); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("consumer-err"))).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` (the abort displaces the consumer-supplied error per SPEC 9.3, matching T-API-66e). After the rejection, assert the tmpdir path no longer exists on disk. Together with T-TMP-24e (`.next()`), T-TMP-24f (`.return()`), T-TMP-24a / T-TMP-24c / T-TMP-24d (the `maxIterations`-reached trigger across the same three settlement interactions), this completes the tmpdir-cleanup-before-settlement coverage matrix across all three settlement interactions × both final-yield triggers (`stop: true` and `maxIterations` reached) enumerated in SPEC 9.3's abort-after-final-yield clause. *(Spec 7.4, 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-24b**: External SIGKILL to loopx itself does not run tmpdir cleanup. Use `runCLIWithSignal` with a fixture that writes both `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and the child PID to marker files, writes `"ready"` to stderr, and then sleeps. After reading the tmpdir path from the marker, send `SIGKILL` to the loopx process itself. Assert: (a) the process terminates due to `SIGKILL`, and (b) the tmpdir path still exists on disk after loopx dies — loopx had no chance to run cleanup. The harness then explicitly kills the orphaned child PID as test cleanup. This covers the SPEC 7.4 "cleanup does not run when loopx itself is killed via SIGKILL" clause. *(Spec 7.4)*

##### Tmpdir Parent Snapshot Timing

**Literal-path expectation (applies to every test in this subsection and to the env-file / `RunOptions.env` tmpdir-parent tests T-TMP-28 / T-TMP-28a / T-TMP-28b / T-TMP-29 / T-TMP-29a–29k).** Test descriptions in this block use illustrative literal paths like `/tmp/loopx-test-parent-A`, `/tmp/loopx-parent-A`, `/tmp/right-parent`, `/tmp/wrong-parent`, etc., for readability. **Each individual test must allocate the actual paths via `fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "loopx-test-parent-"))` (or equivalent per-test mkdtemp helper) at test setup**, then thread the allocated absolute paths through the `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` configuration and the assertions — the literal `/tmp/loopx-...` strings in the descriptions are placeholders, not contract paths. This requirement reinforces the section 4.7 "Test-parent isolation (flakiness guidance — applies suite-wide)" preface for the snapshot-timing block specifically: concurrent test workers must not race on a fixed `/tmp/loopx-...` parent, and stale entries from prior runs must not be miscounted into the snapshot-timing assertions. The "wrong" candidate path (the one loopx must **not** consult, e.g., the env-file value in T-TMP-28 / T-TMP-29 / T-TMP-29a) may be either pre-created via `fs.mkdtemp` or a synthesized non-existent path, since loopx must not consult it; the "right" candidate path (the one loopx **must** consult — typically the inherited-env value) must always be a real `fs.mkdtemp`-allocated directory because loopx's `os.tmpdir()` pass will attempt to `mkdtemp` into it. Test descriptions retain the literal-path notation purely for readability; the test implementation must substitute mkdtemp-allocated paths.

- **T-TMP-25**: CLI — tmpdir parent determined pre-iteration. **Pre-create a writable directory at `/tmp/loopx-test-parent-A/`** before invoking loopx (e.g., via `mkdirSync(parentPath, { recursive: true })` in the test setup), so the tmpdir creation step has a valid parent to `mkdtemp` into. Set `TMPDIR=/tmp/loopx-test-parent-A` in the CLI environment. Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` with fixture observing `LOOPX_TMPDIR`. Assert the tmpdir lives under `/tmp/loopx-test-parent-A/` — the parent was captured at pre-iteration time from loopx's own inherited env. (Per the literal-path expectation above, the test allocates the parent via `fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "loopx-test-parent-"))` and substitutes the result for the literal `/tmp/loopx-test-parent-A` placeholder in the description.) *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-25a**: Tmpdir parent via `TEMP` when `TMPDIR` is unset. SPEC 7.4 specifies the tmpdir parent is `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in loopx's own process — `os.tmpdir()` documents reading `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` (in platform-conventional order) from `process.env`. This test covers the `TEMP` branch. Pre-create a writable directory (e.g., `/tmp/loopx-temp-env-parent/`). Spawn loopx with its inherited environment configured so `TMPDIR` is **unset**, `TMP` is **unset**, and `TEMP` is set to the pre-created directory. Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` with fixture observing `LOOPX_TMPDIR`. Assert the tmpdir lives under the `TEMP` directory **iff** the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise (and as a conservative shared assertion across runtimes) assert that the tmpdir's parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in a child Node/Bun process spawned with the same inherited environment. This keeps the test honest about runtime variation — on platforms/runtimes where `TEMP` is consulted, the assertion is exact; elsewhere it still proves loopx follows `os.tmpdir()` rather than hard-coding `TMPDIR`. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-25b**: Tmpdir parent via `TMP` when `TMPDIR` and `TEMP` are unset. Same pattern as T-TMP-25a for the `TMP` branch. Pre-create a writable directory (e.g., `/tmp/loopx-tmp-env-parent/`). Spawn loopx with its inherited environment configured so `TMPDIR` and `TEMP` are **unset** and `TMP` is set to the pre-created directory. Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` with fixture observing `LOOPX_TMPDIR`. Assert the tmpdir lives under the `TMP` directory **iff** the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert the tmpdir's parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process. Together with T-TMP-25 (`TMPDIR` set) and T-TMP-25a (`TEMP` set), this closes the three-variable coverage for `os.tmpdir()` inputs named in SPEC 7.4. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-26**: `run()` — tmpdir parent captured lazily on first `next()`. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-parent-B/`) before driving the test, so whichever the snapshot resolves to is a valid `mkdtemp` target. `process.env.TMPDIR = "/tmp/loopx-parent-A"; const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TMPDIR = "/tmp/loopx-parent-B"; await gen.next();` (script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`). Assert: the tmpdir lives under `/tmp/loopx-parent-B` — the mutation between `run()` returning and first `next()` does affect the parent (the snapshot is lazy). *(Spec 7.4, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-27**: `runPromise()` — tmpdir parent captured eagerly at call site. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-parent-B/`) before driving the test, so whichever the snapshot resolves to is a valid `mkdtemp` target. `process.env.TMPDIR = "/tmp/loopx-parent-A"; const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TMPDIR = "/tmp/loopx-parent-B"; await p;` (script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`). Assert: the tmpdir lives under `/tmp/loopx-parent-A` — the mutation *after* `runPromise()` returned did not redirect the parent (eager snapshot at call site). *(Spec 7.4, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-26-temp**: `run()` programmatic snapshot-timing variant for `TEMP` (TMPDIR unset). Per SPEC 7.4 the tmpdir parent is `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in loopx's own `process.env`, captured on the same schedule as the inherited-env snapshot — lazy under `run()`. T-TMP-26 covers `TMPDIR`; this test parameterizes the lazy-snapshot timing axis over `TEMP`. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B/`) before driving the test. Configure the driver process's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TMP; process.env.TEMP = "/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A"; const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TEMP = "/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B"; await gen.next();` Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B` **iff** the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert the parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process spawned with `TEMP=/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B`. The mutation between `run()` returning and first `next()` redirects the parent on runtimes where `TEMP` is consulted — proving lazy-snapshot semantics for `TEMP` symmetrically with T-TMP-26 for `TMPDIR`. **Test must restore the original `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` values in a `finally` block.** *(Spec 7.4, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-26-tmp**: `run()` programmatic snapshot-timing variant for `TMP` (TMPDIR and TEMP unset). Same pattern as T-TMP-26-temp for the `TMP` branch. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-B/`). Configure the driver process's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TEMP; process.env.TMP = "/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A"; const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TMP = "/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-B"; await gen.next();` Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25b: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-B` iff the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TMP` in this configuration; otherwise anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process. Together with T-TMP-26 (`TMPDIR`) and T-TMP-26-temp (`TEMP`), this closes the lazy-snapshot timing × three-variable coverage on the `run()` surface. **Restore original env values in `finally`.** *(Spec 7.4, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-27-temp**: `runPromise()` programmatic snapshot-timing variant for `TEMP` (TMPDIR unset). Per SPEC 7.4 / 9.2 the tmpdir parent under `runPromise()` is captured eagerly at the call site. T-TMP-27 covers `TMPDIR`; this test parameterizes the eager-snapshot timing axis over `TEMP`. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B/`) before invocation. Configure the driver process's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TMP; process.env.TEMP = "/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A"; const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TEMP = "/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-B"; await p;` Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A` iff the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert the parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process spawned with `TEMP=/tmp/loopx-temp-parent-A` (the **pre-mutation** value). The mutation *after* `runPromise()` returned does not redirect the parent on runtimes where `TEMP` is consulted — proving eager-snapshot semantics for `TEMP` symmetrically with T-TMP-27 for `TMPDIR`. **Restore original env values in `finally`.** *(Spec 7.4, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-27-tmp**: `runPromise()` programmatic snapshot-timing variant for `TMP` (TMPDIR and TEMP unset). Same pattern as T-TMP-27-temp for the `TMP` branch. **Pre-create both candidate parent directories** (`/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A/` and `/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-B/`). Configure inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TEMP; process.env.TMP = "/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A"; const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); process.env.TMP = "/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-B"; await p;` Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25b: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A` iff the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TMP` in this configuration; otherwise anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process spawned with `TMP=/tmp/loopx-tmp-parent-A`. Together with T-TMP-27 (`TMPDIR`) and T-TMP-27-temp (`TEMP`), this closes the eager-snapshot timing × three-variable coverage on the `runPromise()` surface. **Restore original env values in `finally`.** *(Spec 7.4, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-28**: `TMPDIR` from a global env file does **not** redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent, but **does** reach the spawned script. Place `TMPDIR=/tmp/wrong-parent` in a global env file. Set the loopx inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/right-parent`. Pre-create both `/tmp/wrong-parent` and `/tmp/right-parent` (at the test-isolated parent's level — see section 4.7's tmpdir test-parent isolation guidance) so the test does not rely on the literal `/tmp/...` paths existing. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes both `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMPDIR` via `observe-env` into separate marker files, then emits `stop:true`. Assert: (a) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` — loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection used the inherited `TMPDIR` snapshot from loopx's own `process.env`, not the global env-file value, per Spec 7.4 "`TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` entries in env files or `RunOptions.env` reach spawned scripts but do not mutate loopx's own `process.env` and therefore do not redirect the tmpdir parent", and (b) the script's observed `TMPDIR` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` — the global env-file value still reaches the spawned script's environment (per Spec 8.3 global-env-file injection tier), proving the global env-file entry is delivered as a regular environment variable to the child even though it does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. T-TMP-29a covers the same two-pronged contract on the local (`-e`) env-file tier; T-TMP-28a / T-TMP-28b cover the same contract for `TEMP` / `TMP`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-28a**: `TEMP` from a global env file does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. SPEC 7.4 names `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` together as the variables `os.tmpdir()` reads; the "env files / `RunOptions.env` do not redirect tmpdir parent" rule applies symmetrically across all three. T-TMP-28 covers `TMPDIR` only; this test parameterizes the global env-file tier over `TEMP`. Same structure as T-TMP-28: place `TEMP=/tmp/wrong-parent` in a global env file; configure loopx's inherited environment with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, and `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`; pre-create both directories. Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` against a fixture that observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP`. Apply runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` **iff** the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR`'s parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child Node/Bun process spawned with the same inherited environment (this anchors the assertion on loopx's `os.tmpdir()` call rather than on a hard-coded `TEMP` semantic, keeping the test honest across runtimes). Always assert (b): the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` — the global env-file value reaches the spawned script's environment regardless of whether loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection consulted it. A buggy implementation that mutated loopx's own `process.env` from the global env file would redirect `os.tmpdir()` to `/tmp/wrong-parent` and fail (a) under any runtime that consults `TEMP`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-28b**: `TMP` from a global env file does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Same pattern as T-TMP-28a for the `TMP` branch: configure loopx's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`, and a global env file containing `TMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`; pre-create both directories. Run loopx and observe `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMP`. Apply the same runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b): assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` iff the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TMP` in this configuration; otherwise anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process. Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-TMP-28 (`TMPDIR`) and T-TMP-28a (`TEMP`), this closes the global env-file tier × three-variable coverage for the SPEC 7.4 "env files do not redirect tmpdir parent" rule. *(Spec 7.4, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29**: `TMPDIR` from `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** (the inherited-env value, which is the parent loopx's own `os.tmpdir()` selects) before invocation; the wrong-parent value need not exist on disk because loopx must not consult it. Set loopx inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/right-parent`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { TMPDIR: "/tmp/wrong-parent" } })`. Script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`. Assert tmpdir lives under `/tmp/right-parent`. `RunOptions.env` merges into the child environment; it does not mutate loopx's own `process.env`. T-TMP-29b / T-TMP-29c cover the same contract for `TEMP` / `TMP`. *(Spec 7.4, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29b**: `TEMP` from `RunOptions.env` does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. SPEC 7.4 / 9.5 apply the env-files-and-`RunOptions.env`-do-not-redirect-tmpdir rule symmetrically across `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP`. T-TMP-29 covers `TMPDIR`; this test parameterizes `RunOptions.env` over `TEMP`. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure the driver process's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { TEMP: "/tmp/wrong-parent" } })`. Script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP` via `observe-env`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` iff the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert the parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process. Always assert the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` (the `RunOptions.env` value reached the spawned script's environment per the SPEC 8.3 `RunOptions.env` tier). *(Spec 7.4, 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29c**: `TMP` from `RunOptions.env` does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Same pattern as T-TMP-29b for the `TMP` branch: **pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation; configure inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`; call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { TMP: "/tmp/wrong-parent" } })`. Apply the same runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b). Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-TMP-29 (`TMPDIR`) and T-TMP-29b (`TEMP`), this closes the `RunOptions.env` tier × three-variable coverage. *(Spec 7.4, 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29a**: `TMPDIR` from a local (`-e`) env file does **not** redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Set loopx inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file via `writeEnvFileRaw` containing `TMPDIR=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes both `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMPDIR` via `observe-env`, performs an in-script `stat` of the observed `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path (during execution, recording "exists as a directory" to a marker), then emits `stop:true`. Run `loopx run -e <envfile> -n 1 ralph`. Assert: (a) the child's observed `TMPDIR` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` (local env-file tier injected the override into the spawned script's environment per Spec 8.3), (b) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` (loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection used the inherited `TMPDIR` snapshot from loopx's own `process.env`, not the local env-file value — per Spec 7.4 "`TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` entries in env files or `RunOptions.env` reach spawned scripts but do not mutate loopx's own `process.env` and therefore do not redirect the tmpdir parent"), and (c) the during-run stat marker records "exists as a directory". T-TMP-28 covers the global env-file tier and T-TMP-29 covers `RunOptions.env`; this test closes the local `-e` tier for `TMPDIR`. T-TMP-29d / T-TMP-29e parameterize the local `-e` tier over `TEMP` / `TMP`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29d**: `TEMP` from a local (`-e`) env file does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Local `-e` tier counterpart to T-TMP-28a (global env file) and T-TMP-29b (`RunOptions.env`). **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure loopx's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file via `writeEnvFileRaw` containing `TEMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP`. Run `loopx run -e <envfile> -n 1 ralph`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a (anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process when the active runtime does not consult `TEMP`). Always assert the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29e**: `TMP` from a local (`-e`) env file does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Same pattern as T-TMP-29d for the `TMP` branch: **pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation; inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`; local env file `TMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b). Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-TMP-28a/28b, T-TMP-29b/29c, and T-TMP-29d, this closes the SPEC 7.4 "env files / `RunOptions.env` do not redirect tmpdir parent" rule across the **CLI**-driven injection tiers and all three variables (`TMPDIR`, `TEMP`, `TMP`). T-TMP-29f / T-TMP-29g / T-TMP-29h close the equivalent rule for the **programmatic `runPromise({ envFile })`** surface. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3)*
- **T-TMP-29f**: `TMPDIR` from a programmatic local env file (`runPromise({ envFile })`) does **not** redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Programmatic counterpart to T-TMP-29a (CLI `-e`). T-TMP-29 covers `RunOptions.env`, T-TMP-28 / 28a / 28b cover the global env-file tier, and T-TMP-29a / 29d / 29e cover the CLI `-e` tier; this test fills the programmatic `RunOptions.envFile` tier — distinct from `RunOptions.env` because env files are loaded by reading the file at the resolved path during pre-iteration, while `RunOptions.env` is captured as a shallow copy at the call site. A buggy implementation that funneled local env-file values into loopx's own `process.env` (or otherwise allowed file-loaded `TMPDIR` entries to redirect the tmpdir parent) when the file was supplied programmatically — but not when supplied via CLI `-e` — would pass T-TMP-29a but fail this test. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure loopx's inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file via `writeEnvFileRaw` containing `TMPDIR=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes both `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMPDIR` via `observe-env`, then emits `stop:true`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) the child's observed `TMPDIR` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` (programmatic local env-file tier injected the override into the spawned script's environment per Spec 8.3 tier 3), and (b) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` (loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection used the inherited `TMPDIR` snapshot, not the local env-file value, per Spec 7.4). *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29g**: `TEMP` from a programmatic local env file (`runPromise({ envFile })`) does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Programmatic counterpart to T-TMP-29d (CLI `-e` × `TEMP`). **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure loopx's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file containing `TEMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 })`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a (anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process when the active runtime does not consult `TEMP`). Always assert the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29h**: `TMP` from a programmatic local env file (`runPromise({ envFile })`) does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. Programmatic counterpart to T-TMP-29e (CLI `-e` × `TMP`). **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`; local env file `TMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 })`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b). Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-TMP-29f and T-TMP-29g, this closes the programmatic `RunOptions.envFile` × `runPromise()` × three-variable coverage. T-TMP-29i / T-TMP-29j / T-TMP-29k extend the same contract to the `run()` generator surface. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29i**: `TMPDIR` from a programmatic local env file on the `run()` **generator surface** (`run("ralph", { envFile, maxIterations: 1 })`) does **not** redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. `run()` counterpart to T-TMP-29f (which exercises the same contract on `runPromise()`). Although both `run()` and `runPromise()` share the SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 lazy-vs-eager option-snapshot timing distinction, the env-file loading step itself runs inside the same pre-iteration sequence on both surfaces — but `run()` defers it to first `next()` while `runPromise()` runs it as part of the eager pre-iteration pipeline. A buggy implementation that (a) loaded the env file into loopx's own `process.env` only on the lazy `run()` first-`next()` path (e.g., to "refresh" the parent at first `next()` after the lazy snapshot) but not on the eager `runPromise()` path, or (b) routed the file-loaded values into the tmpdir-parent selection only on the `run()` generator surface, would pass T-TMP-29f but fail this test. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure loopx's inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file via `writeEnvFileRaw` containing `TMPDIR=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes both `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMPDIR` via `observe-env`, then emits `stop:true`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const out of gen) { /* drain */ }` (or equivalent — full settlement is required so cleanup runs and the marker reads can complete deterministically). Assert: (a) the child's observed `TMPDIR` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` (the local env-file value reaches the spawned script's environment per Spec 8.3 tier 3 on the `run()` generator surface, identical to `runPromise()` per Spec 9.1 / 9.2 / 8.3 — `run()` does not skip env-file loading just because option capture is lazy), and (b) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` (loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection used the inherited `TMPDIR` snapshot captured at first `next()` per SPEC 7.4's lazy-snapshot rule for `run()`, not the local env-file value, per Spec 7.4 "`TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` entries in env files or `RunOptions.env` reach spawned scripts but do not mutate loopx's own `process.env` and therefore do not redirect the tmpdir parent"). *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29j**: `TEMP` from a programmatic local env file on the `run()` **generator surface** does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. `run()` counterpart to T-TMP-29g (`runPromise()` × `TEMP`). **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure loopx's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Create a local env file containing `TEMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const out of gen) { /* drain */ }`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a (anchor on `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process when the active runtime does not consult `TEMP`). Always assert the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-TMP-29k**: `TMP` from a programmatic local env file on the `run()` **generator surface** does not redirect loopx's own tmpdir parent. `run()` counterpart to T-TMP-29h (`runPromise()` × `TMP`). **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`; local env file `TMP=/tmp/wrong-parent`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<envfile-path>", maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const out of gen) { /* drain */ }`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b). Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-TMP-29i and T-TMP-29j, this closes the programmatic `RunOptions.envFile` × `run()` × three-variable coverage on the generator surface, completing the SPEC 7.4 "env files / `RunOptions.env` do not redirect tmpdir parent" rule across **all four injection tiers** (global env file, CLI `-e`, programmatic `RunOptions.envFile` on both `runPromise()` and `run()`, `RunOptions.env`) × **both programmatic surfaces** (`runPromise()` via T-TMP-29f / T-TMP-29g / T-TMP-29h, `run()` via T-TMP-29i / T-TMP-29j / T-TMP-29k) × **all three variables**. *(Spec 7.4, 8.2, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*

##### Cleanup Safety (Identity-Fingerprint, Symlink / Non-Directory / Mismatched Directory)

SPEC 7.4 "Identity-fingerprint cleanup safety" defines normative rules for cleanup dispatch: symlinks are unlinked without following, regular files and mismatched directories are left with a warning, and nested symlink entries are unlinked but not traversed. SPEC 7.4 also specifies that **loopx does not reap stale tmpdirs at startup** and **does not chase renamed-away tmpdirs**. These rules are black-box testable and must be covered.

All tests in this block use a fixture that observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker, then performs the relevant filesystem manipulation on the reported path (rename, replacement with symlink, replacement with regular file, or nested symlink creation) before loopx exits. Tests inspect the filesystem after loopx exits to verify the safety rules held.

- **T-TMP-32**: No stale-tmpdir reaping at startup. Before running loopx, pre-create a directory matching the `loopx-*` naming pattern under the test-isolated tmpdir parent selected via `TMPDIR` (per the section 4.7 tmpdir-block preface guidance: set `TMPDIR` to a per-test isolated parent directory, then create `<TMPDIR>/loopx-stale-xyz/` with a marker file inside). Run `loopx run -n 1 ralph` against a normal fixture, with the same `TMPDIR` configured in loopx's inherited environment so loopx's own tmpdir creation also lives under that parent. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the pre-created `loopx-stale-xyz/` directory still exists with its marker file intact, and (b) loopx's own freshly-created `loopx-*` directory (observed via `observe-env` on `LOOPX_TMPDIR`) has been cleaned up. Per SPEC 7.4 "loopx does not reap stale tmpdirs at startup". The test-isolated parent (rather than the shared `os.tmpdir()` default) is required by the suite-wide flakiness-guidance preface — concurrent test workers under `/tmp` would race against this test's stale-entry observation. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-33**: Renamed-away tmpdir is not chased, and no cleanup warning is emitted for the resulting ENOENT at the original path. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, writes a marker `initialized` into it, then renames the tmpdir directory itself (e.g., `mv "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR-renamed"` — performed before the script exits), then emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the original tmpdir path (which is now gone due to the rename) does not exist, (b) the renamed path (`-renamed` suffix) **still exists** with the `initialized` marker intact — loopx did not chase the renamed directory and did not remove it, and (c) stderr contains **no cleanup-related warning** for the ENOENT at the original path — per SPEC 7.4's "If `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` no longer exists at cleanup time, cleanup is a no-op", a missing tmpdir at cleanup time is the expected condition when the script renamed it away, not a warning-worthy anomaly. T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-36 pin down that mismatched-directory and regular-file replacements do emit exactly one warning; this test pins down the symmetric rule that ENOENT-at-cleanup is silent (no warning emitted), so that warning cardinality across the cleanup-dispatch tree is fully characterized. Per SPEC 7.4 "A script that removes or renames its tmpdir during the run defeats automatic cleanup of the moved directory; loopx does not chase renamed tmpdirs." *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-34**: Cleanup of a symlink replacement removes the symlink entry only, not the target, and emits **no** cleanup warning. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, writes a marker `target-marker` inside an external directory (e.g., `$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/target-survives/target-marker`), then `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `ln -s "$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/target-survives" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"`, then emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path no longer exists (loopx unlinked the symlink entry), (b) the external `target-survives/` directory still exists with `target-marker` intact (loopx did not follow the symlink target), and (c) **stderr contains no cleanup-related warning** — SPEC 7.4 enumerates warning emission only on cleanup-failure (lstat fails, unlink fails, recursive removal fails) or non-directory non-symlink at path (rule 3) or mismatched directory (rule 5); the symlink branch (rule 2) is a successful unlink and produces no warning. T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-36 pin down that rules 3 and 5 do emit exactly one warning, and T-TMP-33 pins down that the ENOENT branch (rule 1) emits no warning; this assertion closes the same warning-cardinality contract for the symlink-success branch, distinguishing "symlink unlinked successfully" from "symlink unlink failed" (which would emit a single warning per the cleanup-failure clause). The cleanup-warning text format is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; the assertion here is **negative** (no cleanup-related warning emitted at all on stderr), not text-pattern-specific. Per SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 2: "Path is a symlink: unlink the symlink entry; do not follow the target." *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-35**: Cleanup leaves a regular-file replacement in place with a warning. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, then `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `printf '%s' "regular-file-replacement" > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` (tmpdir path is now a regular file), then emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists and is a regular file with content `regular-file-replacement` (the filesystem-safety outcome per SPEC 7.4 rule 3: the regular file was not unlinked), (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality), and (c) the CLI exit code is 0 (the cleanup warning does not affect the exit code). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4; do **not** require it to mention the path, the type, or any specific phrasing — only that a single cleanup-related warning is emitted. Per SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 3: "Path is a regular file ... : leave in place with a stderr warning." *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-35a**: `runPromise()` outcome is unaffected by a cleanup warning (regular-file replacement). Programmatic-API counterpart to T-TMP-35. SPEC 7.4: "cleanup warnings do not affect the CLI exit code, the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason." T-TMP-35 covers the CLI exit-code clause; this test pins down the promise-outcome clause. Use the same fixture (script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, replaces it with a regular file, emits `stop: true`). Call `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })`. Capture the driver-process stderr for the duration of the promise. Assert: (a) the promise **resolves** (does not reject) — the cleanup warning did not promote to a rejection, (b) the resolved array's single `Output` has `stop: true` (the terminal outcome is what the script produced, not what cleanup observed), (c) the captured stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (d) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file (the filesystem-safety rule held). *(Spec 7.4, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-35b**: `run()` generator outcome is unaffected by a cleanup warning (regular-file replacement). The generator surface counterpart to T-TMP-35a. Same fixture; drive via `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); const first = await gen.next(); const second = await gen.next();`. Assert: (a) `first.done === false` and `first.value.stop === true` (the final output was yielded), (b) `second.done === true` and no error was thrown (the generator settled cleanly — the cleanup warning did not promote to a `throw`), (c) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (d) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file. Together with T-TMP-35 (CLI) and T-TMP-35a (`runPromise`), this closes the cleanup-warning-does-not-affect-outcome coverage across all three surfaces for the regular-file-replacement branch. *(Spec 7.4, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-35c**: Cleanup warning does not mask a script-error exit code, and the surfaced failure is observably the **script failure**, not the cleanup warning. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect the CLI exit code, the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason." T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-35a / T-TMP-35b all use successful (`stop: true`) script termination — the cleanup warning is cardinality-tested against a successful run. This test pins down the error-path variant: a cleanup warning must not promote a `1`-exit-from-the-script into a cleanup-failure exit, nor hide it as `0`, and the error output must identify the script failure as the primary failure cause rather than conflating it with the cleanup-warning category. **Because both the script-failure path and a hypothetical cleanup-promoted-to-failure path would produce exit code `1` on the CLI**, the test cannot distinguish them by exit code alone — it must additionally assert the **presence of a script-failure marker on stderr that is observably disjoint from the cleanup warning**. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, writes a **distinctive script-failure marker line** to stderr before any tmpdir tampering (e.g., `printf 'SCRIPT-FAILURE-MARKER-T-TMP-35C: script about to fail with non-zero exit\n' >&2`), then `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `printf '%s' "regular-file-replacement" > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` (tmpdir path is now a regular file — the cleanup-safety rule 3 path), then `exit 1` (script itself fails). Run CLI. Assert: (a) exit code 1 — from the **script failure**, not the cleanup warning. An implementation that masked the script failure as exit 0 (because cleanup produced "only a warning") would fail this assertion. (b) stderr contains the distinctive `SCRIPT-FAILURE-MARKER-T-TMP-35C` line (the script's own pre-failure output reached stderr), (c) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, (d) the cleanup-related warning and the script-failure marker are **distinct stderr lines** (they do not overlap in content), proving that the cleanup warning did not replace, wrap, or suppress the script-failure output, and (e) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists and is a regular file (SPEC 7.4 rule 3: regular-file replacement is left in place). Together, (a)+(b)+(d) rule out the "cleanup warning masks the script failure" failure mode: the test harness can see the script-originated stderr alongside the cleanup warning, on its own line, so the script failure remains the primary observable failure cause even when cleanup also emits a warning. Parameterize the test over regular-file and mismatched-directory replacement variants (the two warning-emitting branches with a surviving post-cleanup path), using the T-TMP-36-style rename-aside pattern for the mismatched-directory case to guarantee inode-distinctness. This is the CLI-side pinning of the "cleanup warning does not affect outcome" clause under a failing terminal; the `runPromise()` / `run()` counterparts are covered by T-TMP-35d / T-TMP-35e (below) under the same error-terminal. *(Spec 7.4, 12)*
- **T-TMP-35d**: `runPromise()` cleanup warning does not mask a script-error rejection reason. Programmatic-API counterpart to T-TMP-35c. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the promise rejection reason." T-TMP-35a / T-TMP-36a cover the success-terminal (resolve) branch — the cleanup warning does not promote a `stop: true` resolution to a rejection. This test covers the **error-terminal (reject) branch**: a script that exits non-zero and also triggers a cleanup-safety rule must reject with the **script failure** as the rejection reason, not a cleanup-failure error. **Parameterize the test over the two warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches with a surviving post-cleanup path** — matching T-TMP-35c on the CLI surface so the programmatic-counterpart coverage spans the same pair of branches: **(variant a) regular-file replacement** — `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `printf '%s' "regular-file-replacement" > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` (SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 3 path); **(variant b) mismatched-directory replacement** — `mv "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR-original-aside" && mkdir "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && touch "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/mismatched-marker"` using the T-TMP-36-style rename-aside pattern to guarantee inode-distinctness (SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 5 path). For each variant, the fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, performs the variant-specific replacement, then `exit 1` (script fails). Call `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })` per variant. Capture driver-process stderr for the duration of the promise. Assert (per variant): (a) the promise **rejects** (does not resolve), (b) the rejection reason is a **script-failure error** (matching the same shape as T-API-15 / T-API-19's script-non-zero-exit rejections — exact error class implementation-defined, but it must reference the script failure / non-zero exit, not a cleanup-failure / cleanup-safety-rule classification), (c) the captured stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (d) for variant (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file with content `regular-file-replacement` (SPEC 7.4 rule 3); for variant (b) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside (SPEC 7.4 rule 5). A buggy implementation that promoted the cleanup warning to the rejection reason — wrapping the script-failure error or replacing it with a cleanup-related error — would fail (b) on either variant; an implementation that masked the script failure entirely (resolving with the partial output array because cleanup produced "only a warning") would fail (a). *(Spec 7.4, 9.2, 12)*
- **T-TMP-35e**: `run()` generator cleanup warning does not mask a script-error throw. Generator-surface counterpart to T-TMP-35d. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the generator outcome." T-TMP-35b / T-TMP-36b cover the success-terminal (clean settlement) branch. This test covers the **error-terminal (throw) branch**: when a script exits non-zero and also triggers a cleanup-safety rule, the generator must throw the **script-failure error**, not a cleanup-failure error. **Parameterize over the same two warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches as T-TMP-35d** — **(variant a) regular-file replacement** (rule 3) and **(variant b) mismatched-directory replacement** using the T-TMP-36-style rename-aside pattern (rule 5) — so the programmatic-counterpart coverage spans the same pair of branches T-TMP-35c covers on the CLI surface. For each variant, drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 }); let thrown; try { for await (const _ of gen) { /* consume yields if any */ } } catch (e) { thrown = e; }`. Assert (per variant): (a) the generator threw (the `for await` exited via a rejection rather than completing cleanly), (b) the thrown error is a **script-failure error** (same error-shape contract as T-TMP-35d, applied to the generator surface), (c) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (d) for variant (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file; for variant (b) the path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside. Together with T-TMP-35c (CLI) and T-TMP-35d (`runPromise`), this closes the cleanup-warning-does-not-mask-error-path coverage across all three surfaces × both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches, matching the pattern T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-35a / T-TMP-35b / T-TMP-36 / T-TMP-36a / T-TMP-36b establishes for the success-path. *(Spec 7.4, 9.1, 12)*
- **T-TMP-35f**: CLI signal cleanup warning does not mask the signal exit code. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect the CLI exit code, the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason." T-TMP-35 covers the **success-terminal (stop: true)** branch on the CLI; T-TMP-35c covers the **script-error-terminal** branch on the CLI. Neither covers the **signal-terminal** branch — a script that triggers a cleanup-safety rule and then is interrupted by SIGINT / SIGTERM. The signal-terminal case exercises a structurally distinct code path: the cleanup warning must not mask the `128 + signal number` exit code that SPEC 7.3 / 7.4 require for signal-driven termination, and must not be promoted to a cleanup-failure-class exit. **Parameterize over both signals (SIGINT and SIGTERM) and both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches** (regular-file replacement per rule 3; mismatched-directory replacement per rule 5 via the T-TMP-36-style rename-aside pattern). Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker (path external to the tmpdir so it survives cleanup), performs the variant-specific replacement (variant a: `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` then `printf '%s' "regular-file-replacement" > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"`; variant b: `mv "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR-original-aside" && mkdir "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && touch "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/mismatched-marker"`), writes `"ready"` to stderr, then blocks indefinitely (`while true; do sleep 1; done`). Run via `runCLIWithSignal`. Wait for `"ready"` on stderr, then send the variant-specific signal (SIGINT or SIGTERM) to loopx. Assert per variant (signal × cleanup-safety branch — four sub-cases total): (a) loopx exits with the **signal exit code** — `130` for SIGINT, `143` for SIGTERM (the cleanup warning did not mask, replace, or override the signal exit code), (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality from SPEC 7.4 — the signal terminal does not double the warning emission), and (c) for the regular-file-replacement variant the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file with content `regular-file-replacement` (cleanup-safety rule 3 held under the signal terminal); for the mismatched-directory variant the path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside (rule 5 held). Together with T-TMP-35 (success terminal, CLI) and T-TMP-35c (script-error terminal, CLI), this closes the CLI-surface "cleanup warning does not affect outcome" coverage across all three terminal types — success / script-error / signal — × both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches. A buggy implementation that promoted the cleanup warning to a cleanup-failure-class exit code under the signal terminal (e.g., exit `1` instead of the signal code, or some implementation-specific cleanup-failure code) would fail (a). *(Spec 7.4, 7.3, 12)*
- **T-TMP-35g**: `runPromise()` abort cleanup warning does not mask the abort rejection reason. Programmatic-API counterpart to T-TMP-35f for the abort terminal. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the promise rejection reason." T-TMP-35a covers the success terminal and T-TMP-35d covers the script-error terminal on the `runPromise()` surface; this test pins down the **abort terminal**. Same parameterization as T-TMP-35f over both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches (regular-file replacement and mismatched-directory replacement via the T-TMP-36-style rename-aside pattern). Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker, writes its PID to a separate marker, performs the variant-specific replacement, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then blocks indefinitely. Use `runAPIDriverLive()` to obtain a driver-process handle. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const promise = runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal });` `await waitForStderr("ready")` on the driver stream. Capture driver-process stderr for the duration of the promise. Call `c.abort()` from the harness via the driver-handle coordination mechanism (mirroring T-API-10d-promise's coordination pattern). `await expect(promise).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>)`. Assert per variant: (a) the promise **rejects** with the **abort** error (matching T-API-10d-promise / T-API-10e-promise's abort-error contract — the cleanup warning did not promote to a cleanup-failure-class rejection nor replace the abort error), (b) the captured stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (c) for the regular-file-replacement variant the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file (rule 3 held under abort); for the mismatched-directory variant the path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside (rule 5 held). The active child must also be terminated (the script's PID from the marker is no longer running) — matching the abort-driven process-group-termination contract from T-API-10d. A buggy implementation that wrapped the abort error in a cleanup-failure error (or surfaced a cleanup-failure error in place of the abort error) would fail (a). *(Spec 7.4, 9.2, 9.1, 12)*
- **T-TMP-35h**: `run()` generator abort cleanup warning does not mask the abort throw. Generator-surface counterpart to T-TMP-35g. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the generator outcome." T-TMP-35b covers the success terminal and T-TMP-35e covers the script-error terminal on the `run()` surface; this test pins down the **abort terminal**. Same fixture and parameterization as T-TMP-35g (both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches). Drive via `runAPIDriverLive()`: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal }); const nextP = gen.next();` `await waitForStderr("ready")`. Call `c.abort()`. `await expect(nextP).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>)`. Assert per variant: (a) the generator throws the **abort** error (matching T-API-10's abort-error contract), (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (c) for the regular-file-replacement variant the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a regular file; for the mismatched-directory variant the path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside. The active child must also be terminated. Together with T-TMP-35f (CLI signal terminal), T-TMP-35g (`runPromise()` abort terminal), this closes the cleanup-warning-does-not-affect-outcome coverage across all three surfaces × all four terminal types — success (T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-35a / T-TMP-35b / T-TMP-36 / T-TMP-36a / T-TMP-36b), script-error (T-TMP-35c / T-TMP-35d / T-TMP-35e), signal (T-TMP-35f), abort (T-TMP-35g / T-TMP-35h) — × both warning-emitting cleanup-safety branches (regular-file replacement and mismatched-directory replacement). *(Spec 7.4, 9.1, 12)*
- **T-TMP-36**: Cleanup leaves a mismatched-directory replacement in place with a warning. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, then **renames the original tmpdir aside** (`mv "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR-original-aside"`) and creates a **different** directory at the original path (`mkdir "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && touch "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/mismatched-marker"`). The rename-aside step is essential for inode-distinctness: a naive `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && mkdir "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` could let the kernel reuse the original directory's inode for the freshly-created directory on POSIX filesystems where inode allocation does not strictly avoid recently-freed values, which would make the new directory's inode coincidentally match loopx's captured identity fingerprint and the test would observe a successful (rather than mismatched) cleanup — an unrelated kernel-implementation accident, not a SPEC-conformance signal. By keeping the original directory alive at a different path, its inode remains occupied for the lifetime of the script (the test harness cleans up `-original-aside` after the assertions), guaranteeing the freshly-created directory at `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` is allocated a distinct inode. The new directory's device/inode therefore differs from the identity fingerprint loopx captured at tmpdir creation, making it a mismatched directory per SPEC 7.4 rule 5. Script emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists and is a directory, (b) the `mismatched-marker` file inside still exists — loopx did not recursively remove it (the filesystem-safety outcome per SPEC 7.4 rule 5), (c) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality), (d) the CLI exit code is 0, and (e) the renamed-aside copy at `$LOOPX_TMPDIR-original-aside` still exists with its original content (loopx did not chase the renamed-away tmpdir, per the SPEC 7.4 "no chasing renamed tmpdirs" rule already covered by T-TMP-33 — and as a side effect of this rename-aside fixture pattern, this assertion provides incidental defense-in-depth for that contract). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4; do **not** require it to mention the path, the directory-identity mismatch, or any specific phrasing — only that a single cleanup-related warning is emitted. Per SPEC 7.4 rule 5: "Path is a directory whose identity does not match: leave in place with a stderr warning." *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-36a**: `runPromise()` outcome is unaffected by a cleanup warning (mismatched-directory replacement). Programmatic-API counterpart to T-TMP-36, matching the pattern of T-TMP-35a. Use the same fixture (script observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, renames it aside, creates a distinct-inode replacement at the path, emits `stop: true`). Call `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })`. Capture driver-process stderr. Assert: (a) the promise **resolves** (does not reject), (b) the resolved array's single `Output` has `stop: true`, (c) the captured stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (d) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists as a directory with the `mismatched-marker` file inside (the filesystem-safety rule held). Per SPEC 7.4: "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the promise rejection reason." *(Spec 7.4, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-36b**: `run()` generator outcome is unaffected by a cleanup warning (mismatched-directory replacement). Generator-surface counterpart to T-TMP-36a. Same fixture; drive via `for await` until the generator completes naturally. Assert: (a) the `for await` loop iterates the single `Output` with `stop: true` and then completes without throwing, (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (c) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists with `mismatched-marker` intact. Together with T-TMP-36 (CLI) and T-TMP-36a (`runPromise`), closes the cleanup-warning-does-not-affect-outcome coverage across all three surfaces for the mismatched-directory branch. Per SPEC 7.4: "cleanup warnings do not affect ... the generator outcome." *(Spec 7.4, 9.1)*
- **T-TMP-37**: Recursive cleanup unlinks nested symlink entries without traversing targets, and emits **no** cleanup warning. Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, creates an external target (e.g., `$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-dir/` containing `external-file`), then creates a nested symlink `$LOOPX_TMPDIR/nested-link -> $LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-dir`, emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` has been removed (recursive cleanup ran, matching identity), (b) `$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-dir/` still exists with `external-file` intact — the recursive walk unlinked the nested symlink entry but did not follow through to delete its target contents, and (c) **stderr contains no cleanup-related warning** — SPEC 7.4 rule 4 ("Path is a directory whose identity matches the recorded identity: recursively remove. Symlink entries encountered during the walk are unlinked but not traversed") is the success path of recursive cleanup; warnings are emitted only on cleanup-failure or rules 3 / 5. The presence of nested symlinks does not change which rule applies — the top-level entry is still a matching directory, the recursive walk handles each nested entry per its own type, and the whole operation succeeds without producing a warning when no failure / non-directory / mismatched-directory condition is hit. This assertion mirrors T-TMP-34's no-warning-on-success contract for the recursive-cleanup-with-nested-symlinks branch, distinguishing "successful recursive cleanup with nested symlink" from "recursive cleanup encountered an unlink failure on a nested symlink" (which would emit a single warning per the cleanup-failure clause). The cleanup-warning text format is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; the assertion here is **negative** (no cleanup-related warning emitted at all on stderr). Per SPEC 7.4 rule 4: "Symlink entries encountered during the walk are unlinked but not traversed, so symlinks pointing outside the tmpdir do not collateral-delete their targets." *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-37a**: Cleanup leaves a FIFO replacement in place with a warning. SPEC 7.4's cleanup-safety rule 3 applies to "a regular file or other non-directory non-symlink entry" — the rule names regular files, FIFOs (named pipes), and sockets as representative non-directory non-symlink types. T-TMP-35 covers the regular-file branch; this test covers the FIFO branch as a representative additional non-directory type, proving the rule is not special-cased to regular files. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR`, then `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `mkfifo "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` (tmpdir path is now a FIFO), then emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists and is a FIFO (`stat -c '%F' "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" == "fifo"` or the POSIX-equivalent mode check via `fs.lstatSync(path).isFIFO()`) — loopx did not unlink the FIFO, following rule 3's "leave in place with a stderr warning" for non-directory non-symlink entries, (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality rule from SPEC 7.4), (c) the CLI exit code is 0 (the cleanup warning does not affect the exit code, matching T-TMP-35's same clause). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; do not pin specific phrasing. Together with T-TMP-35 (regular file) this establishes that rule 3 covers at least two distinct non-directory types (regular file and FIFO), sufficient to prove the rule is typed generically rather than narrowly special-cased. **Conditional on the test harness being able to `mkfifo` — standard POSIX; not conditional on privileged operations.** *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-37b**: Cleanup leaves a Unix-domain socket replacement in place with a warning. SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 3 explicitly names sockets alongside regular files and FIFOs as representative non-directory non-symlink entries: "Path is a regular file, FIFO, socket, or other non-directory non-symlink: leave in place with a stderr warning." T-TMP-35 covers the regular-file branch and T-TMP-37a covers the FIFO branch; this test covers the socket branch, completing direct coverage for all three explicitly-named non-directory types in SPEC 7.4 rule 3. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.ts` (or `.js` — TS/JS is required because creating a Unix-domain socket from a portable bash one-liner is awkward, while Node's `net.createServer().listen(<path>)` reliably binds a `SOCK_STREAM` Unix-domain socket at a path on every POSIX runtime targeted by this suite) observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` via `process.env.LOOPX_TMPDIR`, writes its value to a marker file for the post-run assertion, then `fs.rmSync(tmpdirPath, { recursive: true, force: true })` and `await new Promise(resolve => { const server = net.createServer(); server.listen(tmpdirPath, resolve); })` (the path is now a bound Unix-domain socket entry — `lstat` returns a socket-typed inode), keeps a reference to `server` until `output({ stop: true })` is emitted (so the socket entry is alive at cleanup time), and then exits. After loopx exits 0, assert: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path still exists and is a Unix-domain socket (`fs.lstatSync(path).isSocket()` returns `true`) — loopx did not unlink the socket, following rule 3's "leave in place with a stderr warning" for non-directory non-symlink entries, (b) stderr contains **exactly one** cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality rule from SPEC 7.4), and (c) the CLI exit code is `0` (the cleanup warning does not affect the exit code, matching T-TMP-35 and T-TMP-37a). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; do not pin specific phrasing. Together with T-TMP-35 (regular file) and T-TMP-37a (FIFO), this test closes coverage for all three SPEC 7.4 rule-3-enumerated non-directory types — proving the rule is typed generically (any non-directory non-symlink is left in place with a warning) rather than special-cased to regular files or FIFO/regular-file pairs. The harness cleanup step removes the leftover socket entry under the test-isolated tmpdir parent after the assertions, matching the cleanup pattern T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-37a use for the regular-file / FIFO leftovers. **Standard POSIX (Linux, macOS); not conditional on privileged operations** — Unix-domain socket creation is unprivileged on POSIX systems and is a baseline capability of the Node and Bun `net` modules used elsewhere in this suite. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-37c**: Cleanup leaves a hard-link replacement in place with a warning, **and the external hard-linked file's `nlink` count is unchanged**. SPEC 7.4 cleanup-safety rule 3 explicitly cites hard-link `nlink` decrement as the underlying rationale: "Unlinking would risk mutating unrelated data (hard-link `nlink` decrement, or data renamed into the path with `nlink == 1`)." T-TMP-35 covers the regular-file branch and asserts the file content remains, but does not prove the `nlink`-decrement-safety guarantee — T-TMP-35's fixture has only one filesystem reference to the regular file, so an erroneous unlink would simply remove the file, not decrement an unrelated reference, and the `nlink`-safety contract that SPEC 7.4 rule 3 names by reference is unexercised. This test pins down the explicit `nlink`-safety guarantee. **Setup.** Outside the tmpdir, create an external regular file (e.g., `$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-file` containing `external-content`). Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker, captures the external file's path into the marker as well (so the harness can post-run `lstat` it), then `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` and `ln "$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-file" "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` — the tmpdir path is now a **hard link** to the external file, with both paths sharing the same inode and `nlink == 2`. Before exiting, the script captures the pre-cleanup `nlink` count of the external path via `stat -c '%h' "$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-file"` (or the POSIX-equivalent `fs.lstatSync(path).nlink`) into a separate marker file (`pre-cleanup-nlink`); reading from the external path is reliable because the external path is not under the tmpdir and thus is not subject to cleanup. Then the script emits `stop:true`. After loopx exits 0, the harness asserts: (a) the `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path **still exists** (loopx did not unlink the hard-link entry — SPEC 7.4 rule 3 leaves it in place), (b) the external `$LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT/external-target-file` path also still exists with content `external-content` (loopx did not follow the hard link or otherwise mutate the external path), (c) the post-cleanup `nlink` count of the external path (read by the harness via `fs.lstatSync(externalPath).nlink`) **equals the pre-cleanup `nlink` count recorded in the marker** — both should be `2`, proving loopx did not unlink the tmpdir-side hard link entry (which would have decremented `nlink` to `1` on the external file), (d) the post-cleanup `nlink` count of the **tmpdir-side** path (`fs.lstatSync(tmpdirPath).nlink`) is also unchanged at `2` — both ends of the hard-link pair preserved the `nlink` count, (e) the post-cleanup `lstat` of the tmpdir path and the external path return the **same inode** (`fs.lstatSync(tmpdirPath).ino === fs.lstatSync(externalPath).ino`) — the hard-link relationship is intact, (f) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality from SPEC 7.4, matching T-TMP-35 / T-TMP-36 / T-TMP-37a / T-TMP-37b), (g) the CLI exit code is 0 (terminal outcome unaffected by the cleanup warning, matching T-TMP-35a / T-TMP-35b / T-TMP-36a / T-TMP-36b's "warnings do not affect outcome" contract). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; do not pin specific phrasing. A buggy implementation that classified the hard-link tmpdir entry as "ordinary regular file, safe to unlink because it has content we don't care about" would unlink it, decrementing `nlink` to `1` on the external path and failing assertion (c) — even though the external file would still exist with its content (the unlink only removes the tmpdir-side entry), the `nlink` change is observable and is precisely the unrelated-data mutation SPEC 7.4 rule 3 forbids. Together with T-TMP-35 (regular file with `nlink == 1`), T-TMP-37a (FIFO), and T-TMP-37b (socket), this test closes coverage for the SPEC 7.4 rule-3 hard-link-safety sub-clause that the existing single-`nlink` regular-file test cannot exercise. **Standard POSIX (Linux, macOS); not conditional on privileged operations** — `ln` (without `-s`) creates a hard link unprivileged when source and target are on the same filesystem; the test's project root and tmpdir parent must therefore be on the same device, and the harness asserts `fs.lstatSync(externalPath).dev === fs.lstatSync(tmpdirPath).dev` during setup, skipping with a clear message if cross-device prevents the hard link. The harness cleanup step removes both paths (the hard link from the tmpdir parent, the external file from the project) after the assertions. *(Spec 7.4)*

##### Cleanup-Safety Failure Branches

SPEC 7.4 specifies a final paragraph after the identity-fingerprint dispatch rules: "If the top-level `lstat` fails for any reason other than ENOENT, the `unlink` in case 2 fails, or the recursive removal in case 4 fails, loopx emits a single stderr warning and makes no further changes." These three failure branches are specified normatively. Black-box reproduction is unreliable on a same-user-owned directory, so T-TMP-40 / T-TMP-41 / T-TMP-42 cover them deterministically via the section 1.4 `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT` seam.

- **T-TMP-40**: Top-level cleanup `lstat` failure (non-ENOENT). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT=lstat-fail` so the cleanup routine observes a simulated `EACCES` from the top-level `lstat` on `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` (per the section 1.4 seam contract). Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into a marker external to the tmpdir and emits `stop:true`. **Parameterize over the three execution surfaces** (CLI / `run()` / `runPromise()`) — each surface drives the script to natural completion so cleanup runs. Assert per surface: (a) the surface-appropriate **success** terminal outcome surfaces (CLI exit 0 / generator settles cleanly without throwing / promise resolves) — per SPEC 7.4 "cleanup warnings do not affect the CLI exit code, the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason," (b) **stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning** — the SPEC 7.4 "single stderr warning, no further changes" rule, and (c) the path observed in the marker still exists on disk (no further cleanup changes happened after the simulated `lstat` failure — loopx emitted the warning and stopped). The warning text is implementation-defined per SPEC 7.4 warning-text-broadness convention; do not pin specific phrasing. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-41**: Symlink `unlink` failure during cleanup (rule 2 failure). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT=symlink-unlink-fail` so the cleanup routine observes a simulated `EACCES` on the rule-2 `unlink` (per the section 1.4 seam contract; the seam fires only when the cleanup dispatch reaches rule 2, i.e., when the path is a symlink). Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into an external marker, then performs `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && ln -s /tmp "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` so the path becomes a symlink (replacing the directory loopx created), and emits `stop:true`. **Parameterize over the three execution surfaces** (CLI / `run()` / `runPromise()`). Drive the script to natural completion. Assert per surface: (a) the success terminal outcome surfaces (the cleanup warning does not affect outcome per SPEC 7.4), (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning, and (c) the symlink still exists at the recorded path (loopx made no further changes after the simulated `unlink` failure — the symlink that the script created is preserved). The harness cleanup step removes the symlink after the assertions. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-42**: Recursive removal failure on identity-matched directory (rule 4 failure). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT=recursive-remove-fail` so the cleanup routine observes a simulated `EACCES` during the rule-4 recursive walk (per the section 1.4 seam contract; the seam fires only when the cleanup dispatch reaches rule 4, i.e., when the path is an identity-matched directory). Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into an external marker, writes a few files inside (`echo content > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/file-1"`, `mkdir "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/sub" && echo more > "$LOOPX_TMPDIR/sub/file-2"`), and emits `stop:true`. **Parameterize over the three execution surfaces** (CLI / `run()` / `runPromise()`). Drive the script to natural completion. Assert per surface: (a) the success terminal outcome surfaces (cleanup warning does not affect outcome — SPEC 7.4), (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (per-run cleanup-warning cardinality), and (c) the path observed in the marker still exists (loopx made no further changes after the simulated recursive-remove failure — the partial walk's effect on disk is implementation-defined, but the SPEC contract is "no further changes" after the warning is emitted, so the path itself remains visible at the originally recorded location). The harness cleanup step removes any leftover content under the test-isolated tmpdir parent. *(Spec 7.4)*
- **T-TMP-42a**: Cleanup-fault warning does not mask **non-zero script exit** as the surfaced outcome. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect the CLI exit code, the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason" — the contract holds for both the **cleanup-safety warning branch** (rule-3 non-directory and rule-5 mismatched-directory leave-in-place warnings, covered by T-TMP-35d / T-TMP-35e for the script-error terminal) and the **cleanup-failure warning branch** (rule-4 recursive-remove failure and the other `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT` seams). T-TMP-40 / T-TMP-41 / T-TMP-42 cover the cleanup-failure warning branch on the **success** terminal across all three surfaces; T-TMP-35d / T-TMP-35e cover the cleanup-**safety** warning branch on the script-error terminal. The combination — cleanup-failure warning branch × script-error terminal — is the missing diagonal. A buggy implementation that wired one stream of "warnings do not mask outcome" handling for rule-3 / rule-5 leave-in-place warnings (passing T-TMP-35d / T-TMP-35e) and a separate stream for rule-4 / lstat-fail / symlink-unlink-fail cleanup-failure warnings (where the second stream might wrap or replace the script-error rejection) would pass T-TMP-40–42 (success terminal) and T-TMP-35d–35e (cleanup-safety branch) and fail this test. **Parameterize over the three cleanup-fault seam values** (`lstat-fail`, `symlink-unlink-fail`, `recursive-remove-fail`) so the warning-not-masking guarantee is pinned across all three SPEC 7.4 cleanup-failure branches. **Parameterize over the three execution surfaces** (CLI / `run()` / `runPromise()`). For each (seam, surface) combination, set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT=<seam>`. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into an external marker, performs the seam-specific path manipulation if needed (for `symlink-unlink-fail`: `rm -rf "$LOOPX_TMPDIR" && ln -s /tmp "$LOOPX_TMPDIR"` so cleanup dispatch reaches rule 2; for `recursive-remove-fail`: writes a few files inside; for `lstat-fail`: no path manipulation needed, the seam fires on the top-level `lstat`), then **exits non-zero** (e.g., `exit 1`) without emitting a `stop`. Drive the script to natural completion. Assert per (seam, surface): (a) the surface-appropriate **non-zero-exit** terminal outcome surfaces — CLI exit 1 / generator throws a script-failure error / promise rejects with a script-failure error — **the original script-failure outcome is preserved**, not masked or replaced by a cleanup-failure outcome, (b) stderr contains exactly one cleanup-related warning (the seam-induced warning; per-run cleanup-warning cardinality from SPEC 7.4 still holds under the script-error terminal), and (c) for the `symlink-unlink-fail` seam the path manipulated by the script still exists at the recorded location (no further cleanup changes happened after the warning); for `recursive-remove-fail` the path still exists; for `lstat-fail` the path still exists (the seam fires on the top-level `lstat`, so loopx never reaches the dispatch rules). A buggy implementation that promoted the cleanup-failure warning to a cleanup-failure-class rejection (CLI exit 2, or a wrapped error class on the API) under the script-error terminal would fail (a). The harness cleanup step removes any leftover content under the test-isolated tmpdir parent and the symlink (for `symlink-unlink-fail`) after the assertions. *(Spec 7.4, 7.2, 9.1, 9.2)*
- **T-TMP-42b**: Cleanup-fault warning does not mask the **abort** as the surfaced outcome. Companion to T-TMP-42a covering the abort terminal axis. SPEC 7.4 says "cleanup warnings do not affect the generator outcome, or the promise rejection reason"; T-TMP-35g / T-TMP-35h cover the cleanup-**safety** warning branch on the abort terminal across `runPromise()` and `run()`; this test pins down the cleanup-**failure** warning branch on the same terminal. The combination — cleanup-failure warning branch × abort terminal — is the second missing diagonal (T-TMP-42a closes the script-error diagonal). **Parameterize over the three cleanup-fault seam values** (`lstat-fail`, `symlink-unlink-fail`, `recursive-remove-fail`) and over the **two programmatic surfaces** (`run()` / `runPromise()`). For each (seam, surface) combination, set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_CLEANUP_FAULT=<seam>`. Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` into an external marker, performs the seam-specific path manipulation if needed (same per-seam pattern as T-TMP-42a), writes its PID to a separate marker, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then **blocks indefinitely** (so the script is still running when the harness fires the abort). Use `runAPIDriverLive()` to obtain a driver-process handle. Drive: `const c = new AbortController();` then for `runPromise`: `const promise = runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal });` for `run`: `const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal }); const nextP = gen.next();`. `await waitForStderr("ready")` on the driver stream. Capture driver-process stderr for the duration of the operation. Call `c.abort()` from the harness via the driver-handle coordination mechanism (mirroring T-TMP-35g / T-TMP-35h's coordination pattern). For `runPromise`: `await expect(promise).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>)`; for `run`: `await expect(nextP).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>)`.
