SPEC 9.2 says `runPromise()` snapshots inherited `process.env` eagerly at the call site, and SPEC 9.5 says relative `envFile` paths "are resolved against ... `process.cwd()` at call time" when `cwd` is not supplied — so under `runPromise()` the call-time pinning is even stricter than under `run()` (no first-`next()` deferral). T-API-21f covers the `run()` generator surface; `runPromise()` has no equivalent direct test. A buggy `runPromise()` implementation that deferred relative-path resolution to a microtask boundary inside the returned promise — for example, by wrapping the body in `await Promise.resolve().then(...)` and reading `process.cwd()` inside the `.then` — would resolve `envFile` against the post-`chdir` value. Use **two full projects** so the call-time vs. async-resolution distinction is end-to-end observable, mirroring T-API-21f's fixture: `projectA/` with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (observes `MARKER_VAR` into an external marker via `observe-env` and emits `stop:true`) and `projectA/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-A`; `projectB/` with the *same* `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and `projectB/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-B`. Drive under a driver that fully isolates `process.cwd()` mutations (e.g., `runAPIDriver`): (1) `process.chdir(projectA);` (2) `const promise = runPromise("ralph", { envFile: "local.env", maxIterations: 1 });` — the call-site captures `process.cwd()` as the default for `cwd`, captures the inherited env eagerly per SPEC 9.2, and resolves `envFile` against `process.cwd()` (= `projectA`) per SPEC 9.5, pinning the env file to `projectA/local.env`. (3) `process.chdir(projectB);` — **mutate `process.cwd()` between the synchronous `runPromise()` return and the `await`**. (4) `const outputs = await promise;`. Assert: (a) the script that ran was `projectA`'s script (the implicit project root captured at call time was `projectA`, so `.loopx/` discovery happened under `projectA`, not `projectB`, even though `process.cwd()` changed before `await`), and (b) the marker records `MARKER_VAR = "from-A"`, proving the relative `envFile` was resolved to `projectA/local.env` at call time. An implementation that deferred relative-path resolution would instead load `projectB/local.env` and write `"from-B"` to the marker, and would also run `projectB`'s `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Together with T-API-21f, this closes the call-time-resolution-of-relative-envFile-under-no-`cwd` gap on both API surfaces. **Test must restore original cwd in a `finally` block** to avoid leaking the chdir to other tests. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-21i**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-21g — explicit relative `cwd` is resolved at **call time**; `process.chdir()` between the synchronous `runPromise()` return and `await` does not redirect resolution. SPEC 9.5: "A relative `cwd` is resolved via `path.resolve(process.cwd(), options.cwd)` once at call time"; SPEC 9.2 makes `runPromise()`'s pre-iteration snapshot eager at the call site. T-API-21g covers the `run()` generator surface; `runPromise()` has no equivalent direct test. A buggy `runPromise()` implementation that deferred relative-`cwd` resolution into the returned promise's body — even though SPEC 9.5 says resolution happens "once at call time" — would resolve against the post-`chdir` value. Create two projects: `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (emits a marker identifying the project and `stop: true`), and `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/` with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (emits a distinct marker). Drive under a driver (e.g., `runAPIDriver`): (1) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-call");` (2) `const promise = runPromise("ralph", { cwd: "./my-project", maxIterations: 1 });` — the relative `cwd` `./my-project` resolves against `/tmp/projects-at-call` via `path.resolve("/tmp/projects-at-call", "./my-project")` = `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` synchronously at the call site. (3) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-next");` — change cwd between `runPromise()` returning and the `await`. (4) `const outputs = await promise;`. Assert the marker recorded is the one from `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` — proving the relative `cwd` was resolved at call time, not re-resolved against the changed `process.cwd()` at promise-body execution. An implementation that deferred relative-`cwd` resolution would instead spawn `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` from `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/`, writing a different marker. **Test must restore original cwd in a `finally` block.** Together with T-API-21g, this closes the call-time / async-body timing gap on both API surfaces for relative `cwd`. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-21j**: `runPromise()` — relative `envFile` resolved against the resolved (relative) `cwd` is pinned at **call time**, even if `process.chdir()` runs between the synchronous `runPromise()` return and `await`. Combines the relative-`cwd` and relative-`envFile` axes of SPEC 9.5: "Relative `envFile` paths are resolved against the resolved project root (which equals the child's cwd) when `cwd` is supplied" — and "A relative `cwd` is resolved via `path.resolve(process.cwd(), options.cwd)` once at call time." T-API-21h covers the no-`cwd` envFile case (envFile resolves against `process.cwd()` at call time); T-API-21i covers the relative-`cwd` case alone (no envFile). This test covers the **interaction** — relative `cwd` resolves to `projectA/` at call time, and the relative `envFile` then resolves under that resolved project root, also at call time, so a chdir between return and `await` must not affect either resolution. A buggy implementation that handled relative-`cwd` resolution at call time but deferred relative-`envFile` resolution to the promise body (or vice versa) would only fail this combined test. Set up: `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (observes `MARKER_VAR` into a marker via `observe-env`, also emits a project-identifier marker so the project axis is observable, and emits `stop: true`) and `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-A`; `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/` with the *same* `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-B`. Drive under a driver (e.g., `runAPIDriver`): (1) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-call");` (2) `const promise = runPromise("ralph", { cwd: "./my-project", envFile: "local.env", maxIterations: 1 });` — the relative `cwd` `./my-project` resolves to `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/`; the relative `envFile` `local.env` then resolves against the resolved project root to `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/local.env`, both at call time. (3) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-next");` — mutate cwd between return and `await`. (4) `const outputs = await promise;`. Assert: (a) the project-identifier marker records the `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` script (proving the resolved `cwd` was pinned at call time), and (b) the `MARKER_VAR` marker records `"from-A"` (proving the relative `envFile` was resolved against the call-time-resolved project root, not against `process.cwd()` at any later moment, and not under `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/`). An implementation that deferred either resolution to the post-return promise body would resolve against `/tmp/projects-at-next/`, write `"from-B"` to the `MARKER_VAR` marker, and run the wrong project's script — failing assertion (a), assertion (b), or both. **Test must restore original cwd in a `finally` block.** This test closes the combined-axis timing gap that T-API-21h (envFile-only) and T-API-21i (cwd-only) leave open on the `runPromise()` surface. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-21k**: `run()` — relative `envFile` resolved against the resolved (relative) `cwd` is pinned at **call time**, even if `process.chdir()` runs between the synchronous `run()` return and the first `next()`. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-21j (`runPromise()`). T-API-21f covers the `run()` no-`cwd` envFile case (envFile resolves against `process.cwd()` at call time), and T-API-21g covers the `run()` relative-`cwd` case alone (no envFile). This test covers the **interaction** on the generator surface — relative `cwd` resolves to `projectA/` at call time, and the relative `envFile` then resolves under that resolved project root, also at call time, so a chdir between `run()` returning and the first `next()` must not affect either resolution. A buggy implementation that handled both axes correctly *separately* on the `run()` surface (passing T-API-21f and T-API-21g) but deferred relative-`envFile` resolution to first `next()` only when `cwd` was also relative — for example, a code path that wired up the joint resolution under a single deferred branch — would pass T-API-21f / T-API-21g and fail this combined test. The `runPromise()` surface has the same gap closed by T-API-21j; the `run()` surface needs its own pin because SPEC 9.1's lazy snapshot timing for `run()` (versus SPEC 9.2's eager snapshot for `runPromise()`) means each surface evaluates the option-snapshot on a different schedule, and the relative-resolution-at-call-site contract must hold under both. Set up: `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (observes `MARKER_VAR` into a marker via `observe-env`, emits a project-identifier marker, and emits `stop: true`) and `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-A`; `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/` with the *same* `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` and `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/local.env` containing `MARKER_VAR=from-B`. Drive under a driver (e.g., `runAPIDriver`): (1) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-call");` (2) `const gen = run("ralph", { cwd: "./my-project", envFile: "local.env", maxIterations: 1 });` — the relative `cwd` `./my-project` resolves to `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` synchronously at the call site; the relative `envFile` `local.env` then resolves against the resolved project root to `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/local.env`, also at call site. (3) `process.chdir("/tmp/projects-at-next");` — mutate `process.cwd()` between `run()` returning and the first `next()`. (4) `const { value } = await gen.next();` — drain to completion. Assert: (a) the project-identifier marker records the `/tmp/projects-at-call/my-project/` script (proving the resolved `cwd` was pinned at call time, matching T-API-21g's contract on the joint axis), (b) the `MARKER_VAR` marker records `"from-A"` (proving the relative `envFile` was resolved against the call-time-resolved project root, not against `process.cwd()` at first `next()`, and not under `/tmp/projects-at-next/my-project/`), and (c) the generator settles cleanly. An implementation that resolved each axis correctly in isolation but deferred `envFile` resolution to first `next()` only when `cwd` was also relative would resolve against `/tmp/projects-at-next/`, write `"from-B"` to the `MARKER_VAR` marker, and may also run the wrong project's script — failing (a), (b), or both. **Test must restore original cwd in a `finally` block.** Together with T-API-21j (`runPromise()`), this closes the combined-axis call-time-resolution gap on both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*

#### `maxIterations` Validation

- **T-API-22**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: -1 })` → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-23**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1.5 })` → generator throws. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-23a**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: NaN })` → generator throws. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-24**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: NaN })` → rejects. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24a**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: -1 })` → rejects. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24b**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1.5 })` → rejects. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24c**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: Infinity as any })` → generator throws on first `next()` with an invalid-`maxIterations` error. Per SPEC 9.5, `maxIterations` must be a non-negative finite integer; `Infinity` is not finite. T-API-22 covers negative, T-API-23 covers non-integer (1.5), T-API-23a / T-API-24 cover `NaN`; this closes the `Infinity` gap, which some implementations accept as "run forever" accidentally via loose `typeof === "number"` checks that do not call `Number.isFinite`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-24d**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: Infinity as any })` → rejects with an invalid-`maxIterations` error. `runPromise` counterpart to T-API-24c. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24e**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: null as any })` → generator throws on first `next()`. `null` is not a number and therefore not a valid `maxIterations`. Some shape-validators accept `null` because `typeof null === "object"` or because a permissive "if present, validate" check treats `null` the same as "not provided." SPEC 9.5 requires `maxIterations`, when supplied, to be a non-negative finite integer; `null` is supplied (it is an explicit own property of the options object) and does not satisfy that predicate. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-24f**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: null as any })` → rejects. `runPromise` counterpart to T-API-24e. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24g**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: "1" as any })` → generator throws on first `next()`. Per SPEC 9.5, the `maxIterations` type is `number | undefined`; a string value — even one that parses to a valid non-negative integer — must be rejected by shape validation. Closes the loose-coercion gap that a `Number("1") === 1` coercion would introduce. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-24h**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: "1" as any })` → rejects. `runPromise` counterpart to T-API-24g. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-24i**: `runPromise()` — `maxIterations` counts loop hops, not just yields, across cross-workflow and intra-workflow goto chains. SPEC 4.2 / 7.1 define `maxIterations` as the number of iterations (each spawn of a script counts as one iteration), and SPEC 2.2 makes `goto` part of the iteration count rather than a free transition. CLI iteration counting across gotos is pinned down by T-LOOP-06 / T-LOOP-07 / T-LOOP-08 and related; the API equivalent is only implicitly covered. This test pins down the explicit API contract. Set up three scripts: `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (outputs `{"goto":"check","result":"A"}`), `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` (outputs `{"goto":"other:step","result":"B"}`), and `.loopx/other/step.sh` (outputs `{"stop":true,"result":"C"}`). Case 1: `await runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 2 })` — the loop should run `index` then `check` and then terminate on the iteration budget, yielding exactly two outputs with `result` values `["A", "B"]`. The third goto target (`other:step`) is not entered because `maxIterations: 2` exhausts the budget after the second iteration. Case 2: `await runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 3 })` — the loop runs all three, yielding exactly three outputs with `result` values `["A", "B", "C"]`. Asserting the output counts and the specific `result` sequence proves (a) `maxIterations` counts each spawn as one iteration regardless of whether it is reached via goto or loop reset, (b) intra-workflow goto (`index` → `check`) consumes one iteration, and (c) cross-workflow goto (`check` → `other:step`) consumes one iteration. A buggy implementation that counted only cross-workflow transitions, or that allowed a goto to exceed the limit "for free", would produce different output counts and fail this test. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5, 7.1, 2.2)*
- **T-API-24j**: `run()` — `maxIterations` counts loop hops across goto chains. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-24i. Same three-script fixture. Case 1: `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 2 })` then iterate to exhaustion via `for await`; assert exactly two yielded outputs with `result` values `["A", "B"]`. Case 2: `maxIterations: 3`; assert exactly three yielded outputs `["A", "B", "C"]`. Together with T-API-24i (`runPromise`), this closes the API `maxIterations`-counts-goto-hops gap across both surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5, 7.1, 2.2)*

#### `runPromise()` with AbortSignal

- **T-API-25**: `runPromise("ralph", { signal })` — aborting rejects with abort error. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-25a**: Pre-aborted signal → rejects immediately, no child spawned. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-25b**: Abort between iterations → rejects. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*

#### `RunOptions.env` (Programmatic Env Injection)

Spec 9.5 adds an `env?: Record<string, string>` field to `RunOptions`. These tests cover shape validation, snapshot semantics, precedence, and boundary cases. All fixtures use `observe-env` to distinguish set-vs-absent.

##### Basic Injection

- **T-API-50**: `RunOptions.env` injects a variable into every spawned script. `runPromise("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "hello" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert marker shows `{ present: true, value: "hello" }`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50a**: `RunOptions.env` applies to every script across iterations. Two iterations; both observe `MYVAR`. Assert both markers show `"hello"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50b**: `RunOptions.env` applies across intra-workflow goto. `ralph:index` gotos `ralph:check`. Both observe `MYVAR`. Assert both show `"hello"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50c**: `RunOptions.env` applies across cross-workflow goto. `alpha:index` gotos `beta:step`. Both observe `MYVAR`. Assert both show `"hello"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50d**: `RunOptions.env` applies on loop reset. Multi-iteration loop where reset returns to the starting target. Observe `MYVAR` on every iteration. Assert every observation shows `"hello"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50f**: Concurrent `runPromise()` calls receive isolated `RunOptions.env` values. Pins down ADR-0004's core motivation that programmatic per-run env values reach scripts without racy global `process.env` mutation. Existing T-API-50–50d / T-API-51e–51f / T-API-52–52e2 cover injection / precedence / snapshot semantics on individual runs, but none directly assert that **two overlapping concurrent runs** with different `RunOptions.env` values do not cross-contaminate. A buggy implementation that internally mutated `process.env` to apply per-run values, even temporarily across the spawn step, would observably leak across concurrent runs and pass every existing test (which uses one run at a time). Set up two distinct workflows in the same project (`alpha` and `beta`) — `.loopx/alpha/index.sh` observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` into a marker at `$LOOPX_TMPDIR/../alpha-marker-<unique>` (or another caller-supplied absolute path supplied through a second env var), then waits for a caller-supplied "release" file to appear, then emits `stop: true`; `.loopx/beta/index.sh` does the same with its own marker / release pair. The wait-for-release mechanism guarantees the two scripts overlap on the `RunOptions.env` snapshot / spawn boundary — when one script blocks waiting for its release file, the other script is already past `runPromise()`-call but has not yet completed its iteration. Drive: `const releaseAlpha = path.join(tmp, "release-alpha"); const releaseBeta = path.join(tmp, "release-beta"); const pAlpha = runPromise("alpha", { env: { MYVAR: "alpha-value", ALPHA_MARKER: alphaMarkerPath, ALPHA_RELEASE: releaseAlpha }, maxIterations: 1 }); const pBeta = runPromise("beta", { env: { MYVAR: "beta-value", BETA_MARKER: betaMarkerPath, BETA_RELEASE: releaseBeta }, maxIterations: 1 });` Wait for both `alphaMarkerPath` and `betaMarkerPath` to exist on disk (each script writes its marker before blocking). At this point both scripts are blocked waiting for their release files, so both `runPromise()` calls have completed their `RunOptions.env` snapshot and spawned their scripts with their respective env values — the runs genuinely overlap. Touch both release files to unblock both scripts. Await `pAlpha` and `pBeta`. Read both markers. Assert: (a) `alphaMarkerPath` records `MYVAR === "alpha-value"` (alpha's run saw only its own env value), (b) `betaMarkerPath` records `MYVAR === "beta-value"` (beta's run saw only its own env value), (c) neither marker contains the other run's value (no cross-contamination), (d) both promises resolve (no rejection). A buggy implementation that mutated `process.env.MYVAR` to apply per-run values would surface as one or both markers observing the wrong value (e.g., both markers showing `"beta-value"` if beta's mutation overwrote alpha's before alpha spawned). T-API-50–50e cover the single-run injection axis; this test pins down the concurrent-isolation axis directly on the `runPromise()` surface; T-API-50g extends the same contract to the `run()` generator surface. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50g**: Concurrent `run()` generator calls receive isolated `RunOptions.env` values. `run()`-surface counterpart to T-API-50f, pinning down the same concurrent-isolation contract on the generator surface. ADR-0004's motivation for `RunOptions.env` is to avoid racy global `process.env` mutation for **both** API surfaces; a buggy implementation that mutated `process.env` to apply per-run values could leak across concurrent runs on either surface, and the two surfaces have different snapshot timing for inherited `process.env` (lazy under `run()` per SPEC 9.1; eager under `runPromise()` per SPEC 9.2), so an implementation that wired `RunOptions.env` correctly on the eager `runPromise()` path while losing isolation on the lazy `run()` path — for example, by re-reading shared mutable state at first `next()` rather than from a captured snapshot — would pass T-API-50f and fail this test. **Same fixture as T-API-50f**: two distinct workflows `alpha` and `beta` in the same project, each with `index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` into a caller-supplied marker path, waits for a caller-supplied release file to appear, then emits `stop: true`. **Drive on the generator surface**: `const releaseAlpha = path.join(tmp, "release-alpha"); const releaseBeta = path.join(tmp, "release-beta"); const ga = run("alpha", { env: { MYVAR: "alpha-value", ALPHA_MARKER: alphaMarkerPath, ALPHA_RELEASE: releaseAlpha }, maxIterations: 1 }); const gb = run("beta", { env: { MYVAR: "beta-value", BETA_MARKER: betaMarkerPath, BETA_RELEASE: releaseBeta }, maxIterations: 1 }); const aNextP = ga.next(); const bNextP = gb.next();` — starting both `next()` calls **without awaiting** forces both generators past their `RunOptions.env` snapshots and into their first child spawns concurrently while the harness retains control. Awaiting `Promise.all([ga.next(), gb.next()])` here would deadlock, because the fixture scripts block at the release-file boundary before yielding their first output, so neither `next()` promise can resolve until the release files are touched. With the un-awaited promises in hand, wait for both `alphaMarkerPath` and `betaMarkerPath` to exist on disk (each script writes its marker before blocking) — at that point both generators are blocked at the release-file boundary, so both `run()` calls have completed their `RunOptions.env` snapshot and spawned their scripts with their respective env values, and the runs genuinely overlap. Touch both release files to unblock both scripts, then `const [aFirst, bFirst] = await Promise.all([aNextP, bNextP]);` to await both first yields. Drain both generators to settlement: `for await (const _ of ga) {} for await (const _ of gb) {}` (or equivalent — both should settle cleanly with the per-iteration limit reached). Read both markers. Assert: (a) `alphaMarkerPath` records `MYVAR === "alpha-value"` (alpha's generator saw only its own env value), (b) `betaMarkerPath` records `MYVAR === "beta-value"` (beta's generator saw only its own env value), (c) neither marker contains the other run's value (no cross-contamination), (d) both generators settle cleanly (no errors thrown). Together with T-API-50f (`runPromise()` surface), this closes the concurrent-`RunOptions.env` isolation contract across both API surfaces, completing the surface symmetry the ADR motivates. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 8.3)*
- **T-API-50e**: `RunOptions.env` accepts an **empty-string value** and the variable reaches the spawned script as the empty string. Per SPEC 9.5 "Shape ... whose own enumerable string-keyed entries all have string values" — an empty string IS a string and is a conforming value. SPEC 8.3 / 9.5 do not single empty values out for special handling, so empty-string entries must propagate through the merge tier and reach the child like any other string-valued entry. The env-file tier has comparable coverage (e.g., empty values from `KEY=` / `KEY=""` in env files), but `RunOptions.env` does not currently have a positive empty-string-value test. A buggy implementation that filtered out falsy entries (`if (value)` rather than `if (typeof value === "string")`) would silently drop the empty value and pass T-API-50 / T-API-50a–d (which all use a non-empty value). Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.ts` containing a TS script that observes `process.env.EMPTY` via the `observe-env` fixture (which JSON-encodes the observed value, so an empty string is distinguishable from `undefined`). Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { EMPTY: "" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) the promise resolves (no rejection — empty string is a valid value, not a shape error), (b) the marker file records `process.env.EMPTY === ""` (present, value is the empty string — **not** absent / `undefined`). A buggy implementation that dropped empty-string entries would produce a marker showing `undefined` / absent and fail (b). *(Spec 8.3, 9.5)*

##### Precedence

- **T-API-51a**: `RunOptions.env` is overridden by protocol variables. `runPromise("ralph", { env: { LOOPX_WORKFLOW: "fake", LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT: "/tmp/fake", LOOPX_WORKFLOW_DIR: "/tmp/fake-dir", LOOPX_TMPDIR: "/tmp/fake-tmp", LOOPX_BIN: "/tmp/fake-bin" } })`. Scripts observe each variable. Assert each marker contains the real injected value (tier-1 protocol), not the `RunOptions.env` (tier-2) value. Per Spec 9.5 "No name validation beyond string-to-string", `LOOPX_*` keys in `RunOptions.env` are silently overridden by protocol injection. **`LOOPX_TMPDIR` rigor**: matching T-TMP-30 / T-TMP-31, the fixture additionally performs an in-script `stat` of the observed `$LOOPX_TMPDIR` path **during execution** (before the script exits), recording the stat result (exists, is-a-directory) to a marker. The during-run stat is essential because SPEC 7.4 cleanup removes the tmpdir on run completion; a post-run stat would observe absence. Assert: (a) the observed `LOOPX_TMPDIR` marker records the real injected tmpdir path (under `os.tmpdir()` or the test-isolated parent, matching the `loopx-*` naming pattern), **not** `/tmp/fake-tmp`, (b) the during-run stat marker records "exists as a directory" — proving it is the real loopx-created tmpdir, not merely a substituted string. A buggy implementation that string-substituted the protocol value but failed to actually create the tmpdir would fail (b). *(Spec 9.5, 8.3, 7.4)*
- **T-API-51b**: `RunOptions.env` overrides local env-file values. Local env file `.env` contains `MYVAR=from-file`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { envFile: ".env", env: { MYVAR: "from-options" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert marker shows `"from-options"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-51c**: `RunOptions.env` overrides global env-file values. Global env file contains `MYVAR=from-global`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "from-options" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert marker shows `"from-options"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-51d**: `RunOptions.env` overrides inherited `process.env` values. Parent process has `MYVAR=inherited`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "from-options" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert marker shows `"from-options"`. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-51e**: Full precedence chain. Inherited `MYVAR=1`, global env file `MYVAR=2`, local env file `MYVAR=3`, `RunOptions.env.MYVAR=4`, protocol variables (unchanged — `MYVAR` is not a protocol name). Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert `"4"` — `RunOptions.env` wins over all lower tiers, and protocol doesn't apply to non-`LOOPX_*` names. *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-51f**: `run()` — full precedence chain on the generator surface. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-51e (`runPromise()`). T-API-51a–51e cover protocol-override / single-tier overrides on `runPromise()`, and T-API-51a uses `run()` indirectly via `runPromise` semantics, but no test directly exercises the **full four-tier** chain (inherited → global env file → local env file → `RunOptions.env`) on the generator surface. The two run surfaces have different snapshot timing for inherited `process.env` (lazy under `run()` per SPEC 9.1; eager under `runPromise()` per SPEC 9.2), and a buggy implementation that wired `RunOptions.env` precedence correctly on the eager-snapshot `runPromise()` path while losing precedence on the lazy-snapshot `run()` path — for example, by re-merging tiers at first `next()` in a different order than the eager path uses — would pass T-API-51e and fail this test. **Setup.** Configure inherited `process.env.MYVAR=1`, global env file containing `MYVAR=2`, local env file (`-e` equivalent — `RunOptions.envFile` here) containing `MYVAR=3`. Place `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` into a marker, then emits `stop:true`. **Drive.** `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<local-envfile-path>", env: { MYVAR: "4" }, maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const _ of gen) {}` (or equivalent generator drive pattern). Assert: (a) the marker records `process.env.MYVAR === "4"` — `RunOptions.env` wins over all three lower tiers on the `run()` surface, matching SPEC 8.3's precedence list (protocol > `RunOptions.env` > local env file > global env file > inherited; `MYVAR` is not a protocol name, so `RunOptions.env` is the effective winner); (b) the generator settled cleanly (no error). Together with T-API-51e (`runPromise()` full chain), this closes the surface asymmetry for `RunOptions.env` precedence under ADR-0004. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 8.3)*

##### Snapshot Semantics

- **T-API-52**: `RunOptions.env` is snapshotted at call time. `const e = { MYVAR: "initial" }; const p = runPromise("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 }); e.MYVAR = "mutated";` Await `p`. Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert marker shows `"initial"` — mutating the original object after `runPromise()` returns has no effect on the running loop. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-52a**: `RunOptions.env` snapshot is shallow. `const e = { MYVAR: "initial" }; const gen = run("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 }); e.NEWVAR = "added"; await gen.next();` Script observes both `MYVAR` and `NEWVAR`. Assert `MYVAR` present with `"initial"` and `NEWVAR` absent — the shallow snapshot captured the initial keys, and the added key is not observed. Paired with T-API-52 (value mutation) to pin down "own enumerable string-keyed properties read once". *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-52b**: `RunOptions.env` snapshot timing under `run()` is eager at call time. `const e = { MYVAR: "A" }; const gen = run("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 }); e.MYVAR = "B"; await gen.next();` Script observes `MYVAR`. Per Spec 9.5 "Shape ... Lifetime. Entries are captured synchronously at call time as a shallow copy" — the snapshot is taken at `run()` call time, not at first `next()`. Assert marker shows `"A"`. This test intentionally differs from T-API-09b (which covers inherited `process.env`, captured lazily) to prove `RunOptions.env` has *eager* snapshot semantics while `process.env` has *lazy* semantics under `run()`. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-52c**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` enumerable getter is invoked exactly once at call time (before `run()` returns), and the captured value reaches the spawned script even after the backing state mutates. SPEC 9.5: "Entries are captured synchronously at call time as a shallow copy — loopx reads the supplied object's own enumerable string-keyed properties once. The capture runs at the `run()` / `runPromise()` call site …". T-API-52 / T-API-52a / T-API-52b cover the **outer-property** snapshot semantics with plain assignments (no getters), so they do not directly observe the "own enumerable string-keyed properties **read once at call time**" contract on the per-entry getter axis — a buggy implementation that deferred entry-getter reads to first `next()` (or re-invoked them per spawn) would still pass T-API-52 / T-API-52a / T-API-52b but fail this test. **Setup.** `let count = 0; let backing = "initial"; const env = {}; Object.defineProperty(env, "MYVAR", { enumerable: true, configurable: true, get() { count += 1; return backing; } });` Place `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` and emits `stop:true`. **Drive.** `const gen = run("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` — at this point, before any `next()`, capture `count` into a local: `const countAfterRun = count;` Mutate the backing state: `backing = "mutated"; const countAfterMutate = count;` Then `await gen.next(); /* yields the script's Output */ await gen.next(); /* settles */;` Capture `count` once more: `const countAfterSettle = count;` Assert: (a) `countAfterRun === 1` — the getter fired exactly once, **synchronously at the `run()` call site**, before the iteration loop began (proves the eager-capture clause of SPEC 9.5); (b) `countAfterMutate === 1` — mutating the backing state after `run()` returned did not re-invoke the getter (the snapshot is frozen); (c) the `observe-env` marker for `MYVAR` records `{ "present": true, "value": "initial" }` — the spawned script saw the captured value, not the post-call mutation; (d) `countAfterSettle === 1` — driving the generator to settlement did not re-invoke the getter (no per-spawn re-read). A buggy implementation that read the getter lazily at first `next()` would satisfy (b) and (c) but fail (a) (`countAfterRun === 0`); a buggy implementation that re-invoked the getter per spawn would fail (b)/(d). Together with T-API-52 / T-API-52a / T-API-52b, this test pins down the per-entry getter side of "read once at call time" on the `run()` surface. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-52d**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-52c — `RunOptions.env` enumerable getter is invoked exactly once **synchronously before `runPromise()` returns**, and the captured value is observed by every script across multiple iterations even after the backing state mutates. SPEC 9.2 / 9.5: snapshot semantics are identical to `run()` except the snapshot fires at the `runPromise()` call site. T-API-52 covers the outer-property snapshot under `runPromise()` with a plain object assignment (no getter); this test pins down the per-entry-getter axis on the `runPromise()` surface. **Setup.** `let count = 0; let backing = "initial"; const env = {}; Object.defineProperty(env, "MYVAR", { enumerable: true, configurable: true, get() { count += 1; return backing; } });` Place `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` and writes a per-iteration marker (so each spawn's observation is distinguishable). Drive a multi-iteration loop (e.g., the script emits `stop: false` for two iterations and `stop: true` on the third — `maxIterations: 3`). **Drive.** `const p = runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 3 }); const countAfterCall = count; backing = "mutated"; const countAfterMutate = count; const outputs = await p; const countAfterResolve = count;` Assert: (a) `countAfterCall === 1` — the getter fired exactly once **synchronously at the `runPromise()` call site**, before the function returned (proves the eager-capture clause for `runPromise()` — `runPromise` snapshots eagerly per SPEC 9.2 vs. `run`'s call-time capture per T-API-52b); (b) `countAfterMutate === 1` — synchronous mutation after `runPromise()` returned did not re-invoke the getter; (c) every per-iteration `observe-env` marker records `{ "present": true, "value": "initial" }` (three iterations, three markers, all `"initial"`) — the spawned scripts saw the captured value despite the post-call mutation, and the value was reused across spawns; (d) `countAfterResolve === 1` — the getter was not re-invoked between spawns (per-spawn re-reads would have produced `count >= 3`). A buggy implementation that re-read getters per spawn would fail (d) and produce `count === 3` (one read per iteration); an implementation that read the getter lazily after the await point would fail (a). Together with T-API-52c (`run()` surface), this test closes the per-entry-getter / snapshot-once / no-re-read coverage across both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-52e**: `RunOptions.env` proxy-success variant — `ownKeys` is invoked exactly once at snapshot time, the `get` trap is invoked exactly once per included key, and neither is re-invoked across iterations. Companion to T-API-52c / T-API-52d for the **proxy** snapshot path. SPEC 9.5 specifies `RunOptions.env` is read once at call time ("loopx reads the supplied object's own enumerable string-keyed properties once") and value reads use ordinary `[[Get]]` semantics ("After loopx has identified the included own enumerable string-keyed keys, it reads each included key's value using ordinary ECMAScript property `[[Get]]` semantics, equivalent to `env[key]`, exactly once during the call-time snapshot"). The per-entry-getter axis is covered by T-API-52c / T-API-52d; the **proxy enumeration and value-read** axis (`ownKeys` and `get` traps) is not otherwise directly pinned down on the success path. T-API-62f / T-API-62f3 cover the throwing-trap **failure** path; a successful proxy must also be enumerated and value-read exactly once — a buggy implementation that re-invoked `ownKeys` or `get` per spawn would silently amplify trap calls without surfacing as an error. **Setup.** `let ownKeysCount = 0; let getCount = 0; let descCount = 0; const valueGetCounts = new Map(); const target = { MYVAR: "initial", OTHERVAR: "second" }; const env = new Proxy(target, { ownKeys(t) { ownKeysCount += 1; return Reflect.ownKeys(t); }, getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { descCount += 1; return Reflect.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key); }, get(t, key) { if (typeof key === "string") { getCount += 1; valueGetCounts.set(key, (valueGetCounts.get(key) ?? 0) + 1); } return Reflect.get(t, key); } });` Place `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observing `MYVAR` and `OTHERVAR` via `observe-env`, emitting `stop: false` for two iterations and `stop: true` on the third. **Parameterize over both API surfaces.** Variant (a) `run()`: `const gen = run("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 3 }); const ownKeysAfterCall = ownKeysCount; const getAfterCall = getCount; const valueGetSnapshot = new Map(valueGetCounts); while (!(await gen.next()).done) {} const ownKeysAfterDone = ownKeysCount; const getAfterDone = getCount; const valueGetAfterDone = new Map(valueGetCounts);` Variant (b) `runPromise()`: `const p = runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 3 }); const ownKeysAfterCall = ownKeysCount; const getAfterCall = getCount; const valueGetSnapshot = new Map(valueGetCounts); await p; const ownKeysAfterDone = ownKeysCount; const getAfterDone = getCount; const valueGetAfterDone = new Map(valueGetCounts);` Assert per variant: (1) **`ownKeysAfterCall === 1`** — the `ownKeys` trap was invoked exactly once during the call-time snapshot (a single enumeration), satisfying SPEC 9.5's "read once" contract on the enumeration axis directly. (2) **`ownKeysAfterDone === ownKeysAfterCall`** — the trap was not re-invoked between or during the three iterations. (3) Every iteration's `observe-env` marker shows `MYVAR = "initial"` and `OTHERVAR = "second"` (the snapshot values reached every spawn). (4) **`getAfterCall === 2`** — the `get` trap was invoked exactly once per included string key (`MYVAR` and `OTHERVAR`) during the call-time snapshot, satisfying SPEC 9.5's `[[Get]]`-semantics-exactly-once contract on the value-read axis. (5) **`valueGetSnapshot.get("MYVAR") === 1` and `valueGetSnapshot.get("OTHERVAR") === 1`** — per-key call count is exactly one. (6) **`getAfterDone === getAfterCall`** and **`valueGetAfterDone.get(key) === valueGetSnapshot.get(key)` for each enumerable string key** — no additional `get`-trap reads across iterations / spawns (the no-per-spawn-re-read invariant on the value axis). (7) **`descCount` is left as a non-asserted observation** — SPEC 9.5 explicitly leaves the enumerability-determination strategy implementation-defined ("The strategy used to determine enumerability may invoke descriptor-related proxy traps and remains otherwise implementation-defined"); `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` may legitimately fire a different number of times depending on whether the implementation uses `Object.keys` (which calls `[[OwnPropertyKeys]]` then filters by descriptor) vs. `Reflect.ownKeys` (which does not invoke the descriptor trap) vs. a `for...in` audit. The strict equalities (1)–(6) are the load-bearing assertions: a buggy implementation that re-invoked `ownKeys` per spawn would fail (2); an implementation that snapshot-time-enumerated more than once would fail (1); a descriptor-based-extraction implementation that skipped `[[Get]]` would observe `getAfterCall === 0` and fail (4); a per-spawn `get` re-read would fail (6). T-API-52a / T-API-52b cover plain-object snapshots; T-API-52c / T-API-52d cover per-entry getters; this test pins down the proxy-trap axis with exact call counts on both the `ownKeys` and `get` axes per SPEC 9.5's normative `[[Get]]`-semantics contract. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 9.2)*
- **T-API-52e2**: `RunOptions.env` proxy-success variant — non-`ownKeys` trap coverage. Companion to T-API-52e covering the success-path no-per-spawn-re-read invariant for the `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` and `get` traps when those are the **only** custom traps installed. T-API-52e covers all three traps simultaneously; this test pins down the success-path no-re-read behavior in two narrow configurations where `ownKeys` is the default `Reflect.ownKeys` (so `ownKeysCount` is not directly observable on the proxy). **Parameterize over two trap configurations:** variant (a) **only `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` is custom-trapped** — `let descCount = 0; const target = { MYVAR: "initial", OTHERVAR: "second" }; const env = new Proxy(target, { getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { descCount += 1; return Reflect.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key); } });` Drive `runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 3 })` against an `observe-env` fixture, capture `descCount` after `runPromise()` returns and after the promise resolves. Assert: (1) `descAfterDone === descAfterCall` — descriptor trap was not re-invoked across iterations / spawns, even if it fires more than once during the snapshot (SPEC 9.5 leaves the enumerability-determination strategy implementation-defined, so the per-call count is not asserted); (2) every iteration observes `MYVAR = "initial"` and `OTHERVAR = "second"` (snapshot values reached every spawn). Variant (b) **only `get` is custom-trapped** — `let getCount = 0; const valueGetCounts = new Map(); const env = new Proxy(target, { get(t, key) { if (typeof key === "string") { getCount += 1; valueGetCounts.set(key, (valueGetCounts.get(key) ?? 0) + 1); } return Reflect.get(t, key); } });` Drive the same `runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 3 })` and capture counts on the same schedule. Assert per SPEC 9.5's `[[Get]]`-semantics-exactly-once contract: (1) **`getAfterCall === 2`** — the `get` trap was invoked exactly once per included string key (`MYVAR` and `OTHERVAR`) during the call-time snapshot; (2) **`valueGetSnapshot.get("MYVAR") === 1` and `valueGetSnapshot.get("OTHERVAR") === 1`** — per-key call count is exactly one; (3) **`getAfterDone === getAfterCall`** and **`valueGetAfterDone.get(key) === valueGetSnapshot.get(key)` for each enumerable string key** — no per-spawn re-read amplification across iterations; (4) every iteration observes the snapshot values `MYVAR = "initial"` and `OTHERVAR = "second"` (the captured snapshot values reach every spawn). A buggy implementation using descriptor-based extraction (`getCount === 0`) would fail (1); an implementation that re-read per spawn would fail (3). Together with T-API-52e (combined-trap configuration), this completes the success-path no-per-spawn-re-read coverage axis across all three proxy-trap shapes (`ownKeys`-only, `getOwnPropertyDescriptor`-only, `get`-only). *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 9.2)*

##### Invalid Shape

- **T-API-53**: `RunOptions.env = null` → generator throws on first `next()` (invalid shape). `run("ralph", { env: null as any })` returns a generator; `.next()` throws with an error mentioning invalid `env`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-53a**: `RunOptions.env = []` (array) → generator throws on first `next()`. Arrays are rejected per Spec 9.5 "non-null, non-array, non-function object". *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-53b**: `RunOptions.env = () => {}` (function) → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-53c**: `RunOptions.env = "string"` → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-53d**: `RunOptions.env = 42` → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-54**: Non-string entry value → generator throws on first `next()`. `run("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: 42 as any } })` — non-string value is rejected. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-54a**: `undefined` entry value → generator throws on first `next()`. `run("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: undefined as any } })`. Per Spec 9.5, all own enumerable string-keyed entries must have string values; `undefined` is not a string. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-54b**: `null` entry value → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-54c**: Object entry value → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-55**: `runPromise` equivalents for invalid shapes — all invalid `RunOptions.env` shapes reject the promise. Parameterized across null, array, function, string, number, and non-string entry values. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*

##### Invalid Shape Under `maxIterations: 0`

SPEC 9.5 explicitly states: "`RunOptions.env` shape validation (invalid object types, non-string values, snapshot-time throws) is independent of spawning and surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count." Existing `maxIterations: 0` tests (T-API-08a–08r) cover target / envFile / global-env / sibling-validation failures under that short-circuit, and T-API-53–55 cover `RunOptions.env` shape validation under normal iteration counts, but the combination is not explicitly pinned down. A buggy implementation could short-circuit `maxIterations: 0` before reading the `env` option and pass all those tests without exercising the SPEC 9.5 rule.

- **T-API-55a**: `run()` — **full invalid `env` shape and entry-value matrix** under `maxIterations: 0`. SPEC 9.5 explicitly says `RunOptions.env` shape validation "is independent of spawning and surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count" — both the invalid-object-type branch (T-API-53–53d) and the non-string-entry-value branch (T-API-54–54c) must surface under the zero-iteration short-circuit. T-API-55b covers the same matrix on the `runPromise()` surface; together they pin down both axes of the shape-validation rule across both API surfaces. **Parameterize over the full SPEC 9.5 invalid-`env` enumeration**: variant (a) **`env: null`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: null as any })`); variant (b) **`env: []`** (array); variant (c) **`env: () => {}`** (function); variant (d) **`env: "string"`**; variant (e) **`env: 42`** (primitive number); variant (f) **non-string entry value — number**: `env: { MYVAR: 42 as any }`; variant (g) **non-string entry value — `undefined`**: `env: { MYVAR: undefined as any }`; variant (h) **non-string entry value — `null`**: `env: { MYVAR: null as any }`; variant (i) **non-string entry value — object**: `env: { MYVAR: { nested: "value" } as any }`; variant (j) **non-string entry value — function**: `env: { MYVAR: (() => "value") as any }`. For each variant, drive `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: <variant> }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/env/i);` Assert per variant: (a) the first `next()` throws an error mentioning invalid `env`, (b) the error surfaces as a shape-validation error — **not** the silent zero-iteration short-circuit (which would resolve `{ done: true }` per T-API-08a etc.) and **not** a spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned (no marker file written), and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created (consistent with SPEC 7.1 step 6 / SPEC 7.4 — tmpdir is not created under `maxIterations: 0`, and shape-validation errors fire before tmpdir creation). A buggy implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` before reading the `env` option would fail on every variant; an implementation that gated the validation only on object-type (rejecting variants (a)–(e)) or only on entry-value (rejecting variants (f)–(j)) would fail the other half. Together with T-API-55c (throwing-getter / proxy-trap branch on the `run()` surface), this completes coverage for SPEC 9.5's three explicitly-enumerated `env` validation branches — invalid object type, invalid entry value, snapshot-time throws — on the generator surface under `maxIterations: 0`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55b**: `runPromise()` — **full invalid `env` shape and entry-value matrix** under `maxIterations: 0`. `runPromise()`-surface counterpart to T-API-55a, parameterized over the same ten variants (a)–(j). For each variant, drive `await expect(runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: <variant> })).rejects.toThrow(/env/i);` Assert per variant: (a) the promise rejects with a shape-validation error mentioning invalid `env`, (b) the rejection is **not** the resolved-with-`[]` zero-iteration short-circuit (which would mean the `env` snapshot was skipped) and **not** a spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. Together with T-API-55a (generator surface, full matrix) and T-API-55d (throwing-getter / proxy-trap branch on the `runPromise()` surface), this completes coverage for SPEC 9.5's three explicitly-enumerated `env` validation branches — invalid object type, invalid entry value, snapshot-time throws — on both API surfaces under `maxIterations: 0`. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55c**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: <throwing-getter-or-proxy> })` → generator throws on first `next()` with the captured snapshot-time exception. SPEC 9.5 explicitly enumerates "snapshot-time throws" alongside invalid object types and non-string values as `RunOptions.env` validation that "is independent of spawning and surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count." T-API-55a covers the invalid-object-type branch and T-API-55b covers the non-string-value branch under `maxIterations: 0`, but the **snapshot-time-throws** branch — which SPEC 9.5 names explicitly — is not currently covered under zero iterations. T-API-62e (throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env`) and T-API-62f (throwing proxy `ownKeys` trap on `options.env`) cover snapshot-time throws under normal iteration counts, but the combination with `maxIterations: 0` is not. A buggy implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` before reading the `env` proxy traps / enumerable getters would pass T-API-55a / T-API-55b (object-type and value-type validation gates) but skip the snapshot-time-throw path entirely. **Parameterize over three variants:** variant (a) **throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env`** — `const env = { A: "a" }; Object.defineProperty(env, "B", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env });`; variant (b) **throwing proxy `ownKeys` trap on `options.env`** — `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { ownKeys() { throw new Error("ownKeys-trap-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env });`; variant (c) **throwing `options.env` getter on the options object itself** — build the **final options object directly** so the throwing getter is preserved as a getter on the object passed to `run()` rather than being invoked at the call site by an object-spread (see "Test-construction sanity for getter/proxy variants" in section 1.3). Construct: `const opts: any = { maxIterations: 0 }; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("options-env-getter-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", opts);`. **Do not use `{ ...opts, maxIterations: 0 }` here**: spreading `opts` invokes the throwing `env` getter in the test harness before `run()` is called, which surfaces the exception at the test call site rather than letting loopx capture it via the SPEC 9.5 snapshot path — invalidating the assertion. The third variant is the SPEC 9.5 snapshot-time-throw class that fires when the **outer** `options.env` property access throws — distinct from variant (a) (throwing entry getter on the env object's own property) and from variant (b) (throwing proxy trap during enumeration of the env object). T-API-62 (throwing getter on options' `env` property under `maxIterations >= 1`) covers this same surface under non-zero iterations; this variant pins down the same branch under `maxIterations: 0`. For each variant, drive `await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/env-entry-getter-boom|ownKeys-trap-boom|options-env-getter-boom/);` Assert per variant: (a) the first `next()` throws the snapshot-time exception (the captured `Error` from the getter / trap), (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error — **not** a spawn-failure error and not the silent zero-iteration short-circuit, (c) no child is spawned (no marker file), and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created (consistent with SPEC 7.1 step 6 / SPEC 7.4 — tmpdir is not created under `maxIterations: 0`, and snapshot-time errors fire before tmpdir creation). Together with T-API-55a (object-type validation under `maxIterations: 0`) and T-API-55b (value-type validation under `maxIterations: 0`), this test closes the third branch of SPEC 9.5's "shape validation ... independent of spawning" enumeration — snapshot-time throws — on the generator surface across all three snapshot-throw sub-paths (inner-entry-getter, inner-proxy-trap, outer-options-env-getter). *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55d**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: <throwing-getter-or-proxy> })` → rejects with the captured snapshot-time exception. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-55c. Same SPEC 9.5 contract: "snapshot-time throws" is one of the three explicitly-enumerated `RunOptions.env` validation branches that surface under `maxIterations: 0`. T-API-55b covers the non-string-value branch on the `runPromise()` surface; this test extends the coverage to the snapshot-time-throws branch on the same surface. **Parameterize over the same three variants as T-API-55c** (throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env`, throwing proxy `ownKeys` trap on `options.env`, and throwing `options.env` getter on the options object itself). For variant (c), build the **final options object directly** so the throwing getter is preserved as a getter on the object passed to `runPromise()` rather than being invoked at the call site by an object-spread (see "Test-construction sanity for getter/proxy variants" in section 1.3). Construct: `const opts: any = { maxIterations: 0 }; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("options-env-getter-boom"); } }); await expect(runPromise("ralph", opts)).rejects.toThrow(/options-env-getter-boom/);`. **Do not use `{ ...opts, maxIterations: 0 }` here**: spreading `opts` invokes the throwing `env` getter in the test harness before `runPromise()` is called, which surfaces the exception at the test call site rather than letting loopx capture it via the SPEC 9.5 snapshot path. For each of the env-entry / proxy variants (a) and (b), drive `await expect(runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env })).rejects.toThrow(/env-entry-getter-boom|ownKeys-trap-boom/);` Assert per variant: (a) the promise rejects with the snapshot-time exception (the captured `Error` from the getter / trap), (b) the rejection is an option-snapshot error — not a spawn-failure error and not the resolved-with-`[]` zero-iteration short-circuit (which would mean the `env` snapshot was skipped), (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. Together with T-API-55c, this completes coverage for the "snapshot-time throws under `maxIterations: 0`" branch across both API surfaces and across all three snapshot-throw sub-paths (inner-entry-getter, inner-proxy-trap, outer-options-env-getter), matching the existing coverage pattern of T-API-55a / T-API-55b for the other two SPEC 9.5 enumerated branches. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*

##### Other Invalid Options Under `maxIterations: 0`

SPEC 9.1 / 9.5 specify that the option-snapshot pass runs at the call site for every `RunOptions` field, and SPEC 9.5 makes shape validation independent of spawning ("`RunOptions.env` shape validation … surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count"). The same independence applies to the other option fields' shape validation: invalid `options` wrapper, invalid `options.signal`, non-string `cwd` / `envFile`, throwing `cwd` / `envFile` getters — none of these depend on a spawn step, so all must surface under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count. T-API-55a–55d pin the rule down for `RunOptions.env`; the tests below pin it down for the remaining option fields. Existing T-API-08a–08r cover `maxIterations: 0` validation for target / envFile-load / global-env / sibling-validation paths but do not exercise the option-shape-validation paths under zero iterations, leaving a buggy implementation that short-circuits `maxIterations: 0` before reading the non-`env` option fields uncaught beyond the env axis.

- **T-API-55e**: `run()` and `runPromise()` — invalid `options` **wrapper** is rejected as an option-snapshot error before any spawn or tmpdir-creation step runs. **Important framing.** This test belongs to the "Other Invalid Options Under `maxIterations: 0`" section because it closes the same "an implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` could have skipped the option-shape gate" gap that T-API-55a–55d / 55f / 55g / 55h close for **field-level** validation, but the `maxIterations: 0` framing applies to it differently from those tests. Per SPEC 9.5, an invalid `options` wrapper (`null`, an array, a function, a primitive) cannot itself carry a `maxIterations` field; wrapper-shape validation happens before field-level processing, so the zero-iteration short-circuit is **not applicable** to this test — the contract under exercise is wrapper-shape rejection in isolation, not wrapper rejection "under" `maxIterations: 0`. T-API-55a–55d / 55f / 55g / 55h pin down the field-level "validation runs even when no iteration would have run" rule for `env`, `signal`, `cwd`, `envFile`; this test pins down the wrapper-level "wrapper-shape validation runs **before** any field-level processing" rule that those field-level tests presuppose. **Setup.** Use a **valid project** with a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` script — no other pre-iteration condition is engineered to fail. Per SPEC 9.3 "Pinned pre-iteration priority", relative priority among invalid `options`, target argument / target syntax validation, `.loopx/` discovery failures, env-file loading errors, target resolution errors, and tmpdir-creation errors is **implementation-defined**, so the test must not combine the invalid wrapper with another competing pre-iteration failure (a previous version of this test paired `":target"` syntax-invalid with no `.loopx/` discovery failure, asserting wrapper-shape rejection wins — which over-pins implementation-defined priority). **Parameterize over both run surfaces × the SPEC 9.5 invalid-`options` enumeration** (null, array, function — the same variants pinned by T-API-61 / T-API-61a / T-API-61b / T-API-61d / T-API-61e under non-zero iterations). For each surface × variant, drive: variant (a) `null`: `run("ralph", null as any)` / `runPromise("ralph", null as any)`; variant (b) `[]` (array): `run("ralph", [] as any)` / `runPromise("ralph", [] as any)`; variant (c) `() => {}` (function): `run("ralph", (() => {}) as any)` / `runPromise("ralph", (() => {}) as any)`. With a valid project and valid target there are no other pre-iteration failures to compete; the only conforming surface is the option-snapshot wrapper-shape rejection. For each surface, assert per variant: (a) the first `next()` throws / the promise rejects with an invalid-`options`-shape error, (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error — **not** a spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created (consistent with SPEC 7.1 step 6 / SPEC 7.4 — tmpdir is not created when pre-iteration fails). T-API-61 / 61a / 61b / 61d / 61e cover the same wrapper shapes under default iterations with non-zero iteration counts; this test pins down that the wrapper-shape rejection still surfaces when the rest of the pre-iteration sequence is otherwise valid, closing the "an implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` early in the option-snapshot pass could have skipped the wrapper-shape gate" gap without depending on implementation-defined priority among competing pre-iteration failures. *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55f**: `run()` and `runPromise()` — invalid `options.signal` rejects under `maxIterations: 0`. **Parameterize over both run surfaces × the SPEC 9.5 invalid-`signal` enumeration** (the same variants pinned by T-API-64 family under normal iteration counts: a string `"not-a-signal"`, a number `42`, a plain object missing `addEventListener`, a plain object missing `aborted`, a plain object whose `addEventListener` is not a function, a plain object whose `addEventListener` throws when invoked). For each surface × variant, drive `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, signal: <variant> as any })` / `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, signal: <variant> as any })`. Assert per surface × variant: (a) the first `next()` throws / the promise rejects with an invalid-`signal`-shape error, (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error — **not** the silent zero-iteration short-circuit and **not** an abort error (per SPEC 9.3, an invalid `options.signal` "captures no signal and does not enter this pathway"), (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. SPEC 9.1 says `options.signal` is read first in the option-snapshot pass; this test pins down that the read still happens (and rejects) under `maxIterations: 0`. A buggy implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` before reading `signal` would silently resolve / settle without error, failing assertion (a). *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55g**: `run()` and `runPromise()` — non-string `cwd` and non-string `envFile` reject under `maxIterations: 0`. **Parameterize over both run surfaces × {`cwd`, `envFile`} × the SPEC 9.5 invalid-string enumeration** (number `42`, boolean `true`, object `{}`, array `[]`, function `() => {}`, null — the same variants the field-shape validators reject under non-zero iterations). For each surface × field × variant, drive `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, [field]: <variant> as any })` / `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, [field]: <variant> as any })`. Assert per surface × field × variant: (a) the first `next()` throws / the promise rejects with a non-string-`<field>`-shape error mentioning the field name, (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error — **not** the silent zero-iteration short-circuit and **not** a project-root-resolution / env-file-loading error (because the field never passes shape validation, neither downstream step runs), (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. SPEC 9.5 says `cwd` and `envFile`, when present and not `undefined`, must be strings; SPEC 9.3's "Pinned pre-iteration priority" says non-string `cwd` / `envFile` surface before the four project-root-dependent failures. Under `maxIterations: 0` the project-root-dependent failures don't fire (no spawn), but the field-shape validation must still run because it is independent of spawning per the SPEC 9.5 generalization. A buggy implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` before reading `cwd` / `envFile` would silently resolve / settle without error. *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-55h**: `run()` and `runPromise()` — throwing `cwd` and throwing `envFile` getters reject under `maxIterations: 0`. **Parameterize over both run surfaces × {`cwd`, `envFile`}**. For each surface × field, build the **final options object directly** so the throwing getter is preserved as a getter on the object passed to `run()` / `runPromise()` rather than being invoked at the call site by an object-spread (see "Test-construction sanity for getter/proxy variants" in section 1.3). Construct: `const opts: any = { maxIterations: 0 }; Object.defineProperty(opts, "<field>", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("<field>-getter-boom"); } });` Drive `await expect(run("ralph", opts).next()).rejects.toThrow(/<field>-getter-boom/);` / `await expect(runPromise("ralph", opts)).rejects.toThrow(/<field>-getter-boom/);`. **Do not use `{ ...opts, maxIterations: 0 }` here**: spreading `opts` invokes the throwing field getter in the test harness before `run()` / `runPromise()` is called, which surfaces the exception at the test call site rather than letting loopx capture it via the SPEC 9.5 snapshot path — invalidating the assertion. Assert per surface × field: (a) the first `next()` throws / the promise rejects with the captured snapshot-time exception, (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error — **not** the silent zero-iteration short-circuit and **not** a project-root-resolution / env-file-loading error, (c) no child is spawned, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. T-API-62b / T-API-62c cover throwing `cwd` / `envFile` getters under non-zero iterations; this test extends the coverage to `maxIterations: 0`, mirroring the T-API-55c / T-API-55d snapshot-time-throw pattern for `env`. A buggy implementation that short-circuited `maxIterations: 0` before invoking the option-field getters would silently resolve / settle, failing assertion (a). Together with T-API-55e (invalid wrapper), T-API-55f (invalid signal), T-API-55g (non-string cwd / envFile), and T-API-55a–55d (env shape / value / snapshot-time-throw), this completes coverage for the SPEC 9.5 generalization that **all** option-shape validation surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` independent of spawning, across every `RunOptions` field. *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.5)*

##### Inherited / Symbol / Non-Enumerable Keys

- **T-API-56**: Inherited keys on the options.env object are ignored. `const proto = { INHERITED: "proto-val" }; const e = Object.create(proto); e.OWN = "own-val"; runPromise("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes both. Assert `OWN` present with `"own-val"` and `INHERITED` absent — loopx reads only own enumerable string-keyed properties. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56a**: Symbol-keyed entries are ignored. `const sym = Symbol("SYM"); const e = { OWN: "own-val", [sym]: "sym-val" }; runPromise("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes `OWN` and (via TS `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols` or simply observing all `LOOPX_`-free env keys) asserts that no symbol-derived key leaked into the child env. Mainly: `OWN` is present, and the child env has no `SYM`-like stringified key. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56b**: Non-enumerable string-keyed entries are ignored. `const e = {}; Object.defineProperty(e, "HIDDEN", { value: "hidden-val", enumerable: false }); e.VISIBLE = "visible-val"; runPromise("ralph", { env: e, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes both. Assert `VISIBLE` present, `HIDDEN` absent. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56c**: Null-prototype `options.env` (`Object.create(null)`) is accepted as a valid env shape — own enumerable string-keyed entries reach the spawned script. SPEC 9.5 specifies the `env` shape as "any non-null, non-array, non-function object whose own enumerable string-keyed entries all have string values" — same structural-not-nominal contract as the parent `options` shape (T-API-61i). A null-prototype env object lacks `Object.prototype` (no `hasOwnProperty`, no `toString`); a buggy implementation that uses `instanceof Object` to validate the env shape (which fails for null-prototype objects) or that calls `env.hasOwnProperty(...)` directly (which throws `TypeError`) instead of the prototype-safe `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(env, key)` or `Object.keys(env)` would silently reject or crash on this fixture. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` and `OTHERVAR` via `observe-env`. Drive: `const env = Object.create(null); env.MYVAR = "from-options"; env.OTHERVAR = "second-value"; await runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise resolves (no rejection — null-prototype is a conforming env shape), (b) the script's `MYVAR` marker records `{ "present": true, "value": "from-options" }` and `OTHERVAR` records `{ "present": true, "value": "second-value" }` — both own enumerable string-keyed entries reached the child env, and (c) no warning on stderr about the env shape (the absence of `Object.prototype` is structurally fine). A buggy implementation that rejected null-prototype env via `instanceof Object` would fail (a); an implementation that called `env.hasOwnProperty(...)` to enumerate would throw at snapshot time and fail (a) with a `TypeError`. Together with T-API-61i / T-API-61j (null-prototype `options`), this closes the null-prototype-acceptance gap across both the outer `options` shape and the inner `options.env` shape. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56d**: Symbol-keyed property with a **throwing getter** on `options.env` is ignored — the symbol-keyed slot is not read, so the getter never fires and the throw never surfaces. SPEC 9.5's enumeration rule is "own enumerable **string-keyed** entries only" — symbol-keyed properties (own or otherwise) are excluded by predicate, not by exception-handling. T-API-56a covers the basic symbol-keyed-ignore case (a value getter that returns a string); this test pins down the stronger contract: the getter's **side effects** (including a thrown error) must not surface, because loopx must not reach the symbol-keyed slot during enumeration. A buggy implementation that enumerated `Reflect.ownKeys(env)` (which includes symbols) and then filtered by `typeof key === "string"` after invoking the getter would surface the symbol getter's throw as a snapshot-time error — but per SPEC 9.5 the symbol entry is never read in the first place, so a throw observed on the symbol getter is a contract violation. Drive: `const sym = Symbol("SYM-throw"); const env = { OWN: "own-val" }; Object.defineProperty(env, sym, { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("symbol-getter-should-never-fire"); } }); await runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise **resolves** (does not reject — the symbol getter was never invoked), (b) the script observed `OWN` with value `"own-val"` (the string-keyed entry reached the child), (c) stderr contains no error referencing `symbol-getter-should-never-fire` and no snapshot-time error (no observable side effect from the symbol getter — proves the symbol slot was filtered before the getter was reached). T-API-56a covers "symbol-keyed entries are absent from the child env"; this test additionally pins down "symbol-keyed slots are never enumerated, so their getters never fire" via the stronger throwing-getter contract. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56e**: Non-enumerable string-keyed property with a **throwing getter** is ignored — the non-enumerable slot is not read, so the getter never fires. Companion to T-API-56d for the non-enumerable-ignore axis. SPEC 9.5's "own enumerable string-keyed entries only" rule excludes non-enumerable properties by predicate; T-API-56b covers the basic non-enumerable-ignore case (with a value descriptor); this test extends it to the throwing-getter variant, proving the non-enumerable slot is never reached during enumeration. Drive: `const env = { VISIBLE: "visible-val" }; Object.defineProperty(env, "HIDDEN", { enumerable: false, get() { throw new Error("non-enumerable-getter-should-never-fire"); } }); await runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise resolves (the non-enumerable getter was not invoked), (b) the script observed `VISIBLE` with value `"visible-val"` (the enumerable entry reached the child), (c) stderr contains no error referencing `non-enumerable-getter-should-never-fire`. A buggy implementation that called `Object.getOwnPropertyNames(env)` (which includes both enumerable and non-enumerable) and filtered after reading would surface the throw and fail (a). T-API-56b covers "non-enumerable entries are absent from the child env"; this test pins down "non-enumerable slots are never read." *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56f**: Inherited property with a **non-string value** on `options.env`'s prototype is ignored — the inherited slot is not read, so its non-string value never triggers shape validation. Companion to T-API-56 for the inherited-ignore axis with a sharper contract. SPEC 9.5's "own enumerable string-keyed entries only" rule excludes inherited properties by predicate; T-API-56 covers the basic inherited-ignore case (with a string value); this test extends it to the **non-string-value** variant, proving the inherited slot is never read — because if it were read, its non-string value would surface as a shape-validation error per T-API-54 (non-string entry value rejected). Construct an env whose prototype has a non-string-valued inherited key alongside an own string-keyed entry: `const proto = { INHERITED_NUMBER: 42 }; const env = Object.create(proto); env.OWN = "own-val"; await runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise resolves (the inherited non-string value never reached the value-shape validator — because the inherited slot was never read), (b) the script observed `OWN` with value `"own-val"` (the own entry reached the child), and `INHERITED_NUMBER` is absent from the child env (the inherited slot is filtered, matching T-API-56's basic absence assertion). A buggy implementation that enumerated via `for ... in` (which walks the prototype chain) and then validated values would surface `INHERITED_NUMBER: 42` to the value validator and reject the env shape, failing (a). T-API-56 covers "inherited slots produce absence in the child"; this test pins down "inherited slots are never even validated for shape, because they are filtered out **before** value validation runs" — a stronger contract that catches buggy enumeration order (validate-then-filter rather than filter-then-validate). *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56g**: Class-instance `options.env` is accepted as a valid env shape — own enumerable string-keyed entries declared as class fields reach the spawned script. SPEC 9.5 specifies the env shape as "any non-null, non-array, non-function object whose own enumerable string-keyed entries all have string values" — the contract is structural (any non-plain object qualifies provided it satisfies the predicate), not nominal (the contract does not require `Object.prototype` membership or a literal `{ ... }` constructor). T-API-56c covers the null-prototype variant (no prototype at all); this test covers the inverse non-plain shape — a non-`Object.prototype` instance with a class prototype chain — which exercises the same structural-not-nominal contract from the opposite direction. A buggy implementation that accepted only plain object literals (e.g., `Object.getPrototypeOf(env) === Object.prototype`, `env.constructor === Object`, `env instanceof Object && Object.getPrototypeOf(env) === Object.prototype`, or any other "is plain" check) would silently reject this shape and surface a snapshot-time env-shape error. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env`. Drive: `class Env { MYVAR = "from-class"; } await runPromise("ralph", { env: new Env() as any, maxIterations: 1 });` (Class field declarations create own enumerable string-keyed properties on the instance, equivalent to `this.MYVAR = "from-class"` in the constructor — this is the canonical ES2022 class-field semantics, not a property on `Env.prototype`.) Assert: (a) the promise resolves (no rejection — the class-instance shape conforms to SPEC 9.5's structural contract), (b) the script's `MYVAR` marker records `{ "present": true, "value": "from-class" }` (the own enumerable string-keyed class-field entry reached the child env), and (c) no warning on stderr about the env shape (a non-plain prototype is structurally fine; the absence of `Object.prototype` does not invalidate the shape). Together with T-API-56c (null-prototype env on the no-prototype side), this closes the structural-not-nominal env-shape gap across both non-plain-object directions and prevents an "implementation only accepts plain object literals" gap. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-56h**: A `Map` passed as `options.env` is **accepted as a valid env shape** but contributes **zero environment variables** to the spawned child — Map entries are stored in the engine-internal `[[MapData]]` slot, not as own enumerable string-keyed properties, and so are filtered out by SPEC 9.5's "own enumerable string-keyed entries" predicate. Companion to T-API-56g for the structural-not-nominal contract: T-API-56g proves that a non-plain object with own string-keyed fields is accepted **and contributes**; this test proves that a non-plain object whose conceptual entries live outside the own-enumerable-string-keyed surface is accepted **but contributes nothing**. The combined coverage prevents an implementation from special-casing `Map` (or any other built-in collection type) into an "auto-enumerate the conceptual entries" code path that would violate SPEC 9.5's strict own-enumerable-string-key predicate. SPEC 9.5 explicitly rejects only `null`, arrays, and functions — `Map` is none of those, so it must be accepted as a shape. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env`, ensuring the test driver scrubs `MYVAR` from the inherited environment before spawning loopx so that any observed `MYVAR` value can only come from the `env` option. Drive: `const env = new Map([["MYVAR", "x"]]) as any; await runPromise("ralph", { env, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise resolves (no rejection — `Map` is a non-null, non-array, non-function object, so the shape is accepted per SPEC 9.5), (b) the script's `MYVAR` marker records `{ "present": false }` — the Map's `[["MYVAR", "x"]]` entry is **not** exposed as an own enumerable string-keyed property on the `Map` object, so SPEC 9.5's predicate filters it out and no `MYVAR` value reaches the child env, and (c) no warning on stderr about the env shape (acceptance is silent — the empty enumeration result is not an error). A buggy implementation that special-cased `Map` and iterated its conceptual entries would surface `MYVAR=x` to the child and fail (b); a buggy implementation that rejected `Map` as an invalid env shape would fail (a). *(Spec 9.5)*

##### Runtime-Rejected Names / Values (Embedded NUL)

- **T-API-57**: `RunOptions.env` entry with embedded NUL in the value surfaces as a spawn failure **and `LOOPX_TMPDIR` is cleaned up before rejection**. `runPromise("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "bad\0val" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Per SPEC 9.5 "No name validation beyond string-to-string" — runtime-level rejections (embedded NUL) surface at child launch / spawn failure.
