Per SPEC 7.2 / 7.4 / 9.3, child-launch / spawn failure after tmpdir creation is a normative cleanup trigger and cleanup runs before the promise rejects. **Run under a test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent** (per the section 4.7 suite-wide isolation guidance) so the post-rejection scan is bounded. Assert: (a) the promise rejects with a spawn-failure error — the error is *not* a shape-validation error (shape validation passed; the rejection comes from the runtime's environment-building layer), (b) the workflow script did not run (no marker file from `ralph:index`), and (c) **`LOOPX_TMPDIR` cleanup ran before the promise rejected** — no `loopx-*` directory remains under the test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent (cleanup is normative per SPEC 7.4 "Cleanup triggers" and the ordering is normative per SPEC 9.3 "Cleanup ordering is observable. When `LOOPX_TMPDIR` cleanup runs as part of an error path ... it runs before the generator throws or the promise rejects"). A buggy implementation that surfaced the spawn failure without running cleanup would leave a `loopx-*` directory under the isolated parent and fail (c). *(Spec 7.2, 7.4, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-57a**: `RunOptions.env` entry with embedded NUL in the key surfaces as a spawn failure **and `LOOPX_TMPDIR` is cleaned up before rejection**. `runPromise("ralph", { env: { "BAD\0KEY": "val" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Same SPEC contract as T-API-57 (runtime rejection at spawn, cleanup is normative, ordering is observable). **Run under a test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent** per the section 4.7 suite-wide isolation guidance. Assert: (a) the promise rejects with a spawn-failure error, (b) the workflow script did not run (no marker file), and (c) **no `loopx-*` directory remains under the test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent** — cleanup ran before the promise rejected. NUL-in-key counterpart to T-API-57 (NUL-in-value); both must satisfy the same cleanup contract. *(Spec 7.2, 7.4, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-57h**: Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-57 — `run("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "bad\0val" }, maxIterations: 1 })` throws a spawn failure on first `next()` and `LOOPX_TMPDIR` is cleaned up before the throw. SPEC 9.1 / 9.3 specify that the generator surface uses the same pre-iteration error path as `runPromise()` for spawn failures, and SPEC 7.4's cleanup trigger list ("Child launch / spawn failure after tmpdir creation") is independent of which run surface is in use. T-API-57 covers `runPromise()`; T-API-57f covers the generator under `maxIterations: 0` (no spawn → no spawn failure). This test closes the non-zero-iteration `run()` branch. **Run under a test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent** per the section 4.7 suite-wide isolation guidance. Drive `const g = run("ralph", { env: { MYVAR: "bad\0val" }, maxIterations: 1 }); await g.next();`. Assert: (a) the first `next()` rejects (the generator throws) with a spawn-failure error — not a shape-validation error, (b) the workflow script did not run, and (c) no `loopx-*` directory remains under the test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent — cleanup ran before the throw, mirroring SPEC 9.3's "Cleanup ordering is observable" clause on the generator surface. *(Spec 7.2, 7.4, 9.1, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-57i**: Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-57a — `run("ralph", { env: { "BAD\0KEY": "val" }, maxIterations: 1 })` throws a spawn failure on first `next()` and `LOOPX_TMPDIR` is cleaned up before the throw. NUL-in-key counterpart to T-API-57h. **Run under a test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent** per the section 4.7 suite-wide isolation guidance. Assert: (a) the first `next()` rejects with a spawn-failure error, (b) the workflow script did not run, and (c) no `loopx-*` directory remains under the test-isolated `TMPDIR` parent. Together with T-API-57 / T-API-57a (`runPromise()`) and T-API-57h (NUL-in-value, `run()`), this completes the four-way matrix (run surface × NUL-position) for `RunOptions.env` runtime-rejection spawn failures, each pinning down the cleanup-before-error-surface contract. *(Spec 7.2, 7.4, 9.1, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-57b**: `RunOptions.env` entry with `=` in the key surfaces per runtime behavior. `runPromise("ralph", { env: { "BAD=KEY": "val" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Per SPEC 9.5, loopx does not reject `=` in env names at the option-shape level; runtime behavior is not pinned beyond "Runtime-level rejections ... surface as child launch / spawn failures at spawn time." On POSIX, an env entry serialized from a key containing `=` may be rejected by the runtime, or may be accepted with runtime/OS-dependent parsing that does **not** preserve the original key name as observable in `process.env`. Assert only: (a) loopx does **not** reject the env object as an option-shape / shape-validation error (the failure surface is never "invalid `options.env` shape"), and (b) one of two outcomes holds — either the spawn fails as a child launch / spawn failure (the promise rejects with a spawn-failure error per SPEC 7.2 / 9.3), or the child spawns successfully (the promise resolves with one Output). Do **not** assert any specific key/value mapping for `"BAD=KEY"` in the spawn-success branch — the runtime's parsing of the malformed serialized entry is OS- and runtime-dependent and may not preserve `"BAD=KEY"` as a recoverable key name; pinning a specific observable mapping would over-pin behavior SPEC 9.5 deliberately leaves implementation-defined. Documents the "runtime does not distinguish tiers" clause: any env tier (not just `RunOptions.env`) with the same `=`-in-name entry has the same outcome envelope. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-57c**: `RunOptions.env` entry with an **empty-string key** is not rejected at the shape level. Per SPEC 9.5 "No name validation beyond string-to-string ... does not reject `=` or empty-string names". Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { "": "empty-key-value" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. The shape is valid (own enumerable string-keyed property with a string value). Outcome is implementation-defined and runtime-dependent: either (a) the runtime rejects the empty-string key at spawn and the promise rejects with a spawn-failure error (not an option-shape-validation error), or (b) the runtime accepts it and the child env has whatever the runtime does with an empty name (observably absent or present — either is conforming). Assert: the outcome is either (a) or (b) — the CLI exit code / generator outcome is never some third category, and in particular the failure surface is never "invalid `options.env` shape". T-API-57 covers NUL in value, T-API-57a covers NUL in key, T-API-57b covers `=` in key; this test closes the empty-string-key branch, completing shape-level-accepted runtime-rejected coverage. *(Spec 9.5, 9.3)*
- **T-API-57d**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: { MYVAR: "bad\0val" } })` resolves `[]` rather than rejecting with a spawn failure. SPEC 9.5 / 4.2 / 7.1 specify that `maxIterations: 0` (and CLI `-n 0`) "executes zero iterations" — no child is spawned, and the runtime-rejection paths that surface as spawn failures (NUL in env name or value, etc.) cannot fire because there is no spawn step. SPEC 9.5 "No name validation beyond string-to-string" says shape validation is independent of spawning and surfaces under `maxIterations: 0` just as under any other iteration count, **but NUL in a value is not a shape-validation failure** — it is a runtime-rejection that surfaces only at spawn time. Therefore under `maxIterations: 0` the NUL value silently survives the (skipped) spawn step, and the promise resolves with the empty array per SPEC 9.5's `maxIterations: 0` contract. Assert: (a) the promise resolves (does not reject), (b) the resolved value is `[]` (no Output objects yielded), (c) no spawn-failure error appears on stderr, and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created (consistent with SPEC 7.1 step 6 / SPEC 7.4 — tmpdir is not created under `maxIterations: 0`). This is the zero-iteration counterpart to T-API-57 (which exercises the spawn-failure path at `maxIterations: 1`); it pins down that the spawn-failure path is **gated on the spawn step actually running**, not on the env value being parsed. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2, 7.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-57e**: `runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: { "BAD\0KEY": "val" } })` resolves `[]` — NUL in **key** under zero iterations also does not surface as a spawn failure. Same rationale as T-API-57d: the runtime-rejection path requires a spawn step; under `maxIterations: 0` no spawn occurs, so the NUL key survives silently and the promise resolves with `[]`. Assert: (a) resolves, (b) value is `[]`, (c) no spawn-failure error on stderr, (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` created. Together with T-API-57d, this completes zero-iteration coverage for both NUL-in-value and NUL-in-key branches of the runtime-rejection path. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2, 7.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-57f**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: { MYVAR: "bad\0val" } })` (the `run()` generator surface, not `runPromise()`) — generator completes immediately on first `next()` with `{ done: true, value: undefined }` and yields no outputs. Same SPEC contract as T-API-57d: under `maxIterations: 0` no spawn occurs, so the NUL value does not surface as a spawn failure on the generator surface either. The generator surface is a separate observation point per SPEC 9.1 vs. 9.2; this test is the `run()` counterpart to T-API-57d. Assert: (a) the first `next()` returns `{ done: true, value: undefined }` (no first yield), (b) no spawn-failure error on stderr, (c) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` created. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 7.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-57f2**: `run("ralph", { maxIterations: 0, env: { "BAD\0KEY": "val" } })` — generator completes immediately with `{ done: true, value: undefined }` and yields no outputs. NUL-in-**key** counterpart to T-API-57f (NUL in value) on the `run()` generator surface, completing the zero-iteration matrix: T-API-57d covers `runPromise()` + NUL-in-value, T-API-57e covers `runPromise()` + NUL-in-key, T-API-57f covers `run()` + NUL-in-value, and this test covers the remaining `run()` + NUL-in-key branch. Same SPEC rationale: under `maxIterations: 0` no spawn occurs, so the runtime-rejection path for a NUL-containing name never fires, and the generator completes silently. Assert: (a) first `next()` returns `{ done: true, value: undefined }` (no yield), (b) no spawn-failure error on stderr, (c) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` created. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1, 7.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-57g**: Positive coverage — `RunOptions.env` accepts names that do **not** match the POSIX `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*` pattern, and those names reach the spawned script. Per SPEC 9.5: "loopx does not enforce the POSIX `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*` name pattern". Existing T-API-57 / T-API-57a / T-API-57b / T-API-57c cover the runtime-rejection paths (NUL in value, NUL in key, `=` in key, empty-string key) — they prove loopx itself does not enforce POSIX name validation, but they pivot on outcomes that are implementation-defined or surface as errors. None of them assert the **positive case**: a non-POSIX name that the runtime accepts must reach the spawned script unchanged. Without a positive test, an implementation that wrongly applied env-file POSIX-name validation (the SPEC 8.1 env-file key pattern) to `RunOptions.env` would silently drop these names and pass T-API-57 / T-API-57a / T-API-57b / T-API-57c — none of those would catch a missing positive case. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.ts` containing a TS script that reads `process.env["1BAD"]` and `process.env["FOO-BAR"]` and writes both observed values to a marker file (e.g., JSON-encoded so empty / undefined values are distinguishable from the supplied strings). Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { "1BAD": "ok-digit-prefix", "FOO-BAR": "ok-dash-interior" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) the promise resolves (no rejection — these names violate the POSIX pattern but loopx does not validate), (b) the marker file records `process.env["1BAD"] === "ok-digit-prefix"` and `process.env["FOO-BAR"] === "ok-dash-interior"` — both names reached the child env unchanged. The `1BAD` name (digit-first) and `FOO-BAR` name (dash-interior) are the same two violation classes that the env-file key validator (T-SUB-10 digit-first, T-SUB-11b dash-interior) would reject; this test pins down that `RunOptions.env` does **not** apply that validator. **Note on runtime rejection.** Node.js (and Bun) do not reject these names at the spawn / `process.env` level — unlike NUL bytes, non-POSIX names propagate through `child_process.spawn`'s `env` option and appear in the child's `process.env` exactly as supplied. So both observation values are deterministic on the runtimes this suite targets (Node ≥ 20.6, Bun ≥ 1.0). If a future runtime did reject these names, the failure would surface as a spawn-failure error per the "runtime does not distinguish tiers" clause (T-API-57b's outcome shape applies); but on current runtimes the positive assertions hold. *(Spec 9.5)*

##### `LOOPX_*` Keys in `RunOptions.env`

- **T-API-58**: `RunOptions.env` supplying a `LOOPX_*` key is silently overridden by protocol injection (no error, no warning). `runPromise("ralph", { env: { LOOPX_WORKFLOW: "user-fake", CUSTOM: "user-val" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes both. Assert: `LOOPX_WORKFLOW` shows the real workflow name, `CUSTOM` shows `"user-val"`. Stderr contains no warning about `LOOPX_WORKFLOW` being overridden — loopx does not warn (Spec 9.5). *(Spec 9.5, 8.3)*
- **T-API-58a**: `RunOptions.env.LOOPX_DELEGATED` reaches spawned scripts (not a script-protocol-protected tier). Per Spec 9.5, `LOOPX_DELEGATED` is startup-reserved only, not script-protocol-protected — `RunOptions.env` may supply it to spawned scripts. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { LOOPX_DELEGATED: "user-supplied" } })`. Script observes `LOOPX_DELEGATED`. Assert marker shows `"user-supplied"`. This distinguishes the five script-protocol-protected names (T-API-51a/58) from the startup-only `LOOPX_DELEGATED`. *(Spec 9.5, 3.2, 8.3, 13)*

##### `RunOptions.env` Does Not Affect loopx's Own Lookups

- **T-API-59**: `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect global env-file lookup. Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME=/real/config/path`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { XDG_CONFIG_HOME: "/fake/config/path" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` — assert marker shows `"/fake/config/path"` (the `RunOptions.env` value reaches the child). But also: place an env file at `/real/config/path/loopx/env` containing `MARKER=real`, and at `/fake/config/path/loopx/env` containing `MARKER=fake`. Script observes `MARKER`. Assert marker shows `"real"` — the global env file was resolved using loopx's own `process.env` (not `RunOptions.env`). Per Spec 8.1 "`RunOptions.env` does **not** affect global env-file path resolution". *(Spec 8.1, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-59a**: `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect global env-file lookup via `HOME`. Similar structure to T-API-59 but using `HOME` instead of `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (the fallback path). Unset `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, set `process.env.HOME=/real/home`. Call with `RunOptions.env.HOME=/fake/home`. Place env files at both `/real/home/.config/loopx/env` (`MARKER=real`) and `/fake/home/.config/loopx/env` (`MARKER=fake`). Assert script's `MARKER` observation is `"real"` (loopx read from `$HOME/.config/loopx/env` using loopx's own `HOME`, not the child's). *(Spec 8.1, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-59b**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect global env-file lookup via `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-59 (`runPromise()`). The two run surfaces have **different snapshot timing** for inherited `process.env` (lazy under `run()` per SPEC 9.1; eager under `runPromise()` per SPEC 9.2) but both must apply SPEC 8.1's "`RunOptions.env` does **not** affect global env-file path resolution" rule. A buggy implementation that re-read `RunOptions.env` at first `next()` and merged it into the lazy `process.env` snapshot before resolving the global env-file path on the `run()` path — but performed eager resolution correctly under `runPromise()` — would pass T-API-59 but fail this test. **Setup.** Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME=/real/config/path`. Place env files at `/real/config/path/loopx/env` containing `MARKER=real`, and at `/fake/config/path/loopx/env` containing `MARKER=fake`. Place `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (or `.ts`) that observes both `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and `MARKER` via `observe-env`, then emits `stop:true`. **Drive.** `const gen = run("ralph", { env: { XDG_CONFIG_HOME: "/fake/config/path" }, maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const _ of gen) {}` (or equivalent generator drive pattern). Assert: (a) the marker records `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME === "/fake/config/path"` — the `RunOptions.env` value reaches the child via the SPEC 8.3 tier-2 `RunOptions.env` injection, (b) the marker records `process.env.MARKER === "real"` — the global env file was resolved using loopx's own `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (the inherited-env value `/real/config/path`), **not** the `RunOptions.env` value, and (c) the generator settled cleanly (no error). Together with T-API-59 (`runPromise()`), this closes the SPEC 8.1 / 9.5 contract on both API surfaces. *(Spec 8.1, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-59c**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect global env-file lookup via `HOME`. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-59a (`runPromise()`), and the `HOME`-fallback counterpart to T-API-59b (`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`). Same structure as T-API-59b but exercising the `HOME` fallback path: unset `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` from loopx's inherited environment, set `process.env.HOME=/real/home`, place env files at both `/real/home/.config/loopx/env` (`MARKER=real`) and `/fake/home/.config/loopx/env` (`MARKER=fake`). **Drive.** `const gen = run("ralph", { env: { HOME: "/fake/home" }, maxIterations: 1 }); for await (const _ of gen) {}`. Assert: (a) the marker records `process.env.HOME === "/fake/home"` — the `RunOptions.env` value reaches the child, (b) the marker records `process.env.MARKER === "real"` — loopx read from `$HOME/.config/loopx/env` using loopx's own `HOME`, not the child's `HOME` from `RunOptions.env`, and (c) the generator settled cleanly. Together with T-API-59 / T-API-59a / T-API-59b, this closes the `RunOptions.env`-does-not-redirect-global-env-file-path-resolution rule across both surfaces × both fallback variables. *(Spec 8.1, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-60**: `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect tmpdir parent selection. Set `process.env.TMPDIR=/tmp/real-parent`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { env: { TMPDIR: "/tmp/fake-parent" }, maxIterations: 1 })`. Script observes both `TMPDIR` and `LOOPX_TMPDIR`. Assert: (a) child's `TMPDIR` is `"/tmp/fake-parent"` (the `RunOptions.env` value reaches the child), (b) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/real-parent/` (loopx's own tmpdir parent was captured from loopx's own `process.env`). *(Spec 7.4, 8.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-60a**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect tmpdir parent selection. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-60 (`runPromise()`) and T-TMP-29 (which uses `runPromise()` even though it lives in the tmpdir block). The two run surfaces have **different snapshot timing** for inherited env / tmpdir parent (lazy on `run()` per SPEC 9.1 / 7.4; eager on `runPromise()` per SPEC 9.2 / 7.4) but **identical timing** for `RunOptions.env` (eager / call-site capture on both surfaces per SPEC 9.5). A buggy implementation that special-cased the tmpdir-parent / `RunOptions.env` interaction on the lazy-snapshot `run()` surface — perhaps by re-reading `RunOptions.env` at first `next()` and merging it into the lazy `process.env` snapshot before computing `os.tmpdir()` — would pass T-API-60 / T-TMP-29 but fail this test. **Pre-create `/tmp/real-parent/`** before invocation (the inherited-env value, which is the parent loopx's own `os.tmpdir()` selects); the wrong-parent value need not exist on disk because loopx must not consult it. Set loopx inherited `TMPDIR=/tmp/real-parent`. Drive: `process.env.TMPDIR = "/tmp/real-parent"; const gen = run("ralph", { env: { TMPDIR: "/tmp/fake-parent" }, maxIterations: 1 }); await gen.next();` Fixture `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` observes both `TMPDIR` and `LOOPX_TMPDIR` via `observe-env`, then emits `stop:true`. Assert: (a) child's observed `TMPDIR` is `"/tmp/fake-parent"` (the `RunOptions.env` value reached the child via the SPEC 8.3 tier-2 `RunOptions.env` injection), (b) `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/real-parent/` (loopx's own tmpdir-parent selection used the lazy inherited-env snapshot of `process.env.TMPDIR` per SPEC 7.4 / 9.1, not the eager `RunOptions.env` capture). Together with T-API-60 (`runPromise()`) and T-TMP-29 / 29b / 29c (parameterized over `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` on `runPromise()`), this closes the `RunOptions.env` × tmpdir-parent contract on both run surfaces. *(Spec 7.4, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-60b**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect tmpdir parent selection via `TEMP`. SPEC 7.4 / 9.5 apply the `RunOptions.env`-does-not-redirect-tmpdir rule symmetrically across `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP`. T-API-60a covers `TMPDIR` on the `run()` surface, and T-TMP-29b covers `TEMP` on the `runPromise()` surface; this test parameterizes the `run()` generator surface over `TEMP`. A buggy implementation that special-cased the `TEMP` branch of `os.tmpdir()` resolution on the lazy-snapshot `run()` surface — perhaps by re-reading `RunOptions.env.TEMP` at first `next()` and merging it into the lazy `process.env` snapshot — would pass T-API-60a / T-TMP-29b but fail this test. **Pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation. Configure the driver process's inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TMP` **unset**, `TEMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TMP; process.env.TEMP = "/tmp/right-parent"; const gen = run("ralph", { env: { TEMP: "/tmp/wrong-parent" }, maxIterations: 1 }); await gen.next();` Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TEMP` via `observe-env`, then emits `stop:true`. Apply the runtime-aware expected-parent logic from T-TMP-25a: assert `LOOPX_TMPDIR` lives under `/tmp/right-parent` **iff** the active runtime's `os.tmpdir()` consults `TEMP` in this configuration; otherwise assert the parent matches `os.tmpdir()` evaluated in an identically-configured child process. Always assert the child's observed `TEMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent` (the `RunOptions.env` value reached the spawned script's environment per the SPEC 8.3 `RunOptions.env` tier). **Restore original `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP` values in a `finally` block.** *(Spec 7.4, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-60c**: `run()` — `RunOptions.env` does **not** redirect tmpdir parent selection via `TMP`. Same pattern as T-API-60b for the `TMP` branch: **pre-create `/tmp/right-parent/`** before invocation; configure inherited env with `TMPDIR` **unset**, `TEMP` **unset**, `TMP=/tmp/right-parent`. Drive: `delete process.env.TMPDIR; delete process.env.TEMP; process.env.TMP = "/tmp/right-parent"; const gen = run("ralph", { env: { TMP: "/tmp/wrong-parent" }, maxIterations: 1 }); await gen.next();` Fixture observes `LOOPX_TMPDIR` and `TMP`. Apply the same runtime-aware expected-parent logic (T-TMP-25b). Always assert the child's observed `TMP` equals `/tmp/wrong-parent`. Together with T-API-60a (`run()` × `TMPDIR`), T-API-60b (`run()` × `TEMP`), T-API-60 (`runPromise()` × `TMPDIR`), and T-TMP-29 / 29b / 29c (`runPromise()` × `TMPDIR` / `TEMP` / `TMP`), this closes the `RunOptions.env` × tmpdir-parent contract across both run surfaces × all three variables. **Restore original env values in `finally`.** *(Spec 7.4, 8.3, 9.1, 9.5)*

#### Advanced `options` / `options.signal` Semantics

Spec 9.1 / 9.3 / 9.5 describe option-snapshot behavior, pre-abort precedence, pinned-priority, pre-first-`next()` carve-out, and other advanced semantics. These tests close coverage.

##### `options` Shape Validation

- **T-API-61**: `run("ralph", null)` → generator throws on first `next()`. Per Spec 9.5, `options` must be omitted, `undefined`, or a non-null non-array non-function object. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-61a**: `run("ralph", [])` (array) → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-61b**: `run("ralph", () => {})` (function) → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-61c**: `run("ralph", "string")` → generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-61d**: `runPromise("ralph", null)` → rejects. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-61e**: `runPromise("ralph", [])` → rejects. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-61f**: `run("ralph", undefined)` is equivalent to `run("ralph")` — explicit `undefined` is accepted as "no options supplied". SPEC 9.5 says `options` may be omitted or `undefined`; existing T-API-61 / T-API-61a / T-API-61c / T-API-61e cover the rejected-shape cases, but explicit `undefined` is a conforming value and must be pinned down positively so a buggy implementation cannot silently tighten acceptance to "omitted only". Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (emits `stop: true`). Drive `run("ralph", undefined)`, iterate to completion. Assert: (a) the generator returns normally (no throw on call or first `next()`), (b) the script executed exactly once (marker file written), and (c) behavior is indistinguishable from `run("ralph")` with `options` omitted — same yield count, same cwd (defaults to `process.cwd()`), same (absent) env-file load. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-61g**: `runPromise("ralph", undefined)` is equivalent to `runPromise("ralph")`. `runPromise` counterpart to T-API-61f. Drive `await runPromise("ralph", undefined)` on the same fixture. Assert: the promise resolves (does not reject), the resolved array has exactly one Output entry, and behavior matches `runPromise("ralph")` with `options` omitted. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-61i**: `run("ralph", Object.create(null))` is accepted as a valid options shape — null-prototype objects are conforming. SPEC 9.5 specifies the options shape as "non-null, non-array, non-function object" — the contract is structural ("is it an object that is not specifically excluded"), not nominal ("is it an `instanceof Object` instance with the standard prototype"). A null-prototype object created via `Object.create(null)` is a non-null, non-array, non-function object; it lacks `Object.prototype` (no `hasOwnProperty`, no `toString`, no `__proto__`), but those absences are irrelevant to the SPEC 9.5 contract. T-API-61 / T-API-61a–61c cover the rejected-shape negative cases (null, array, function, string, number); T-API-61f / T-API-61g cover explicit `undefined` as the absent-options case; this test pins down the **null-prototype positive case** so a buggy implementation that uses `instanceof Object` for shape validation (which fails for null-prototype objects since they have no prototype chain to walk to `Object.prototype`) or that calls `options.hasOwnProperty(...)` directly (which throws `TypeError: options.hasOwnProperty is not a function` on null-prototype objects) would be caught. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (emits `stop: true`). Drive: `const opts = Object.create(null); opts.maxIterations = 1; opts.cwd = projectDir; const gen = run("ralph", opts);` then iterate to completion. Assert: (a) the generator returns normally (no throw on call or first `next()`), (b) the script executed exactly once (marker file written), and (c) the iteration count, cwd resolution, and absence of env-file load match a corresponding plain-object call (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1, cwd: projectDir })`) — the prototype chain difference is invisible to the consumer. A buggy implementation that rejected null-prototype objects via `instanceof Object` would fail (a); an implementation that read options via `hasOwnProperty` without falling back to `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(opts, key)` would throw at call time and fail (a); an implementation that walked the prototype chain to read field defaults would observe `undefined` for every field and apply the defaults (which is conforming and indistinguishable from absent fields) — the test does not over-pin that detail. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-61j**: `runPromise("ralph", Object.create(null))` is accepted — null-prototype options are conforming on the `runPromise()` surface. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-61i. Same rationale: SPEC 9.5's "non-null, non-array, non-function object" contract holds across both run surfaces, so the null-prototype acceptance must hold on `runPromise()` as well. Drive: `const opts = Object.create(null); opts.maxIterations = 1; const result = await runPromise("ralph", opts);` Assert: (a) the promise resolves (does not reject), (b) the resolved array has exactly one Output entry, and (c) behavior matches the plain-object call. Together with T-API-61i, this completes null-prototype-options coverage on both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-61h**: `runPromise("ralph", { cwd: undefined, envFile: undefined, maxIterations: undefined, signal: undefined, env: undefined })` treats every option field as absent. SPEC 9.5 says each option field, when present and `undefined`, is equivalent to the field being omitted — `cwd` defaults to `process.cwd()`, `envFile` is unused, `maxIterations` has no bound, `signal` captures no signal, `env` contributes no entries. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that emits `stop: true` on first iteration (so the run terminates naturally with no `maxIterations` bound). Call `runPromise` with an `options` object whose five pinned fields all explicitly carry `undefined`. Assert: (a) the promise resolves (does not reject) — all five `undefined` values are valid per SPEC 9.5's `T | undefined` field types, (b) the resolved array has exactly one Output entry, (c) the child ran in `process.cwd()` (cwd not redirected), and (d) the spawned environment does not contain any marker-only env key that would only be present if an `env`/`envFile` had been supplied. A buggy shape validator that rejected explicit-`undefined` fields as "not provided but defined" would fail (a); a buggy precedence layer that treated `undefined` as a distinct value and applied null-object semantics in the spawn-environment builder would also fail (c) / (d). Together with T-API-61f / T-API-61g, this pins down explicit-`undefined` acceptance across both API surfaces and all five pinned fields. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*

##### Throwing Getters

- **T-API-62**: Throwing getter on `options` object itself — generator throws on first `next()`. `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { get() { throw new Error("boom"); } }); run("ralph", opts);` Generator is returned without throwing at call site; on first `next()`, throws. The error is surfaced as pre-iteration error, not escaping at call site. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62a**: Throwing getter on `options.signal` — generator throws on first `next()`. Signal is read first (Spec 9.1), so a throwing `signal` getter captures as a snapshot error. Generator is still returned normally. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62b**: Throwing getter on `options.cwd` — generator throws on first `next()`. The `cwd` getter is a pinned-priority option per Spec 9.3 "Pinned pre-iteration priority". *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62c**: Throwing getter on `options.envFile` — generator throws on first `next()`. Pinned-priority option. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62d**: Throwing getter on `options.maxIterations` — generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62e**: Throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env` — generator throws on first `next()`. `const env = { A: "a" }; Object.defineProperty(env, "B", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("boom"); } }); run("ralph", { env })`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62f**: Proxy `ownKeys` trap that throws while enumerating `options.env` — generator throws on first `next()`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62f2** (observational, not a direct conformance pin): Proxy `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` trap behavior while enumerating `options.env`. SPEC 9.5's enumeration of snapshot-time-captured throws explicitly names `Proxy` `ownKeys` traps, throwing enumerable getters, throwing `Proxy` `get` traps on included keys, and throwing `options.env` getters on the options object — but does **not** name the `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` trap. Whether the descriptor trap is invoked during the snapshot depends on enumeration strategy (`Object.keys` invokes it; a `Reflect.ownKeys` + `for ... in`-with-`hasOwnProperty` strategy can avoid it). SPEC explicitly leaves the enumerability-determination strategy implementation-defined ("The strategy used to determine enumerability may invoke descriptor-related proxy traps and remains otherwise implementation-defined"), so SPEC does not pin down a single conforming outcome for a throwing descriptor trap, and this test does not claim direct traceability to any SPEC clause. It is retained as observational coverage to characterize implementation behavior on the descriptor-trap axis without overclaiming a SPEC requirement that does not exist. Construct: `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { getOwnPropertyDescriptor() { throw new Error("descriptor-trap-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { env });` Drive `await gen.next()` and observe the outcome. The test passes if **exactly one** of the following holds: (a) the generator threw on first `next()` with the captured trap exception, the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error (not a spawn failure), no child was spawned, and no tmpdir was created — implementation invoked the descriptor trap during the snapshot, captured the throw, and surfaced it via the pre-iteration error path; or (b) the snapshot completed without surfacing the trap throw and the run reached the spawn step normally — implementation did not invoke the descriptor trap during enumeration. The test fails if some third outcome occurs (e.g., the descriptor exception escaped at the call site without being captured, or the run produced a corrupted env and a spawn failure with a misleading message). T-API-62f3 (throwing `get` trap) is the strict-conformance counterpart for the value-read axis: SPEC 9.5 normatively requires ordinary `[[Get]]` semantics for value reads, so a throwing `get` trap on an included key is a SPEC-pinned snapshot-error contract. T-API-62f remains the strict-conformance pin for the `ownKeys` trap. *(Observational; not directly traced to SPEC.)*
- **T-API-62f3**: Throwing `Proxy` `get` trap on an included `options.env` key is captured and surfaced as a pre-iteration option-snapshot error. SPEC 9.5 normatively specifies ordinary ECMAScript `[[Get]]` semantics for value reads ("After loopx has identified the included own enumerable string-keyed keys, it reads each included key's value using ordinary ECMAScript property `[[Get]]` semantics, equivalent to `env[key]`, exactly once during the call-time snapshot"), and SPEC 9.5's enumeration of snapshot-time-captured throws explicitly names "a throwing `Proxy` `get` trap on an included string key" alongside `ownKeys` / enumerable-getter / outer-`options.env`-getter throws. SPEC 9.1's option-snapshot prose mirrors the enumeration. Construct: `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a", B: "b" }, { ownKeys() { return ["A", "B"]; }, getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { return { enumerable: true, configurable: true, value: undefined, writable: true }; }, get(t, key) { throw new Error("get-trap-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { env });` Drive `await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/get-trap-boom/);` Assert: (a) the generator throws on first `next()` with the captured trap exception, (b) the error surfaces as an option-snapshot error (not a spawn failure), (c) no child is spawned (no marker file), and (d) no `LOOPX_TMPDIR` was created. A buggy implementation that used descriptor-based extraction (delegating value reads through `Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor` rather than ordinary `[[Get]]`) would not invoke the `get` trap and would silently reach the spawn step, failing (a). A buggy implementation that let the trap exception escape at the call site would fail SPEC 9.1's "never throws at the call site" rule, observable as a synchronous throw from `run()`. Together with T-API-62f (strict `ownKeys` trap pin) and T-API-62e / T-API-62h10 (entry-getter throws), this closes proxy-trap and entry-throw snapshot-error coverage across the SPEC 9.5-enumerated branches. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62g**: Throwing getter under `runPromise` — rejects (not synchronous throw). `runPromise("ralph", new Proxy({}, { get(_, k) { if (k === "env") throw new Error("boom"); return undefined; } }))`. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*

##### Option-Field Single-Read Contract

SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 specify: "Each option field is read at most once per call, and a throwing getter or proxy trap is not re-invoked to retry." Existing tests cover many individual throwing-getter / proxy-trap failure cases but do not assert the **single-read** contract itself — a buggy implementation could work for non-throwing getters by reading them multiple times without any test catching the violation.

- **T-API-62h**: `run()` reads each option field at most once per call. Set up counter-based getters on every pinned `RunOptions` field: `signal`, `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, `env`. Each getter increments its own counter and returns a **meaningful, used** value: `signal` returns a fresh `new AbortController().signal`, `cwd` returns a real project-root path, `envFile` returns the path to a valid `.env` file, `maxIterations` returns `2`, and `env` returns `{ MYVAR: "value" }` — each is a value the implementation will actually consume when building the spawn environment, loop bound, and so on. Call `run("ralph", opts, ...)` where `opts` has these five counter-based getters. Iterate the generator to completion. After the generator settles, assert each counter is **exactly 1** — no field was read twice during the option snapshot. The exact-one-read assertion is justified only because each getter returns a meaningful non-`undefined` value that the implementation must consume; SPEC 9.1 itself normatively says "each option field is read at most once per call." A buggy implementation that re-reads options across iterations or retries a getter on a caught exception would fail this assertion. An alternative phrasing that matches SPEC's "at most once" wording directly — "no supplied getter is read more than once" — is equally conformant and would pass under implementations that legitimately skip reading a field whose value would be unused; when it is known that every supplied value is consumed (as in this test's fixture), the exact-one-read assertion is the stronger equivalent. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h2**: `runPromise()` reads each option field at most once per call. Same counter-based getter fixture as T-API-62h (each getter returns a meaningful, used value per the T-API-62h rationale), but via `runPromise("ralph", opts)`. Await the promise to resolution. Assert each counter is exactly 1. This is the `runPromise` counterpart to T-API-62h; the same "at most once vs. exactly once" equivalence discussion in T-API-62h applies. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h3**: `run()` does not retry a throwing option-field getter. Per SPEC 9.1 "a throwing getter or proxy trap is not re-invoked to retry" — the single-read contract covers the success path (T-API-62h); this test pins down the throwing path. Set up an `opts` object whose `env` is a throwing counter-getter: `let count = 0; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { get() { count++; throw new Error("env-getter-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/env-getter-boom/);` Assert `count === 1` — loopx read the `env` getter once, captured the exception, and did **not** re-invoke the getter during the pre-iteration error surfacing, cleanup, or any other pathway. A buggy implementation that retried the getter in the error-reporting path (e.g., to build a fuller error message) or in a separate cleanup scan would increment the counter past 1. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h4**: `runPromise()` does not retry a throwing option-field getter. Same fixture as T-API-62h3, but via `runPromise("ralph", opts)`. `await expect(runPromise("ralph", opts)).rejects.toThrow(/env-getter-boom/);` Assert `count === 1`. This is the `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-62h3, proving the no-retry contract holds on both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h5**: `run()` does not retry a throwing `Proxy` `ownKeys` trap inside `options.env`. Per SPEC 9.1, throwing proxy traps are also covered by the no-retry contract. Build a proxy env: `let count = 0; const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { ownKeys() { count++; throw new Error("ownKeys-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { env });` Iterate and await the first `next()`. Assert: (a) the generator throws with an error referencing the trap failure, and (b) `count === 1`. Proxy-trap counterpart to T-API-62h3; exercises the proxy-enumeration branch of the snapshot codepath rather than the getter branch. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h6**: `runPromise()` does not retry a throwing `Proxy` `ownKeys` trap inside `options.env`. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-62h5 — proves the SPEC 9.2 "Identical to `run()` (section 9.1)" / no-retry contract holds on the proxy-enumeration branch under the promise surface, not just the getter branch (which is covered by T-API-62h4). Build the same proxy env: `let count = 0; const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { ownKeys() { count++; throw new Error("ownKeys-boom"); } });` Drive: `await expect(runPromise("ralph", { env })).rejects.toThrow(/ownKeys-boom/);` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with an error referencing the trap failure, and (b) `count === 1` — loopx invoked the proxy `ownKeys` trap once, captured the exception, and did not re-invoke it during the pre-iteration error surfacing, cleanup, or any other pathway. A buggy `runPromise()` implementation that retried the proxy trap during the rejection-construction or aggregate-cleanup paths would increment the counter past 1. Together with T-API-62h3 (`run()` getter no-retry), T-API-62h4 (`runPromise()` getter no-retry), and T-API-62h5 (`run()` proxy-trap no-retry), this completes the no-retry contract coverage across the {`run()` × `runPromise()`} × {throwing getter × throwing proxy `ownKeys` trap} matrix. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h9**: `run()` and `runPromise()` invoke the `Proxy` `get` trap on an included `options.env` key exactly once during the snapshot and do not retry on throw. SPEC 9.5 normatively specifies "`[[Get]]` semantics ... exactly once during the call-time snapshot" for included-key value reads, and SPEC 9.1's no-retry contract ("a throwing getter or proxy trap is not re-invoked to retry") applies to the throwing case. T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 cover the no-retry contract for the `ownKeys` trap; this test pins down the same contract for the `get` trap on the value-read axis now that SPEC 9.5 mandates `[[Get]]` semantics. **Parameterize over both run surfaces.** Construct: `let count = 0; const env = new Proxy({ A: "a", B: "b" }, { ownKeys() { return ["A", "B"]; }, getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { return { enumerable: true, configurable: true, value: undefined, writable: true }; }, get(t, key) { count++; throw new Error("get-trap-boom"); } });` Variant (a) `run()`: `const gen = run("ralph", { env }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/get-trap-boom/);` Variant (b) `runPromise()`: `await expect(runPromise("ralph", { env })).rejects.toThrow(/get-trap-boom/);` Assert per variant: (a) the generator throws / the promise rejects with an error referencing `get-trap-boom`, and (b) `count === 1` — the implementation invoked the `get` trap, observed the throw on the first included key, captured it, and did not re-invoke during error surfacing or cleanup. A buggy implementation that used descriptor-based extraction (`count === 0`) would fail SPEC 9.5's `[[Get]]`-semantics requirement and (a). A buggy implementation that retried the trap (`count > 1`) would fail SPEC 9.1's no-retry contract. Together with T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 (`ownKeys` no-retry) and T-API-62h10 / T-API-62h11 (entry-getter no-retry), this closes the no-retry contract across all SPEC 9.5-enumerated snapshot-throw paths on both run surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h10**: `run()` does not retry a throwing **own enumerable getter on an entry inside `options.env`**. Per SPEC 9.1 / 9.5, the snapshot reads `options.env`'s own enumerable string-keyed entries; an entry implemented as a throwing getter (via `Object.defineProperty`) is one of the snapshot-time-throw paths SPEC 9.5 explicitly enumerates ("a throwing enumerable getter"). T-API-62e covers the *surfacing* of that throw (the generator throws on first `next()`); T-API-62h3 / T-API-62h4 cover the no-retry counter for a throwing **`env` getter on the options object itself**; T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 / T-API-62h9 cover the no-retry counter for `Proxy` `ownKeys` / `get` traps inside `options.env`. The remaining branch — the no-retry counter for an own enumerable getter on a **named entry** inside a plain `env` object (the structurally distinct counterpart to T-API-62e's surfacing assertion) — is not pinned down. A buggy implementation that re-invoked the entry getter during error surfacing (e.g., to interpolate the throwing entry's name or value into a fuller error message), during cleanup scanning, or during a defensive "read every entry one more time" pass would increment the counter past 1 without any existing test catching it. **Test-construction note (per the section 1.1 getter / proxy-variant rule):** build the env object directly via `Object.defineProperty` on the same object passed as `options.env` — never via object spread, since spreading invokes the throwing getter in the test harness before `run()` is called. Construct: `let count = 0; const env = { A: "a" }; Object.defineProperty(env, "B", { enumerable: true, get() { count++; throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", { env }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/env-entry-getter-boom/);` Assert: (a) the generator throws on first `next()` with the captured exception, (b) the error is an option-snapshot error (not a spawn failure), (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created, and (e) `count === 1` — the entry getter fired once, threw, was captured, and was not re-invoked during error surfacing, cleanup, or any other pathway. The entry-getter no-retry counter is the structurally distinct counterpart to T-API-62h3 (env-on-options-object getter), T-API-62h5 (env-as-proxy-`ownKeys` trap), and T-API-62h9 (env-as-proxy-`get` trap); a bug that affected only the named-entry getter axis (e.g., a per-entry error-message-construction loop that re-read the entry) would slip past those three tests but be caught here. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h11**: `runPromise()` does not retry a throwing own enumerable getter on an entry inside `options.env`. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-62h10 — proves the SPEC 9.2 "Identical to `run()` (section 9.1)" / no-retry contract holds for the named-entry getter axis on the promise surface. **Test-construction note** matches T-API-62h10 (use `Object.defineProperty`, never object spread). Construct: `let count = 0; const env = { A: "a" }; Object.defineProperty(env, "B", { enumerable: true, get() { count++; throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } }); await expect(runPromise("ralph", { env })).rejects.toThrow(/env-entry-getter-boom/);` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with the captured exception, (b) the error is an option-snapshot error (not a spawn failure), (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created, and (e) `count === 1`. Together with T-API-62h10 (`run()` surface), T-API-62h3 / T-API-62h4 (env-on-options-object getter no-retry), T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 (env-as-proxy-`ownKeys` trap no-retry), and T-API-62h9 (env-as-proxy-`get` trap no-retry), this completes the no-retry counter coverage for the `options.env` snapshot path across all three structural variants — entry getter, proxy `ownKeys` trap, proxy `get` trap — on both run surfaces. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h7**: `run()` does not retry a throwing `options.signal` getter. Per SPEC 9.1 / 9.5, `signal` is read first in the option-snapshot ordering, and the no-retry contract ("a throwing getter or proxy trap is not re-invoked to retry") applies to every option field — not just `env`. T-API-62h3 / T-API-62h4 cover the no-retry contract for the `env` getter on both surfaces, T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 cover the proxy-trap branch, and T-API-62a covers the surfacing of a throwing `signal` getter, but the no-retry counter for the `signal`-getter case specifically is not pinned down. A buggy implementation that retried a throwing `signal` getter — for example to re-attempt listener registration after the first throw — would silently violate the SPEC contract. Construct: `let count = 0; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { enumerable: true, get() { count++; throw new Error("signal-getter-boom"); } }); const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/signal-getter-boom/);` Assert `count === 1`. This is the `signal`-getter counterpart to T-API-62h3 (env-getter); exercises the **first-read** field's no-retry contract specifically, since `signal` is read before any other field. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62h8**: `runPromise()` does not retry a throwing `options.signal` getter. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-62h7. Same fixture: `let count = 0; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { enumerable: true, get() { count++; throw new Error("signal-getter-boom"); } }); await expect(runPromise("ralph", opts)).rejects.toThrow(/signal-getter-boom/);` Assert `count === 1`. Together with T-API-62h7 (run surface), this pins down no-retry for the `signal` getter across both run surfaces, completing the matrix established by T-API-62h3 / T-API-62h4 (env getter) and T-API-62h5 / T-API-62h6 (env proxy trap). *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62i**: `run()` reads `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, and `env` getters synchronously at the call site, before returning the generator. SPEC 9.1 "Option-snapshot timing" says: "`run()` reads its `options` argument at the call site as a synchronous snapshot ... Each option field is read at most once per call." T-API-64k pins down call-site timing for `signal` (via duck-typed `addEventListener` counter); T-API-62h pins down the at-most-once contract across all fields by counting reads after settlement. Neither test directly proves that the **non-signal** getters fire synchronously at the call site rather than lazily on first `next()` — a buggy implementation that deferred all non-signal reads to first `next()` would still pass T-API-62h (counters end at 1) and T-API-64k (signal-only). Construct an `opts` object with **separate counter-based getters** on each non-signal field, each returning a value the implementation will actually consume (so the call-site read is load-bearing — a getter whose return value is `undefined` could be skipped by an implementation that short-circuits on absent values, weakening the "must have been read at call site" claim): `let cwdCount = 0, envFileCount = 0, maxIterCount = 0, envCount = 0; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "cwd", { enumerable: true, get() { cwdCount++; return projectDir; /* a real project directory */ } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "envFile", { enumerable: true, get() { envFileCount++; return validEnvFilePath; /* a valid .env file path */ } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "maxIterations", { enumerable: true, get() { maxIterCount++; return 1; } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { envCount++; return { MYVAR: "value" }; } });` Each getter returns a meaningful value the implementation must consume to build the spawn environment, resolve cwd, locate the env file, and bound the loop. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts);` and **immediately — without driving `gen.next()`** — assert all four counters are exactly `1`. After the assertion, drive `gen.next()` to drain. Assert: (a) `cwdCount === 1`, `envFileCount === 1`, `maxIterCount === 1`, `envCount === 1` immediately after `run()` returns (synchronous call-site reads of every non-signal field, mirroring the pattern T-API-64k uses for `signal`), (b) after the generator settles, the counters remain at `1` each (no re-reads on iteration; complements T-API-62h's at-most-once assertion at the post-settlement observation point with a stronger pre-`next()` observation point). The exact ordering between non-signal getters is implementation-defined per SPEC 9.1 and is intentionally not asserted; the contract under test is that all four fire **before** `run()` returns. A buggy implementation that deferred non-signal option reads to first `next()` would observe all four counters at `0` immediately after `run()` returned and fail this test. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62i2**: `runPromise()` reads `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, and `env` getters synchronously at the call site, before returning the promise. SPEC 9.2 "Option-snapshot timing" says: "Identical to `run()` (section 9.1): each option field read at most once, `options.signal` first, throwing getters / traps captured rather than escaping at the call site." T-API-64k2 pins down call-site timing for `signal` on the `runPromise()` surface; this test extends that coverage to the non-signal fields. Use the same counter-based getter fixture as T-API-62i — each getter returns a meaningful value the implementation will consume (`cwd: projectDir`, `envFile: validEnvFilePath`, `maxIterations: 1`, `env: { MYVAR: "value" }`), so the "must have been read at call site" claim is load-bearing rather than satisfied by short-circuiting on `undefined`. Drive: `const p = runPromise("ralph", opts);` and **immediately — without awaiting `p`** — assert all four counters are exactly `1`. After the assertion, `await p` to let the run settle. Assert: (a) all four counters are `1` synchronously after `runPromise()` returns (synchronous call-site reads on the `runPromise()` surface, mirroring T-API-64k2 for `signal`), (b) after the promise settles, the counters remain at `1` each. Together with T-API-62i, this pins down the SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 call-site option-snapshot rule for all non-signal fields across both run surfaces. A buggy `runPromise()` implementation that deferred non-signal option reads to its internal pre-iteration sequence (run after promise creation) would observe all four counters at `0` immediately after `runPromise()` returned and fail this test. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62i3**: `run()` — a throwing non-signal option-field getter is invoked exactly once **at the call site** without escaping synchronously, and the captured error is surfaced later via the first `next()`. SPEC 9.1 says: "Any exception raised during the snapshot — a throwing option-field getter, ... — is captured and surfaced via the standard pre-iteration error path on the first `next()`, not at the call site." Existing T-API-62 / T-API-62a–62g cover the "surfaces on first `next()`" behavior, and T-API-62h3 covers the no-retry contract via post-`next()` count. Neither directly proves the **call-site invocation timing** for a throwing non-signal getter — a buggy implementation that deferred the read to first `next()` would still pass T-API-62 (error surfaces) and T-API-62h3 (count is 1 after `next()`). **Parameterize over each non-signal field**: variant (a) `cwd`, variant (b) `envFile`, variant (c) `maxIterations`, variant (d) `env`. For each variant, construct a counter-based throwing getter on the named field and a non-throwing counter on a sibling field for control: `let throwCount = 0; let siblingCount = 0; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "<field>", { enumerable: true, get() { throwCount++; throw new Error("<field>-getter-boom"); } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "maxIterations", { enumerable: true, get() { siblingCount++; return 1; } });` (skip the sibling getter when `<field>` is `maxIterations`; use `cwd` as the sibling instead). Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts);` and **immediately — without driving `gen.next()`** — assert (a) `run()` did **not** throw synchronously at the call site (the call returned a generator, matching SPEC 9.1 "never throws at the call site"), and (b) `throwCount === 1` (the throwing getter was invoked once at call time, captured rather than escaping). Then drive `await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/<field>-getter-boom/);` Assert: (c) the captured exception surfaces on first `next()`, and (d) `throwCount === 1` post-`next()` — the captured error was not re-derived by re-invoking the getter (no-retry contract from T-API-62h3 reaffirmed at the call-site-observed timing). The "captured exactly once at call site" combined assertion (b) + (d) is the new surface this test pins down beyond the existing coverage. A buggy implementation that deferred the read to first `next()` would observe `throwCount === 0` immediately after `run()` returned and fail (b). An implementation that re-invoked the getter during error surfacing would fail (d). The order in which non-signal fields are read is implementation-defined per SPEC 9.1, so when the throwing field is read first the sibling counter may end at `0` (sibling read was short-circuited by the early throw) and when the throwing field is read after the sibling, the sibling counter ends at `1`. Both outcomes are conforming; the test does not constrain the sibling counter beyond ensuring the throwing-field counter is exactly `1`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-62i4**: `runPromise()` — a throwing non-signal option-field getter is invoked exactly once **at the call site** without escaping synchronously, and the captured error is surfaced later via promise rejection. `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-62i3. SPEC 9.2 "Option-snapshot timing. Identical to `run()` (section 9.1)" — the same call-site invocation contract holds, and the captured error surfaces via promise rejection rather than first-`next()` throw. **Parameterize over each non-signal field** (same variants as T-API-62i3: `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, `env`). Use the same throwing-getter + sibling-counter fixture as T-API-62i3 for each variant. Drive: `const p = runPromise("ralph", opts);` and **immediately — without awaiting `p`** — assert (a) `runPromise()` did **not** throw synchronously at the call site (the call returned a promise, matching SPEC 9.2 "the call itself always returns a promise"), and (b) `throwCount === 1` (the throwing getter was invoked once at call time, captured rather than escaping). Then drive `await expect(p).rejects.toThrow(/<field>-getter-boom/);` Assert: (c) the captured exception surfaces as the promise rejection, and (d) `throwCount === 1` post-rejection — no retry. Same sibling-counter caveat as T-API-62i3 applies (implementation-defined order between non-signal fields means the sibling may or may not have been read before the throw). Together with T-API-62i3, this completes call-site throwing-getter timing coverage for non-signal fields across both run surfaces. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*

##### Generator-Returns-Without-Throwing Invariants

- **T-API-63**: `run()` returns a generator without throwing, even under every invalid-options scenario. Parameterize over {invalid-target, invalid-options-shape, throwing-option-getter, already-aborted-signal, invalid-cwd, invalid-envFile, invalid-maxIterations, invalid-env}. For each input, assert that `run(...)` returns a non-null object with `.next()`, `.return()`, `.throw()` methods without raising at the call site. The error is surfaced on first `next()`. This is Spec 9.1's "run() still returns a generator without throwing" invariant — critical because any call-site throw would break consumer composition patterns. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-63a**: `runPromise()` returns a promise (not a thrown error) for every invalid input. Parameterize same cases as T-API-63. Assert `runPromise(...)` returns a `Promise` instance (check with `typeof p.then === "function"`) that then rejects. Spec 9.2 — call never throws; failure surfaces as rejection. *(Spec 9.2)*

##### Signal Semantics — Duck-Typed / Reentrant

- **T-API-64**: Invalid `options.signal` shape is an option error, not an abort. `run("ralph", { signal: "not-a-signal" as any })` returns a generator; on first `next()`, throws an option-validation error (not an abort error). The error message references the invalid signal shape. Spec 9.3 — an invalid `options.signal` "remains an ordinary option-snapshot error". *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64a**: Duck-typed signal compatibility. A non-`AbortSignal` object that exposes `aborted: boolean` and `addEventListener("abort", fn)` is accepted as a signal. Create a duck-typed signal `{ aborted: false, addEventListener(type, fn) { if (type === "abort") this._listener = fn; }, _fire() { this.aborted = true; this._listener?.(); } }`. Call `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, ... })`, then `duck._fire()`. Assert the promise rejects with the abort error. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-64b**: Reentrant `addEventListener` — duck signal's `addEventListener` synchronously invokes the listener during registration. loopx treats the signal as aborted. Duck signal constructor: `addEventListener(type, fn) { fn(); }`. `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an abort error on first observation — no child is spawned. Per Spec 9.5, "if `addEventListener` synchronously invokes the registered listener during registration ... loopx treats the signal as aborted". *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-64c**: Duck-typed signal with `aborted: true` at capture time — loopx treats as aborted, no child spawned. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-64d**: Real pre-aborted `AbortSignal`. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with abort error, no child was spawned. Real `AbortSignal` instances passed already-aborted must always be observed as aborted. *(Spec 9.5)*
- **T-API-64e**: Signal getter is read first. `const opts = {}; let order = []; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { get() { order.push("signal"); return undefined; } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { get() { order.push("env"); return undefined; } }); run("ralph", opts);` Await `.next()`. Assert `order[0] === "signal"`. Per Spec 9.1, signal is read before other fields. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-64f**: Duck-typed signal with a throwing `addEventListener` surfaces as an option-snapshot error, not a call-site throw. Per SPEC 9.5 the `addEventListener` method must be "callable and returns without throwing when loopx invokes it". Construct a duck signal whose `addEventListener` throws synchronously: `const duck = { aborted: false, addEventListener() { throw new Error("listener-register-failed"); } };` Call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous throw at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal (or wrapping the listener-register exception) — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error and not a runtime spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. This is the throwing-`addEventListener` counterpart to T-API-64b (reentrant `addEventListener` that fires the listener — treated as aborted) and T-API-64 (non-signal shape — option error). A throwing register does not qualify as aborted (the listener did not actually fire `true` on `aborted`) and does not qualify as a usable signal (the contract is violated), so the correct surface is the option-snapshot error path. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64g**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64f — throwing `addEventListener` on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same setup as T-API-64f. `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error (not an abort error, not a spawn-failure error). *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-64h**: Duck-typed signal whose `aborted` getter throws surfaces as an option-snapshot error, not a call-site throw and not an abort. Per SPEC 9.5 the contract requires "a readable `aborted` property" — a getter that throws is not "readable" in the contract sense. Construct `const duck = { addEventListener() {}, get aborted() { throw new Error("aborted-getter-failed"); } };` Call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous throw at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal (or wrapping the getter exception) — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error and not a runtime spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. This is the throwing-`aborted`-getter counterpart to T-API-64f (throwing `addEventListener`); both pin down that contract violations during the option-snapshot read surface as option-snapshot errors, not as call-site throws and not as aborts. A throwing `aborted` getter does not qualify as "the signal is aborted" (the implementation cannot read the value `true`) and does not qualify as a usable signal (the contract is violated), so the correct surface is the option-snapshot error path, mirroring T-API-64f's reasoning. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64h2**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64h — throwing `aborted` getter on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same `aborted`-throws duck signal as T-API-64h. `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error (not an abort error, not a spawn-failure error). *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-64i**: Duck-typed signal with a non-callable `addEventListener` (e.g., a plain string or number stored on the property name) surfaces as an option-snapshot error. Per SPEC 9.5 the `addEventListener` method must be "callable and returns without throwing when loopx invokes it." A non-callable value violates the "callable" half of the contract. Construct `const duck = { aborted: false, addEventListener: "not-a-function" };` Call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous TypeError at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error and not a runtime spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. This is the non-callable-`addEventListener` counterpart to T-API-64f (throws on call) and T-API-64h (throwing `aborted` getter); together the three pin down the three distinct ways a duck-typed signal's `addEventListener` / `aborted` contract can fail (throws on call, throws on read, not callable / not present). The option-snapshot read attempts to invoke `addEventListener`, observes that the property value is not a function, and surfaces the option-shape error rather than calling it (which would otherwise raise a runtime TypeError). *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64i2**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64i — non-callable `addEventListener` on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same non-callable-`addEventListener` duck signal as T-API-64i. `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-64i3**: Duck-typed signal whose `addEventListener` property is **missing entirely** (the property does not exist on the object) surfaces as an option-snapshot error. Per SPEC 9.5 the `AbortSignal`-compatibility contract requires "a readable `aborted` property" **and** "an `addEventListener('abort', listener)` method" — both halves must hold. T-API-64i covers the **non-callable-but-present** sub-case (the property exists with a non-function value like a string or number); this test pins down the **missing-property** sub-case (the property does not exist on the object at all), which exercises a slightly different code path in any implementation that distinguishes `prop in obj` / `obj.prop !== undefined` / `typeof obj.prop === "function"` lookups. A buggy implementation that checked only `typeof obj.addEventListener === "function"` for non-callable rejection but special-cased a missing property as "not present, so don't validate" — silently skipping listener registration and letting the run proceed without a usable abort signal — would pass T-API-64i but fail this test. Construct a duck signal with **only** an `aborted` property: `const duck = { aborted: false };` (no own `addEventListener` property; reading `duck.addEventListener` returns `undefined`). Call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous TypeError at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error and not a runtime spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. This is the missing-`addEventListener` counterpart to T-API-64i (non-callable but present) and T-API-64f (throws on call); together they cover the three distinct ways the `addEventListener` half of the contract can fail (throws on call, not callable, not present at all). *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64i4**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64i3 — missing `addEventListener` property on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same `{ aborted: false }` duck signal as T-API-64i3 (no own `addEventListener` property). `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error (not an abort error, not a spawn-failure error). Together with T-API-64f / T-API-64g (throws on call), T-API-64h / T-API-64h2 (throwing `aborted` getter), T-API-64i / T-API-64i2 (non-callable `addEventListener`), and T-API-64j / T-API-64j2 (missing/non-boolean `aborted`), this completes the duck-typed-signal contract-violation coverage across both run surfaces and across all four contract-violation modes (`addEventListener` throws / not callable / not present; `aborted` throws / missing / non-boolean). *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-64j**: Duck-typed signal whose `aborted` property is missing or non-readable / non-boolean is not AbortSignal-compatible and surfaces as an option-snapshot error. Per SPEC 9.5 the contract requires both "a readable `aborted` property" and "an `addEventListener('abort', listener)` method" — both halves must hold, and a missing or non-readable / non-boolean `aborted` value means the object is not AbortSignal-compatible. **Parameterize over each missing/non-boolean variant**: variant (a) **missing own `aborted` property** — `const duck = { addEventListener() {} };` (reading `duck.aborted` returns `undefined`); variant (b) **non-boolean `aborted: undefined` explicitly set** — `const duck = { aborted: undefined as any, addEventListener() {} };`; variant (c) **non-boolean `aborted: "false"` (string)** — `const duck = { aborted: "false" as any, addEventListener() {} };`; variant (d) **non-boolean `aborted: 0` (number)** — `const duck = { aborted: 0 as any, addEventListener() {} };`. For each variant, call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous throw at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error (in particular, `aborted === undefined` must not be coerced to "aborted: false" and accepted, because a missing or non-boolean `aborted` is a contract violation distinct from `aborted: false`), and not a spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. This is the missing/non-boolean-`aborted` counterpart to T-API-64f / T-API-64h / T-API-64i; together they cover the contract-violation modes on a duck-typed signal: throwing `addEventListener` (T-API-64f), throwing `aborted` getter (T-API-64h), non-callable `addEventListener` (T-API-64i), and missing-or-non-boolean `aborted` (this test). A buggy implementation that defaulted a missing `aborted` to `false` (or coerced a string/number `aborted` to truthy/falsy) would silently treat the duck signal as a usable signal and proceed, breaking the contract. *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64j2**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64j — missing or non-boolean `aborted` on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same parameterized variant set (a)–(d) as T-API-64j. For each variant, call `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error. Together with the parallel runPromise tests T-API-64g / T-API-64h2 / T-API-64i2, this completes runPromise parity for the contract-violation modes. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-64m**: Duck-typed signal whose `addEventListener` **getter** throws on property read surfaces as an option-snapshot error, not a call-site throw and not an abort. Per SPEC 9.5 the `addEventListener` "method must be callable and returns without throwing when loopx invokes it" — but the contract is also violated when the property *read itself* throws, before loopx can ever attempt to invoke the result. T-API-64f covers `addEventListener` that is a **function that throws when called** (the property read returns a function, the call throws); this test covers the structurally different case where the property's **getter** throws on read (loopx never observes any value for `addEventListener` because the read itself raises). The two cases share the same observable contract (option-snapshot error on the first `next()`), but a buggy implementation could plausibly handle one and not the other — for example, an implementation that wrapped the call site (`duck.addEventListener("abort", listener)`) in a try/catch but read the property first as a separate uncaught expression (`const fn = duck.addEventListener; try { fn(...); } catch { ... }`) would let the getter exception escape before the snapshot-capture try/catch had a chance to fire. Construct a duck signal whose `addEventListener` is exposed via a throwing getter: `const duck: any = { aborted: false }; Object.defineProperty(duck, "addEventListener", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("ae-getter-boom"); } });` Call `run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert: (a) `run()` returns a generator (not a synchronous throw at the call site), (b) on the first `next()`, the generator throws an option-snapshot error referencing the invalid signal (or wrapping the `ae-getter-boom` exception) — the error must be an **option-shape / signal-validation** error, not an abort error and not a runtime spawn-failure error, (c) no child is spawned, (d) no tmpdir is created. **Test-construction note.** The duck signal must use `Object.defineProperty` with a throwing getter, **not** an object literal `{ get addEventListener() { throw ... } }` passed to `run()` directly through any spread / harness path that reads `addEventListener` during construction — because the test harness or the JS engine could otherwise invoke the getter before `run()` is called and surface the exception at the test call site rather than letting loopx capture it via the SPEC 9.5 snapshot path. The `Object.defineProperty` form on a fresh object guards against that hazard. This test is the throwing-`addEventListener`-getter counterpart to T-API-64h (throwing `aborted` getter), T-API-64f (function that throws when called), T-API-64i (non-callable property), and T-API-64i3 (missing property); together they cover the full enumeration of contract-violation modes for the `addEventListener` half — throws-on-call (T-API-64f), throws-on-read (this test), not callable (T-API-64i), not present (T-API-64i3). *(Spec 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-64m2**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64m — throwing `addEventListener` getter on duck signal rejects with an option-snapshot error. Same throwing-`addEventListener`-getter duck signal as T-API-64m. `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert the promise rejects with an option-shape / signal-validation error (not an abort error, not a spawn-failure error). Together with T-API-64g (function that throws when called), T-API-64h2 (throwing `aborted` getter), T-API-64i2 (non-callable `addEventListener`), and T-API-64i4 (missing `addEventListener`), this completes runPromise parity for the throwing-`addEventListener`-getter contract-violation mode and rounds out the symmetric coverage of all five `addEventListener`-side and `aborted`-side contract-violation modes across both run surfaces. *(Spec 9.5, 9.2)*

##### Call-Site Capture Timing

SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 / 9.5 specify that `options.signal` is **read at the call site as a synchronous snapshot** — both run surfaces capture the signal before returning. Existing tests prove the *effects* of various option fields after iteration starts (option-snapshot errors surface lazily on first `next()`, abort errors are throwable, etc.), but they do not directly prove that loopx **registered an abort listener at call time** for a duck-typed signal — distinct from "loopx noticed the signal during pre-iteration". A buggy implementation that deferred all option reads (including `signal.addEventListener`) until first `next()` would still pass the existing abort-during-iteration tests (the listener registration would happen lazily but in time to observe a same-tick abort), but would break the SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 call-site snapshot contract — observable by callers who supply a duck signal whose `addEventListener` increments a counter.

- **T-API-64k**: `run()` registers the `signal.addEventListener('abort', …)` at the call site, not lazily on first `next()`. Construct a duck-typed signal whose `addEventListener` increments an external counter: `let count = 0; const duck = { aborted: false, addEventListener(type, fn) { if (type === "abort") { count++; this._listener = fn; } } };` Call `const gen = run("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 });` and **immediately** — without driving `gen.next()` — assert `count === 1`. The increment must be observable synchronously after `run()` returns, before any consumer interaction with the generator. After the assertion, drive `gen.next()` to drain (clean up resources / let the generator settle). The post-call assertion is the SPEC 9.1 "synchronous snapshot at the call site" surface — a buggy implementation that registered the listener inside the first-`next()` pre-iteration sequence would observe `count === 0` immediately after `run()` returned and fail this test. The duck-typed-signal pathway (rather than a real `AbortSignal`) is required because real `AbortSignal.addEventListener` is not directly observable as a counter without monkeypatching the prototype; the duck signal is the SPEC-blessed compatibility shape (SPEC 9.5 "Duck-typed signal compatibility") and gives a clean observation surface. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-64k2**: `runPromise()` registers the `signal.addEventListener('abort', …)` at the call site, before `runPromise()` returns. Same duck-typed counter-incrementing signal as T-API-64k. Call `const p = runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, maxIterations: 1 });` and **immediately** — without awaiting `p` — assert `count === 1`.
