The increment must be observable synchronously after `runPromise()` returns, before any iteration starts. After the assertion, `await p` to let the run settle (so the test does not leak resources). This is the `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-64k; together they pin down the SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 call-site snapshot contract for `signal` on both run surfaces. SPEC 9.2 specifies that `runPromise()` snapshot timing is **eager** ("captured synchronously at the `runPromise()` call site"), so the same observation applies. *(Spec 9.2, 9.5)*
- **T-API-64k3**: `options.signal` is **read** before other option fields. SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 say "`options.signal` is read **before** other `RunOptions` fields, so an already-aborted signal is captured before any other option-field read can produce a snapshot exception." The SPEC ordering rule is on the **field read**, not on full listener-registration completion — pinning the test to listener-registration ordering would over-pin behavior beyond SPEC. **Parameterize the test over both run surfaces — `run()` and `runPromise()`** so each is exercised independently (SPEC 9.1 and SPEC 9.2 both carry the same ordering rule, but the two surfaces share no code path the test can rely on by transitivity, and a buggy implementation could honor the ordering on one surface but not the other). For each surface, construct an `opts` object whose **`signal` is exposed via an enumerable getter** that pushes `"signal"` to an `order` array on read and returns a minimal duck signal, and whose **`env` is exposed via an enumerable getter** that pushes `"env"` to the same `order` array on read: `let order = []; const duckSignal = { aborted: false, addEventListener() {} }; const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { enumerable: true, get() { order.push("signal"); return duckSignal; } }); Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { order.push("env"); return undefined; } });` **Variant (a) `run()`**: Call `run("ralph", opts);` and **immediately** — without driving `gen.next()` — assert `order[0] === "signal"` (proving the `signal` getter ran before the `env` getter, regardless of whether `addEventListener` was invoked yet). After the assertion, drive the generator to drain (so the test does not leak resources). **Variant (b) `runPromise()`**: Call `runPromise("ralph", opts);` and **immediately** — without awaiting the promise — assert `order[0] === "signal"`. After the assertion, await the promise to let the run settle. For both variants, do **not** assert that `duckSignal.addEventListener` runs before the `env` getter — SPEC 9.1 / 9.2 require `signal` to be read first but do not require listener registration to complete before subsequent option-field reads. This is the ordering counterpart to T-API-64k / T-API-64k2 — they prove `signal` is read at call time on each surface; this proves it is read **first** on each surface. T-API-64e covers a related ordering observation via throwing-getter sequencing; this test pins the rule down at the option-field-read surface on both surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 9.2, 9.5)*

##### Abort Precedence

- **T-API-65**: Abort beats missing env-file. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, envFile: "nonexistent.env", maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert promise rejects with abort error (not env-file-missing error). Per Spec 9.3 "Abort precedence over pre-iteration failures", once a usable signal is captured and aborts before the first child spawn, it displaces all other pre-iteration failure modes. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65a**: Abort beats invalid target. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise(":bad", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert abort error, not invalid-target error. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65a2**: Abort beats **non-string target arguments**. SPEC 9.3's abort-precedence rule displaces "target argument / target syntax validation" — the SPEC enumeration covers both target-syntax errors (invalid target strings like `":bad"` covered by T-API-65a) and target-argument errors (non-string `target` per SPEC 9.1: "runtime-invalid `target` values (e.g., `undefined`, `null`, `42`, or any non-string) are rejected lazily"). T-API-65a covers the syntax branch; the **target-argument** branch (non-string) is uncovered. A buggy implementation that routed target-syntax validation through the abort-precedence pathway but rejected non-string targets via a separate eager type-check that escaped the precedence rule would pass T-API-65a yet fail this test. **Parameterize over both surfaces and multiple non-string target values.** Variants: **(i) `target = undefined`**, **(ii) `target = null`**, **(iii) `target = 42`**. For each variant, run two surface variants:
    - **(promise)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const p = runPromise(<variant> as any, { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with the **abort** error, **not** the invalid-target / non-string-target error, (b) no child was spawned (no marker file), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created (assertion scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 preface).
    - **(generator)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run(<variant> as any, { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);` Same three assertions.
  
  Together with T-API-65a / T-API-65l (target-syntax branch on both surfaces), this closes both target-argument-validation sub-branches under SPEC 9.3's "target argument / target syntax validation" displacement. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 9.2)*
- **T-API-65b**: Abort beats discovery failure (missing `.loopx/`). *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65c**: Abort beats env-file-load failure (unreadable local env file, mode 000). Create `unreadable.env` at mode 000, pre-abort the signal via `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, envFile: "unreadable.env", maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert promise rejects with abort error, not env-file-unreadable error. Per SPEC 8.1 / 8.2, malformed `.env` lines are parser warnings (not fatal), so this test uses an **unreadable** env file — which is a real pre-iteration failure path per SPEC 8.2 / 9.3. T-API-65 covers missing env file (also SPEC 8.2 fatal); this test covers the unreadable-file variant of the same fatal path. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 8.2)*
- **T-API-65d**: Abort beats tmpdir-creation failure. Simulate tmpdir-creation failure by setting `TMPDIR` to a path whose parent is read-only. Pre-abort the signal. Assert abort error, not tmpdir-creation error. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 7.4)*
- **T-API-65e**: Invalid `options.signal` shape does not enter the abort pathway. `run("ralph", { signal: "not-a-signal" as any, envFile: "nonexistent.env" })`. Per Spec 9.3 "An invalid `options` value or non-`AbortSignal`-compatible `options.signal` captures no signal and does not enter this pathway". Assert the first-`next()` error references the invalid signal shape (or the missing env file — implementation-defined ordering between those two), **not** an abort error. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65v**: `runPromise()` — invalid `options` **wrapper** does not enter the abort pathway, even when the wrapper carries an aborted `AbortSignal`. SPEC 9.3 enumerates two distinct "no usable signal" carve-outs from abort precedence: (i) "An invalid `options` value … captures no signal and does not enter this pathway", and (ii) "non-`AbortSignal`-compatible `options.signal` captures no signal and does not enter this pathway." T-API-65e covers (ii) — the invalid-`signal`-shape branch. The (i) branch — invalid `options` **wrapper** — is currently uncovered at the abort-precedence intersection. Without this case, a buggy implementation could route the invalid-options-wrapper failure through the abort pathway when an aborted signal happens to be attached to the wrapper, even though SPEC 9.3 explicitly forbids it: the invalid options value "captures no signal" before the signal is ever inspected for usability. **Construction.** Build the invalid-options wrapper as an array (one of the SPEC 9.5 "non-conforming values" — arrays are explicitly excluded by the "non-array" clause), then attach an aborted signal as an own property: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const opts: any = []; opts.signal = c.signal;` then call `runPromise("ralph", opts)`. Assert: (a) the promise rejects with an **invalid-options error** (matching the same error class as T-API-61e — `runPromise()` with an array as `options`) — **not** an abort error, (b) no child was spawned (no marker file), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created (assertion is scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 preface). A buggy implementation that read `opts.signal` before validating the wrapper shape, observed the aborted state, and routed through abort precedence would fail (a). *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.2)*
- **T-API-65w**: `run()` — invalid `options` **wrapper** does not enter the abort pathway, even when the wrapper carries an aborted `AbortSignal`. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-65v. Same construction (`const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const opts: any = []; opts.signal = c.signal;`), then `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(<invalid-options error>);`. Assert: (a) the first `next()` throws an **invalid-options error** (matching the same error class as T-API-61a — `run()` with an array as `options`) — **not** an abort error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created. Together with T-API-65v (`runPromise()`), this closes the SPEC 9.3 "invalid `options` value … captures no signal and does not enter this pathway" carve-out across both API surfaces, complementing T-API-65e (invalid `options.signal` shape, the second SPEC 9.3 carve-out). *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-65h**: Abort beats target-resolution failure — missing workflow. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (valid), target a workflow that does not exist. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("nonexistent-workflow", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert promise rejects with **abort** error, not missing-workflow error. Per SPEC 9.3 "Abort precedence over pre-iteration failures", target resolution is one of the enumerated pre-iteration failure modes displaced by a pre-aborted signal. T-API-65 covers env-file failure; T-API-65b covers `.loopx/` discovery failure; this test closes the missing-workflow branch of target resolution — a distinct failure category per SPEC 7.1 step 3 that existing abort-precedence tests do not exercise directly. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 7.1)*
- **T-API-65i**: Abort beats target-resolution failure — missing script in existing workflow. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (valid, `index` present) but no `check` script. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("ralph:check", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert promise rejects with **abort** error, not missing-script error. This is the missing-script counterpart to T-API-65h (missing workflow) — SPEC 7.1 step 3 enumerates missing workflow and missing script as distinct target-resolution sub-paths, and SPEC 9.3's abort-precedence rule must cover both. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 7.1, 4.1)*
- **T-API-65j**: Abort beats target-resolution failure — missing default entry point (workflow has scripts but no `index`). Set up `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` only (no `index.*`). `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`. Assert promise rejects with **abort** error, not missing-default-entry-point error. This is the missing-default-entry-point counterpart to T-API-65h (missing workflow) and T-API-65i (missing script under qualified target) — the bare target `"ralph"` resolves to `ralph:index`, which fails with a distinct error path when no `index.*` exists. SPEC 7.1 step 3 enumerates this as the third sub-path of target resolution, and SPEC 9.3's abort-precedence rule covers the whole step. Together T-API-65h/65i/65j pin down abort-precedence across all three target-resolution sub-paths. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 7.1, 4.1, 2.1)*
- **T-API-65k**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats missing env-file failure. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, envFile: "nonexistent.env", maxIterations: 1 });` Assert the first `next()` throws the abort error, not the env-file error. Also assert no child was spawned and no tmpdir was created. This is the generator counterpart to T-API-65. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-65l**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats invalid target. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run(":bad", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert the first `next()` throws the abort error, not the invalid-target error. This is the generator counterpart to T-API-65a. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-65m**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats missing `.loopx/` discovery failure. Create a fixture with no `.loopx/` directory, call `run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 })`, and assert the first `next()` throws the abort error, not the discovery error. This is the generator counterpart to T-API-65b. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1)*
- **T-API-65n**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats tmpdir-creation failure. Configure an unwritable tmpdir parent (as in T-TMP-12b), pass a pre-aborted signal, and assert the first `next()` throws the abort error, not the tmpdir-creation error. Also assert no child was spawned and no tmpdir was created. This is the generator counterpart to T-API-65d. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-65o**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats target-resolution failures. Parameterize over (a) missing workflow, (b) missing script in an existing workflow, and (c) bare workflow target with no `index` script. For each fixture, assert the first `next()` throws the abort error, not the target-resolution error. This is the generator counterpart to T-API-65h / 65i / 65j. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-65r**: Pre-aborted signal beats **unreadable global env file** failure on both surfaces. SPEC 9.3's abort-precedence rule explicitly displaces "env-file loading" failures, and SPEC 7.1 step 2 covers both local `-e` and global env files in that loading step. T-API-65 / T-API-65k cover missing **local** env file, T-API-65c covers unreadable **local** env file (mode 000), but the **unreadable global env file** sub-path (the same sub-path as T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable on the no-abort surface, and as T-SIG-30 on the CLI signal-wins surface) is uncovered on the programmatic abort-precedence surface. Without this case, an implementation could bypass the abort-precedence pathway for the global-env failure mode specifically while still passing the local-env tests. Set up: an isolated `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` with `loopx/env` written and `chmod 000`'d (the same fixture pattern as T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable; injected into the loopx process's inherited environment, not via `RunOptions.env`, since SPEC 8.1 reads `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` from the inherited environment). A valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Run two surface variants:
    - **(r-promise)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const p = runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with the **abort** error, **not** the global-env-file-read error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created (assertion scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 preface).
    - **(r-generator)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);` Same three assertions as the promise variant, but on the generator surface.

  Together with T-SIG-30 (CLI signal-wins over unreadable global env file) and T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable (no-abort surface), this closes the global-env-unreadable failure mode across all three precedence-relevant surfaces (CLI signal-wins, programmatic abort-precedence, no-abort tmpdir-non-creation). **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** (root can read mode-000 files). *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 8.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-65s**: Pre-aborted signal beats **discovery validation failure** (sibling validation) on both surfaces. SPEC 9.3 says abort-precedence displaces `.loopx/` discovery failures; T-API-65b / T-API-65m cover the **missing-`.loopx/`** sub-path (existence check), but the **sibling-validation** sub-path (SPEC 5.4 fatal validation in run mode — e.g., a sibling workflow with a same-base-name collision or an invalid workflow/script name) is the second SPEC 5.1 / 5.4 discovery-failure path and has its own programmatic-surface coverage at T-TMP-12-programmatic-discovery-validation (no-abort) and T-SIG-25 (CLI signal-wins). The programmatic abort-precedence surface is uncovered. Set up: a valid target workflow `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` alongside a sibling with a fatal validation error (the same fixture used by T-TMP-12-programmatic-discovery-validation — e.g., `.loopx/broken/check.sh` + `.loopx/broken/check.ts` name collision, or `.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh` invalid workflow name). Run two surface variants:
    - **(s-promise)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const p = runPromise("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 });` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with the **abort** error, **not** the sibling-validation error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created.
    - **(s-generator)**: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);` Same three assertions as the promise variant.

  Together with T-API-65b / T-API-65m (missing-`.loopx/` sub-path, abort-precedence) this closes both SPEC 5.1 / 5.4 discovery-failure sub-paths on the programmatic abort-precedence surface, completing the abort-precedence × discovery × surface matrix that mirrors the CLI signal-wins coverage at T-SIG-24 (missing) and T-SIG-25 (sibling-validation). *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 5.1, 5.4)*
- **T-API-65t**: Pre-aborted signal beats **invalid workflow / script name** discovery validation failure on both surfaces. Companion to T-API-65s, parameterized specifically over the SPEC 5.3 name-restriction failure path (invalid workflow name like `.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh` or invalid script name like `.loopx/broken/-bad.sh`). T-API-65s exercises a same-base-name collision sibling-validation failure; this test exercises the name-restriction sibling-validation failure as a distinct sub-path. The two failure paths share the SPEC 5.4 "fatal in run mode" classification, but the **error class** and the validation code path are distinct (collision vs. name-pattern), and a buggy implementation could route one through the abort-precedence pathway and the other not. Set up: a valid target workflow `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` alongside a sibling with an invalid name (parameterize the test over (a) `.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh` invalid workflow name, and (b) `.loopx/broken/-bad.sh` invalid script name). Run both surface variants for each invalid-name fixture (so the test exercises 2 × 2 = 4 sub-cases total): `(t-promise)` analogous to `(s-promise)` and `(t-generator)` analogous to `(s-generator)`. Assert per sub-case: (a) the promise / first `next()` rejects with the **abort** error, **not** the discovery name-restriction error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 5.1, 5.3, 5.4)*
- **T-API-65u**: Reentrant duck-signal abort wins over a later pre-iteration failure on the same call. SPEC 9.5 says "if `addEventListener` synchronously invokes the registered listener during registration ... loopx treats the signal as aborted" — the same observable state as a pre-aborted real `AbortSignal`. SPEC 9.3's abort-precedence rule then displaces all other pre-iteration failure modes once a usable signal is captured and aborts. T-API-64b covers the basic reentrant-`addEventListener`-fires-listener case (no other failure to compete with), and T-API-65 / 65a / 65b / 65c / 65d / 65h–65j cover abort-precedence over individual pre-iteration failures using **real** pre-aborted `AbortSignal` instances. The combination — duck signal whose `addEventListener` synchronously fires the listener **plus** a separately-configured later pre-iteration failure on the same call — is uncovered for failure modes that surface during **pre-iteration** (after option-snapshot completes). A buggy implementation that routed real-`AbortSignal` precedence through the abort pathway but treated the synchronously-fired duck-listener case as a separate "synchronously aborted at register time" code path (perhaps surfacing the captured signal-state directly without entering abort-precedence) could pass the existing tests yet surface the later pre-iteration failure here. **Scope of variants — option-snapshot failures excluded.** This test deliberately excludes pairing the reentrant duck signal with a competing **option-snapshot** failure (e.g., a throwing `options.cwd` / `options.envFile` getter, an invalid `options.maxIterations`, an invalid `options.env` shape, or any other field captured at call time). SPEC 9.5 leaves "ordering between reading `aborted` and registering the listener for duck-typed signals" implementation-defined, so an implementation that reads `aborted` first (false), reads the throwing / invalid field next (captured as an option-snapshot error), and never invokes `addEventListener` on the duck (because option-snapshot failed before listener registration was reached) would surface the option-snapshot error without violating SPEC 9.5 — the duck listener was never given the opportunity to fire reentrantly, so the "treats the signal as aborted" precondition was never satisfied. The "abort beats option-snapshot failure" axis is already covered comprehensively for **real** pre-aborted `AbortSignal` instances by T-API-65p / T-API-65q (which enumerate every SPEC 9.5 / 9.1 option-snapshot failure mode); pinning the same axis to a duck-listener trigger would over-pin SPEC 9.5's impl-defined ordering and is intentionally out of scope. The variants below all use failure modes that surface during **pre-iteration** (after option-snapshot completes), where the duck listener has had the opportunity to be registered — and to fire reentrantly — before the pre-iteration failure is detected, making abort precedence observable without depending on intra-snapshot ordering. **Parameterize over both run surfaces and over multiple later-failure variants.** Construct the reentrant duck signal: `const duck = { aborted: false, addEventListener(type, fn) { if (type === "abort") { this.aborted = true; fn(); } } };` Pair it with a later-pre-iteration failure on the same call. Variants: **(i) missing local env file** — `runPromise("ralph", { signal: duck, envFile: "nonexistent.env", maxIterations: 1 })`; **(ii) target-resolution failure** — fixture has `.loopx/other/index.sh`; call with target `"ralph"` (missing workflow). Drive each variant on each surface:
    - **(promise)**: `await expect(runPromise(<target>, opts)).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);`
    - **(generator)**: `const gen = run(<target>, opts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);`
  
  Assert per variant per surface: (a) the surfaced rejection / throw is the **abort** error, **not** the configured later pre-iteration failure (env-file-missing or missing-workflow), (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created (assertion scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 preface). A buggy implementation that surfaced the later pre-iteration failure despite the reentrant duck listener having already transitioned the signal to aborted would fail (a). Together with T-API-64b (reentrancy in isolation), T-API-65 / 65a / 65b / 65c / 65d / 65h–65j (real-`AbortSignal` precedence), T-API-65p / T-API-65q (real-pre-aborted-signal precedence × the full enumerated option-snapshot failure set), and T-API-65k–65t (precedence × additional pre-iteration failure modes), this closes the SPEC 9.5 reentrant-duck-signal × SPEC 9.3 abort-precedence intersection on the pre-iteration axis. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5, 9.1, 9.2)*
- **T-API-65p**: `runPromise()` — pre-aborted signal beats **the full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set plus a representative selection of remaining option-snapshot failures**. SPEC 9.3 "Abort precedence over pre-iteration failures": "Once a usable `AbortSignal` has been captured (a real `AbortSignal` or a duck-typed signal that satisfies the section 9.5 contract), an already-aborted signal at call time, or a signal that aborts during pre-iteration before the first child spawn, displaces all other pre-iteration failure modes on the same call: captured option-snapshot errors, target argument / target syntax validation, `.loopx/` discovery, env-file loading, target resolution, and tmpdir creation." T-API-65g covers a single option-snapshot variant (throwing later-option getter on `env`); T-API-65–65d / 65h–65j cover the non-option-snapshot failure modes (env-file, invalid-target, discovery, env-file-unreadable, tmpdir, target-resolution sub-paths). This test extends the option-snapshot category by parameterizing **exhaustively** over every invalid `maxIterations` value treated as invalid elsewhere in TEST-SPEC.md (so the abort-precedence × `maxIterations` matrix is a strict superset of the validation matrix in T-API-22 etc.) and **representatively** over the remaining option-snapshot families (invalid `cwd` / `envFile` types, throwing getters, invalid `env` shape and entry value, throwing `env` entry getter, throwing `env` proxy `ownKeys` trap). The full invalid `env` shape/value matrix (every shape from T-API-53–53d and every entry-value variant from T-API-54–54c) is intentionally **not** exhaustively re-parameterized here — those validation paths converge on a single "captured option-snapshot error" code path that the abort-precedence pathway must displace, and the representative sample is sufficient to detect a buggy implementation that bypassed abort-precedence on the option-snapshot category. The cost-benefit favors the broader coverage that lighter env sampling allows in the matrix; tests at the env-shape-validation surface (T-API-53–55, T-API-55a–55d) carry the exhaustive env-shape coverage without abort-precedence pinning. **Invalid `options.signal` is excluded** per SPEC 9.3 "An invalid `options` value or non-`AbortSignal`-compatible `options.signal` captures no signal and does not enter this pathway"; that case is covered separately by T-API-65e and is **not** an abort-precedence path. **Parameterize over each variant.** Drive each variant by constructing options so the variant's invalid value is preserved on the final options object. **Construction rule**: build the **final options object directly** — for plain-data variants, `const opts: any = { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1, ...<variant-specific-data-fields> }` is fine; for **getter / proxy variants** (viii, x, xiii, xiv), the throwing accessor or proxy must be installed directly on `opts` (or on the env object referenced by `opts.env`) using `Object.defineProperty` or by constructing `opts` field-by-field — **never via `{ ...opts, ... }`**, because spreading invokes enumerable getters and proxy `ownKeys` traps in the test harness before `runPromise()` is called, which surfaces the exception at the test call site rather than letting loopx capture it via the SPEC 9.5 snapshot path (see "Test-construction sanity for getter/proxy variants" in section 1.3). For variants (i)–(vi), the `maxIterations` default of `1` may be replaced by the variant value or omitted entirely so the variant's value is the only one set. Then: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const p = runPromise("ralph", opts);` Assert per variant: (a) the promise rejects with the **abort** error — not the configured option-snapshot error, (b) no child was spawned (no marker file), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created (assertion is scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 preface). **Variants:** **(i) invalid `maxIterations: -1`** (the variant must be the effective `maxIterations`, not overwritten by the default); **(ii) invalid `maxIterations: 1.5`** (same); **(iii) invalid `maxIterations: NaN`** (same); **(iv) invalid `maxIterations: Infinity`** (same — non-finite positive infinity, treated as invalid alongside `NaN` per the SPEC 9.5 finiteness requirement); **(v) invalid `maxIterations: null as any`** (same — `null` is not a finite non-negative integer); **(vi) invalid `maxIterations: "1" as any`** (same — string values are rejected at the type-shape gate, distinct from the numeric-range gates exercised by (i)–(v); pins down that abort-precedence displaces the type-validation failure mode in addition to the value-validation failure modes); **(vii) non-string `cwd: 42 as any`**; **(viii) throwing `cwd` getter** — construct: `const opts: any = { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }; Object.defineProperty(opts, "cwd", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("cwd-getter-boom"); } });` and call `runPromise("ralph", opts)`; **(ix) non-string `envFile: 42 as any`**; **(x) throwing `envFile` getter** — analogous to (viii): `const opts: any = { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }; Object.defineProperty(opts, "envFile", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("envFile-getter-boom"); } });`; **(xi) representative invalid `env` shape — `env: [] as any`** (representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-53–53d shape-validation matrix; other invalid shapes — `null`, function, string, number — share the same code path); **(xii) representative non-string env entry value — `env: { KEY: 42 as any }`** (representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-54–54c entry-value-validation matrix; other invalid entry values — `undefined`, `null`, object, function — share the same code path); **(xiii) throwing enumerable getter inside `env`** — `const env: any = {}; Object.defineProperty(env, "KEY", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } }); const opts: any = { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1, env };` (the `env` value is a plain object whose entry getter throws — assigning it as `opts.env = env` is safe because reading `opts.env` reads the plain reference, not a getter); **(xiv) throwing `env` proxy `ownKeys` trap** — `const env: any = new Proxy({}, { ownKeys() { throw new Error("ownKeys-trap-boom"); } }); const opts: any = { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1, env };` (same rationale: the proxy is the value of `opts.env`, not a getter on `opts`, so reading `opts.env` does not trigger the trap; the trap fires only when loopx enumerates the proxy's own keys during snapshot). The "abort wins" outcome must hold across all fourteen variants. **Test-construction sanity check**: before invoking `runPromise`, the test should assert (e.g., for variants (i)–(vi)) that `opts.maxIterations` is the variant value (`-1`, `1.5`, `NaN`, `Infinity`, `null`, or `"1"`), not the default `1` — proving the construction order actually exposes the invalid value. **For getter / proxy variants (viii), (x), (xiii), (xiv)**: the test must additionally guard against accidental harness-side invocation by reading the relevant accessor / trap **never** during construction; constructing `opts` field-by-field (rather than via spread or initializer literals that include `...opts`) is sufficient. A buggy implementation that read any of these option fields before snapshotting `options.signal`, or that surfaced the captured option-snapshot error despite a usable pre-aborted signal, would fail at least one variant. T-API-65g already proves the principle on the throwing-`env`-getter-on-options-object surface; this test extends the principle systematically across the full invalid-`maxIterations` enumeration plus the representative remaining option-snapshot families. *(Spec 9.2, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65q**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats **the full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set plus a representative selection of remaining option-snapshot failures**. Generator-surface counterpart to T-API-65p. Same parameterized variant set (i)–(xiv) — exhaustive over invalid `maxIterations` values (i)–(vi) (including `Infinity`, `null`, and string value variants alongside the numeric-range and `NaN` variants) and representative over the remaining option-snapshot families (vii)–(xiv) (invalid `cwd` / `envFile` types, throwing getters, representative invalid `env` shape and entry value, throwing `env` entry getter, throwing `env` proxy `ownKeys` trap) — and the same construction rule: build the **final options object directly** — for getter / proxy variants (viii), (x), (xiii), (xiv), install the throwing accessor or proxy directly on the object passed to `run()` using `Object.defineProperty` or field-by-field construction; **never via `{ ...opts, ... }`**, because spreading invokes enumerable getters and proxy `ownKeys` traps in the test harness before `run()` is called (see "Test-construction sanity for getter/proxy variants" in section 1.3). For variants (i)–(vi) the `maxIterations` default of `1` may be replaced by the variant value or omitted entirely. The full invalid `env` shape/value matrix is intentionally **not** exhaustively re-parameterized here; tests at the env-shape-validation surface (T-API-53–55, T-API-55a–55d) carry the exhaustive env-shape coverage without abort-precedence pinning, while this test pins the abort-precedence outcome via representative env variants. Drive each variant: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);` Assert per variant: (a) the first `next()` throws the **abort** error — not the configured option-snapshot error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir was created. **Test-construction sanity check** as in T-API-65p: for variants (i)–(vi), `opts.maxIterations` must be the variant value (`-1`, `1.5`, `NaN`, `Infinity`, `null`, or `"1"`) before invocation; for getter / proxy variants (viii), (x), (xiii), (xiv), the test must guard against accidental harness-side invocation by avoiding object-spread of objects whose getters / proxy traps are the behavior under test. T-API-65f already proves the principle on the throwing-`env`-getter-on-options-object surface for `run()`; this test extends the principle systematically across the full invalid-`maxIterations` enumeration plus the representative remaining option-snapshot families on the generator surface. Together with T-API-65p, this closes the abort-precedence-over-option-snapshot category across both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65f**: `run()` — pre-aborted signal beats a throwing later-option getter. Per SPEC 9.1 "Option-snapshot timing" and SPEC 9.5 "`signal`", `options.signal` is read **before** other `RunOptions` fields, so an already-aborted signal is captured before any subsequent option-field read can produce a snapshot exception. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const throwingOpts = { signal: c.signal, get env() { throw new Error("env-getter-boom"); }, maxIterations: 1 }; const gen = run("ralph", throwingOpts); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(/abort/i);` Assert: (a) the first-`next()` error is the abort error, **not** the `env-getter-boom` option-snapshot error, (b) no child was spawned (marker file absent if any), (c) no tmpdir was created. A buggy implementation that read `options.env` before `options.signal` would surface `env-getter-boom` instead and fail this test. This directly exercises the "signal first" rule rather than merely inferring it from the abort-vs-pre-iteration-failure precedence tests (T-API-65–65d). *(Spec 9.1, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-65g**: `runPromise()` — pre-aborted signal beats a throwing later-option getter. Same fixture as T-API-65f: `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const throwingOpts = { signal: c.signal, get env() { throw new Error("env-getter-boom"); }, maxIterations: 1 }; const p = runPromise("ralph", throwingOpts);` Assert: (a) the promise rejects with the abort error, **not** the `env-getter-boom` option-snapshot error, (b) no child was spawned, (c) no tmpdir was created. Per SPEC 9.2 "Option-snapshot timing" ("Identical to `run()`: each option field read at most once, `options.signal` first"), the same signal-first rule holds under `runPromise()`. This is the `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-65f and completes signal-first coverage across both API surfaces. *(Spec 9.2, 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-66**: Abort after final yield but before settlement produces abort error. Mirror T-TMP-23 / Spec 9.3 "Abort after final yield". `const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); const { value } = await gen.next(); c.abort(); await gen.next();` — expect the second `next()` to throw the abort error, not to resolve `{ done: true }` silently. *(Spec 9.3)*
- **T-API-66a**: Abort after final yield but before settlement produces abort error on subsequent `.return()`. Per SPEC 9.3: "Abort after final yield ... produces the abort error on the next generator interaction — `g.next()`, `.return()`, or `.throw()`." T-API-66 covers the `.next()` branch; this covers `.return()`. `const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); const { value } = await gen.next(); c.abort(); await expect(gen.return(undefined)).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` Assert the `.return()` call rejects with the abort error rather than settling `{ done: true, value: undefined }` silently. *(Spec 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-API-66b**: Abort after final yield but before settlement produces abort error on subsequent `.throw()`. Same setup as T-API-66a. `const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 1 }); const { value } = await gen.next(); c.abort(); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("consumer-err"))).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` Assert the `.throw()` call rejects with the **abort** error (the signal displaces the consumer-supplied error, per SPEC 9.3's abort-after-final-yield rule). Together, T-API-66 / T-API-66a / T-API-66b cover all three settle-triggering interactions enumerated in SPEC 9.3 under the **`maxIterations`-reached** final-yield trigger. *(Spec 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-API-66c**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield but before settlement produces abort error. Companion to T-API-66 for the script-driven final-yield branch. SPEC 9.3 says the final `Output` may be final because of "`stop: true` or `maxIterations` reached" — the abort-after-final-yield rule applies symmetrically across both triggers. T-API-66 covers the `maxIterations: 1`-driven final-yield case; this test pins down the `stop: true`-driven case so a buggy implementation that recognized "final yield" only when the iteration counter reached the limit (and treated `stop: true` as a separate "the script asked to stop, settle silently" code path that bypassed abort-precedence) would pass T-API-66 yet silently resolve `{ done: true }` on the next `.next()` despite the live abort. Set up `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` that emits `{"stop":true}` on its first iteration. Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next();` (the iteration limit is `5`, but the script's `stop: true` makes the first yielded `Output` final). Verify `first.value.stop === true`. Then `c.abort(); await expect(gen.next()).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` Assert: (a) the second `.next()` rejects with the **abort** error, **not** `{ done: true }`, (b) the rejection error is the abort error class (matching T-API-66's abort-error contract), and (c) cleanup ran before the rejection surfaced (assertion shape mirrors T-TMP-24a's "tmpdir removed before throw" assertion; pin down the cleanup-ordering side via the T-TMP-24e companion test below). A buggy implementation that special-cased `stop: true` to skip the abort-precedence check would silently resolve `{ done: true }` and fail (a). *(Spec 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-API-66d**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield produces abort error on subsequent `.return()`. `.return()` counterpart to T-API-66c, mirroring T-API-66a's relationship to T-API-66. Same fixture as T-API-66c (script emits `{"stop":true}` on first iteration). Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next(); /* first.value.stop === true */ c.abort(); await expect(gen.return(undefined)).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` Assert the `.return()` call rejects with the abort error rather than settling `{ done: true, value: undefined }` silently. The abort-after-final-yield rule applies regardless of which settlement-triggering interaction follows — `.next()` (T-API-66c) and `.return()` (this test) must both surface the abort, not silent settlement. *(Spec 9.3, 9.1)*
- **T-API-66e**: Abort after **`stop: true`-driven** final yield produces abort error on subsequent `.throw()`, displacing the consumer-supplied error. `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-66c / T-API-66d, mirroring T-API-66b's relationship to T-API-66 / T-API-66a. Same fixture (script emits `{"stop":true}` on first iteration). Drive: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal, maxIterations: 5 }); const first = await gen.next(); /* first.value.stop === true */ c.abort(); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("consumer-err"))).rejects.toThrow(<abort error>);` Assert the `.throw()` call rejects with the **abort** error (the signal displaces the consumer-supplied error per SPEC 9.3's abort-after-final-yield rule, identical contract to T-API-66b but driven by the `stop: true` final-yield trigger rather than `maxIterations`-reached). Together with T-API-66c (`.next()`) and T-API-66d (`.return()`), this test closes coverage for all three settle-triggering interactions × the `stop: true`-driven final-yield trigger, completing the T-API-66/66a/66b × `stop: true`/`maxIterations` matrix. *(Spec 9.3, 9.1)*

##### Pinned Priority

- **T-API-67**: `run()` — invalid `options.cwd` (non-string) surfaces before all four project-root-dependent failures named in Spec 9.3. Parameterize over fixture variants that would otherwise fail via (a) `.loopx/` discovery error, (b) env-file loading error, (c) target-resolution error, and (d) tmpdir-creation error. In every variant, assert the first `next()` throws the invalid-`cwd` error, not the downstream project-root-dependent failure. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67a**: `run()` — throwing `options.cwd` getter surfaces before the same four project-root-dependent failures as T-API-67. Parameterize over the same variants; in every case, assert the first `next()` throws the `cwd`-getter error, not the downstream failure. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67b**: `run()` — invalid `options.envFile` (non-string) surfaces before downstream project-root-dependent failures. Parameterize over fixture variants that would otherwise fail via (a) `.loopx/` discovery error, (b) **env-file-loading error** (a global env file present and unreadable, or otherwise configured to surface a load-failure independent of the invalid `envFile` path — e.g., a global `XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/env` of mode `000`), (c) target-resolution error, and (d) tmpdir-creation error. Assert the first `next()` throws the invalid-`envFile` error in every variant. SPEC 9.3 names all four project-root-dependent failures (discovery, env-file loading, target resolution, tmpdir creation) as the set that pinned `options.envFile`-validation must precede; this test exercises the env-file-loading branch alongside the other three. (Note: the *invalid `envFile`* path's own load is never reached because shape validation fires first; the env-file-loading branch is exercised via the **independent** global env file's load failure, which would otherwise have been the surfaced error.) Variant (b) is **conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** — root bypasses POSIX permission checks. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67c**: `run()` — throwing `options.envFile` getter surfaces before the same downstream project-root-dependent failures as T-API-67b, including the env-file-loading branch. Parameterize over the same four variants (discovery, **env-file loading**, target resolution, tmpdir creation); in every case, assert the first `next()` throws the `envFile`-getter error, not the downstream failure. The env-file-loading variant uses an independently-failing global env file (mode `000` `XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/env` or equivalent) so that shape-validation pinning is observable against a real env-file-load failure, not just the invalid `envFile` path's own deferred load. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** for the env-file-loading variant. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67d**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-67 — invalid `options.cwd` (non-string) surfaces before the same four project-root-dependent failures (discovery, env-file loading, target resolution, tmpdir creation). Assert the promise rejects with the invalid-`cwd` error, not the downstream failure. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67e**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-67a — throwing `options.cwd` getter surfaces before the same four project-root-dependent failures. Assert the promise rejects with the `cwd`-getter error, not the downstream failure. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67f**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-67b — invalid `options.envFile` (non-string) surfaces before the same four downstream project-root-dependent failures (discovery, **env-file loading**, target resolution, tmpdir creation). The env-file-loading variant uses an independently-failing global env file (mode `000` `XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/env` or equivalent) so the load-failure path is exercised against the invalid-`envFile`-pinning rule rather than the invalid `envFile`'s own deferred load. Assert the promise rejects with the invalid-`envFile` error in every variant. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** for the env-file-loading variant. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*
- **T-API-67g**: `runPromise()` counterpart to T-API-67c — throwing `options.envFile` getter surfaces before the same four downstream project-root-dependent failures (discovery, **env-file loading**, target resolution, tmpdir creation). Same env-file-loading-variant configuration as T-API-67f. Assert the promise rejects with the `envFile`-getter error, not the downstream failure, in every variant. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** for the env-file-loading variant. *(Spec 9.3, 9.5)*

##### Pre-First-`next()` Carve-out

Spec 9.1 defines a carve-out: `.return()` or `.throw()` as the consumer's *first* interaction with the generator returned by `run()` settles silently, suppressing all pre-iteration errors.

- **T-API-68**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses invalid-target error. `const gen = run(undefined as any); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert no error is thrown. No child is spawned, no tmpdir created. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68a**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses discovery failure. Call in a fixture without `.loopx/`. `const gen = run("ralph"); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert no error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68b**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses env-file-load failure. `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "nonexistent.env" }); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert no error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68c**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses option-snapshot failure. `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: -1 }); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert no error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68d**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses pre-aborted signal. `const c = new AbortController(); c.abort(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal }); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert no error. Per Spec 9.1: "A `signal` captured at call time is not consulted; even an already-aborted signal does not surface the abort error through this path." *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68e**: `.return()` as first interaction does not create a tmpdir. Snapshot `os.tmpdir()` before and after the `.return()` call. Assert no new `loopx-*` directory was created. *(Spec 9.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-68g**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses tmpdir-creation failure. Configure a tmpdir-creation failure by setting `TMPDIR` to an unwritable path (e.g., a parent directory with mode 000) so that `mkdtemp` would fail pre-iteration. `const gen = run("ralph"); await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) no error is thrown, (b) the `.return()` resolves cleanly with `{ done: true }`, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir exists under the configured unwritable parent (the only parent loopx would have attempted under this `TMPDIR` snapshot — assertion is scoped to that parent, not a global `os.tmpdir()` scan, per the test-parent isolation guidance in section 4.7's tmpdir-block preface), and (d) no tmpdir-creation-failure error, warning, or other surfacing of the configured failure appears on stderr or via the generator settlement — the pre-first-`next()` carve-out suppressed the configured tmpdir-creation failure entirely. (Whether loopx black-box "did not attempt `mkdtemp`" is not directly observable without a seam; assertions (c) and (d) together pin the observable outcome of suppression.) Per SPEC 9.1 "pre-first-`next()` consumer-cancellation carve-out": "tmpdir creation failures are all suppressed." T-API-68/68a–68d cover target / discovery / env-file / option / pre-aborted-signal suppression; T-API-68e covers the negative observation (no tmpdir created); this test closes the tmpdir-creation-failure branch enumerated in SPEC 9.1's carve-out list. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 9.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-68f**: `.return(value)` as first interaction settles with the supplied value per standard async-generator semantics. Per SPEC 9.1 "settles the generator per standard JS async-generator semantics" — the value propagates through the iterator result. `const gen = run(undefined as any); const result = await gen.return("my-return-value"); expect(result).toEqual({ value: "my-return-value", done: true });` Also verify the generator is exhausted: a subsequent `await gen.next()` resolves to `{ value: undefined, done: true }`. This pins down the value-propagation contract — T-API-68/68a–68d cover "no error is thrown" under the carve-out but do not verify that the iterator-result `value` field carries the consumer-supplied value. A buggy implementation that force-returned `{ value: undefined, done: true }` ignoring the passed-in value would pass T-API-68 but fail this assertion. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-68h**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.env` getter. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "throwing option-field getters" and "throwing `options.env` enumerable getters or proxy traps" among the snapshot errors that are suppressed when the consumer's first interaction is `.return()`. T-API-68c covers a plain invalid option (`maxIterations: -1`); this test pins down the throwing-getter branch specifically. Construct an options object with a throwing `env` getter: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-env-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned (no marker), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created (consistent with T-API-68e), and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-env-getter"` error. Per the carve-out: captured option-snapshot errors — including throwing `env` getters on the options object — are fully suppressed. An implementation that re-invoked the getter during settlement, or that propagated the captured snapshot error despite the carve-out, would fail this test. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68j**: `.return(value)` as first interaction suppresses an abort that fires on a **live** signal *between* `run()` returning and the consumer's first interaction. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out: "A `signal` captured at call time is not consulted; even an already-aborted signal does not surface the abort error through this path." T-API-68d covers the **already-aborted-at-call-time** case (the signal was aborted *before* `run()` was called, so the listener never fires for a fresh state transition). This test covers the **live-signal-aborts-after-call-time** case: the signal is **not aborted** when `run()` is called (so the abort listener registered at call time per T-API-64k attaches to a live, not-yet-aborted signal), then the consumer aborts the signal **before** their first interaction with the generator (so the listener actually fires for a state transition observed by loopx), and *then* the consumer's first interaction is `.return(value)`. The carve-out must still apply: even though the abort listener has fired and loopx has observed a state transition on the captured signal, the consumer's first interaction being `.return()` settles per standard async-generator semantics with no abort-error surfacing. This pins down the stronger "captured signal is not consulted" reading — the captured signal is not consulted *even when its state transitioned during the carve-out window* — over the weaker reading "captured signal is not consulted only if it never transitioned." Construct: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal }); c.abort(); /* listener fires synchronously */ const result = await gen.return("my-return-value");` Assert: (a) `result` resolves to `{ value: "my-return-value", done: true }` — standard async-generator `.return()` settlement, with the consumer-supplied value propagated per the same contract as T-API-68f, (b) **no abort error is thrown** — the carve-out suppresses the abort observation that fired on the captured signal, (c) no child is spawned (no marker file written; assertion-scoped to the test fixture), and (d) **no `loopx-*` tmpdir is created** under the test-isolated tmpdir parent (per the section 4.7 tmpdir-block preface). T-API-68d covers pre-aborted suppression (no listener fires) and T-API-68e / T-API-68g cover the no-tmpdir / suppressed-tmpdir-failure observations on the `.return()` path; this test fills the "live signal aborts after call but before first interaction" gap that the existing tests do not directly exercise. A buggy implementation that surfaced the abort because the listener fired during the carve-out window (rather than because the signal was consulted on the carve-out path) would fail (b). *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68k**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.signal` getter. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "throwing option-field getters" — including the `signal` getter, which SPEC 9.1 reads first per the option-snapshot ordering rule — among the snapshot errors that are suppressed when the consumer's first interaction is `.return()`. T-API-68h covers the `options.env` getter on the options object (which is read after `signal`); this test pins down the **`signal`-getter** branch specifically so a buggy implementation that surfaced a `signal`-snapshot exception despite the carve-out — perhaps because `signal` is read first and the carve-out check fires later in the call-time sequence — would be caught. Construct an options object with a throwing `signal` getter: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-signal-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created (consistent with T-API-68e), and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-signal-getter"` error. Per the carve-out: captured option-snapshot errors — including throwing getters on every pinned `RunOptions` field — are fully suppressed when the consumer's first interaction is `.return()`. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68l**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.cwd` getter. Pinned-priority option per SPEC 9.3 "Pinned pre-iteration priority". Companion to T-API-68k; pins down the throwing-`cwd`-getter branch of the carve-out's "throwing option-field getters" enumeration. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "cwd", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-cwd-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }`, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-cwd-getter"` error. A buggy implementation that surfaced the pinned-priority `cwd`-getter exception under the carve-out path would fail (a). *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68m**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.envFile` getter. Pinned-priority option per SPEC 9.3 "Pinned pre-iteration priority". Companion to T-API-68k / T-API-68l; pins down the throwing-`envFile`-getter branch. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "envFile", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-envFile-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }`, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-envFile-getter"` error. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68n**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.maxIterations` getter. Companion to T-API-68k / T-API-68l / T-API-68m; pins down the throwing-`maxIterations`-getter branch. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "maxIterations", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-maxIterations-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }`, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-maxIterations-getter"` error. Together with T-API-68k / T-API-68l / T-API-68m / T-API-68h, this completes coverage for throwing getters on every pinned `RunOptions` field — `signal`, `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, `env` — under the `.return()` carve-out. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-68o**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses an **unreadable global env file** failure. SPEC 9.1's carve-out enumerates "env-file loading failures" — covering both local (`-e` / `RunOptions.envFile`, SPEC 8.2) and global (`$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/env`, SPEC 8.1) env files per SPEC 7.1 step 2. T-API-68b covers the local missing-envFile sub-path; this test pins down the global-env-file unreadable sub-path so an implementation that suppressed local env-file failures via the carve-out path but routed unreadable global env files through a different, suppression-blind code path (e.g., a synchronous `fs.readFileSync` that throws and is rethrown before the carve-out check) would be caught. Set up: an isolated `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (e.g., `<test-dir>/xdg-config`) with `loopx/env` written and then `chmod 000`'d so it exists but is unreadable; this `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is injected into loopx's inherited environment (the same injection pattern as T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable / T-API-65r). A valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the global-env-file-read error. Together with T-API-65r (abort-precedence over the same failure mode) and T-SIG-30 (CLI signal-wins), this closes the global-env-unreadable sub-path on the `.return()` carve-out surface. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** (root can read mode-000 files). *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-68p**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses **local env-file parser warnings**. SPEC 9.1's carve-out says "the loop body is never entered. **No pre-iteration step runs and no pre-iteration error is surfaced**" — the "no pre-iteration step runs" clause is broader than the enumerated error list and applies to *warning-only* pre-iteration work as well as failure paths. T-API-68b / T-API-68o pin down env-file *failure* suppression (missing local file, unreadable global file); this test pins down env-file *warning* suppression on the local-`envFile` path: a malformed-but-readable local env file would normally produce a SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning on stderr (e.g., the `1BAD=val` line is ignored with a warning per T-CLI-22c / T-ENV-15c), but the carve-out must suppress that warning because the env-file parsing step never runs. Set up: a project root with a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Write a local env file (e.g., `<test-dir>/local.env`) with content `1BAD=val\nOK=fine\n` (the `1BAD` line is the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key warning trigger; the `OK=fine` line would parse normally). Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<test-dir>/local.env" }); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned (no marker file from `ralph:index`), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created (assertion-scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent), and (d) **stderr does not contain the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning for `1BAD`** — i.e., stderr does not mention `1BAD` and does not surface the env-file invalid-key warning text. A buggy implementation that ran the env-file parse step (producing the warning) before checking whether the consumer had already canceled via `.return()` would fail (d) — the warning would leak to stderr even though no error was surfaced. This test, paired with T-API-68b (missing-file failure suppression on the same `envFile` path), pins down both error and warning suppression on the local env-file pre-iteration step. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 8.2, 7.1)*
- **T-API-68q**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses **global env-file parser warnings**. Global-env-file counterpart to T-API-68p. SPEC 8.1's "Key validation" rule applies symmetrically to global and local env files — both emit invalid-key warnings on stderr when a malformed-but-readable line is encountered. T-API-68p covers local env-file warning suppression via the `envFile` path; this test covers global env-file warning suppression via the inherited `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` path that loopx resolves on its own (no `envFile` option supplied). T-API-68o covers the global-env-file *failure* path (mode 000 — unreadable); this test covers the *warning* path (readable but malformed). Set up: an isolated `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (e.g., `<test-dir>/xdg-config`) with `loopx/env` written and containing `1BAD=val\nOK=fine\n` (the `1BAD` line is the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key warning trigger). The `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is injected into loopx's inherited environment (same injection pattern as T-API-68o / T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable). A valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) **stderr does not contain the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning for `1BAD`** — the global-env-file parse step never ran. Together with T-API-68p (local) and T-API-68o (global-failure), this closes the global-env-file warning-suppression branch of SPEC 9.1's "no pre-iteration step runs" clause. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-68r**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses **workflow `package.json` warnings** from the starting-workflow version check (SPEC 7.1 step 5). SPEC 3.2 enumerates four `package.json` failure modes that produce warnings on stderr at runtime: unsatisfied semver range (T-VER-01), invalid JSON (T-VER-08 / T-VER-09), invalid semver range (T-VER-09a), and unreadable `package.json` (T-VER-07). T-TMP-12f / T-TMP-12f2 / T-TMP-12f3 / T-TMP-12f4 pin down that step 5 runs *before* tmpdir creation in the CLI surface and emits each of these four warnings even when step 6 fails. The `.return()` carve-out's "no pre-iteration step runs" clause must additionally suppress those warnings entirely when the consumer cancels before first `next()`: step 5 never executes, so no `package.json` warning fires. **Parameterize over the four warning branches** to ensure all four `package.json` failure-mode warnings are suppressed: **(i) unsatisfied range** — `.loopx/ralph/package.json` declares `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": ">=999.0.0" } }`; **(ii) invalid JSON** — `.loopx/ralph/package.json` contains `{broken`; **(iii) invalid semver range** — `.loopx/ralph/package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }`; **(iv) unreadable `package.json`** — `.loopx/ralph/package.json` contains valid content (e.g., `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "*" } }`) and is `chmod 000`'d so it exists but is unreadable. Each variant has a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. For each variant, drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` Assert (per variant): (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) **stderr does not contain the `package.json` warning text** for that variant (no version-mismatch / invalid-JSON / invalid-semver / unreadable-`package.json` warning surfaces, even though SPEC 3.2 enumerates them as runtime warnings on workflow entry). Variant (iv) is **conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** (root can read mode-000 files). T-CLI-119 / T-CLI-119c / T-CLI-119e / T-CLI-119f cover the analogous CLI-`-n 0` carve-out (the `-n 0` short-circuit similarly skips step 5); this test pins down the `.return()` carve-out's symmetric warning-suppression on the programmatic surface. A buggy implementation that read and parsed `package.json` (producing the warning) before checking whether the consumer had already canceled via `.return()` would fail (d) on each variant. *(Spec 9.1, 7.1, 3.2)*
- **T-API-68i**: `.return()` as first interaction suppresses **the full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set plus a representative selection of remaining option-snapshot and target-resolution failures**. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out lists "captured option-snapshot errors (invalid `options` values, invalid `options.signal`, non-string `options.cwd` / `options.envFile`, invalid `options.maxIterations`, invalid `options.env` shape, throwing option-field getters, throwing `options.env` enumerable getters or proxy traps), invalid `target` values and target-syntax validation, `.loopx/` discovery failures, env-file loading failures, target resolution failures, and tmpdir creation failures" — all suppressed. Existing T-API-68 / 68a–68h / 68k–68o pin down individual branches (target-syntax, discovery, env-file load, single `maxIterations: -1` option-snapshot, pre-aborted signal, no-tmpdir negative observation, return-value propagation, tmpdir-creation failure, throwing `options.env` getter, throwing `options.signal` / `options.cwd` / `options.envFile` / `options.maxIterations` getters, unreadable global env file); this test extends coverage to (i) the **full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set** (every value treated as invalid in the validation matrix at T-API-22 etc., parameterized exhaustively so the carve-out × `maxIterations` matrix is a strict superset of the validation matrix), (ii) a **representative selection** of the remaining option-snapshot families (invalid `options` shape, invalid `options.signal`, invalid `cwd` / `envFile` types, representative invalid `env` shape and entry value, throwing `env` entry getter, throwing `env` proxy traps), (iii) the target-resolution sub-paths, and (iv) target-syntax validation — in a single parameterized test, mirroring the parameterized T-API-69g but for the `.return()` surface. The full invalid `env` shape/value matrix (every shape from T-API-53–53d and every entry-value variant from T-API-54–54c) is intentionally **not** exhaustively re-parameterized here — those validation paths converge on a single "captured option-snapshot error" code path that the carve-out must suppress, and the representative sample is sufficient to detect a buggy implementation that bypassed the carve-out on the option-snapshot category; T-API-53–55 and T-API-55a–55d carry the exhaustive env-shape coverage without carve-out pinning. **Parameterize over each variant**, set up the relevant fixture, drive `const gen = run(<target>, <opts>); const settled = await gen.return(undefined);` and assert per variant: (a) `settled` resolves to `{ done: true, value: undefined }` — no error is thrown, (b) no child spawned (no marker file written), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir is created (assertion is scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 tmpdir-block preface), and (d) stderr does not surface the configured error. **Variants:** **(i) invalid `options` shape — `null`** (`run("ralph", null as any)`); **(ii) invalid `options` shape — array** (`run("ralph", [] as any)`); **(iii) invalid `options` shape — function** (`run("ralph", (() => {}) as any)`); **(iv) invalid `options` shape — primitive number** (`run("ralph", 42 as any)`); **(v) invalid `options.signal` — non-`AbortSignal`-shaped value** (`run("ralph", { signal: "not-a-signal" as any })`); **(vi) non-string `options.cwd`** (`run("ralph", { cwd: 42 as any })`); **(vii) non-string `options.envFile`** (`run("ralph", { envFile: 42 as any })`); **(viii) invalid `options.maxIterations: 1.5`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1.5 })` — non-integer; T-API-68c covers `-1` separately); **(ix) invalid `options.maxIterations: NaN`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: NaN })`); **(x) invalid `options.maxIterations: Infinity`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: Infinity })` — non-finite); **(xi) invalid `options.maxIterations: null as any`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: null as any })` — null is not a finite non-negative integer); **(xii) invalid `options.maxIterations: "1" as any`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: "1" as any })` — string value rejected at the type-shape gate, distinct from the numeric-range gates of (viii)–(xi)); **(xiii) representative invalid `options.env` shape — array** (`run("ralph", { env: [] as any })` — representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-53–53d shape-validation matrix); **(xiv) representative invalid `options.env` non-string entry value** (`run("ralph", { env: { KEY: 42 as any } })` — representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-54–54c entry-value-validation matrix); **(xv) throwing `options.env` proxy `ownKeys` trap** — construct `const env = new Proxy({}, { ownKeys() { throw new Error("ownKeys-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })`; **(xvi) target-resolution — missing workflow** (fixture has `.loopx/other/index.sh`; call `run("ralph")`); **(xvii) target-resolution — missing script in existing workflow** (fixture has `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`; call `run("ralph:check")`); **(xviii) target-resolution — missing default `index`** (fixture has `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` only; call bare `run("ralph")`); **(xix) invalid target syntax** — call `run("a:b:c")` (three-segment target violates the `<workflow>[:<script>]` grammar; this exercises the target-syntax-validation branch of SPEC 9.1's carve-out, distinct from T-API-68's `undefined`-target argument-validation case and from variants (xvi)–(xviii)'s post-syntax target-resolution branches); **(xx) throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env`** — construct `const env = {}; Object.defineProperty(env, "KEY", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })` (this exercises the "throwing enumerable getters inside `options.env`" sub-clause of SPEC 9.1's carve-out, distinct from variant (xv)'s proxy `ownKeys` trap and from T-API-68h's throwing getter on the **options object's `env` property** itself); **(xxi) throwing `options.env` proxy `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` trap** (observational on the trap-invocation axis) — construct `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { getOwnPropertyDescriptor() { throw new Error("descriptor-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })`. SPEC 9.1's carve-out covers "throwing `options.env` ... proxy traps" generally, but SPEC 9.5's snapshot-error enumeration names only the `ownKeys` trap explicitly; whether the descriptor trap is invoked during the snapshot is implementation-defined (see T-API-62f2). This variant exercises the carve-out only on implementations that invoke the descriptor trap and capture its throw — for those implementations, the captured exception must be suppressed under the carve-out. On implementations that do not invoke the descriptor trap, no exception is captured, the carve-out has nothing to suppress, and assertions (a)–(d) hold vacuously; **(xxii) throwing `options.env` proxy `get` trap** — construct `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { ownKeys() { return ["A"]; }, getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { return { enumerable: true, configurable: true, value: undefined, writable: true }; }, get() { throw new Error("get-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })` (extends proxy-trap suppression coverage to the value-read `get` trap; the `get` trap is necessarily invoked when loopx reads each entry's value, so this variant is a strict suppression pin — the captured snapshot exception must be suppressed under the carve-out, no vacuous-pass branch). Variant (xxi) is observational on the descriptor-trap-invocation axis; variant (xxii) is a strict suppression pin. For each variant, all four assertions (a)–(d) hold. The "no `loopx-*` tmpdir created" assertion (c) closes the negative observation that the carve-out short-circuited before tmpdir creation in every option-snapshot and target-resolution branch — a buggy implementation that performed tmpdir creation under the carve-out would fail this assertion in addition to whatever else it surfaced. **Variant (xvi)–(xviii) note:** these target-resolution sub-paths are distinct from the target-syntax-validation branch covered by variant (xix) and from T-API-68's argument-validation branch (which passed `undefined` as target). Together with T-API-68 and T-API-68a–68h, this test completes the `.return()` carve-out coverage across the SPEC 9.1 enumeration with exhaustive `maxIterations` coverage and representative env-shape coverage. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5, 7.1, 7.4, 4.1, 2.1)*
- **T-API-69**: `.throw()` as first interaction — generator settles by throwing (consumer-supplied) error. `const gen = run(undefined as any); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` The consumer's error surfaces, not the invalid-target error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-69a**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses env-file-load failure. `await gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))` where gen was created with invalid envFile. Consumer-error surfaces, not env-file error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-69b**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses pre-aborted signal. Consumer-error surfaces, no abort error. *(Spec 9.1)*
- **T-API-69c**: `.throw()` as first interaction does not create a tmpdir. *(Spec 9.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-69d**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses tmpdir-creation failure. Configure a tmpdir-creation failure by setting `TMPDIR` to an unwritable path (e.g., a parent directory with mode 000) so that `mkdtemp` would fail pre-iteration. `const gen = run("ralph"); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not the tmpdir-creation error), (b) no `loopx-*` tmpdir exists under the configured unwritable parent (the only parent loopx would have attempted under this `TMPDIR` snapshot — assertion is scoped to that parent, not a global `os.tmpdir()` scan, per the test-parent isolation guidance in section 4.7's tmpdir-block preface), and (c) no tmpdir-creation-failure error, warning, or other surfacing of the configured failure appears on stderr or via the rejected-promise / throw path — the pre-first-`next()` carve-out suppressed the configured tmpdir-creation failure entirely. (Whether loopx black-box "did not attempt `mkdtemp`" is not directly observable without a seam; assertions (b) and (c) together pin the observable outcome of suppression.) This is the `.throw()`-variant counterpart to T-API-68g (`.return()` carve-out vs tmpdir-creation failure), closing the tmpdir-creation-failure branch of SPEC 9.1's carve-out list for the `.throw()` surface too. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 9.1, 7.4)*
- **T-API-69e**: `.throw()` as first interaction surfaces the consumer-supplied error even when a throwing `options.env` getter would otherwise have produced a snapshot error. Companion to T-API-68h. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out says: for `.throw()` as the first interaction, the consumer's error surfaces (per standard async-generator semantics) and the captured snapshot errors — including throwing `options.env` getters — are suppressed. Construct an options object with a throwing `env` getter: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "env", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-env-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not `"evil-env-getter"` and not any snapshot error), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-env-getter"` error. This is the `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68h (`.return()` suppression of the same throwing getter), closing the throwing-`options.env`-getter branch of SPEC 9.1's carve-out list for the `.throw()` surface. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69f**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses `.loopx/` discovery failure. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "`.loopx/` discovery failures" among the suppressed pre-iteration errors. T-API-68a covers the `.return()` surface for this branch; this test is the symmetric `.throw()` counterpart, completing the discovery-failure coverage across both consumer-driven first-interaction surfaces. Set up a fixture without `.loopx/` (the standard missing-`.loopx/` condition from T-CLI-42 / T-DISC-10a). Drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not the missing-`.loopx/` discovery error), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created (snapshot the test-isolated tmpdir parent before / after; matches T-API-69c's no-tmpdir guarantee), and (d) stderr contains no missing-`.loopx/` discovery error or warning — the carve-out fully suppressed the discovery failure on the `.throw()` settlement, exactly mirroring T-API-68a's `.return()` suppression. A buggy implementation that surfaced the discovery error in addition to (or instead of) the consumer-supplied error would fail this test. *(Spec 9.1)*
