- **T-API-69g**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses target-resolution failures (missing workflow, missing script in existing workflow, missing default `index` for bare workflow target). SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "target resolution failures" among the suppressed pre-iteration errors; SPEC 7.1 step 3 enumerates three target-resolution sub-paths (missing workflow, missing script in an existing workflow, missing default entry point), and T-SIG-21 / T-SIG-27 / T-SIG-28 already cover all three on the CLI signal-wins surface and T-API-65h / T-API-65i / T-API-65j cover them on the abort-precedence surface. Existing `.return()` carve-out coverage (T-API-68 and friends) is largely structured around invalid-target / option / discovery / env-file / tmpdir branches but not the missing-workflow / missing-script / missing-index target-resolution sub-branches specifically. **Parameterize this test over the three target-resolution sub-paths**: (i) **missing workflow** — fixture with `.loopx/other/index.sh` (a different workflow), drive `run("ralph")`; (ii) **missing script in existing workflow** — fixture with `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` (workflow exists but no `check` script), drive `run("ralph:check")`; (iii) **missing default `index`** — fixture with `.loopx/ralph/check.sh` only (no `index.*`), drive bare `run("ralph")`. For each variant, drive `await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert (per variant): (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not the target-resolution error of any of the three sub-paths), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr contains no target-resolution error or warning of the corresponding sub-path. This is the `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68's invalid-target suppression but on the **target-resolution** branch (post-syntax, pre-spawn) rather than the syntax-validation branch — pinning down that all three SPEC 7.1 target-resolution sub-paths are suppressed under the carve-out for `.throw()`. Together with T-API-69 (invalid-target syntax — the implicit "target arg validation" branch of the carve-out) and T-API-69f (`.loopx/` discovery), this closes the `.throw()`-side coverage for the entire SPEC 9.1 carve-out list across consumer-driven first interactions, mirroring the existing `.return()` coverage. *(Spec 9.1, 7.1, 4.1, 2.1)*
- **T-API-69i**: `.throw(err)` as first interaction surfaces the consumer-supplied error even when an abort fires on a **live** signal between `run()` returning and the consumer's `.throw()`. `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68j. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out: "A `signal` captured at call time is not consulted; even an already-aborted signal does not surface the abort error through this path." T-API-69b covers the **already-aborted-at-call-time** case on the `.throw()` surface (no listener-firing transition); this test covers the **live-signal-aborts-after-call-time** case for `.throw()`, mirroring T-API-68j on the throw surface. The signal is **not aborted** at call time, the consumer aborts it **after** `run()` returns and **before** invoking `.throw()` (so the captured signal's abort listener actually fires on a transition observed by loopx), and the consumer's first interaction is `.throw(err)`. The carve-out must still apply: the consumer-supplied error surfaces per standard async-generator `.throw()` semantics, **not** the abort error that fired on the captured signal. Construct: `const c = new AbortController(); const gen = run("ralph", { signal: c.signal }); c.abort(); /* listener fires synchronously */ await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces as the thrown / rejected error — **not** an abort error and **not** a combined error wrapping both, (b) no child is spawned (assertion-scoped to the test fixture), and (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir is created under the test-isolated tmpdir parent (per the section 4.7 tmpdir-block preface). Together with T-API-68j (`.return(value)` counterpart), this test pins down the stronger "captured signal is not consulted *even when its state transitioned during the carve-out window*" reading on both consumer-driven first-interaction surfaces. A buggy implementation that surfaced the abort error in addition to (or instead of) `"my-err"` because the listener fired during the carve-out window would fail (a). *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69j**: `.throw()` as first interaction surfaces the consumer-supplied error even when a throwing `options.signal` getter would otherwise have produced a snapshot error. Companion to T-API-68k. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "throwing option-field getters" — including the `signal` getter, which SPEC 9.1 reads first — among the snapshot errors that are suppressed when the consumer's first interaction is `.throw()`. T-API-69e covers the throwing `options.env` getter; this test pins down the throwing-`signal`-getter branch on the `.throw()` surface. Construct an options object with a throwing `signal` getter: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "signal", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-signal-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not `"evil-signal-getter"` and not any snapshot error), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-signal-getter"` error. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69k**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.cwd` getter. Pinned-priority option per SPEC 9.3. Companion to T-API-68l on the `.throw()` surface. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "cwd", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-cwd-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not `"evil-cwd-getter"`), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-cwd-getter"` error. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69l**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.envFile` getter. Pinned-priority option per SPEC 9.3. Companion to T-API-68m on the `.throw()` surface. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "envFile", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-envFile-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not `"evil-envFile-getter"`), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-envFile-getter"` error. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69m**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses a throwing `options.maxIterations` getter. Companion to T-API-68n on the `.throw()` surface. Construct: `const opts = {}; Object.defineProperty(opts, "maxIterations", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("evil-maxIterations-getter"); } });` Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", opts); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not `"evil-maxIterations-getter"`), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the `"evil-maxIterations-getter"` error. Together with T-API-69e / T-API-69j / T-API-69k / T-API-69l, this completes throwing-getter coverage for every pinned `RunOptions` field — `signal`, `cwd`, `envFile`, `maxIterations`, `env` — under the `.throw()` carve-out, mirroring T-API-68k / T-API-68l / T-API-68m / T-API-68n / T-API-68h on the `.return()` surface. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5)*
- **T-API-69n**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses an **unreadable global env file** failure. Companion to T-API-68o on the `.throw()` surface. Set up an isolated `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` with `loopx/env` written and `chmod 000`'d (the same fixture as T-API-68o / T-API-65r / T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable). A valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not the global-env-file-read error), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) stderr does not surface the global-env-file-read error. Together with T-API-68o (`.return()` carve-out for the same failure mode), T-API-65r (abort-precedence), and T-SIG-30 (CLI signal-wins), this closes the global-env-unreadable sub-path on the `.throw()` carve-out surface. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-69o**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses **local env-file parser warnings**. `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68p. SPEC 9.1's "no pre-iteration step runs" clause must apply on the `.throw()` surface as well: the consumer's error surfaces per standard async-generator semantics and the env-file parse step never runs, so no SPEC 8.1 invalid-key warning fires. Set up: a project root with a valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Write a local env file (e.g., `<test-dir>/local.env`) with content `1BAD=val\nOK=fine\n` (the `1BAD` line is the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key warning trigger). Drive: `const gen = run("ralph", { envFile: "<test-dir>/local.env" }); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not any env-file load or parse error), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) **stderr does not contain the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning for `1BAD`** — the env-file parse step never ran. A buggy implementation that ran the env-file parse step before checking whether the consumer had already invoked `.throw()` would fail (d). Together with T-API-68p (`.return()` counterpart) and T-API-69a (missing-file failure on `.throw()`), this closes the local env-file warning-suppression coverage across both consumer-driven first-interaction surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 8.2, 7.1)*
- **T-API-69p**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses **global env-file parser warnings**. `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68q. Set up: an isolated `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (e.g., `<test-dir>/xdg-config`) with `loopx/env` written and containing `1BAD=val\nOK=fine\n`; the `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is injected into loopx's inherited environment (same injection pattern as T-API-68q / T-API-68o / T-TMP-12-global-env-unreadable). A valid `.loopx/ralph/index.sh`. Drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces, (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) **stderr does not contain the SPEC 8.1 invalid-key parser warning for `1BAD`** — the global-env-file parse step never ran. Together with T-API-68q (`.return()` counterpart) and T-API-69n (global-env-unreadable failure on `.throw()`), this closes the global env-file warning-suppression branch of SPEC 9.1's "no pre-iteration step runs" clause across both consumer-driven first-interaction surfaces. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1, 7.1)*
- **T-API-69q**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses **workflow `package.json` warnings** from the starting-workflow version check. `.throw()` counterpart to T-API-68r. **Parameterize over the four SPEC 3.2 warning branches** (unsatisfied range, invalid JSON, invalid semver range, unreadable `package.json`) using the same per-variant fixtures as T-API-68r. For each variant, drive: `const gen = run("ralph"); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` Assert (per variant): (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces (not any version-check or `package.json`-related error or warning chained onto it), (b) no child spawned, (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir created, and (d) **stderr does not contain the `package.json` warning text** for that variant. Variant (iv) (unreadable) is **conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`**. Together with T-API-68r (`.return()` counterpart), this closes the workflow `package.json` warning-suppression branch of SPEC 9.1's "no pre-iteration step runs" clause across both consumer-driven first-interaction surfaces and across all four SPEC 3.2 `package.json` failure-mode warning branches. *(Spec 9.1, 7.1, 3.2)*
- **T-API-69h**: `.throw()` as first interaction suppresses **the full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set plus a representative selection of remaining option-snapshot failures**. SPEC 9.1's pre-first-`next()` carve-out enumerates "captured option-snapshot errors (invalid `options` values, invalid `options.signal`, non-string `options.cwd` / `options.envFile`, invalid `options.maxIterations`, invalid `options.env` shape, throwing option-field getters, throwing `options.env` enumerable getters or proxy traps)" among the suppressed pre-iteration errors. T-API-69e covers the throwing `options.env` getter case; this test extends coverage to (i) the **full enumerated invalid `maxIterations` set** (every value treated as invalid in the validation matrix at T-API-22 etc., parameterized exhaustively) and (ii) a **representative selection** of the remaining option-snapshot families (invalid `options` shape, invalid `options.signal`, invalid `cwd` / `envFile` types, representative invalid `env` shape and entry value, throwing `env` entry getter, throwing `env` proxy traps) — in a single parameterized test, mirroring T-API-68i but for the `.throw()` surface. The full invalid `env` shape/value matrix (every shape from T-API-53–53d and every entry-value variant from T-API-54–54c) is intentionally **not** exhaustively re-parameterized here — those validation paths converge on a single "captured option-snapshot error" code path that the carve-out must suppress, and the representative sample is sufficient to detect a buggy implementation that bypassed the carve-out on the option-snapshot category; T-API-53–55 and T-API-55a–55d carry the exhaustive env-shape coverage without carve-out pinning. **Target-resolution failures are not parameterized here** — they are already covered by T-API-69g, which keeps the parameterized target-resolution test separate from the option-snapshot test on the `.throw()` surface. **Parameterize over each variant**, set up the relevant fixture, drive `const gen = run("ralph", <opts>); await expect(gen.throw(new Error("my-err"))).rejects.toThrow("my-err");` and assert per variant: (a) the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` surfaces — not the configured option-snapshot error, (b) no child spawned (no marker file written), (c) no `loopx-*` tmpdir is created (assertion is scoped to the test-isolated tmpdir parent per the section 4.7 tmpdir-block preface), and (d) stderr does not surface the configured option-snapshot error. **Variants:** **(i) invalid `options` shape — `null`** (`run("ralph", null as any)`); **(ii) invalid `options` shape — array** (`run("ralph", [] as any)`); **(iii) invalid `options` shape — function** (`run("ralph", (() => {}) as any)`); **(iv) invalid `options` shape — primitive number** (`run("ralph", 42 as any)`); **(v) invalid `options.signal` — non-`AbortSignal`-shaped value** (`run("ralph", { signal: "not-a-signal" as any })`); **(vi) non-string `options.cwd`** (`run("ralph", { cwd: 42 as any })`); **(vii) non-string `options.envFile`** (`run("ralph", { envFile: 42 as any })`); **(viii) invalid `options.maxIterations: -1`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: -1 })`); **(ix) invalid `options.maxIterations: 1.5`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1.5 })` — non-integer); **(x) invalid `options.maxIterations: NaN`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: NaN })`); **(xi) invalid `options.maxIterations: Infinity`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: Infinity })` — non-finite); **(xii) invalid `options.maxIterations: null as any`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: null as any })` — null is not a finite non-negative integer); **(xiii) invalid `options.maxIterations: "1" as any`** (`run("ralph", { maxIterations: "1" as any })` — string value rejected at the type-shape gate, distinct from the numeric-range gates of (viii)–(xii)); **(xiv) representative invalid `options.env` shape — array** (`run("ralph", { env: [] as any })` — representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-53–53d shape-validation matrix); **(xv) representative invalid `options.env` non-string entry value** (`run("ralph", { env: { KEY: 42 as any } })` — representative sample of the SPEC 9.5 / T-API-54–54c entry-value-validation matrix); **(xvi) throwing `options.env` proxy `ownKeys` trap** — construct `const env = new Proxy({}, { ownKeys() { throw new Error("ownKeys-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })`; **(xvii) invalid target syntax** — call `run("a:b:c")` (three-segment target violates the `<workflow>[:<script>]` grammar; this exercises the target-syntax-validation branch of SPEC 9.1's carve-out, distinct from T-API-69's `undefined`-target argument-validation case and from T-API-69g's post-syntax target-resolution branches); **(xviii) throwing enumerable getter inside `options.env`** — construct `const env = {}; Object.defineProperty(env, "KEY", { enumerable: true, get() { throw new Error("env-entry-getter-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })` (this exercises the "throwing enumerable getters inside `options.env`" sub-clause of SPEC 9.1's carve-out, distinct from variant (xvi)'s proxy `ownKeys` trap and from T-API-69e's throwing getter on the **options object's `env` property** itself); **(xix) throwing `options.env` proxy `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` trap** (observational on the trap-invocation axis) — construct `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { getOwnPropertyDescriptor() { throw new Error("descriptor-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })`. As with T-API-68i variant (xxi), SPEC does not require the descriptor trap to be invoked during the snapshot (see T-API-62f2). This variant exercises the carve-out only on implementations that invoke the descriptor trap and capture its throw — for those implementations, the captured exception must be suppressed and the consumer-supplied error must surface. On implementations that do not invoke the descriptor trap, no snapshot exception is captured, and the consumer-supplied error surfaces as it would on any other non-throwing-snapshot path; **(xx) throwing `options.env` proxy `get` trap** — construct `const env = new Proxy({ A: "a" }, { ownKeys() { return ["A"]; }, getOwnPropertyDescriptor(t, key) { return { enumerable: true, configurable: true, value: undefined, writable: true }; }, get() { throw new Error("get-trap-boom"); } });` and call `run("ralph", { env })` (extends proxy-trap suppression coverage to the value-read `get` trap; the `get` trap is necessarily invoked when loopx reads each entry's value, so this variant is a strict suppression pin — the captured snapshot exception must be suppressed and the consumer-supplied error must surface). Variant (xix) is observational on the descriptor-trap-invocation axis; variant (xx) is a strict suppression pin. For each variant, all four assertions (a)–(d) hold and the consumer-supplied `"my-err"` is the surfaced rejection reason — not any captured snapshot error. A buggy implementation that surfaced the captured snapshot error (or any combined error) instead of the consumer-supplied error would fail (a). An implementation that performed tmpdir creation under the carve-out before settling would fail (c). Together with T-API-69 (target-argument validation), T-API-69a (env-file), T-API-69b (pre-aborted signal), T-API-69c (no-tmpdir negative observation), T-API-69d (tmpdir-creation), T-API-69e (throwing getter on `options.env` property), T-API-69f (discovery), and T-API-69g (target-resolution), this test completes the `.throw()` carve-out coverage across the SPEC 9.1 enumeration with exhaustive `maxIterations` coverage and representative env-shape coverage. *(Spec 9.1, 9.5, 7.4)*
- **T-API-70**: `runPromise` has no carve-out. `runPromise(undefined as any, { envFile: "nonexistent.env" })` always rejects; there is no `.return()` / `.throw()` path on a promise. Explicitly documents Spec 9.1 "This carve-out applies only to `run()`; `runPromise()` has no equivalent." The test is a pair of "both rejections happen" assertions on `runPromise` for each invalid input, matching T-API-68a/68b/68c/68d. *(Spec 9.2)*

##### Snapshot Timing (Inherited Env)

- **T-API-71**: `run()` inherited-env snapshot is lazy (captured at first `next()`). Set `process.env.MYVAR=A`. `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 });` — no snapshot yet. Set `process.env.MYVAR=B` **before** `gen.next()`. Await `.next()`. Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert marker shows `"B"` — the mutation between `run()` returning and first `next()` is observed. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1)*
- **T-API-71a**: `run()` inherited-env snapshot is frozen at first `next()` (later mutations not observed). Set `process.env.MYVAR=A`. `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 2 }); await gen.next();` (first iteration captures `"A"`). Set `process.env.MYVAR=B`. `await gen.next();` (second iteration). Assert both iterations saw `"A"`. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1)*
- **T-API-72**: `runPromise()` inherited-env snapshot is eager (captured at call site). Set `process.env.MYVAR=A`. `const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 });` — snapshot taken synchronously. Set `process.env.MYVAR=B`. Await `p`. Script observes `MYVAR`. Assert marker shows `"A"` — mutation after return did not propagate. *(Spec 9.2, 8.1)*
- **T-API-72a**: `runPromise()` inherited-env snapshot is **reused across iterations** — the eager snapshot taken at the call site applies to every spawn in the run, not just the first. T-API-72 proves the snapshot is captured synchronously before `runPromise()` returns for a single-iteration run, but it does not prove the snapshot is reused for the *second* spawn in a multi-iteration run. A buggy implementation that captured the inherited env eagerly at the call site (passing T-API-72) but re-read `process.env` between iterations (re-snapshotting before each spawn) would propagate mid-run mutations to later iterations and silently violate SPEC 8.1's "inherited `process.env` is snapshotted once per run ... mutations between spawns do not propagate". This test pins down the cross-iteration reuse contract on the `runPromise()` surface. **Setup.** Set `process.env.MYVAR = "A"`. Compute a unique release-file path outside any tmpdir (e.g., `<test-tmp>/release-<uuid>.flag`) and an external observation-marker path per-iteration (so observations survive cleanup). `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` reads `OBSERVED_MARKER_PREFIX` and `RELEASE_FILE` from its environment, observes `MYVAR` via `observe-env` into `${OBSERVED_MARKER_PREFIX}-iter-${ITER}.json` (where `ITER` is derived from a counter file the script maintains on disk under the marker prefix — first iteration writes "1", second writes "2"), then on the **first** iteration only, writes `"ready"` to a coordination marker and **blocks on a sentinel** by polling for `RELEASE_FILE` to exist (e.g., `until [ -e "$RELEASE_FILE" ]; do sleep 0.05; done`) before exiting cleanly with `{}` (no `goto`, no `stop` — the loop resets to the starting target for iteration 2). On the second iteration, the script observes `MYVAR`, writes its marker, and emits `{"stop":true}` to terminate. **Drive.** Pass the per-run paths via `RunOptions.env` (`{ env: { OBSERVED_MARKER_PREFIX: "<abs-path>", RELEASE_FILE: "<abs-path>" } }` — note: this puts the *paths* in `RunOptions.env`, not the test variable `MYVAR`, which must come from inherited env to exercise the inherited-env snapshot path). Call `const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 2, env: { OBSERVED_MARKER_PREFIX, RELEASE_FILE } })`. The harness then waits (via the iter-1 marker file's appearance) for the first iteration to complete its observation and reach the sentinel-poll, **mutates** `process.env.MYVAR = "B"`, and creates the release file (e.g., `fs.writeFileSync(RELEASE_FILE, "")`) so the first iteration's blocking-poll exits and the script terminates. The second iteration spawns; the loop reset re-runs `ralph:index`. Await `p`. **Assert.** (a) the promise resolves (no rejection), (b) the iter-1 marker records `MYVAR === "A"` (the eager snapshot was used for the first spawn), (c) the iter-2 marker records `MYVAR === "A"` — **the same snapshot was reused for the second spawn even though `process.env.MYVAR` was mutated to `"B"` between iterations** — proving SPEC 8.1's "snapshotted once per run ... mutations between spawns do not propagate" rule on the `runPromise()` surface, and (d) `process.env.MYVAR === "B"` in the harness after the promise settles (the harness's mutation took effect in its own process; the assertion (c) is solely about whether that mutation propagated to the spawned scripts). The complementary `run()`-surface test for cross-iteration snapshot reuse is T-API-71a (set `MYVAR=A`, drive `gen.next()` twice with `MYVAR=B` between, assert both iterations observed `"A"`); T-API-71a relies on the consumer to drive iterations via successive `next()` calls, but `runPromise()` has no such consumer-driven boundary — the harness must use a release-file sentinel to synchronize the mid-run mutation with iteration boundaries. *(Spec 9.2, 8.1)*

##### Global Env File Resolution Snapshot Timing (`XDG_CONFIG_HOME` / `HOME`)

SPEC 8.1 says global env-file path resolution (`$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/loopx/env`, or `$HOME/.config/loopx/env` fallback) reads `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` / `HOME` from the inherited environment on the **same schedule as the inherited-env snapshot**: lazy under `run()` (captured at first `next()`), eager under `runPromise()` (captured at the call site). T-API-71 / T-API-71a / T-API-72 cover inherited-env snapshot timing but do not exercise the `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` / `HOME` resolution path specifically — a buggy implementation could snapshot `process.env` lazily but read `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` / `HOME` eagerly (or vice versa).

- **T-API-73**: `run()` — `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` resolution is lazy (captured at first `next()`). Create two isolated config directories, `/tmp/xdg-A/loopx/env` (with `MARKER=from-A`) and `/tmp/xdg-B/loopx/env` (with `MARKER=from-B`). Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME = "/tmp/xdg-A"`. Call `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })`. Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME = "/tmp/xdg-B"` **before** `await gen.next()`. Script observes `MARKER`. Assert the marker shows `"from-B"` — the mutation between `run()` returning and first `next()` redirected global env-file lookup. A buggy implementation that read `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` eagerly would show `"from-A"`. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1)*
- **T-API-73a**: `runPromise()` — `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` resolution is eager (captured at call site). Same two-config setup as T-API-73. Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME = "/tmp/xdg-A"`. Call `const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })` — snapshot taken synchronously. Set `process.env.XDG_CONFIG_HOME = "/tmp/xdg-B"`. Await `p`. Script observes `MARKER`. Assert the marker shows `"from-A"` — mutation after return did not redirect global env-file lookup. *(Spec 9.2, 8.1)*
- **T-API-73b**: `run()` — `HOME` fallback resolution is lazy (captured at first `next()`). Unset `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` in `process.env` so loopx falls back to `$HOME/.config/loopx/env`. Create two isolated home directories, `/tmp/home-A/.config/loopx/env` (with `MARKER=from-A`) and `/tmp/home-B/.config/loopx/env` (with `MARKER=from-B`). Set `process.env.HOME = "/tmp/home-A"`. Call `const gen = run("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })`. Set `process.env.HOME = "/tmp/home-B"` before `await gen.next()`. Script observes `MARKER`. Assert the marker shows `"from-B"` — the `HOME` mutation between `run()` returning and first `next()` redirected global env-file lookup via the fallback path. *(Spec 9.1, 8.1)*
- **T-API-73c**: `runPromise()` — `HOME` fallback resolution is eager (captured at call site). Same two-home setup as T-API-73b. Set `process.env.HOME = "/tmp/home-A"`, unset `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`. Call `const p = runPromise("ralph", { maxIterations: 1 })`. Set `process.env.HOME = "/tmp/home-B"`. Await `p`. Script observes `MARKER`. Assert the marker shows `"from-A"` — mutation after return did not affect the fallback path. *(Spec 9.2, 8.1)*

### 4.10 Install Command

**Spec refs:** 10.1–10.10

All install tests use local servers (HTTP, file:// git repos). No network access.

**Auto-install-awareness rule for install tests.** SPEC 10.10 means any successful install of a workflow with a top-level `package.json` will run `npm install` by default and synthesize `.gitignore`. Tests outside the auto-install block (`Auto-install of Workflow Dependencies`, T-INST-110+) often install workflows whose sources happen to contain a top-level `package.json` (because version-checking, classification, or atomicity scenarios commonly use `package.json` to declare a `loopx` range or to populate workflow content). A conforming implementation will invoke real or fake `npm` for those workflows, which can make non-auto-install tests flaky, slow, or wrong (especially when the workflow declares an unsatisfied `loopx` range, or when no fake-`npm` shim is installed and the test harness has no real `npm` on a scrubbed `PATH`).

**Rule (suite-wide).** Unless a test is explicitly exercising SPEC 10.10 auto-install behavior, **any test in this document — anywhere, in any section — that invokes `loopx install`** against a source whose selected workflows are actually committed and contain a top-level `package.json` must either pass `--no-install` or wrap the command with `withFakeNpm` (section 2.3). The rule is **not scoped to section 4.10**: it also covers install invocations in section 4.12 (CLI delegation × install-time version checking — e.g., T-DEL-25), section 4.13 (workflow-level version checking install-time variants — the T-VER-12*, T-VER-13*, T-VER-15*, T-VER-17 family), and any future section that introduces an `loopx install` invocation. The rule applies only to tests that reach the post-commit auto-install pass; tests where install fails before any commit (e.g., blocking preflight failures like a version-mismatch refusal — see T-INST-72 / T-INST-72a) have no auto-install candidate and do not need `--no-install`, though they may pass it harmlessly. Prefer `--no-install` for classification, copy, collision, atomicity, delegation, and version-check tests where auto-install is incidental. Tests in the T-INST-110 through T-INST-120b auto-install block intentionally omit `--no-install` and use `withFakeNpm` because auto-install is the contract under test. Specifically:

- Classification and copy tests with workflow-internal `package.json`: pass `--no-install` (e.g., T-INST-50, T-INST-54a, T-INST-83a).
- Collision tests using sources that include a `package.json` (e.g., T-INST-67/T-INST-67a/T-INST-70-family when the source workflow has one): pass `--no-install`.
- Install atomicity tests with workflow-internal `package.json` (e.g., T-INST-80): pass `--no-install`.
- Install-time `package.json` warning-cardinality tests with committed workflows (T-INST-80d, T-INST-80e, T-INST-80f): pass `--no-install`. These tests live in the install-atomicity block by ID range but their contract is the install-time `package.json` warning behavior described in SPEC 3.2 / 10.6 — "once per affected workflow" — for unreadable, invalid-semver, and invalid-JSON `package.json` content respectively. Each test installs two workflows that successfully commit, and each workflow has a top-level `package.json` that would otherwise be considered for the post-commit auto-install pass; the auto-install pass is incidental and must not run in these tests.
- Install-time version-check tests where the test's selected workflows actually commit (e.g., T-INST-73/T-INST-73a, T-INST-60j, T-INST-74/T-INST-74a, T-INST-75/T-INST-75a, T-INST-76/T-INST-76a, T-INST-60q/T-INST-60r/T-INST-60s — these install successfully despite warnings, or override version-mismatch via `-y`): pass `--no-install`. The test's purpose is the version-check / parse-warning behavior, not the auto-install pass; T-INST-120b separately covers the `-y`-overrides-version-mismatch × auto-install combination with `withFakeNpm`. **Blocking-preflight version-check tests** that reject install before any commit (e.g., T-INST-72, T-INST-72a, T-INST-60i — version-mismatch error, no `-y`): the rule does not apply because no commit means no auto-install candidate; passing `--no-install` is unnecessary (and the tests' bodies note this explicitly).
- `-y` × version-mismatch tests where the auto-install-after-`-y` interaction is **not** the contract under test (e.g., T-INST-60j): pass `--no-install`.
- **Cross-section install tests outside section 4.10.** The same `--no-install`-or-`withFakeNpm` requirement applies. Concretely: **T-DEL-25** (section 4.12 — delegation × install-time version checking; the source workflow declares a satisfied `loopx` range in its `package.json`, which would otherwise drive the post-commit auto-install pass) must pass `--no-install`. The install-time version-check tests in section 4.13 — **T-VER-12** (satisfied-range install sub-case), **T-VER-12a / 12b / 12c** (where auto-install is incidental to the version-check / `package.json`-shape behavior), **T-VER-13 / 13a / 13b / 13d** (selective `optionalDependencies` / `peerDependencies` / non-`loopx` field cases that install successfully), the `-y` success sub-cases inside **T-VER-13c** and **T-VER-15a** (`-y` unblocks the mismatch and the post-commit auto-install pass would otherwise run), **T-VER-15b** (satisfied-range `devDependencies`-only install), and **T-VER-17** (no `loopx` declared, install succeeds) — all must pass `--no-install` because their contract is the version-check / `package.json`-shape behavior, not auto-install. **T-VER-15c remains as-is** with `withFakeNpm` because that test explicitly asserts the SPEC 10.10 malformed-`package.json` auto-install-skip contract (the fake-npm shim's empty log is the assertion surface for "auto-install was skipped"). **T-INST-120b** also remains as-is for the same reason it always has — it intentionally uses `withFakeNpm` to assert the `-y`-then-auto-install combination. The principle: any `loopx install` outside the `withFakeNpm`-wrapped tests in 4.10 / 4.12 / 4.13 must use `--no-install` whenever the source has a top-level `package.json`, because conforming SPEC 10.10 auto-install behavior would otherwise spawn a real or fake `npm` and make the test flaky, slow, or PATH-dependent.

T-INST-120b remains the canonical "`-y` unblocks preflight, then auto-install runs fresh" test and intentionally uses `withFakeNpm` (no `--no-install`). Tests that use `withFakeNpm` already inhibit real-npm flakiness; they do not also need `--no-install`.

**Affected existing tests.** The rule applies unconditionally to every test in this document that invokes `loopx install` and whose source has a top-level `package.json`. Where the test's source description above already lists a workflow `package.json`, treat that test as passing `--no-install` even if the test text predates this rule. This includes **T-DEL-25** in section 4.12 and the install-time version-check tests in section 4.13 enumerated in the bullet above. The auto-install block (T-INST-110 through T-INST-120b) and **T-VER-15c** use `withFakeNpm` and do not pass `--no-install`.

#### Source Detection

- **T-INST-01**: `loopx install myorg/my-workflow` is treated as a git source (github shorthand). Verify by using `withGitURLRewrite` to redirect to a local bare repo, and asserting workflows are installed into `.loopx/`. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-01a**: `loopx install myorg/my-workflow.git` → error, exit code 1. The shorthand must not end in `.git`. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-02**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo` → treated as git (known host). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-03**: `loopx install https://gitlab.com/org/repo` → treated as git. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-04**: `loopx install https://bitbucket.org/org/repo` → treated as git. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-05**: `loopx install https://example.com/repo.git` → treated as git (.git suffix). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-05a**: `loopx install https://example.com/repo.git/` (trailing slash on a non-known-host `.git` URL) → rejected as an unsupported source. SPEC 10.1 rule 2 explicitly permits an optional trailing slash on the `/<owner>/<repo>.git` pathname for **known-host** URLs (`github.com`, `gitlab.com`, `bitbucket.org` — covered for the trailing-slash variant by T-INST-08h). SPEC 10.1 rule 3 says "Any **other** URL ending in `.git` is treated as a git source" — a URL whose pathname ends in `.git/` (with trailing slash) does **not** end in `.git` per the literal text of rule 3, so the generic non-known-host fallback does not classify it as git. The URL also does not end in `.tar.gz` / `.tgz`, so rule 4 (tarball) does not match. The URL therefore falls through to rule 5 (rejection). T-INST-05 covers the no-trailing-slash form on a non-known-host (accepted by rule 3); T-INST-08h covers the trailing-slash form on a **known** host (accepted by rule 2 with the explicit optional-trailing-slash clause). This test pins down the asymmetry between rule 2 (explicit trailing-slash allowance) and rule 3 (no trailing-slash allowance), which would otherwise be discoverable only by inspecting the parser. Run `loopx install https://example.com/repo.git/`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr references the source-detection rejection (not a downstream git/tarball failure — the rejection fires at source classification, before any network operation), and (c) no `git clone` invocation is recorded against `https://example.com/repo.git/` (verifiable via `withFakeGit` showing zero clone invocations for this URL, or by relying on the pre-network exit-1 surface). A permissive implementation that normalized away a trailing slash before applying rule 3 would wrongly accept this URL — this test catches that relaxation. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-06**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz` → treated as tarball. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-07**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tgz` → treated as tarball. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/script.ts` → rejected (single-file URL install is not supported). Error, exit code 1. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08a**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo/archive/main.tar.gz` → treated as tarball (not git). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08c**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo/` → treated as git (trailing slash allowed). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08d**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz?token=abc` → treated as tarball. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08e**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/some-file.js` → rejected. Any URL that is not git or tarball is an error. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08f**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo/tree/main` → rejected. Known-host git detection only matches exact `/<owner>/<repo>` or `/<owner>/<repo>.git` paths. A URL with additional path segments (like `/tree/main`) is not treated as a git repo. Since it is also not a tarball URL, it is rejected with an error (exit code 1). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08g**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo?x=1` → treated as git (known host, query string does not alter source classification). Per SPEC 10.1 rule 2, known-host git detection matches on the URL **pathname** and is unaffected by query string or fragment. **Assert classification and install behavior, not an exact clone URL spelling.** SPEC 10.1 pins down *classification* (the URL is a git source), but does **not** specify whether the query string (or fragment) is retained or stripped before the URL is passed to `git clone` — both normalized forms (preserving `?x=1` vs. stripping it) are SPEC-conforming. A buggy implementation that misclassified the query-bearing URL as a tarball or rejected it outright would fail; a conforming implementation that normalizes the URL differently than the test harness expects must still pass. To exercise this without over-pinning the clone-URL spelling, use `withFakeGit` (section 2.3) to capture loopx's actual `git clone` argv, and configure `withGitURLRewrite` to map **both** possible normalized forms (`https://github.com/org/repo` and `https://github.com/org/repo?x=1`) to the same local bare repo — this way loopx's choice of either normalization succeeds. Run `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo?x=1` and assert: (a) the source was classified as git (via the `withFakeGit` log showing a `git clone` invocation, not a tarball download), (b) the clone argv contains a URL whose pathname is `/org/repo` (the SPEC-pinned classification surface — query string / fragment presence is not asserted), (c) the workflow was installed into `.loopx/<repo>/`, and (d) exit code 0. Query-string counterpart to T-INST-02 (bare known-host URL). Together with T-INST-08c (trailing slash), this pins down that both of the "same-path" URL variants (`?…` and `/`) classify as git on a known host. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08g2**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo#section` → treated as git (known host, fragment does not alter source classification). Per SPEC 10.1 rule 2, known-host git detection matches on the URL pathname and is unaffected by query string or fragment — the pathname `/org/repo` classifies as git regardless of what follows `#`. Fragment counterpart to T-INST-08g (query string). **Assert classification and install behavior, not an exact clone URL spelling**, for the same reason as T-INST-08g: SPEC 10.1 does not specify whether the fragment is retained or stripped before `git clone`. Use `withFakeGit` to capture loopx's `git clone` argv and configure `withGitURLRewrite` to map **both** possible normalized forms (`https://github.com/org/repo` and `https://github.com/org/repo#section`) to the same local bare repo. Run `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo#section` and assert: (a) the source was classified as git (via the `withFakeGit` log showing a `git clone` invocation), (b) the clone argv contains a URL whose pathname is `/org/repo` (the SPEC-pinned classification surface — fragment presence is not asserted), (c) the workflow was installed into `.loopx/<repo>/`, and (d) exit code 0. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08h**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo.git/` → treated as git (known host, `.git` pathname with trailing slash). Per SPEC 10.1 rule 2, the pathname `/<owner>/<repo>.git` with optional trailing slash is the explicit `.git`-form of the known-host rule. T-INST-05 covers the generic `.git`-suffix rule (rule 3), T-INST-02 covers the no-`.git` known-host form, and T-INST-08c covers the trailing-slash variant without `.git`; this test closes the `.git/`-with-trailing-slash variant on a known host. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08i**: `loopx install org/repo/extra` → rejected with an error. The `org/repo` shorthand matches only exactly one slash with no additional path segments; this input is not shorthand and is not a supported URL source. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08j**: `loopx install org/repo/` → rejected with an error. SPEC 10.1 describes the `org/repo` shorthand as "exactly one slash and no extra path segments"; a trailing slash adds an empty-string path segment that does not match the single-slash shorthand shape. T-INST-08c covers the URL-form `https://github.com/org/repo/` (which *is* accepted because the known-host URL rule permits trailing slash), but shorthand and URL-form are distinct grammars — pinning trailing-slash acceptance for one does not constrain the other. T-INST-08i covers the `org/repo/<extra>` non-empty-extra-segment case; this test covers the `org/repo/` empty-extra-segment case. Assert: (a) exit code 1, and (b) stderr references the invalid source / shorthand rejection. *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08k**: `loopx install org//repo` → rejected with an error. The `org/repo` shorthand requires a single slash separating two non-empty segments; `org//repo` has two slashes (or, equivalently, an empty middle segment). A permissive implementation that split on `/` and ignored empty segments would wrongly accept this as `org/repo` — this test catches that relaxation. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr references the invalid source / shorthand rejection, and (c) no git clone attempt is made against a `github.com/org/repo` URL (verifiable via `withFakeGit` log being empty of clone invocations for the rewritten URL, or by simply relying on the fact that exit 1 is raised at source detection before any network/git operation). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08l**: `loopx install https://github.com/org` (one path segment only) → rejected. SPEC 10.1 rule 2 requires the URL pathname to be exactly `/<owner>/<repo>` or `/<owner>/<repo>.git` (optionally trailing slash). A pathname with a single segment `/org` does not match either form. Existing T-INST-08f covers the "extra path segments after `/<owner>/<repo>`" rejection (`/org/repo/tree/main`); this test covers the symmetric "missing `<repo>` segment" rejection. A permissive implementation that accepted any github.com URL without enforcing the `/<owner>/<repo>` shape would wrongly try to clone `https://github.com/org` and fail later in the pipeline (or worse, succeed against an unintended target). Run `loopx install https://github.com/org`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr references the source-detection / unsupported-URL rejection (not a generic clone failure — the rejection must fire at source classification, before any git operation), and (c) no `git clone` attempt is made (verifiable via `withFakeGit` showing zero clone invocations, or by relying on the pre-network exit-1 surface). *(Spec 10.1)*
- **T-INST-08m**: `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo.git/extra` → rejected. SPEC 10.1 rule 2 specifies the pathname must be exactly `/<owner>/<repo>` or `/<owner>/<repo>.git` (optionally trailing slash). A pathname with **additional** path segments after `.git` (e.g., `/org/repo.git/extra`) does not match — it is neither a known-host git URL (rule 2 fails) nor classifiable as a tarball (rule 4 requires `.tar.gz`/`.tgz`). T-INST-08f covers the no-`.git` extra-segments rejection (`/org/repo/tree/main`); this test covers the `.git`-with-extra-segments rejection — a distinct branch of the parser since the `.git` suffix could be a tempting normalization target. A permissive implementation that stripped `.git` and then accepted the resulting pathname, or that strip-suffixed `.git` and then ignored trailing segments, would wrongly accept this URL. Run `loopx install https://github.com/org/repo.git/extra`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr references the source-detection rejection (not a downstream git failure), and (c) no `git clone` attempt is made. Together with T-INST-08l (missing-segment rejection), T-INST-08f (extra-segments-without-`.git` rejection), and T-INST-08h (`.git/`-with-trailing-slash acceptance), these four tests pin down the four shapes the known-host parser must handle: accept exact `/<owner>/<repo>` (T-INST-02 / T-INST-08c), accept `/<owner>/<repo>.git` (T-INST-08h covers trailing-slash variant; T-INST-05 covers generic `.git` rule), reject extra segments (T-INST-08f), reject `.git`-then-extra-segments (this test), and reject single-segment paths (T-INST-08l). *(Spec 10.1)*

#### Install CLI Parsing

- **T-INST-40**: `loopx install` with no source → usage error, exit code 1. *(Spec 4.2, 10)*
- **T-INST-40a**: `loopx install -w ralph` with no source → usage error, exit code 1. The `-w` flag does not substitute for the required `<source>` argument. *(Spec 4.2, 10)*
- **T-INST-40b**: `loopx install --workflow ralph` with no source → usage error, exit code 1. Same as T-INST-40a using the long-form `--workflow` flag. *(Spec 4.2, 10)*
- **T-INST-40c**: `loopx install -y` with no source → usage error, exit code 1. The `-y` flag does not substitute for the required `<source>` argument. *(Spec 4.2, 10)*
- **T-INST-40d**: `loopx install <source1> <source2>` → usage error, exit code 1. The install grammar accepts exactly one positional source argument; extra positionals are rejected. SPEC 4.3 / 10 require exactly one `<source>` positional ("`<source>` is required"), and SPEC 12's usage-error contract for parser-level surface errors makes "extra positionals on install" a direct usage error. *(Spec 4.2, 10, 12)*
- **T-INST-40e**: `loopx install -w ralph <source1> <source2>` → usage error, exit code 1. Extra positionals are rejected even when combined with the `-w` flag. Same grammar reasoning as T-INST-40d applied with `-w` present. *(Spec 4.2, 10, 12)*
- **T-INST-40f**: `loopx install --no-install` with no source → usage error, exit code 1. `--no-install` does not substitute for the required `<source>` argument. *(Spec 4.2, 10)*
- **T-INST-41**: `loopx install -h` → install help, exit 0. Source not required. Assert that the help text explicitly lists: (a) `-w` / `--workflow` option with description, (b) `-y` option with description, (c) **`--no-install` option with description** (per SPEC 4.2 / 11.3 — suppresses auto-install of workflow dependencies per Spec 10.10), (d) supported source types (git repos, tarballs, `org/repo` shorthand), (e) does NOT mention single-file URL install as a supported source type — single-file URL install was removed (ADR-0003) and the help text must not advertise it, and (f) **does NOT advertise any short alias for `--no-install`** — SPEC 4.2 explicitly says `--no-install` has "No short form". The help text's option line for `--no-install` (and any synopsis / usage block listing install-scoped flags) must not list `-n`, `-N`, `-i`, `-I`, or any other single-character form alongside `--no-install`. A buggy implementation that introduced a hidden `-n` / `-i` alias and advertised it in the help text would fail (f); the parser side is pinned separately by T-INST-44f. *(Spec 4.2, 10.1, 10.10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-41a**: `loopx install --help` produces identical stdout, stderr, and exit code as `loopx install -h`. Run both in the same fixture. Assert: (a) stdout is byte-identical between the two invocations, (b) stderr is byte-identical, and (c) both exit 0. This proves the `--help` long form is a true alias for `-h`, consistent with the top-level and run help `--help` parity tests (T-CLI-03, T-CLI-41). *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42**: `loopx install -h --unknown` → install help, exit 0 (unknown flag ignored under `-h`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-42a**: `loopx install -h http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz` → install help, exit 0. Start a local HTTP server that tracks incoming requests. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) the HTTP server received zero requests, and (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified. Proves that `-h` makes no network requests even when a valid source is provided. *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42b**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz -h` → install help, exit 0. Same assertions as T-INST-42a (zero HTTP requests, `.loopx/` untouched). Proves `-h` after the source argument also short-circuits without network activity. *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42c**: `loopx install -h` with `.loopx/` containing a broken workflow tree — an invalid workflow name (e.g., `.loopx/-bad-workflow/index.sh`), a name collision (e.g., `check.sh` and `check.ts` in the same workflow), and an invalid script name (e.g., `-bad.sh`) — still exits 0. Assert: (a) help text is shown, (b) stderr contains no discovery or validation warnings, and (c) exit code is 0. This proves install help does not inspect `.loopx/`, per Spec 11.3. *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42d**: `loopx install --help http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz` → install help, exit 0. Start a local HTTP server that tracks incoming requests. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) the HTTP server received zero requests, and (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified. This is the long-form counterpart to T-INST-42a. T-INST-41a proves `--help` and `-h` produce identical output, but output parity alone would not catch an implementation that fetches the source before short-circuiting — this test verifies the behavioral guarantee that `--help` makes no network requests or filesystem changes. *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42e**: `loopx install --help --unknown` → install help, exit 0. The long-form `--help` in leading position short-circuits, ignoring the unrecognized `--unknown` flag. This is the `--help`-first counterpart to T-INST-42 (`-h --unknown`) and T-INST-49d (`--unknown --help`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-42f**: `loopx install --help --workflow` → install help, exit 0. The long-form `--help` in leading position short-circuits, ignoring the `--workflow` flag (which has no operand). This is the `--help` long-form counterpart to T-INST-48 (`-h -w`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-42g**: `loopx install -h <source1> <source2>` → install help, exit 0. Start a local HTTP server that tracks incoming requests. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) the HTTP server received zero requests, and (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified. The extra positionals do not prevent the help short-circuit and no network requests are made. This is the help-side counterpart to T-INST-40d (extra positionals without `-h` → usage error). *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42h**: `loopx install <source1> <source2> --help` → install help, exit 0. Start a local HTTP server that tracks incoming requests. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) the HTTP server received zero requests, and (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified. The `--help` after multiple positionals still short-circuits. This is the trailing-help counterpart to T-INST-42g. *(Spec 4.2, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42i**: `loopx install --help -w a -w b` → install help, exit 0. The long-form `--help` short-circuits, ignoring the duplicate `-w` flags. This is the `--help`-first counterpart to T-INST-49b (`-w a -w b -h`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-42j**: `loopx install --help -y -y` → install help, exit 0. The long-form `--help` short-circuits, ignoring the duplicate `-y` flags. This is the `--help`-first counterpart to T-INST-49c (`-y -y -h`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-42k**: `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/script.ts -h` → install help, exit 0. Start a local HTTP server that tracks incoming requests and serves a file at `/script.ts`. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) the HTTP server received zero requests (the rejected source triggered no network activity), (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified, and (d) stderr contains no single-file-URL-rejection error. This proves help short-circuit suppresses source classification errors — a single-file URL is normally rejected (T-INST-08) with an error under ADR-0003, but the help short-circuit must fire before source classification. T-INST-42a/42b cover help with valid sources (no network); this test covers the stronger guarantee that help also suppresses source-type validation. *(Spec 4.2, 10.1, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-42l**: `loopx install org/repo.git --help` → install help, exit 0. Assert: (a) help is shown, (b) stderr contains no error about the `.git` suffix being disallowed in the `org/repo` shorthand, and (c) `.loopx/` was not created or modified. The `org/repo.git` form is normally rejected (T-INST-01a) because the shorthand must not end in `.git`, but the `--help` short-circuit must fire before shorthand validation. This is the long-form `--help` counterpart to T-INST-42k and proves help suppresses a second distinct source classification error (invalid `org/repo` shorthand), complementing the single-file URL coverage in T-INST-42k. *(Spec 4.2, 10.1, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-43**: `loopx install -w a -w b <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (duplicate `-w`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-43a**: `loopx install --workflow a --workflow b <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (duplicate `--workflow`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-43b**: `loopx install -w a --workflow b <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (duplicate workflow flag — mixed short and long forms still count as a duplicate). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-44**: `loopx install -y -y <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (duplicate `-y`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-44a**: `loopx install --no-install --no-install <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (duplicate `--no-install`). Per Spec 4.2, repeating `--no-install` is a usage error unless `-h` / `--help` is present. This extends the duplicate-flag rejection pattern established by T-INST-43 (`-w`), T-INST-43a (`--workflow`), T-INST-43b (mixed), and T-INST-44 (`-y`) to the new `--no-install` flag added by Spec 10.10. *(Spec 4.2, 10.10)*
- **T-INST-44b**: `loopx install -h --no-install <source>` → install help, exit 0. The `-h` short-circuit ignores `--no-install` entirely (no validation of its presence or absence). Source is present, but help short-circuit suppresses install. Assert: (a) help stdout, (b) exit 0, (c) no source download / network activity, (d) `.loopx/` untouched. Extends T-INST-42 / T-INST-42a pattern to `--no-install`. *(Spec 4.2, 10.10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-44c**: `loopx install --help --no-install --no-install <source>` → install help, exit 0. The `--help` short-circuit also suppresses duplicate-`--no-install` usage errors — Spec 4.2 says the short-circuit ignores all other install-level arguments unconditionally (including duplicate or extraneous `--no-install`). Assert help output, exit 0, no duplicate-flag error on stderr. *(Spec 4.2, 10.10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-44d**: `loopx install --no-install -h <source>` → install help, exit 0. `--no-install` appearing before `-h` does not block the late-help short-circuit pattern (T-INST-49a–49g). Assert help output, exit 0. *(Spec 4.2, 10.10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-44e**: `loopx install --no-install --no-install -h <source>` → install help, exit 0. The late-help short-circuit fires unconditionally even when the preceding tokens include a duplicate `--no-install` (which would be a usage error per T-INST-44a if `-h` / `--help` were absent). Per SPEC 4.2, the `-h` / `--help` short-circuit ignores all other install-level arguments unconditionally — including duplicate `--no-install`. T-INST-44a covers the duplicate-flag rejection without help, T-INST-44c covers the `--help`-first-with-trailing-duplicate form, and T-INST-44d covers a single `--no-install` before late `-h`; this test pins down the specific combination of **duplicate `--no-install`** appearing **before** late `-h`. **Parameterize over both forms**: variant (a) `loopx install --no-install --no-install -h <source>`, variant (b) `loopx install --no-install --no-install --help <source>`. For each variant, assert: (a) install help on stdout (matches T-INST-41a's install-help reference), (b) exit code 0, (c) no duplicate-flag error on stderr (the short-circuit must suppress the would-be usage error from T-INST-44a), (d) no source download / network activity (verify via `withFakeGit` log being empty of clone invocations or by relying on the pre-network exit-0 surface), and (e) `.loopx/` (if pre-existing) is untouched. A buggy implementation that validated `--no-install` duplicates before applying the help short-circuit would surface the usage error and fail (b)/(c). *(Spec 4.2, 10.10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-44f**: `--no-install` has **no short form** — common single-character alias candidates are unrecognized install-scoped short flags. SPEC 4.2's `--no-install` row explicitly says "No short form." T-INST-45a (generic `-x` rejection) proves the parser rejects an arbitrary unknown short flag, but does not pin down the specific candidate aliases (`-n`, `-N`, `-i`, `-I`) that an implementer would most plausibly add as a hidden alias for `--no-install` based on the long-form's name. A buggy implementation that introduced a hidden `-n` or `-i` alias for `--no-install` (or that special-cased one of these short forms to *suppress* auto-install while still being parsed as a known install-scoped flag) would pass T-INST-45a — because that test exercises only `-x` — yet violate the SPEC 4.2 "No short form" clause. **Parameterize over the four most-plausible candidates**: variant (a) `loopx install -n <source>`, variant (b) `loopx install -N <source>`, variant (c) `loopx install -i <source>`, variant (d) `loopx install -I <source>`. For each variant, use a valid source (e.g., `org/repo` shorthand routed through `withFakeGit` to a local bare repo with one workflow containing `index.sh` + `package.json`, mirroring the T-INST-44a-class fixture shape). Wrap with `withFakeNpm({ logFile })` so any unintended auto-install would be observable. Assert: (a) **exit code 1** — the short flag is unrecognized at the install scope per SPEC 4.2 "Unrecognized flags within `install` ... are usage errors", (b) stderr references the unrecognized-flag rejection (matching T-INST-45 / T-INST-45a's usage-error surface; the exact wording is not pinned), (c) **the fake-npm shim log records zero invocations** — the install never reaches the auto-install pass; this catches a buggy implementation that silently aliased one of these short flags to `--no-install` and proceeded with the file-level install (which would surface as exit 0 plus a successful commit with no `npm install`), and (d) **`.loopx/` (if pre-existing) is untouched and no new workflow directory is committed** — the usage error fires at parser time, before any source acquisition or commit, identical to T-INST-45 / T-INST-45a's pre-network exit surface; verify via `withFakeGit`'s log being empty of clone invocations. Together with the strengthened T-INST-41 assertion (f) (no short alias advertised in install help text), this closes the SPEC 4.2 "No short form" clause across both surfaces — help text and parser. *(Spec 4.2, 10.10)*
- **T-INST-45**: `loopx install --unknown <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (unrecognized flag). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-45a**: `loopx install -x <source>` → usage error, exit code 1 (unrecognized short flag). This is the install-scoped counterpart to T-CLI-72 (`loopx run -x ralph`), ensuring short flags are rejected identically to long flags. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-46**: `loopx install -h -w a -w b` shows install help and exits 0 (duplicate `-w` not rejected under help). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-47**: `loopx install -h -y -y` shows install help and exits 0 (duplicate `-y` not rejected under help). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-48**: `loopx install -h -w` shows install help and exits 0 (missing `-w` operand not rejected under help). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49**: `loopx install -w` (no operand for `-w`, no source) exits with code 1 (usage error). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49e**: `loopx install --workflow` (no operand for `--workflow`, no source) exits with code 1 (usage error). This is the long-form counterpart to T-INST-49. *(Spec 4.2)*

#### Late-Help Short-Circuit (Invalid Args Before `-h`)

- **T-INST-49a**: `loopx install --unknown -h` shows install help and exits 0. The unrecognized flag before `-h` does not prevent the help short-circuit. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49b**: `loopx install -w a -w b -h` shows install help and exits 0. The duplicate `-w` before `-h` does not prevent the help short-circuit. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49c**: `loopx install -y -y -h` shows install help and exits 0. The duplicate `-y` before `-h` does not prevent the help short-circuit. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49d**: `loopx install --unknown --help` shows install help and exits 0. Long-form `--help` after invalid args also short-circuits. *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49f**: `loopx install -w -h` shows install help and exits 0. A naive parser would consume `-h` as the operand for `-w`, but the `-h` short-circuit fires unconditionally when `-h` is present — all other install-level arguments are ignored. This is the missing-operand counterpart to T-INST-49a–49d (which cover invalid or duplicate flags before `-h`). *(Spec 4.2)*
- **T-INST-49g**: `loopx install --workflow --help` shows install help and exits 0. Long-form counterpart to T-INST-49f: `--workflow` would consume `--help` as its operand in naive parsing, but the help short-circuit ignores all other arguments unconditionally. *(Spec 4.2)*

#### Install-scoped `--` Rejection

SPEC 4.2 enumerates only `-w` / `--workflow`, `-y`, `--no-install`, and `-h` / `--help` as recognized install-scoped flags. `--` is not a recognized install-scoped token (no end-of-options separator semantics, no positional separator). T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-01 through 07 cover the run-scoped surface, T-CLI-TOP-DASHDASH-01 covers the top-level surface, and T-INST-45 covers `--unknown` rejection generally; this block pins down the install-scoped `--` rejection specifically and its interaction with the `-h` / `--help` short-circuit (which Spec 4.2 says ignores all other install-level arguments unconditionally).

- **T-INST-DASHDASH-01**: `loopx install -- <source>` → usage error, exit code 1. The `<source>` argument follows the `--` token and would be the install source if `--` were silently treated as an end-of-options separator, but `--` is not a recognized install-scoped token per SPEC 4.2. **The `<source>` must be a genuinely valid supported source** (per SPEC 10.1: `org/repo` shorthand, a `file://…repo.git` URL pointing to a local bare repo, or a localhost tarball URL) that would succeed as a normal `loopx install <source>` invocation — otherwise the test has a false-pass hazard: a buggy implementation that silently consumed `--` could still fail later with an "unsupported source" error on an unsupported local-path source and accidentally exit 1 for the wrong reason, making exit-code-1 a non-distinguishing signal. Run `loopx install -- <valid-source>` where `<valid-source>` is, e.g., a `file:///tmp/bare-repos/my-workflow.git` URL served by `startLocalGitServer` (section 2.3), or a localhost `.tar.gz` URL served by `startLocalHTTPServer`. To remove any residual ambiguity about whether the `--` rejection fired or a later source-resolution step did, pre-verify the same `<valid-source>` succeeds in a separate `loopx install <valid-source>` invocation (without the `--` token) against a fresh project to confirm the source is genuinely installable; then in the test proper, assert: (a) exit code 1 (usage error per SPEC 12 / SPEC 4.2 unrecognized-flag rule), (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error / unrecognized-token category mentioning `--` — the exact wording is implementation-defined but must distinguish a usage-error-category message from a "source unsupported" message (i.e., the failure must be about the `--` token, not about the source), (c) no source download / clone / network activity occurred (verify by asserting the `withFakeGit` or `startLocalGitServer` / `startLocalHTTPServer` log records zero hits for the test URL during this invocation), (d) no `.loopx/<workflow>` was committed, and (e) `.loopx/` (if pre-existing) is untouched. A buggy implementation that silently consumed `--` as an end-of-options marker and then proceeded with `<valid-source>` as the install source would produce exit 0 and surface a successful install — an observable distinct failure mode. *(Spec 4.2, 10, 10.1, 12)*
- **T-INST-DASHDASH-02**: `loopx install <source> --` → usage error, exit code 1. Trailing `--` after the source is rejected just like leading `--`. Run `loopx install <valid-source> --` where `<valid-source>` is the same genuinely valid source used by T-INST-DASHDASH-01 (e.g., `file:///tmp/bare-repos/my-workflow.git` or a localhost `.tar.gz` URL), pre-verified to succeed without the trailing `--`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces a usage-error / unrecognized-token category mentioning `--` (not "source unsupported"), (c) no source download / clone / network activity occurred (verify via the same fake-server / shim log as in T-INST-DASHDASH-01), (d) no `.loopx/<workflow>` was committed, and (e) `.loopx/` is untouched. Trailing-`--` counterpart to T-INST-DASHDASH-01 (leading), proving `--` is rejected at any position when the help short-circuit is not present. Using a valid source for both positional forms ensures the test cannot false-pass by an unrelated "unsupported source" rejection downstream of a buggy `--` acceptance. *(Spec 4.2, 10, 10.1, 12)*
- **T-INST-DASHDASH-03**: `loopx install -h -- <source>` → install help, exit 0. The `-h` short-circuit fires before any `--` rejection, suppressing the would-be usage error from T-INST-DASHDASH-01. Per SPEC 4.2 install short-circuit clause: "When `-h` / `--help` is present, loopx shows install help, exits 0, and ignores all other install-level arguments unconditionally" — this includes `--` and any subsequent tokens. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stdout is install help (matches T-INST-41a's install-help reference), (c) no source download / network activity occurred, (d) `.loopx/` is untouched, and (e) stderr contains no usage-error message about `--`. Help-short-circuit counterpart for `--`, mirroring the patterns in T-INST-42 (`-h --unknown`), T-INST-44b (`-h --no-install <source>`), and T-CLI-RUN-DASHDASH-04 (`run -h -- ralph`). *(Spec 4.2, 10, 11.3)*
- **T-INST-DASHDASH-04**: `loopx install --help -- <source>` → install help, exit 0. Long-form counterpart to T-INST-DASHDASH-03, completing help-short-circuit-suppresses-`--` coverage for both `-h` and `--help`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stdout is install help, (c) no source download / network activity, (d) `.loopx/` is untouched, (e) stderr contains no usage-error message about `--`. Mirrors the long-form-help-short-circuit pattern from T-INST-42e / T-INST-49d. *(Spec 4.2, 10, 11.3)*

#### Workflow Classification — Single-Workflow Source

- **T-INST-50**: Git repo with root-level `index.ts`, a non-script file (`package.json`), and a pure non-script root directory (`docs/` containing only `.md` files — no supported script extensions) → single-workflow source. Entire repo installed as `.loopx/<repo-name>/`. Assert the workflow directory contains all root-level files including non-script files (`package.json`) **and** non-script directories (`docs/`). This covers the ADR-0003 rule that all root files and directories are copied for single-workflow sources, including directories that contain no script-extension files. **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the workflow's top-level `package.json` would otherwise trigger auto-install, and this test's contract is classification/copy, not auto-install. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-50a**: Git repo with root-level `index.js` → single-workflow source. Installed as `.loopx/<repo-name>/`. This extends install-path extension coverage (T-INST-50 covers `.ts`) to positively verify that `.js` is recognized as a supported script extension in the install single-workflow classification codepath. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-50b**: Parameterized: single-workflow root classification for all five supported extensions. For each extension in [`.sh`, `.js`, `.jsx`, `.ts`, `.tsx`], create a git repo with a single root-level `index.<ext>` file. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: the source is classified as single-workflow and installed as `.loopx/<repo-name>/`. T-INST-50 covers `.ts` and T-INST-50a covers `.js` individually; this parameterized test closes the gaps for `.sh`, `.jsx`, and `.tsx` and exercises all five extensions uniformly in the single-workflow root classification codepath. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-51**: Git repo with root-level `index.ts` and a `lib/` subdirectory containing `.ts` files → still single-workflow (root-level script files take precedence unconditionally). `lib/` is workflow content, not a separate workflow. Assert `.loopx/<repo-name>/lib/` exists. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-52**: Root-level script files force single-workflow classification even when subdirectories also contain scripts. Git repo with root-level `index.sh` and two subdirectories `tools/` and `helpers/` that each contain their own top-level script files (e.g., `tools/build.sh`, `helpers/setup.ts`) — subdirectories that would themselves qualify as workflows in a multi-workflow source. `loopx install <source>` → single-workflow install. Assert: (a) `.loopx/<repo-name>/` is created (single workflow, named after the repo), (b) `.loopx/<repo-name>/tools/build.sh` and `.loopx/<repo-name>/helpers/setup.ts` exist (subdirectories are copied inside the workflow as content), (c) `.loopx/tools/` and `.loopx/helpers/` do **not** exist (the subdirectories are NOT installed as sibling workflows). This pins down the ADR-0003 "root-level script files take precedence unconditionally" rule — the presence of `index.sh` at the root forces single-workflow classification, and the would-be-workflow subdirectories are treated as workflow internals. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-52a**: Root-level "config-style" file with a supported extension (e.g., `eslint.config.js` or `vitest.config.ts`) at the repository root forces single-workflow classification, even when subdirectories also qualify as workflows (contain their own top-level script files). The config file is a root-level file with a supported script extension, so it triggers the single-workflow rule unconditionally. Additionally, the config file is a top-level script candidate whose base name (e.g., `eslint.config`) contains a dot — violating the script naming restriction `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`. Assert that install classifies the source as single-workflow AND fails with a validation error about the invalid script name. The sibling subdirectories are not treated as separate workflows. *(Spec 10.3, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-52c**: Root-level non-index script file forces single-workflow classification even when subdirectories qualify as workflows. Git repo with root-level `setup.ts` (but no `index.*` file) and two subdirectories `ralph/` and `other/` that each contain their own top-level script files (e.g., `ralph/index.sh`, `other/index.sh`) — subdirectories that would qualify as workflows in a multi-workflow source. `loopx install <source>` → single-workflow install. Assert: (a) `.loopx/<repo-name>/` is created (single workflow, named after the repo), (b) `.loopx/<repo-name>/setup.ts`, `.loopx/<repo-name>/ralph/index.sh`, and `.loopx/<repo-name>/other/index.sh` exist (subdirectories are copied as workflow content), (c) `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` do **not** exist (the subdirectories are NOT installed as sibling workflows), and (d) no error about missing `index` — a missing default entry point is allowed at install time. T-INST-52 uses `index.sh` at the root and T-INST-52a uses a root-level file with an invalid name; this pins down that **any** valid root-level script file — not just one named `index` — forces single-workflow classification independently of the default entry point convention. *(Spec 10.3, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-52d**: Root-level "config-style" file with a supported extension in a tarball source forces single-workflow classification, same as for git sources. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains `eslint.config.js` at the root level, plus subdirectories that would themselves qualify as workflows (e.g., `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.sh`). `loopx install <tarball-url>` → the source is classified as single-workflow (the root-level `.js` file triggers the rule unconditionally), and install fails with a validation error about the invalid script name (`eslint.config` contains a dot that violates the naming restriction `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*`). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the error references the invalid script name, (c) `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` do NOT exist (sibling subdirectories were not installed as separate workflows). This is the tarball counterpart to T-INST-52a (git). Per SPEC 10.2, classification after extraction is source-type-agnostic, so the "root-level supported-extension config file forces single-workflow classification" rule must hold for both source types. *(Spec 10.2, 10.3, 10.4)*

#### Workflow Classification — Multi-Workflow Source

- **T-INST-53**: Git repo with no root-level script files but two subdirectories `ralph/` and `other/` each containing an `index.sh` → multi-workflow source. Both installed as `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/`. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-53a**: Multi-workflow git repo where one subdirectory qualifies via `index.jsx`. Source contains `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.jsx`, with no root-level script files → both installed as `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/`. This extends install-path extension coverage (T-INST-53 covers `.sh`) to positively verify that `.jsx` is recognized in the install multi-workflow classification codepath. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-53b**: Multi-workflow git repo where one subdirectory qualifies via `index.tsx`. Source contains `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.tsx`, with no root-level script files → both installed as `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/`. This extends install-path extension coverage to positively verify that `.tsx` is recognized in the install multi-workflow classification codepath. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-53c**: Parameterized: multi-workflow subdirectory classification for all five supported extensions. For each extension in [`.sh`, `.js`, `.jsx`, `.ts`, `.tsx`], create a multi-workflow git repo where one subdirectory (`target/`) qualifies via a single `index.<ext>` file, alongside another valid workflow `other/index.sh`, with no root-level script files. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: both `target` and `other` are installed. T-INST-53 covers `.sh`, T-INST-53a covers `.jsx`, and T-INST-53b covers `.tsx` individually; this parameterized test closes the gaps for `.js` and `.ts` and exercises all five extensions uniformly in the multi-workflow subdirectory classification codepath. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-54**: Multi-workflow repo: repo-root support files (README, LICENSE, `package.json`, etc.) are NOT copied into `.loopx/`. Only workflow directories are installed. Assert that a root `package.json` in the source does not create `.loopx/package.json` **and** that a root `README.md` in the source does not create `.loopx/README.md`. Both file types are repo-level support files that must be excluded when only workflow directories are copied. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-54a**: Multi-workflow install preserves workflow-internal non-script files and subdirectories. Source repo contains `ralph/index.ts`, `ralph/package.json`, `ralph/lib/helpers.ts`, and `ralph/README.md` (plus `other/index.sh` to force multi-workflow classification). After install, assert `.loopx/ralph/package.json`, `.loopx/ralph/lib/helpers.ts`, and `.loopx/ralph/README.md` are all present. This verifies that the entire workflow directory — not just discovered script files — is copied. **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — `ralph/package.json` would otherwise trigger the post-commit auto-install pass, and this test's contract is the file-copy preservation rule, not auto-install behavior. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-54b**: Multi-workflow source-root `package.json` is ignored during validation and version checking. Source repo contains `ralph/index.sh`, `other/index.sh`, and a repo-root `package.json` that is either invalid JSON (e.g., `{broken`) or declares a loopx version range not satisfied by the running version. `loopx install <source>` succeeds, installing both workflows. Assert: no warning or error on stderr related to `package.json` or version mismatch. In the workflow model, only each workflow's own `package.json` matters for version checking — the repo-root file is a support file that is neither copied (T-INST-54) nor read for validation. *(Spec 10.3, 10.6)*
