- **T-INST-54c**: Multi-workflow install does not copy root support directories. Source repo contains `ralph/index.sh`, `other/index.sh`, `docs/README.md`, and `shared/config.json`. After install, assert `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` exist, but `.loopx/docs/` and `.loopx/shared/` do not exist. This is the directory counterpart to T-INST-54 (which covers root support files) — T-INST-55 verifies that non-workflow subdirectories are silently skipped during classification, but does not assert their absence from the destination. A buggy installer that correctly classifies workflows but still copies all subdirectories into `.loopx/` would pass T-INST-55 but fail here. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-54d**: Multi-workflow tarball source-root `package.json` is ignored during validation and version checking. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains workflow subdirectories `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.sh`, plus a source-root `package.json` that is either invalid JSON (e.g., `{broken`) or declares a loopx version range not satisfied by the running version. `loopx install <tarball-url>` succeeds, installing both workflows. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) both `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` are installed, (c) no warning or error on stderr related to `package.json` or version mismatch, and (d) `.loopx/package.json` does not exist (the source-root file was not copied). This is the tarball counterpart to T-INST-54b (git multi-workflow): per SPEC 10.2, classification after extraction is source-type-agnostic, so the "source-root `package.json` is not read for multi-workflow classification or version checking" rule must hold for both source types. *(Spec 10.2, 10.3, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-55**: Multi-workflow repo: subdirectories with no script files are silently skipped. They don't cause failure. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-55a**: Non-recursive workflow detection during install classification. Multi-workflow source contains a candidate directory `tools/` with only nested supported-extension files (e.g., `tools/lib/helper.ts`) and no top-level script files (no `tools/*.sh|js|jsx|ts|tsx`). That directory is skipped as a non-workflow — only top-level files count for workflow detection. Other valid workflow directories in the source are installed normally. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-55c**: Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains only `index.mjs` (unsupported extension) alongside valid workflow `other/` (containing `index.sh`). `ralph/` is skipped as a non-workflow — `.mjs` is not a supported script extension, so the directory has no top-level script files. Assert: (a) only `.loopx/other/` is installed, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` does not exist, and (c) no warning or error about `ralph/`. This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-06/07, which cover `.mjs`/`.cjs` being unrecognized at runtime discovery. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-55b**: Multi-workflow source with an invalid-named non-workflow subdirectory. Source repo contains `ralph/index.sh`, `other/index.sh`, and `-bad-dir/README.md` (no script files — not a workflow). Install succeeds, installing only `ralph` and `other`. The `-bad-dir` directory is silently skipped with no error or warning about its invalid name. This locks down the rule that name validation applies only to discovered workflows — directories that don't qualify as workflows are never validated, even if their names would fail the naming restriction pattern. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-55e**: Multi-workflow source containing a legacy-style directory-script subdirectory is handled correctly: the legacy subdirectory is skipped, and only valid workflow directories are installed. Source repo has `good/index.sh` plus `legacy/package.json` (with `{ "main": "src/run.js" }`) and `legacy/src/run.js`, but **no** top-level files with supported script extensions inside `legacy/` (the only supported-extension file is nested in `src/`). `loopx install <source>` → exits 0. Assert: (a) `.loopx/good/` is installed and contains `index.sh`, (b) `.loopx/legacy/` does **not** exist — the `legacy/` directory had no top-level script files and was silently skipped as a non-workflow. This is the multi-workflow-subdirectory counterpart to T-INST-56a (which covers the source-root legacy directory-script as a zero-workflow case). T-CLI-104c and T-DISC-20c cover runtime non-discovery of legacy directory-script layouts; this covers the install classification path for a legacy subdirectory coexisting with valid workflows. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-55d**: Root-level unsupported script-like files (`.mjs`/`.cjs`) do not force single-workflow classification. Source root contains `index.mjs` (or `config.cjs`) plus `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.sh`. Because `.mjs` and `.cjs` are not supported script extensions (Spec 2.1), the root has no files with supported extensions and the source is classified as multi-workflow — both `ralph` and `other` are installed. Assert: `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` exist. This pins down the boundary between T-INST-50a (supported root-level files force single-workflow) and T-INST-56c/55c (unsupported extensions are ignored) for the case where valid workflow subdirectories coexist with unsupported root files. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*

#### Workflow Classification — Zero-Workflow Source

- **T-INST-56**: Git repo with no root-level script files and no subdirectories that qualify as workflows → error. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-56a**: A source repo structured as a legacy directory script — root `package.json` with `main` pointing to a deeply nested file (e.g., `{ "main": "src/app/run.js" }`), with the script file only inside a nested subdirectory (`src/app/`) and no top-level files with supported script extensions. The top-level `src/` directory contains no top-level script files (`src/*.{sh,js,jsx,ts,tsx}`) so it does not qualify as a workflow — the source is classified as zero-workflow and rejected with an error. Assert exit code 1 and that the error indicates no installable workflows were found. This proves the install mechanism does not fall back to legacy directory-script install behavior — the `main` field is not used for workflow classification. *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-56e**: Legacy directory-script source is rejected as zero-workflow without migration-guidance messaging. Create a source root with a `package.json` whose `main` points to a nested file (e.g., `{ "main": "src/app/run.js" }`) and no top-level files with supported script extensions (the script file lives only inside a nested subdirectory like `src/app/run.js`). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the error indicates no installable workflows were found, and (c) — **structurally** rather than by prose blacklisting — stderr does not contain a migration-guidance or deprecation warning as a separate output category alongside the zero-workflow error. Concretely: the stderr output is limited to the standard "no installable workflows found" error (and any unrelated warnings that would be emitted regardless of the legacy layout). There is no additional warning/notice/advisory line whose subject is the legacy directory-script layout (the `main` field, the nested script file, or the `package.json`-without-top-level-scripts shape are not flagged in any extra output category beyond the routine zero-workflow rejection). Prose-level phrase matching is intentionally avoided — the check targets the presence of a distinct migration-warning output category, not specific wording. T-INST-56a proves the zero-workflow rejection for this shape; this test extends it with the structural anti-migration-messaging assertion, aligning install behavior with ADR-0003's explicit "no legacy compatibility or migration tooling" consequence and mirroring runtime coverage in T-DISC-10a (flat script) and T-DISC-20c (directory script). *(Spec 10.3)*
- **T-INST-56c**: Source root containing only `index.mjs` at root level (no files with supported extensions) → zero-workflow error. `.mjs` is not a supported script extension (Spec 2.1), so no script files are found at the root level and no subdirectories qualify as workflows. This is the install-classification counterpart to T-DISC-06, which covers `.mjs` being unrecognized at runtime discovery. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-56d**: Source root containing only `index.cjs` at root level → zero-workflow error. `.cjs` is not a supported script extension. This is the install-classification counterpart to T-DISC-07. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-56f**: Source root containing only `index.SH` at root level (uppercase extension) → zero-workflow error. Per SPEC 2.1 the supported script extensions are `.sh`, `.js`, `.ts`, `.jsx`, `.tsx` (lowercase); uppercase variants (`.SH`, `.JS`, etc.) are not supported. The install-classification codepath must enforce the same extension rules as runtime discovery (T-DISC-15c covers runtime uppercase-unsupported). Prepare a source whose root contains only `index.SH` (uppercase) and no other files with supported extensions. `loopx install <source>` → exit code 1 with a zero-workflow error. Together with T-INST-56c / T-INST-56d (`.mjs` / `.cjs` unsupported), this pins down the install-classification extension-matching rule parity with runtime discovery. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-56g**: Multi-workflow candidate subdirectory containing only `index.TS` (uppercase) is skipped as non-workflow. Per SPEC 2.1 extension rules (lowercase-only), a subdirectory whose only top-level file is an uppercase-extension script does not qualify as a workflow. Prepare a multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains `index.sh` (valid) and `other/` contains only `index.TS` (uppercase, unsupported). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed with `index.sh`, (c) `.loopx/other/` does **not** exist (the subdirectory was skipped because `index.TS` does not match the supported-extension pattern — it was not classified as a workflow). A buggy implementation that case-insensitively matched extensions would install `other/` as a workflow and fail this test. *(Spec 10.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-56h**: Single-workflow source with a valid root-level `index.sh` plus an additional uppercase-extension file (`helper.TS`) at the root level: the uppercase file is copied as **non-script content**, not treated as a script. SPEC 10.3 says install preserves the source's non-script content alongside recognized script files; a file whose extension is not in the supported set is non-script content. Prepare a source whose root contains `index.sh` (valid single-workflow trigger) and `helper.TS` (uppercase — not a supported script extension). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/<name>/` contains `index.sh` (classified and copied as a script), (c) `.loopx/<name>/helper.TS` exists byte-for-byte (copied as non-script content — not validated, not renamed, not discovered as a script), and (d) `loopx run -h` does not list `helper` as a script of the installed workflow (validating that the runtime-discovery extension rules also reject `helper.TS` at runtime, confirming round-trip parity). Together with T-INST-56f / T-INST-56g, this completes the uppercase-extension install-classification coverage: rejected at the root-only and subdirectory-only layers (T-INST-56f / T-INST-56g) and preserved-as-non-script-content in valid-workflow sources (this test). *(Spec 10.3, 2.1, 5.1)*
- **T-INST-56b**: Tarball source with no installable workflows → error. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after wrapper-directory stripping) contains no root-level script files and no subdirectories that qualify as workflows. For example, the tarball root contains `README.md` (non-script content) and `lib/nested/helper.ts` (a `.ts` file placed **inside a nested subdirectory below** `lib/`, not at `lib/*.ts` top-level). `lib/` therefore does **not** qualify as a workflow because workflow detection per SPEC 10.3 / 5.1 requires at least one top-level supported-extension script file directly inside the subdirectory; a `.ts` file nested one level deeper does not satisfy that rule. Explicitly: there must be no files matching `lib/*.{sh,js,ts,jsx,tsx}` at the top level of `lib/`. `loopx install <tarball-url>` → exits with code 1 with an error indicating no installable workflows were found. This is the tarball counterpart to T-INST-56 (git zero-workflow) — the classification logic is source-type-agnostic, so both source types must reject zero-workflow sources. *(Spec 10.3, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-56i**: Source root containing only a directory whose name has a supported script extension → zero-workflow error. SPEC 10.3 / 5.1 require single-workflow classification's "root-level script files" trigger to match top-level **files** with supported extensions; a top-level directory whose name happens to end in `.sh`/`.ts`/`.js`/`.jsx`/`.tsx` is not a file and does not satisfy the trigger. Prepare a git source whose root contains a single top-level directory named `index.sh/` (created with `mkdir -p index.sh`, with optional unrelated content nested inside, e.g., `index.sh/notes.md`) and **no** top-level files with supported extensions and **no** subdirectories that qualify as workflows. Run `loopx install <source>` → exit code 1 with a zero-workflow error. Assert: (a) the source is **not** classified as single-workflow (the directory `index.sh/` did not satisfy the root-level-script-file trigger), and (b) the error is the "no installable workflows found" error, not a classification-then-validation error (e.g., not "invalid script name `index.sh`" — `index.sh/` was never treated as a script in the first place because it is a directory). This is the file-vs-directory counterpart to T-INST-56f / T-INST-56g (which test uppercase-extension files); together they pin down that install classification matches both on extension case-sensitivity and on entry type (file, not directory). A buggy implementation that enumerated root-level entries by name pattern (matching `*.{sh,ts,js,jsx,tsx}`) without checking whether the entry resolves to a regular file would wrongly classify the source as single-workflow and either fail to copy `index.sh` (it is a directory, not a file) or copy it as a directory and then fail script-name validation at install-time. *(Spec 10.3, 10.4, 5.1, 2.1)*

#### Install Source Symlinks

SPEC 10.11 pins down install-time symlink semantics: install-time discovery follows symlinks under the same entry-type policy as runtime (SPEC 5.1) — a symlinked workflow directory can qualify as a workflow and a symlinked script file can qualify as a script, with names derived from the symlink entry's own name. During staging / copy, symlinks are **materialized** rather than preserved: the destination contains a real directory or file copied from the symlink target, never a symlink pointing back into the temporary clone / extraction directory. Symlinks that are part of a selected workflow must resolve to existing paths within the install source root; broken symlinks, cycles, and out-of-source targets are preflight-atomic rejection failures. Under `-w`, only the selected workflow's symlinks are validated and materialized.

Per SPEC 10.2 the post-acquisition classification path is source-type-agnostic, so each test below is parameterized over **both** source types — git source (local bare repo, no network) and tarball source (local HTTP server, no network) — to prove the symlink behavior is enforced on both code paths.

- **T-INST-55f**: Install source containing a symlinked workflow subdirectory is materialized as a real directory. Multi-workflow source root containing a top-level real directory `internal/real-workflow/` (with `index.sh`) and a top-level symlink `alias -> internal/real-workflow` (i.e., a multi-workflow source where `alias/` is a symlinked workflow entry). Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`, so all qualifying workflows are installed). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/alias/` exists and is a **real directory** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`), (c) `.loopx/alias/index.sh` exists and is a **real file** with the same byte-for-byte content as the symlink target's `index.sh`, (d) the workflow runs successfully via `loopx run -n 1 alias` (proving the materialized copy is executable, not just present), (e) `.loopx/internal/` does not exist as a workflow at the destination (a non-workflow source-root subdirectory containing a workflow subdirectory does not count as a top-level workflow per SPEC 10.3 — top-level workflow detection is non-recursive), and (f) the workflow name is `alias` (the symlink entry's own name), not `real-workflow` (the target's basename). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the test's contract is materialization, not auto-install behavior. *(Spec 10.11, 10.3, 5.1, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55g**: Install source containing a symlinked entry script inside a workflow is materialized as a real file, with the script name derived from the symlink entry's own name (not the target's basename). Source workflow `ralph/` containing a real helper file `ralph/lib/original-entry.sh` (with a marker `echo` and `exit 0`) and a top-level symlink `ralph/index.sh -> lib/original-entry.sh` (i.e., `index.sh` is a symlinked script entry within the workflow whose target has a **distinct basename** — `original-entry` ≠ `index` — so the test discriminates between symlink-name and target-basename derivation). The target `lib/original-entry.sh` is a nested file (inside the non-top-level `lib/` subdirectory), so it is not itself a top-level script per SPEC 2.1's non-recursive script-discovery rule. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/index.sh` exists and is a **real file** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`), (c) the file content matches the symlink target's content byte-for-byte (`lib/original-entry.sh`'s contents), (d) `loopx run -n 1 ralph` runs the materialized script and the marker side effect is observed (proving the copy is functional, not just byte-equivalent — and that the script is reachable as the workflow's default `index` entry, not via the target basename), and (e) `original-entry` is **not** a top-level discovered script name in `.loopx/ralph/` — `loopx run -n 1 "ralph:original-entry"` fails with a missing-script error. A buggy implementation that derived the script name from the symlink target's basename (`original-entry`) rather than from the symlink entry's own name (`index`) would either create a script named `original-entry` (failing assertion (d) because there would be no `index` to run as default) or expose `original-entry` as a discoverable script (failing assertion (e)). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the source workflow's directory does not contain a top-level `package.json` in this fixture so auto-install would not fire anyway, but `--no-install` is included for consistency with the rest of the symlink suite. *(Spec 10.11, 10.3, 5.1, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55h**: Broken or out-of-source symlink in a selected workflow is rejected preflight-atomically. Three sub-cases, each tested independently. The three sub-cases must keep the SPEC 10.11 failure classes **distinct** — a "broken" case must have an unresolvable target, a "cycle" case must form a self-referential symlink chain, and an "out-of-source" case must point to a target that **actually exists on disk** but resolves outside the install source root (otherwise the out-of-source branch collapses into the broken-symlink branch and the test fails to discriminate the two rejection paths):
    - **(h-broken)**: Source workflow `ralph/` contains `ralph/index.sh -> nonexistent-target` (a symlink whose target does not exist anywhere on disk).
    - **(h-cycle)**: Source workflow `ralph/` contains `ralph/index.sh -> a` and `ralph/a -> b` and `ralph/b -> a` (a cycle in the symlink chain — the script entry resolves through a cycle that never terminates at a real file).
    - **(h-out-of-source)**: Before constructing the install source, the test creates a real, controlled file at an absolute path **outside** the install source root (e.g., `<test-isolated-tmp>/outside-of-source/real-target.sh` containing valid script content — a `bash` script with `exit 0`); the install source's `ralph/index.sh` is a symlink whose **target is the absolute path** of that out-of-source file (or a reliably resolving relative path that traverses **above** the install source root and lands on the controlled file). The test must verify before invoking `loopx install` that the symlink resolves to an existing file via `fs.realpath` from the install source root — this discriminates this sub-case from `(h-broken)`. The target is created in a test-isolated temp directory (not in `/etc/`, `/usr/`, or any system path) so the test does not depend on system files such as `/etc/passwd`, which may not exist in minimal containers and pollutes the failure semantics across sub-cases.

  For each sub-case, run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — `.loopx/` either does not exist or contains no `.loopx/ralph/` entry (no partial install was committed), and (c) stderr contains an error message identifying the failing symlink (the test does not pin the exact wording, only that `ralph` and `index.sh` are mentioned in the error context). For sub-case `(h-out-of-source)` specifically, the test must additionally verify that the controlled out-of-source target file still exists after the install fails — proving the rejection happened before any copy step touched it (and giving an extra signal that the test's preflight-traversal verification ran on a real existing file, not a broken target). **Do not assert on the implementation-private staging directory** — its path/name is not black-box observable without a seam (see known gap T-INST-79a — section 4.10 "Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility"); this test is limited to the externally visible "no destination workflow committed" surface. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the install fails before commit so auto-install would not fire anyway, but `--no-install` is included for consistency. *(Spec 10.11, 10.7, 10.9)*
- **T-INST-55i**: Under `-w`, a bad symlink in an unselected sibling workflow does not block install of the selected workflow, but the same bad symlink in the selected workflow is fatal. Per SPEC 10.11, "only symlinks that are part of the selected workflow are validated and materialized" — so the unselected-sibling-invisibility rule must apply uniformly to **all three** SPEC 10.11 failure classes (broken, cycle, out-of-source), not just broken symlinks. The test parameterizes the bad-symlink class across all three failure classes, mirroring the T-INST-55h structure for the selected-workflow case. Multi-workflow source containing two workflows: `ralph/index.sh` (a real, valid script file) and a sibling `broken/` workflow whose `index.sh` is bad in one of three ways (each sub-case below uses a distinct fixture). The same fixture-distinctness requirement as T-INST-55h applies — out-of-source uses a real, controlled file outside the install source root via absolute path:
    - **(i-broken)**: `broken/index.sh -> nonexistent-target` (broken).
    - **(i-cycle)**: `broken/index.sh -> a`, `broken/a -> b`, `broken/b -> a` (cycle).
    - **(i-out-of-source)**: Before constructing the install source, the test creates a real, controlled file at `<test-isolated-tmp>/outside-of-source/real-target.sh`. The install source's `broken/index.sh` is a symlink whose target is the absolute path of that out-of-source file (or a reliably resolving relative path landing on it). The test must verify before invoking `loopx install` that the symlink resolves to an existing file via `fs.realpath` from the install source root — distinguishing this sub-case from `(i-broken)`.

  For each sub-case, perform two invocations:
    - **(unselected-bad)**: Run `loopx install --no-install -w ralph <source>` (selecting only the valid workflow). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed and contains `index.sh` as a real file, (c) `.loopx/broken/` does not exist (selective install only writes the selected workflow), and (d) stderr contains no error or warning about the bad symlink in `broken/` — per SPEC 10.11 "Symlinks in unselected workflow directories ... are ignored" combined with the SPEC 10.8 selective-validation rule, the bad symlink in the unselected sibling is not validated regardless of which SPEC 10.11 failure class it represents. For `(i-out-of-source)` specifically, additionally assert that the controlled out-of-source target file still exists after install completes — proving no copy step crossed the source-root boundary even though the unselected sibling's symlink pointed there.
    - **(selected-bad)**: Run `loopx install --no-install -w broken <source>` (selecting the workflow with the bad symlink). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — neither `.loopx/broken/` nor `.loopx/ralph/` is committed, and (c) stderr contains an error identifying the bad symlink in `broken/`. **Do not assert on the implementation-private staging directory** for the same reason as T-INST-55h (see known gap T-INST-79a — section 4.10 "Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility").

  Together the three sub-cases × two invocations prove the SPEC 10.11 + SPEC 10.8 interaction across the full SPEC 10.11 failure-class set: any unselected-sibling bad symlink is invisible under `-w` (regardless of class), but the same defect in the selected workflow is always fatal — `-w` does not relax symlink validation for the selected workflow, and the unselected-invisibility rule does not depend on which class of bad symlink the sibling has. A buggy implementation that special-cased the broken-symlink class but eagerly walked cycles or resolved out-of-source targets on unselected siblings (e.g., infinite-looping on the cycle, or copying out-of-source content) would fail this test. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.8, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-55j**: Symlinked **non-script file** inside a selected workflow is materialized as a real file. SPEC 10.11 normatively says "Symlinked non-script files or directories inside a selected workflow's copied content are likewise materialized as real files or directories." T-INST-55g covers a symlinked **script** entry; this test extends that materialization rule to **non-script** content inside a selected workflow, which is a separate code path (workflow-content copy walks all files, not just discovered scripts). Source workflow `ralph/` containing a real script entry `ralph/index.sh` (so the workflow definitely qualifies), a real helper file `ralph/docs/source.md` (with deterministic content `"non-script content"`), and a top-level non-script symlink `ralph/README.md -> docs/source.md`. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/README.md` exists and is a **real file** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`), (c) the file content matches `docs/source.md` byte-for-byte (`"non-script content"`), and (d) `.loopx/ralph/docs/source.md` also exists as a real file with the same content (the symlink target inside the workflow is preserved alongside the materialized symlink — both copies exist on disk). A buggy implementation that only materialized symlinks for files matching script-extension patterns would either preserve `README.md` as a symlink (failing assertion (b)) or skip it entirely (failing assertion (c)). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.3, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55k**: Symlinked **non-script directory** inside a selected workflow is materialized as a real directory. SPEC 10.11 normatively says "Symlinked non-script files or directories inside a selected workflow's copied content are likewise materialized as real files or directories." T-INST-55f covers a symlinked top-level **workflow** directory; this test extends the materialization rule to **non-script directories nested inside** a selected workflow's copied content (a separate code path — top-level workflow detection follows symlinks for classification, but this test exercises symlinked subdirectories that are part of workflow content rather than the workflow root itself). Source workflow `ralph/` containing a real script entry `ralph/index.sh` (so the workflow qualifies), a real shared-assets directory `ralph/shared-assets/` containing files `ralph/shared-assets/asset1.txt` (content `"asset-one"`) and `ralph/shared-assets/asset2.txt` (content `"asset-two"`), and a top-level symlinked directory `ralph/assets -> shared-assets`. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/assets` exists and is a **real directory** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`), (c) `.loopx/ralph/assets/asset1.txt` and `.loopx/ralph/assets/asset2.txt` are both real files with content `"asset-one"` and `"asset-two"` respectively, and (d) `.loopx/ralph/shared-assets/` also exists as a real directory with the same files (both the symlinked spelling `assets/` and the original target `shared-assets/` are present in the destination). A buggy implementation that only materialized file symlinks (and preserved directory symlinks) would fail assertion (b). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.3, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55l**: Broken / cyclic / out-of-source **non-script** symlinks inside a selected workflow are rejected preflight-atomically. SPEC 10.11's rejection rule applies to "symlinks that are part of a selected workflow" without restricting to script entries — non-script symlinked content inside a selected workflow must satisfy the same target-resolution rule. T-INST-55h covers the rejection rule for symlinked **script** entries; this test extends the rejection rule to **non-script** symlinked content. The same fixture-distinctness requirement as T-INST-55h applies — the three sub-cases must keep the SPEC 10.11 failure classes distinct (broken vs. cycle vs. existing-but-out-of-source). Three sub-cases, each tested independently, each retaining a real `ralph/index.sh` so the workflow definitely qualifies and the test isolates the non-script-symlink rejection branch:
    - **(l-broken)**: Source workflow `ralph/` contains real `ralph/index.sh` plus a non-script symlink `ralph/README.md -> nonexistent-target` (target does not exist anywhere on disk).
    - **(l-cycle)**: Source workflow `ralph/` contains real `ralph/index.sh` plus three non-script symlinks `ralph/README.md -> a`, `ralph/a -> b`, `ralph/b -> a` (a cycle in the symlink chain — `README.md` resolves through a cycle that never terminates at a real file).
    - **(l-out-of-source)**: Before constructing the install source, the test creates a real, controlled file at an absolute path **outside** the install source root (e.g., `<test-isolated-tmp>/outside-of-source/real-readme.md` containing deterministic content — a plain `"out-of-source non-script content"` text file); the install source's `ralph/README.md` is a non-script symlink whose **target is the absolute path** of that out-of-source file (or a reliably resolving relative path that traverses **above** the install source root and lands on the controlled file). The test must verify before invoking `loopx install` that the symlink resolves to an existing file via `fs.realpath` from the install source root — this discriminates this sub-case from `(l-broken)`. The target is created in a test-isolated temp directory (not in `/etc/`, `/usr/`, or any system path).

  For each sub-case, run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — `.loopx/` either does not exist or contains no `.loopx/ralph/` entry (no partial install was committed), and (c) stderr contains an error message identifying the failing non-script symlink (the test does not pin the exact wording, only that `ralph` and `README.md` are mentioned in the error context). For sub-case `(l-out-of-source)` specifically, the test must additionally verify that the controlled out-of-source target file still exists after the install fails — proving the rejection happened before any copy step touched it (and giving an extra signal that the test's preflight-traversal verification ran on a real existing file, not a broken target). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** A buggy implementation that validated only script-entry symlinks (T-INST-55h) and ignored non-script symlinked content would commit a workflow with a broken `README.md` reference. *(Spec 10.11, 10.7, 10.9, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55m**: Top-level workflow **directory** symlink resolving outside the install source root is rejected. SPEC 10.11's "Symlink targets must resolve to existing paths within the install source root" rule applies to all symlinks that are part of selected workflows, including the top-level workflow directory entry itself (not just symlinks nested inside a workflow). T-INST-55h sub-case (h-out-of-source) covers a **script-entry** symlink resolving outside the source root; this test extends that rule to a **top-level workflow directory** symlink. Multi-workflow source root containing a top-level symlink `alias -> /tmp/outside-workflow` where `/tmp/outside-workflow` is created outside the install source root and contains `index.sh` (so the symlink target itself qualifies as a workflow shape — proving the test isolates "out-of-source" rejection rather than "target is not a workflow" classification skip). Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`, so all qualifying workflows would be installed). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — `.loopx/alias/` does not exist (no partial install was committed), and (c) stderr contains an error message identifying the failing top-level workflow symlink `alias`. A buggy implementation that validated script-entry symlinks (T-INST-55h sub-case h-out-of-source) but skipped validation for top-level workflow-directory symlinks would commit `.loopx/alias/` containing arbitrary out-of-source content, defeating the SPEC 10.11 source-containment guarantee. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. For the tarball source, the test creates the symlink during tarball construction (e.g., `tar` preserves symlinks); for git, the symlink is committed as a symlink object via standard git mechanisms. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.7, 10.9, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55n**: Symlinked workflow content whose target is **inside the install source root** but **outside the selected workflow** is materialized into the destination, while the source-root support directory holding the target is itself **not** copied. SPEC 10.11 says symlink targets must resolve within the **install source root** — not within the workflow directory itself, and SPEC 10.3 says non-workflow source-root subdirectories are silently skipped (do not appear in the destination). The combination is non-trivial: a workflow can legitimately reference content from a sibling source-root support directory (e.g., a `shared/` directory of READMEs or assets), and the install must materialize that referenced content into the workflow's destination directory while still suppressing the support directory itself from `.loopx/`. T-INST-55j / T-INST-55k cover symlinks whose targets are inside the **workflow directory**; this test closes the gap for symlinks whose targets are inside the source root but **outside** the workflow directory — a separate code path because the materialization walk must reach across the workflow boundary to the source root, not stay within the workflow. Two sub-cases, each tested independently (parameterized across git and tarball sources per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11):
    - **(n-non-script-file)**: Source root contains a workflow directory `ralph/` (with a real `ralph/index.sh` so it qualifies) plus a sibling **support directory** `shared/` (containing the real file `shared/README.md` with deterministic content `"shared readme content"`). Inside `ralph/`, a non-script symlink `ralph/README.md -> ../shared/README.md` points up to the source-root sibling. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is committed and contains real `index.sh` and a real materialized `README.md` (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false` for `.loopx/ralph/README.md`), (c) the materialized `README.md` content matches `shared/README.md` byte-for-byte (`"shared readme content"`), (d) `.loopx/shared/` does **not** exist — the source-root support directory holding the symlink target is not copied as a top-level entry in `.loopx/` (per SPEC 10.3 self-containment for non-workflow subdirectories), and (e) `.loopx/ralph/shared-assets` (or any other manifestation of the support directory inside the workflow) does not appear unless the test placed it there. The "shared" directory is referenced through a symlink but is not itself a workflow nor a workflow's content.
    - **(n-non-script-directory)**: Source root contains a workflow directory `ralph/` (with a real `ralph/index.sh`) plus a sibling support directory `shared-assets/` containing real files `shared-assets/asset1.txt` (content `"asset-one"`) and `shared-assets/asset2.txt` (content `"asset-two"`). Inside `ralph/`, a top-level symlinked directory `ralph/assets -> ../shared-assets/` points up to the source-root sibling. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/assets` is a **real directory** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`) containing real files `asset1.txt` and `asset2.txt` with content `"asset-one"` and `"asset-two"` respectively, and (c) `.loopx/shared-assets/` does **not** exist as a top-level destination entry — the source-root support directory is not copied as a top-level workflow.

    Additionally for both sub-cases, run a `-w ralph` selective-install variant (`loopx install --no-install -w ralph <source>`) and assert the same destination shape — proving the cross-workflow-to-source-root materialization works under selective install too (SPEC 10.8 / 10.11 interaction). A buggy implementation that resolved symlink targets only relative to the workflow directory (rather than the install source root) would reject this fixture as "out-of-source" — incorrectly equating the workflow boundary with the source-root boundary. This test is critical because the SPEC 10.11 + SPEC 10.3 + SPEC 10.8 interaction has no other coverage: existing symlink tests have targets either inside the workflow itself (T-INST-55j / T-INST-55k) or strictly outside the source root (T-INST-55h / T-INST-55l / T-INST-55m), but neither exercises the legitimate "in-source-root, out-of-workflow" middle ground that SPEC 10.11 explicitly permits. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.3, 10.8, 10.2)*

- **T-INST-55o**: Install-time symlink **alias-name validation** uses the alias's own name, not the target's. SPEC 10.11 says install-time discovery follows symlinks under the same entry-type policy as runtime, and that names are derived from the symlink entry's own name. Combined with SPEC 10.4's name-restriction rule (which applies to install-time validation), this means a symlink alias whose **own** name is invalid must fail install, even when the target's basename is valid (and vice-versa for the runtime counterparts at T-DISC-40c / T-DISC-40d / T-DISC-40h / T-DISC-40i). The install-source-symlink policy on the negative side is currently uncovered. Two sub-cases, each tested independently:
    - **(o-workflow-alias-bad)**: Multi-workflow source root containing a real workflow directory `internal/good-workflow/` (with `index.sh`) and a top-level **invalid-named** symlink `-bad-alias -> internal/good-workflow/`. The alias's own name (`-bad-alias`) violates SPEC 5.3's name pattern (leading `-`); the target's basename (`good-workflow`) is valid. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`, so all qualifying workflows including the symlinked alias would otherwise be installed). Assert: (a) exit code 1 with a name-restriction error identifying `-bad-alias` (not the target basename), (b) `.loopx/-bad-alias/` does not exist, and (c) per atomicity (SPEC 10.7) no other workflow is committed either when the install fails preflight. This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-40c (run-mode runtime).
    - **(o-script-alias-bad)**: Single-workflow source whose root contains a workflow directory `ralph/` (with a real `ralph/index.sh`) and a top-level invalid-named **script** symlink `ralph/-bad.sh -> /<source-root>/lib/valid-helper.sh` (where the target file exists in the source root and has a valid basename). The script alias's own basename (`-bad`) violates SPEC 5.3; the target's basename (`valid-helper`) is valid. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 with a name-restriction error identifying `-bad.sh` in the `ralph` workflow (not the target's basename), (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is not committed (atomic preflight failure). This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-40d (run-mode runtime).

  Together with the runtime counterparts (T-DISC-40c / T-DISC-40d for invalid alias, T-DISC-40h / T-DISC-40i for valid-alias-bad-target), this closes the install-time symlink-alias-name validation surface. A buggy implementation that derived names from target basenames at install time (rather than from the symlink entry's own name per SPEC 10.11) would either accept this install (deriving the valid target basename and failing assertion (a)), or fire under the wrong path with the wrong identifier in the error message. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.4, 5.3, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-55p**: Install-time symlink **entry-type policy** rejects non-workflow / non-script targets uniformly with runtime. SPEC 10.11 explicitly says install-time discovery follows symlinks under the **same entry-type policy as runtime** (SPEC 5.1) — symlinks to non-directory targets at the workflow level, or symlinks to non-file targets at the script level, do not qualify as workflows or scripts respectively. T-DISC-40j / T-DISC-40k cover the runtime side (silently ignored at runtime); the install-time entry-type-mismatch surface is uncovered. Two sub-cases, each tested independently:
    - **(p-workflow-symlink-to-file)**: Source root containing a real workflow directory `ralph/` (with `index.sh`, valid) and a top-level symlink `notaworkflow -> README.md` where `README.md` is a real **regular file** in the source root with text content. The symlink resolves to a file, not a directory, so it does not qualify as a workflow per SPEC 5.1's entry-type policy. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is committed with `index.sh`, (c) `.loopx/notaworkflow` does **not** exist as a workflow at the destination (the symlink-to-file is silently skipped during workflow classification, same as T-DISC-40j at runtime), and (d) stderr emits no error or warning about `notaworkflow` (the entry-type-mismatch is not a name-restriction failure or a symlink-target-resolution failure — it is a non-workflow that gets silently skipped per SPEC 10.3 / 5.1).
    - **(p-script-symlink-to-directory)**: Single-workflow source where workflow `ralph/` contains a real `ralph/index.sh` (the workflow definitely qualifies) and a top-level symlink `ralph/notascript.sh -> docs/` where `docs/` is a real **directory** inside the workflow with non-script content (e.g., `ralph/docs/note.md` containing the deterministic byte-string `"docs note content"`). The symlink resolves to a directory, not a file, so it does not qualify as a script per SPEC 5.1's entry-type policy. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is committed and contains `index.sh`, (c) the `notascript` script does **not** appear as a discoverable script — verified by `loopx run -n 1 "ralph:notascript"` failing with a missing-script error (mirroring T-DISC-40k at runtime), (d) `.loopx/ralph/notascript.sh` exists, is a **real directory** (`fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false` and `fs.lstat(...).isDirectory()` is `true`), and contains `note.md` with byte-for-byte content `"docs note content"` matching the source `docs/` target — per SPEC 10.11's "Symlinked non-script files or directories inside a selected workflow's copied content are likewise materialized as real files or directories" rule (the entry is not classified as a script, but it is still a symlinked directory inside a selected workflow's copied content and must be materialized; this is the same rule pinned down at T-INST-55k for the symlinked-non-script-directory branch generally, applied here to the specific case where the symlink's name shape *would* satisfy script-naming if the entry-type test passed), and (e) stderr emits no name-restriction or entry-type warning. A buggy implementation that conflated "not classified as a script" with "skip from copy" would either omit `.loopx/ralph/notascript.sh` entirely (failing assertion (d)) or preserve the symlink unmaterialized (failing the `isSymbolicLink()` half of (d)).

  Together with the runtime counterparts (T-DISC-40j workflow-symlink-to-file, T-DISC-40k script-symlink-to-directory), this closes the install-time entry-type-policy parity surface. SPEC 10.11's "same entry-type policy as runtime" clause must hold uniformly across both sub-cases. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 5.1, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-55q**: Install-time symlink alias-name validation uses the alias's own name on the **positive** path — a valid alias name with a target whose basename is invalid still installs successfully. SPEC 10.11 says install-time discovery follows symlinks under the same entry-type policy as runtime, and that names are derived from the symlink entry's own name, not the target's basename. T-INST-55o covers the negative side (invalid alias name → install fails), but the positive symmetry — that an invalid **target** basename is irrelevant when the alias name itself is valid — is uncovered on the install-time surface (T-DISC-40h / T-DISC-40i are the runtime counterparts). A buggy implementation that derived names from target basenames at install time would fail this test in addition to T-INST-55o. Two sub-cases, each tested independently:
    - **(q-workflow-alias-good-target-bad)**: Before constructing the install source, the test creates a real workflow directory at an absolute path **outside the install source root** with an invalid basename (e.g., `<test-isolated-tmp>/-bad-real-workflow/` containing `index.sh`). The install source root contains a top-level symlink `goodalias -> <test-isolated-tmp>/-bad-real-workflow/`. *(Note: the symlink target is outside the install source root and would normally be rejected by SPEC 10.11's "Symlink targets must resolve to existing paths within the install source root" rule. To keep this test focused on the alias-name-positive-path question rather than the source-root-resolution rule, place the real workflow **inside** the install source root under a hidden support subdirectory: e.g., source root contains `internal/-bad-real-workflow/index.sh` and a top-level symlink `goodalias -> internal/-bad-real-workflow/`. The target's basename `-bad-real-workflow` is invalid, but the symlink resolves inside the source root, satisfying SPEC 10.11's source-containment rule. The source root also contains a real workflow directory `ralph/index.sh` so the source is unambiguously multi-workflow.)* Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/goodalias/` is committed and contains `index.sh` with the materialized target's content, (c) `.loopx/ralph/` is also committed with `index.sh`, (d) `.loopx/-bad-real-workflow/` does not exist as a top-level destination (the source-root support subdirectory `internal/` and its target are not copied as workflows — only the symlink alias materializes), and (e) stderr emits no name-restriction warning for either `goodalias` or `-bad-real-workflow`. This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-40h.
    - **(q-script-alias-good-target-bad)**: Source workflow `ralph/` contains a real `ralph/index.sh` and a real helper script with an invalid basename inside a workflow-internal support directory (e.g., `ralph/internal/-bad-real-script.sh` with executable bash content `#!/bin/bash` and a marker write). The workflow also contains a script symlink `ralph/goodcheck.sh -> internal/-bad-real-script.sh` (relative path, resolving inside the workflow). Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is committed and contains `index.sh` and `goodcheck.sh` (`goodcheck.sh` is materialized as a real file with the symlink target's content — `fs.lstat(...).isSymbolicLink()` is `false`), (c) the installed workflow is runnable via `loopx run -n 1 "ralph:goodcheck"` (the script is discovered using the alias basename `goodcheck`, not the target basename `-bad-real-script`), and (d) stderr emits no name-restriction warning for either `goodcheck.sh` or `-bad-real-script.sh`. This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-40i. The fixture places the bad-named target inside the workflow's own subdirectory (rather than outside the source root) so the test isolates the alias-name positive-path question from SPEC 10.11's source-containment rule; the workflow-internal support file is not classified as a script (install validation is non-recursive within workflows per T-INST-64a / T-INST-64d), so its invalid basename is never validated.
  
  Together with T-INST-55o (negative: invalid alias rejects), the runtime counterparts T-DISC-40h / T-DISC-40i (valid alias good target — basename ignored), and T-DISC-40c / T-DISC-40d (invalid alias name rejects at runtime), this closes the install-time alias-name-validation parity surface across both alias-name validity polarities. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.4, 5.3, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-55r**: Install-time same-base-name script collision fires when one of the colliding scripts is a symlink. SPEC 10.4 / 10.11 say install-time validation uses the same discovery rules as runtime (SPEC 5.1 / 5.2), and SPEC 10.11 says install-time discovery follows symlinks under the same entry-type policy as runtime. T-DISC-40e covers the runtime side (symlinked script participates in same-base-name collision detection — runtime fatal); T-INST-62 / T-INST-62a cover install-time same-base-name collisions for **regular** files; the install-time **symlinked-script** participation in same-base-name collision detection is uncovered. A buggy implementation that resolved symlinks during install-time discovery but skipped them during install-time same-base-name collision evaluation would pass T-DISC-40e and T-INST-62 / T-INST-62a yet fail to detect this collision at install time. Source workflow `ralph/` contains a real `ralph/check.sh` (with marker-write content), a real workflow-internal helper file `ralph/lib/helper.ts` (also with deterministic content — placed inside a subdirectory so it is not itself a top-level script), and a top-level script symlink `ralph/check.ts -> lib/helper.ts` (relative path, resolving inside the workflow). The real `check.sh` and the symlinked `check.ts` share the base name `check`, the same way as T-DISC-40e at runtime. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` does not exist (preflight-atomic per SPEC 10.7), and (c) stderr surfaces a same-base-name collision error that names both the regular entry `check.sh` and the symlinked entry `check.ts` in the `ralph` workflow. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.4, 5.1, 5.2, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-55s**: Top-level workflow-symlink **broken** target and **cyclic** target are rejected preflight-atomically. T-INST-55m covers a top-level workflow-directory symlink resolving outside the install source root; the broken-target and cycle branches of the same SPEC 10.11 rule ("Symlink targets must resolve to existing paths within the install source root") on top-level workflow-directory symlinks are uncovered. (T-INST-55h covers script-entry symlinks with broken / cycle / out-of-source targets; T-INST-55l covers non-script symlinks inside a workflow with the same three classes; T-INST-55m covers top-level workflow symlinks for the out-of-source class only.) Two sub-cases, each tested independently. The source root in both sub-cases also contains a real workflow directory `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) so the source is unambiguously multi-workflow.
    - **(s-broken)**: Source root contains a top-level symlink `alias -> missing-target` where `missing-target` does not exist anywhere on disk (neither inside the source root nor outside). Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — neither `.loopx/alias/` nor `.loopx/ralph/` is committed, and (c) stderr contains an error message identifying the failing top-level workflow symlink `alias` (the test does not pin exact wording, only that `alias` is mentioned and that the failure is symlink-target related — broken / unresolved).
    - **(s-cycle)**: Source root contains three top-level symlinks `alias -> a`, `a -> b`, `b -> a` — a cycle in the symlink chain. The chain never resolves to a real directory. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the install fails preflight-atomically — none of `.loopx/alias/`, `.loopx/a/`, `.loopx/b/`, or `.loopx/ralph/` is committed, and (c) stderr contains an error message identifying the failing top-level workflow symlink (the test does not pin which of `alias` / `a` / `b` is named first, only that at least one is named and the failure is symlink-cycle related). The test must also verify the implementation does not infinite-loop on the cycle (the install command must exit within a reasonable timeout — e.g., 10 seconds — proving symlink-cycle detection ran rather than the implementation looping until OS limits intervene).

  Together with T-INST-55m (top-level workflow symlink, out-of-source target — already covered) and T-INST-55h sub-cases (script-entry symlinks: broken, cycle, out-of-source), this closes the SPEC 10.11 broken / cycle / out-of-source rejection rule across the three symlink-entry kinds: script entries (T-INST-55h), non-script content inside workflows (T-INST-55l), and top-level workflow directories (T-INST-55m + this test). A buggy implementation that handled out-of-source rejection on top-level workflow symlinks (passing T-INST-55m) but skipped broken-target or cycle detection on the same code path would fail this test. **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.7, 10.9, 10.2)*
- **T-INST-55t**: Bad symlinks in **source-root support directories** are ignored under both full-multi-workflow install and `-w` selective install. SPEC 10.11 says "When `-w <name>` / `--workflow <name>` is used, only symlinks that are part of the selected workflow are validated and materialized. Symlinks in unselected workflow directories or source-root support directories are ignored, consistent with the selective validation rule in section 10.8." For full multi-workflow installs (no `-w`), SPEC 10.3's source-root-self-containment rule says non-workflow source-root directories are silently skipped — so symlinks in source-root support directories are also not validated or materialized at the destination. T-INST-55i covers bad symlinks in **unselected sibling workflow directories** under `-w`; the **source-root support-directory** branch is uncovered for both invocations (full and `-w`). A buggy implementation that walked source-root support directories during preflight (e.g., to compute the staging copy plan) and validated their symlinks would fail this test even though it passes T-INST-55i. Source root contains:
  - A real workflow directory `ralph/` (with `index.sh`).
  - A real workflow directory `other/` (with `index.sh`).
  - A source-root support directory `docs/` containing three sub-cases of bad symlinks side-by-side: `docs/broken-link -> nonexistent-target` (broken), `docs/cycle-a -> cycle-b`, `docs/cycle-b -> cycle-a` (cycle), and `docs/out-of-source -> /tmp/<test-isolated-out-of-source>/some-file` where the out-of-source target is a real, existing file outside the install source root (verified to exist via `fs.realpath` before invoking install — discriminates the out-of-source class from the broken class). Place all three classes inside `docs/` simultaneously so the test exercises all three SPEC 10.11 failure-class branches in the source-root-support-directory code path together.
  
  Two invocations:
    - **(t-full)**: Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (no `-w`). Assert: (a) exit code 0 (the source-root support directory is silently skipped per SPEC 10.3, so its bad symlinks are never validated), (b) `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` are committed with their respective `index.sh` files, (c) `.loopx/docs/` does not exist as a top-level destination (per SPEC 10.3 self-containment, source-root support directories are not copied), (d) `.loopx/ralph/docs/` and `.loopx/other/docs/` likewise do not exist (the support directory was not migrated into either workflow), and (e) stderr emits no symlink-target-resolution error or warning identifying any of `broken-link`, `cycle-a`, `cycle-b`, or `out-of-source`.
    - **(t-selective)**: Run `loopx install --no-install -w ralph <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is committed with `index.sh`, (c) `.loopx/other/` does not exist (selective install excludes other workflows), (d) `.loopx/docs/` does not exist, and (e) stderr emits no symlink-target-resolution error or warning. The selective-validation rule in SPEC 10.8 / 10.11 limits validation to the selected workflow only; the source-root support directory is not part of the selected workflow.
  
  Together with T-INST-55i (bad symlinks in unselected sibling workflow directories under `-w`, ignored), this closes the "bad symlinks outside the validated scope are ignored" surface across both off-scope locations (sibling workflow dirs vs. source-root support dirs) and both invocation modes (full and `-w`). **Parameterized across git and tarball sources** per SPEC 10.2 / 10.11. **`--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule.** *(Spec 10.11, 10.8, 10.3, 10.2)*

This block does **not** affect runtime symlink coverage or install-destination-collision symlink coverage (T-INST-70 and related), which remain separately covered.

#### Selective Workflow Installation

- **T-INST-57**: `loopx install -w ralph <multi-workflow-source>` installs only `ralph`. Assert `.loopx/ralph/` exists but `.loopx/other/` does not. *(Spec 10.8)*
- **T-INST-57a**: `loopx install --workflow ralph <multi-workflow-source>` is equivalent to `loopx install -w ralph <source>`. Assert `.loopx/ralph/` exists but `.loopx/other/` does not. This tests the long-form `--workflow` flag. *(Spec 4.2, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-57b**: `loopx install -w ralph <multi-workflow-source>` where the source root also contains `README.md`, `package.json`, `docs/`, `shared/`, and a sibling workflow `other/` (with `index.sh`) → only `.loopx/ralph/` is created. Assert: no source-root support files or directories (`README.md`, `package.json`, `docs/`, `shared/`) are copied into `.loopx/`, and `.loopx/other/` does not exist. This is the `-w` counterpart to T-INST-54 / T-INST-54c, which prove self-containment for full multi-workflow installs — the same rule must hold on the selective `-w` code path. *(Spec 10.3, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-57c**: `loopx install -w ralph <multi-workflow-tarball>` installs only `ralph`. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains subdirectories `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `other/` (with `index.sh`), with no root-level script files. Run `loopx install -w ralph <tarball-url>`. Assert: (a) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed and contains `index.sh`, and (b) `.loopx/other/` does not exist. T-INST-57 / T-INST-57a / T-INST-57b cover the `-w` selective-install path with git-backed sources; this test explicitly exercises the tarball source path. Per SPEC 10.2, classification after extraction is source-type-agnostic, so the selective-install behavior must hold identically for tarball and git sources — this closes the gap left by the otherwise git-heavy `-w` coverage. *(Spec 10.2, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-58**: `loopx install -w nonexistent <multi-workflow-source>` → error (workflow not in source). *(Spec 10.8)*
- **T-INST-59**: `loopx install -w ralph <single-workflow-source>` → error (`-w` is invalid for single-workflow sources). The source must have root-level script files (e.g., `index.ts`) **and** subdirectories containing supported-extension files (e.g., `lib/helpers.ts`, `src/utils.js`), so the test genuinely proves that root-level scripts force single-workflow classification even in the presence of subdirectories with script-extension files. Using `-w` is an error regardless of the name provided. *(Spec 10.3, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-59a**: `loopx install --workflow ralph <single-workflow-source>` → error. Long-form `--workflow` parity with T-INST-59: `--workflow` is invalid for single-workflow sources, same as `-w`. *(Spec 10.8, 4.2)*
- **T-INST-59b**: `loopx install -w ralph <single-workflow-source>` → error, where the source root contains a **non-index** script file (e.g., `setup.ts`) **and** subdirectories that would themselves qualify as workflows (e.g., `lib/helpers.ts`, `src/utils.js`). T-INST-59 / T-INST-59a use a root-level `index.ts` and so can be satisfied by an implementation that only treats `index.*` as the single-workflow trigger; this test uses a non-index root script so it proves the `-w` rejection fires for **any** root-level script file, not just the default entry point. Aligns the `-w` rejection coverage with T-INST-52c (which proves the same non-index root-script classification rule at the classification level). *(Spec 10.3, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-60**: With `-w`, only the selected workflow is validated. Invalid sibling workflows don't block installation. *(Spec 10.8)*
- **T-INST-60a**: With `-w`, source-validation failures in unselected sibling workflows do not block installation. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` is valid (contains `index.sh`) and `broken/` has an invalid script name (e.g., `broken/-bad.sh`). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds and `.loopx/ralph/` is created — the invalid script name in `broken/` is not evaluated because `-w ralph` limits preflight to the selected workflow only. *(Spec 10.8, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-60b**: With `-w`, version mismatches in unselected sibling workflows do not block installation and emit no warnings. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` has no version declaration (no `package.json`) and `other/` declares a loopx range not satisfied by the running version. `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds — `other/`'s version mismatch is not evaluated because `-w ralph` limits preflight to the selected workflow only. Assert: exit code 0 **and** stderr contains no version-mismatch warning for `other/`. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-60c**: With `-w`, destination collisions for unselected sibling workflows do not block installation. Multi-workflow source contains `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `other/` (with `index.sh`). `.loopx/other/` already exists as a workflow (contains at least one script file). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds without `-y` — `other/`'s destination collision is not evaluated because `-w ralph` limits preflight to the selected workflow only. *(Spec 10.8, 10.5)*
- **T-INST-60d**: With `-w`, an invalid workflow name on an unselected sibling does not block installation. Multi-workflow source contains `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `-bad-name/` (with `index.sh` — valid scripts but invalid workflow name). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds — the invalid workflow name in `-bad-name/` is not evaluated because `-w ralph` limits preflight to the selected workflow only. *(Spec 10.8, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-60e**: With `-w`, a same-base-name collision in an unselected sibling does not block installation. Multi-workflow source contains `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `other/` (with `check.sh` and `check.ts`). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds — `other/`'s same-base-name collision is not evaluated because `-w ralph` limits preflight to the selected workflow only. *(Spec 10.8, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-60f**: With `-w`, a broken `package.json` in an unselected sibling workflow emits no warning. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` is valid (contains `index.sh`, no `package.json`) and `broken/` contains `index.sh` plus a `package.json` with invalid JSON content (e.g., `{broken`). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` succeeds. Assert: no `package.json`-related warning or error on stderr. The broken sibling's `package.json` is never read because `-w ralph` limits all validation — including version checking — to the selected workflow only. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-60g**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own invalid script names are still fatal. Multi-workflow source where `broken/` contains `-bad.sh` (invalid script name) and `ralph/` is valid (contains `index.sh`). `loopx install -w broken <source>` → exits with code 1. T-INST-60a proves unselected siblings' invalid names are ignored; this proves the selected workflow itself is not exempt from install-time validation under `-w`. *(Spec 10.8, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-60h**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own same-base-name collisions are still fatal. Multi-workflow source where `broken/` contains `check.sh` and `check.ts` (same base name, different extensions) and `ralph/` is valid (contains `index.sh`). `loopx install -w broken <source>` → exits with code 1. T-INST-60e proves unselected siblings' collisions are ignored; this proves the selected workflow itself is still checked. *(Spec 10.8, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-60i**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own version mismatch is still a blocking error. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains `index.sh` plus a `package.json` declaring a `loopx` version range not satisfied by the running version, and `other/` is valid (contains `index.sh`, no `package.json`). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` → exits with code 1. T-INST-60b proves unselected siblings' version mismatches are ignored; this proves the selected workflow's version declaration is still enforced under `-w`. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-60j**: With `-w -y`, the selected workflow's version mismatch is overridden. Same source as T-INST-60i. `loopx install -w ralph -y --no-install <source>` succeeds (the command line is written with `-w ralph` separated from `-y` so that `ralph` is unambiguously the `-w` operand and not treated as a `-y` operand or positional). Assert `.loopx/ralph/` is created and contains `index.sh` and the workflow's `package.json` with its version declaration preserved. **`--no-install` is included per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the workflow has a top-level `package.json`, so auto-install would otherwise fire after the `-y` override unblocks the commit; this test's contract is the `-w -y` version-mismatch-override behavior. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-60k**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own destination collision is still a blocking error. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains `index.sh` and `other/` is valid (contains `index.sh`). `.loopx/ralph/` already exists as a workflow (contains at least one top-level script file). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` → exits with code 1. T-INST-60c proves unselected siblings' destination collisions are ignored; this proves the selected workflow's collision is still enforced under `-w`. *(Spec 10.8, 10.5)*
- **T-INST-60l**: With `-w -y`, the selected workflow's destination collision is overridden. Same setup as T-INST-60k. `loopx install -w ralph -y <source>` succeeds (the command line is written with `-w ralph` separated from `-y` so that `ralph` is unambiguously the `-w` operand and not treated as a `-y` operand or positional). Assert `.loopx/ralph/` contains the new workflow content from the source (not the old content). `.loopx/other/` does not exist — only the selected workflow is installed. *(Spec 10.8, 10.5)*
- **T-INST-60p**: With `-w -y`, a non-workflow destination for the selected workflow still causes failure. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains `index.sh` and `other/` is valid (contains `index.sh`). `.loopx/ralph` already exists but is not a workflow by structure (e.g., a plain file, an empty directory, or a symlink to a non-workflow target). `loopx install -w ralph -y <source>` → exits with code 1. The `-y` flag overrides workflow-path collisions and version mismatches, but does not override non-workflow destination paths — even on the selective `-w` code path. T-INST-60k/60l cover the `-w` collision path where the destination is a real workflow; T-INST-68/69/70a/70c cover non-workflow collision refusal on the generic (non-`-w`) path. This combination is worth testing because `-w` often gets its own code path. *(Spec 10.8, 10.5)*
- **T-INST-60m**: With `-w`, selecting a workflow with an invalid name causes install to fail. Multi-workflow source contains `bad.name/` (with `index.sh` — valid scripts but invalid workflow name because `.` is not permitted by SPEC 5.3's `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*` pattern) and `good/` (with `index.sh`). `loopx install -w bad.name <source>` → exits with code 1. T-INST-60d proves an unselected invalid workflow name does not block `-w`; this proves the selected workflow's name is still validated even under `-w`. The invalid name is deliberately chosen to **not** begin with `-` so that `-w bad.name` cannot be misread as `-w` consuming a hypothetical unrecognized flag — the test validates selected-workflow name validation, not flag-parsing edge cases. *(Spec 10.8, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-60n**: `--workflow` long form exercises the same selective-validation semantics as `-w`. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` is valid (contains `index.sh`) and `broken/` has an invalid script name (e.g., `broken/-bad.sh`). `.loopx/` initially empty. `loopx install --workflow ralph <source>` succeeds and `.loopx/ralph/` is created — the invalid script name in `broken/` is not evaluated. This mirrors T-INST-60a but uses the `--workflow` long form to verify that selective validation is not inadvertently tied to the short `-w` parsing path. *(Spec 10.8, 10.4, 4.2)*
- **T-INST-60o**: With `-w`, selecting a subdirectory that does not qualify as a workflow is an error. Multi-workflow source contains `good/index.sh` and `legacy/package.json` (with `{ "main": "src/run.js" }`) plus `legacy/src/run.js`, but no top-level script files inside `legacy/`. `loopx install -w legacy <source>` → exits with code 1 (named workflow does not exist in source). `legacy/` is not a workflow because it has no top-level supported-extension files — the `-w` flag does not cause loopx to relax workflow detection rules. This is the selective-install counterpart to T-INST-55e (which covers the same legacy subdirectory being silently skipped during full multi-workflow install). *(Spec 10.8, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-60q**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own `package.json` with invalid JSON emits a single non-fatal warning and the install still succeeds. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` contains `index.sh` plus a `package.json` with invalid JSON content (e.g., `{broken`), and `other/` is valid (contains `index.sh`, no `package.json`). `loopx install -w ralph <source>` → exit code 0. Assert: (a) stderr contains exactly one `package.json` parse-failure warning for `ralph`, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed and contains both `index.sh` and the broken `package.json` (file is preserved as-is), and (c) `.loopx/other/` does not exist. T-INST-75 proves invalid JSON emits a non-fatal warning on the generic install path; T-INST-60f proves the warning is suppressed for unselected siblings under `-w`; this test pins down that the selected workflow under `-w` still receives the normal non-fatal warning behavior — the `-w` code path does not accidentally promote the warning to a fatal error or suppress it entirely for the selected workflow. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-60r**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own unreadable `package.json` emits a single non-fatal warning and the install still succeeds. Same structure as T-INST-60q, but `ralph/package.json` is set to mode 000 (unreadable) instead of containing invalid JSON. **Source must be a tarball** (or another non-git source path), since Git does not reliably preserve mode 000 file permissions across clone. `loopx install -w ralph <tarball-url>` → exit code 0. Assert: (a) stderr contains exactly one `package.json` read-failure warning for `ralph`, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed and contains `index.sh` and a `package.json` entry at `.loopx/ralph/package.json` (the file's committed permission mode is **not** asserted, for the same reason as T-INST-74a — SPEC 10.6 / 3.2 / 10.7 require the warning and the install to proceed but do not require destination mode 000 preservation), and (c) `.loopx/other/` does not exist. This is the unreadable-file counterpart to T-INST-60q (invalid JSON) — T-INST-74 covers the generic path; this test covers the selective `-w` path. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 10.8, 10.6, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-60s**: With `-w`, the selected workflow's own `package.json` with an invalid semver range for `loopx` emits a single non-fatal warning and the install still succeeds. Same structure as T-INST-60q, but `ralph/package.json` contains valid JSON with an invalid semver range (e.g., `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }`) instead of invalid JSON. `loopx install -w ralph <source>` → exit code 0. Assert: (a) stderr contains exactly one invalid-semver warning for `ralph`, (b) `.loopx/ralph/` is installed and contains `index.sh` and the `package.json` with its original (invalid) range preserved, and (c) `.loopx/other/` does not exist. This is the invalid-semver counterpart to T-INST-60q (invalid JSON) and T-INST-60r (unreadable) — T-INST-76 covers the generic path; this test covers the selective `-w` path. Together, T-INST-60q/60r/60s prove the `-w` code path preserves the non-fatal-warning behavior across all three `package.json` failure branches for the selected workflow. *(Spec 10.8, 10.6, 3.2)*

#### Install-time Validation

- **T-INST-61**: Workflow with invalid script name (e.g., `-bad.sh`) → install fails. *(Spec 10.4)*
- **T-INST-62**: Workflow with same-base-name collision (e.g., `check.sh` and `check.ts`) → install fails. *(Spec 10.4)*
- **T-INST-62a**: Workflow source with both `index.sh` and `index.ts` → install fails. This is the `index`-specific variant of T-INST-62 — a buggy installer could special-case `index` and still pass the generic same-base-name collision test (T-INST-62). *(Spec 10.4, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-63**: Derived workflow name must match name restriction rules. Invalid name → install fails. *(Spec 10.4)*
- **T-INST-63a**: Digit-start workflow name is valid at install time. Single-workflow source whose derived name starts with a digit (e.g., repo named `3checks` containing `check.sh`). `loopx install <source>` succeeds and installs to `.loopx/3checks/`. This covers the install-time workflow-name validation path separately from runtime discovery (T-DISC-26a/26b), since install has its own name-validation code path. *(Spec 10.4, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-63b**: Install rejects a workflow name containing `:`. Source whose derived workflow name includes a colon (e.g., a tarball archive named `my:workflow.tar.gz` containing root-level `index.sh`). `loopx install <source>` → exits with code 1. The `:` is the reserved target delimiter and is explicitly disallowed in names. This mirrors runtime discovery coverage (T-DISC-32) at the install-time validation code path. *(Spec 10.4, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-63c**: Install rejects a script base name containing `:`. Single-workflow source containing a script file with `:` in its base name (e.g., `check:ready.sh` alongside a valid `index.sh`). `loopx install <source>` → exits with code 1. This mirrors runtime discovery coverage (T-DISC-31) at the install-time validation code path. *(Spec 10.4, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-63d**: Numeric script names are valid at install time. Single-workflow source containing `index.sh`, `1start.sh`, and `42.sh`. `loopx install <source>` succeeds. Assert `.loopx/<repo-name>/` contains all three scripts. This is the install-time positive counterpart to T-DISC-30a/30b (digit-start and all-digit script names valid at runtime discovery) — runtime naming allows these, but install-time script-name validation is only covered explicitly with negative cases (T-INST-61, T-INST-63c) without a symmetric positive case for numeric names. *(Spec 10.4, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-63g**: Underscore-prefix script base name is valid at install time. SPEC 10.4 / 5.3's name pattern accepts `_` as the leading character. Single-workflow source containing `index.sh` and `_check.sh` (a non-`index` script with a leading underscore). `loopx install <source>` succeeds. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/<repo-name>/_check.sh` is present with its original name, and (c) the installed workflow is runnable via `loopx run -n 1 "<repo-name>:_check"`. This is the install-time positive counterpart to T-DISC-30e (runtime), closing the underscore-prefix script-name coverage gap on the install-time name-validation code path. T-INST-63d already covers digit-first and all-digit script names at install time; this test extends that pattern to the underscore-prefix branch, exercising the same `_`-as-first-character branch of the name-pattern validator that workflow-name coverage exercises via T-DISC-26 (`_underscore`). *(Spec 10.4, 5.3, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-63f**: Uppercase workflow and script names are valid at install time.
