Multi-workflow source whose derived workflow name is `MyWorkflow` (directory `MyWorkflow/` at the source root), containing `MyWorkflow/index.sh` and `MyWorkflow/CheckReady.sh` (uppercase-starting script base name alongside the `index` entry). `loopx install <source>` succeeds. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the installed workflow directory is exactly `.loopx/MyWorkflow/` (case-preserving, not lowercased), (c) both `.loopx/MyWorkflow/index.sh` and `.loopx/MyWorkflow/CheckReady.sh` are present with their original case, and (d) the installed workflow is runnable via the case-preserving target `loopx run -n 1 "MyWorkflow:CheckReady"`. This is the install-time positive counterpart to T-DISC-30c / T-DISC-30d — the name-pattern validator at install time (SPEC 10.4) must accept the same uppercase forms that runtime discovery (SPEC 5.3) accepts. An implementation that lowercased derived workflow names during install-time validation would install to `.loopx/myworkflow/` and break the case-preserving guarantee. *(Spec 10.4, 5.3)*
- **T-INST-63e**: Install rejects a multi-workflow subdirectory name containing `:`. Multi-workflow source whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains a subdirectory named with `:` (e.g., `foo:bar/index.sh`) alongside a valid sibling workflow (e.g., `other/index.sh`) and no root-level script files. `loopx install <source>` → exits with code 1 with a name-restriction error identifying the offending subdirectory name. Per the install atomicity rules (Spec 10.7), no workflows are written — assert `.loopx/foo:bar/` does not exist AND `.loopx/other/` does not exist. T-INST-63b covers the single-workflow tarball archive-name derivation path (archive name `my:workflow.tar.gz`); this test exercises the **second** install-time workflow name source — multi-workflow subdirectory-name derivation — ensuring ADR-0003's reserved-delimiter `:` rule fires on both derivation paths. A buggy implementation could reject `:` only in the archive-name path and silently install (or skip) a `:`-named subdirectory in the multi-workflow path. *(Spec 10.4, 10.3, 5.3, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-64**: Missing `index` script is allowed at install time for single-workflow sources. A single-workflow source whose root contains `check.sh` but no `index.*` file. `loopx install <source>` succeeds. Assert the installed workflow is present under `.loopx/<repo-name>/` and contains `check.sh`. This explicitly covers the single-workflow classification path introduced by ADR-0003 — root-level script files make the source single-workflow, and missing `index` is still allowed. A bug could exist where a multi-workflow subdirectory can omit `index` but a single-workflow source incorrectly requires it. *(Spec 10.4, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-64b**: Missing `index` script is allowed at install time for multi-workflow sources. Multi-workflow source with `tools/check.sh` (no `tools/index.*`) and `other/index.sh` (to force multi-workflow classification). `loopx install <source>` succeeds. Assert both `.loopx/tools/` and `.loopx/other/` are installed, and `.loopx/tools/` contains `check.sh`. This complements the single-workflow path (T-INST-64) — a bug could allow missing index in single-workflow sources but incorrectly require it in multi-workflow subdirectories. *(Spec 10.4, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-64c**: Missing `index` script is allowed at install time for multi-workflow sources with `-w` scoping. Same source as T-INST-64b. `loopx install -w tools <source>` succeeds. Assert `.loopx/tools/` is installed and contains `check.sh`, and `.loopx/other/` does not exist. This covers the selective-install path for a workflow without an index script. *(Spec 10.4, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-64a**: Install-time validation is non-recursive within a workflow. Single-workflow source has a top-level `index.ts` plus a nested `lib/` subdirectory containing `-bad.ts` (invalid script name) and both `check.sh` and `check.ts` (same-base-name collision). Install succeeds because nested files within a workflow are not discovered or validated as scripts — only top-level files are checked. Assert exit code 0 and that `.loopx/<repo-name>/lib/-bad.ts`, `.loopx/<repo-name>/lib/check.sh`, and `.loopx/<repo-name>/lib/check.ts` are all present in the installed workflow (copied as workflow content, not rejected as invalid scripts). *(Spec 10.4, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-64d**: Install-time validation is non-recursive within workflows in a multi-workflow source. Multi-workflow source with two workflows: `ralph/index.sh` plus `ralph/lib/-bad.ts` (invalid script name) and `ralph/lib/check.sh` + `ralph/lib/check.ts` (same-base-name collision), and `other/index.sh` (a second workflow to force multi-workflow classification). Install succeeds because nested files within a workflow are not discovered or validated as scripts — only top-level files are checked. Assert exit code 0 and that `.loopx/ralph/lib/-bad.ts`, `.loopx/ralph/lib/check.sh`, and `.loopx/ralph/lib/check.ts` are all present in the installed workflow (copied as workflow content, not rejected as invalid scripts). This is the multi-workflow counterpart to T-INST-64a (which covers the single-workflow path). *(Spec 10.4, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-64e**: Install copies workflow content that includes a top-level directory whose name shape matches a script extension, but does **not** discover or validate that directory as a script. SPEC 10.4 says install-time validation uses the same discovery rules as runtime (SPEC 5.1), and SPEC 5.1 requires scripts to be top-level **files** with supported extensions; a directory whose name happens to end in `.sh`/`.ts`/`.js`/`.jsx`/`.tsx` is not a file. Set up a single-workflow source whose root contains a valid `index.sh` (a real file) **plus** a top-level directory named `bad.name.ts/` (created with `mkdir -p bad.name.ts`, optionally containing inner content e.g., `bad.name.ts/notes.md`). The directory name `bad.name.ts` would be invalid as a script base name (contains `.`) if it were treated as a script, so any implementation that wrongly classifies it as a script will surface as an install failure. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>` (the workflow has no top-level `package.json`, but `--no-install` is included for forward-compatibility under the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/<name>/index.sh` exists (classified and copied as a script), (c) `.loopx/<name>/bad.name.ts/` exists as a directory and `.loopx/<name>/bad.name.ts/notes.md` exists byte-for-byte (the entire entry is preserved as workflow content under SPEC 10.3's self-containment rule), (d) stderr contains no install-time validation warning for `bad.name.ts` (the directory was not validated as a script — install validation is non-recursive within workflows per T-INST-64a/T-INST-64d, and the top-level directory was not classified as a script in the first place because it is not a file), and (e) at runtime, `loopx run -h` lists `<name>` with `index` as its only discovered script — `bad.name.ts` does not appear. A buggy implementation that walked workflow contents by name pattern without checking entry type would wrongly validate `bad.name.ts` as a script and fail install with an invalid-script-name error. This is the install-time counterpart to T-DISC-09b (runtime non-discovery of directory-shaped script-named entries). *(Spec 10.3, 10.4, 5.1, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-52b**: Multi-workflow source with `ralph/index.sh`, `ralph/eslint.config.js`, and `other/index.sh` → install fails because every top-level file with a supported extension inside a workflow directory is a discovered script, and `eslint.config` violates the script name restriction `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*` (the dot is not allowed). Assert exit code 1 and that the error references the invalid script name. This is the install-time mirror of T-DISC-15a / T-DISC-15b, which test the same "no opt-out" rule at runtime discovery — a buggy installer could special-case config/helper-style files during workflow-directory scanning and still pass the current suite. *(Spec 2.1, 5.3, 10.4)*

#### Collision Handling

- **T-INST-65**: Path does not exist → workflow installed without collision check. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-66**: Path exists and is a workflow by structure → install refused with error. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-67**: Path exists and is a workflow by structure, with `-y` → existing workflow replaced. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-67a**: Path exists and is a workflow by structure but would fail runtime validation, with `-y` → existing workflow replaced. Set up `.loopx/foo/` containing a script with an invalid name (`-bad.sh`) and a same-base-name collision (`check.sh` + `check.ts`). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a valid workflow named `foo`. Assert the existing invalid workflow is replaced successfully. The collision structural check requires only that the destination path is a directory with at least one top-level file with a supported script extension — it does not perform full runtime validation. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-68**: Path exists but is NOT a workflow by structure (e.g., directory with no script files, or a non-directory entry) → install refused with error, even with `-y`. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-69**: `-y` must not replace non-workflow entries. A plain file at `.loopx/<name>` is refused even with `-y`. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70**: Symlinked workflow collision: structural check follows the symlink target. `.loopx/foo` is a symlink pointing to a directory that contains at least one top-level file with a supported script extension. `loopx install <source>` where the source contains a workflow named `foo` → install refused with collision error. The structural check followed the symlink and found a workflow at the target. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70b**: Symlinked workflow with `-y`: removes the symlink itself, not the target directory. `.loopx/foo` is a symlink pointing to a directory that qualifies as a workflow (contains a top-level script file). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a workflow named `foo` → install succeeds. Assert: (a) `.loopx/foo` is now a regular directory (not a symlink) containing the new workflow content, and (b) the original symlink target directory still exists and is unmodified. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70a**: Symlink-to-non-workflow collision refusal. `.loopx/foo` is a symlink whose target is a directory with no top-level script files (e.g., the target contains only `lib/helper.ts` nested in a subdirectory). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a workflow named `foo` → install refuses with an error, even with `-y`. The structural check follows the symlink, finds the target is not a workflow (no top-level script files), and treats it as a non-workflow destination path. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70e**: Broken-symlink collision — install refuses as non-workflow destination even with `-y`, and `-y` does not remove the broken symlink. Per SPEC 10.5, "Collision is determined by whether any filesystem entry (file, directory, or symlink) already exists at `.loopx/<workflow-name>`" — a broken symlink (dangling target) is a filesystem entry, so collision applies. And "Path exists but is not a workflow by structure ... : The install is refused with an error, even with `-y`. This prevents `-y` from accidentally deleting non-workflow data." A broken symlink cannot be a workflow by structure (it has no target directory), so `-y` must not replace it. Setup: create `.loopx/foo` as a symlink whose target does **not** exist (e.g., `ln -s /tmp/nonexistent-target/ /project/.loopx/foo`). Case A: run `loopx install <source>` (without `-y`) where the source contains a valid workflow named `foo`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 with a collision / non-workflow-destination error, (b) `.loopx/foo` is still the original broken symlink (symlink entry unchanged, target path still non-existent), and (c) no workflow content was written. Case B: run `loopx install -y <source>`. Assert the same three outcomes — `-y` must **not** treat a broken symlink as a replaceable entry, because a broken symlink is not a workflow by structure and SPEC 10.5 forbids `-y` from deleting non-workflow data. Together with T-INST-70/70a/70b/70c/70d (various symlink-to-something cases), this closes the "broken symlink" branch of symlink-based collision coverage — the symlink structural check follows symlinks per Spec 10.5, so a dangling target is observable as "the target does not qualify as a workflow" and must be refused. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70c**: Symlink-to-file collision refusal. `.loopx/foo` is a symlink whose target is a regular file (not a directory). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a workflow named `foo` → install refuses with an error, even with `-y`. A symlink to a file is a non-directory filesystem entry — it cannot be a workflow by structure (workflows are directories containing script files), so `-y` must not replace it. This completes the symlink/non-directory branch: T-INST-70/70b cover symlink-to-workflow-directory, T-INST-70a covers symlink-to-non-workflow-directory, and this test covers symlink-to-file. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-70d**: Symlink-to-structurally-valid-but-full-validation-invalid workflow is replaceable with `-y`. Create a directory outside `.loopx/` (e.g., `/tmp/invalid-workflow/`) that is structurally a workflow (contains at least one top-level file with a supported script extension, e.g., `index.sh`) but would fail full runtime validation — it contains a same-base-name collision (`check.sh` and `check.ts`) and an invalid script name (`-bad.sh`). Then symlink `.loopx/foo → /tmp/invalid-workflow/`. Run `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a valid workflow named `foo`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/foo` is now a regular directory (symlink replaced) containing the new workflow content, and (c) the original symlink target directory `/tmp/invalid-workflow/` still exists and is unmodified (symlink itself was removed, not the target). T-INST-67a proves this "local structural check only" rule for regular-directory replacements, and T-INST-70b proves symlink-to-valid-workflow replaces; this test completes the coverage by proving the rule holds on the symlink path even when the target would fail full validation. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-71**: `-y` replaceability check is local to `.loopx/<workflow-name>` — broken siblings elsewhere under `.loopx/` do not affect collision evaluation. Set up: `.loopx/foo/` exists as a replaceable workflow (contains at least one top-level file with a supported script extension), and an unrelated sibling `.loopx/broken/` exists as a non-workflow directory (e.g., contains only a `README.md` and no script files). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a valid workflow named `foo`. Assert: install succeeds and `.loopx/foo/` is replaced with the new workflow content. The structural check inspects only the target path `.loopx/foo/`, not the entire `.loopx/` tree — the broken sibling is irrelevant. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-71a**: `-y` replaceability check is local to `.loopx/<workflow-name>` — invalid sibling *workflows* elsewhere under `.loopx/` do not affect collision evaluation. Set up: `.loopx/foo/` exists as a replaceable workflow (contains `index.sh`), and `.loopx/broken/` exists as a workflow that would fail runtime validation (e.g., contains `check.sh` and `check.ts` — same-base-name collision, or `-bad.sh` — invalid script name). `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a valid workflow named `foo`. Assert: install succeeds and `.loopx/foo/` is replaced with the new workflow content. This strengthens T-INST-71 (which uses a non-workflow sibling) to prove that even invalid workflows elsewhere under `.loopx/` are ignored during collision evaluation — the install mechanism inspects only the target path. *(Spec 10.5)*
- **T-INST-97c**: Installing workflow `foo` succeeds when a non-workflow file `.loopx/foo.sh` exists. The collision check targets `.loopx/foo/` (directory), not `.loopx/foo.sh` (file) — they are different filesystem paths, so there is no collision. *(Spec 10.5)*

#### Version Checking on Install

- **T-INST-72**: Workflow declares a loopx version range not satisfied by running version → install refused with error. *(Spec 10.6)*
- **T-INST-73**: With `-y`, version mismatch is overridden and install proceeds. Version declaration preserved. **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — this test's contract is the `-y`-version-mismatch-override behavior, not the post-commit auto-install pass. T-INST-120b separately covers the `-y` × auto-install interaction with `withFakeNpm`. *(Spec 10.6)*
- **T-INST-72a**: Install-time version check for a no-index workflow with version mismatch. Single-workflow source containing `check.sh` (no `index` script) and a `package.json` declaring a loopx range not satisfied by the running version. `loopx install <source>` → exits with code 1 (version mismatch error). The version-mismatch error fires before the auto-install pass, so this test does not require `--no-install` (no commit, therefore no auto-install candidate); the rule's "pass `--no-install`" recommendation is for tests where the install would otherwise commit and auto-install would fire. This combines the no-index install path (T-INST-64) with version checking (T-INST-72) — both covered separately, but the combination tests that the version check still runs for workflows that lack a default entry point. *(Spec 10.6, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-73a**: Same setup as T-INST-72a with `-y`. `loopx install -y <source>` → exits 0. Assert the workflow is installed with `check.sh` and `package.json` version declaration preserved. This is the `-y` override counterpart to T-INST-72a. **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — the `-y` override commits the workflow and the workflow has a top-level `package.json`, so auto-install would fire by default; this test's contract is the version-mismatch-override behavior, not auto-install. *(Spec 10.6, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-74**: Workflow `package.json` unreadable → warning, install proceeds (no version validation). **Source must be a tarball** (or another non-git source path), since Git does not reliably preserve mode 000 file permissions across clone. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** **Implementer note:** SPEC 10.6 / 3.2 require an unreadable workflow `package.json` to warn and install to proceed, while SPEC 10.7 / 10.9 treat a generic unreadable non-script file in staging as a staging failure (T-INST-79). The mode-000 `package.json` fixture therefore exercises a path where the implementation must explicitly exempt `package.json` from the generic unreadable-file staging-failure treatment — reading it is expected to fail (triggering the documented warning), but the file itself must still be copied/preserved during staging without the whole install aborting. An implementation that shares one unreadable-file code path for staging and version-checking will likely fail here. T-INST-74a/75a/76a and T-INST-60q/60r/60s inherit the same exemption requirement. This is a SPEC tension that the test suite exercises by design; no SPEC change is suggested. *(Spec 10.6, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-74a**: Install-time `package.json` unreadable warning for a no-index workflow. Single-workflow source containing `check.sh` (no `index.*` script) and a `package.json` file with mode 000 (unreadable). **Source must be a tarball** (or another non-git source path). Run `loopx install <tarball-url>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stderr contains exactly one unreadable-file warning for the workflow, (c) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/` and contains `check.sh`, and (d) a `package.json` entry exists at `.loopx/<name>/package.json` (the file's committed permission mode is **not** asserted — SPEC 10.6 / 3.2 / 10.7 require the warning to fire and the install to proceed but do not require loopx to preserve mode 000 / unreadability at the destination; an implementation that chmods the staged file to a readable mode before commit is conforming). The mode-000 fixture in the source is the test-harness mechanism for triggering the SPEC 3.2 unreadable-file warning path; the destination's permission bits are an implementation detail and are intentionally left unconstrained by this assertion. This combines the no-index install path (T-INST-64) with the unreadable-`package.json` warning path (T-INST-74) — both covered separately, but the combination tests that the `package.json` failure warning still fires for workflows without a default entry point. A buggy implementation could scope the `package.json` read/warn code to the same guard that triggers version comparison, and silently skip both on no-index workflows. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 10.6, 3.2, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-75**: Workflow `package.json` invalid JSON → warning, install proceeds. *(Spec 10.6, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-75a**: Install-time `package.json` invalid-JSON warning for a no-index workflow. Single-workflow source containing `check.sh` (no `index.*` script) and a `package.json` containing invalid JSON (e.g., `{broken`). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stderr contains an invalid-JSON warning for the workflow, and (c) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/` and contains `check.sh` and the broken `package.json` (file is preserved as-is). This is the invalid-JSON counterpart to T-INST-74a (unreadable), combining the no-index install path (T-INST-64) with the invalid-JSON warning path (T-INST-75). *(Spec 10.6, 3.2, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-76**: Workflow `package.json` invalid semver range → warning, install proceeds. *(Spec 10.6, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-76a**: Install-time `package.json` invalid-semver-range warning for a no-index workflow. Single-workflow source containing `check.sh` (no `index.*` script) and a `package.json` containing valid JSON with an invalid semver range for `loopx` (e.g., `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }`). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stderr contains an invalid-semver warning for the workflow, and (c) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/` and contains `check.sh` and the `package.json` with its original (invalid) range preserved. This is the invalid-semver counterpart to T-INST-74a (unreadable) and T-INST-75a (invalid JSON), combining the no-index install path (T-INST-64) with the invalid-semver warning path (T-INST-76). Together T-INST-74a/75a/76a close the install-time `package.json` failure-warning coverage gap for no-index workflows across all three non-blocking failure branches — complementing T-INST-72a/73a which cover the blocking version-mismatch branch on the same path. *(Spec 10.6, 3.2, 10.4)*

#### Install Atomicity

- **T-INST-77**: Multi-workflow install: all workflows pass preflight → all installed. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-78**: Multi-workflow install: one workflow fails preflight (e.g., name collision at destination) → entire install fails, no workflows written. Assert `.loopx/` unchanged. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-79**: Staging failure leaves `.loopx/` unchanged. Multi-workflow source (git or tarball — the failure is driven by the seam, not by source shape) with two valid workflows `ralph/` (containing `index.sh`) and `broken/` (also containing `index.sh`). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_INSTALL_FAULT=staging-fail:broken` (section 1.4) to deterministically inject an `EACCES` during staging of `broken/`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) `.loopx/` is unchanged (neither `ralph/` nor `broken/` was written — the staging directory is cleaned up and the commit phase never runs, per SPEC 10.7), and (c) the error message identifies the failing workflow (`broken`) and the underlying cause (staging write failure / `EACCES`). The seam is used instead of a mode-000 file because SPEC 10.7 defines the failure *outcome* ("the staging directory is cleaned up and `.loopx/` is left unchanged") but does not require any specific source-shape trigger to cause staging to fail — an implementation that streams archive content into staging, chmods extracted files before copying, or otherwise avoids a literal copy of an unreadable file is conforming but would not fail staging on a mode-000 fixture. The seam decouples the SPEC-required outcome from an unspecified implementation detail. Runnable under any user (including `root`). *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80**: Multi-workflow install with `-y` succeeds despite destination collisions and version mismatches. Multi-workflow source where `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) has a destination collision (`.loopx/ralph/` already exists as a workflow with different content) and `other/` (with `index.sh` and a `package.json` declaring a loopx version range not satisfied by the running version) has a version mismatch. `loopx install -y <source>` → exits 0. Assert: `.loopx/ralph/` contains the new content from the source (replaced), and `.loopx/other/` is installed with its `package.json` version declaration preserved. **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — `other/` has a top-level `package.json` and would trigger the post-commit auto-install pass; this test's contract is atomicity (`-y` overrides preflight failures and commits both workflows), not auto-install. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80a**: Multi-workflow install with multiple preflight failures across different workflows (e.g., workflow A has a name collision at destination, workflow B has an invalid script name, workflow C declares a loopx version range not satisfied by the running version) → single aggregated error report listing all failures across all workflows, including the version mismatch. No workflows written. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80b**: `-y` replacement preserves the existing workflow when staging fails. Multi-workflow source (git or tarball — the failure is driven by the seam, not by source shape) with two valid workflows `ralph/` (containing `index.sh`) and `other/` (also containing `index.sh`). `.loopx/ralph/` already exists as a workflow with known content. Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_INSTALL_FAULT=staging-fail:other` (section 1.4) to deterministically inject an `EACCES` during staging of `other/`. Run `loopx install -y <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, and (b) the existing `.loopx/ralph/` is still intact with its original content — not removed or modified, because under SPEC 10.7's stage-then-commit model, `-y` replacements are removed from `.loopx/` only during the commit phase, which never runs when staging fails. Uses the seam rather than a mode-000 source file for the same reason as T-INST-79: SPEC 10.7 defines the outcome, not a specific filesystem-permission trigger. Runnable under any user. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80c**: Commit-phase failure reports which workflows were and were not committed. Multi-workflow source with three workflows: `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/` (each with `index.sh`). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_INSTALL_FAULT=commit-fail-after:1` in the process environment to simulate a rename failure after committing one workflow (see section 1.4, install fault injection seam — the seam is only honored when `NODE_ENV=test`). Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) error output identifies which workflow(s) were successfully committed and which were not, and (c) exactly one workflow directory exists in `.loopx/`. *(Spec 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80c2**: Commit-phase failure skips the post-commit auto-install pass — `npm install` does **not** run even on workflows committed before the failure. SPEC 10.10 specifies "After the commit phase (section 10.7) completes, unless `--no-install` is present, loopx performs a post-commit auto-install pass over the committed workflows" — the natural reading of "the commit phase completes" is "the commit phase completes successfully" (a partial / failed commit phase has not completed). Under that interpretation, a commit-phase failure gates the auto-install pass entirely: no committed-before-failure workflow receives an auto-install pass. T-INST-80c covers the commit-failure reporting behavior; this test pins down the auto-install gating behavior on the same failure. Multi-workflow source with three workflows: `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/` — each has `index.sh` **and a top-level `package.json`** (so each would trigger auto-install if the pass ran). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })` to capture any `npm install` invocation, and set `NODE_ENV=test` / `LOOPX_TEST_INSTALL_FAULT=commit-fail-after:1` to fail the commit phase after the first workflow commits. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (the commit failure is the terminal error), (b) exactly one workflow directory exists in `.loopx/` (matching T-INST-80c's commit-failure outcome), (c) **the fake-npm shim log is empty** — zero `npm install` invocations occurred, even though the committed workflow has a `package.json` that would normally trigger auto-install, and (d) no `.gitignore` was synthesized in the committed workflow's directory (`.gitignore` safeguard runs under the same trigger as `npm install` per SPEC 10.10, so when auto-install is gated, the safeguard is also gated). A buggy implementation that ran auto-install on the committed workflow after a commit failure would produce at least one shim invocation, failing (c). An implementation that ran the safeguard but skipped `npm install` would produce a `.gitignore` and fail (d). This test pins down SPEC 10.10's "commit phase completes successfully" preamble — pairing this test with T-INST-80c under a single commit-failure fixture cleanly distinguishes "which workflows committed" (reporting) from "which workflows received auto-install" (gating). *(Spec 10.10, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80d**: Install-time `package.json` warning behavior is "once per affected workflow" and is not accidentally deduplicated by runtime-style first-entry logic. Install two workflows (**from a tarball source** or another non-git source path — Git does not reliably preserve mode 000 file permissions across clone), each with an unreadable `package.json` → each workflow's warning appears independently (two warnings, not one). **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — both workflows have a top-level `package.json` and would otherwise trigger the post-commit auto-install pass; this test's contract is install-time `package.json` warning cardinality, not auto-install. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 3.2, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-80e**: Install-time `package.json` warning behavior for invalid semver ranges is "once per affected workflow." Install two workflows, each with a valid `package.json` containing an invalid semver range for `loopx` (e.g., `"loopx": "not-a-range"`) → each workflow's warning appears independently (two warnings, not one). Install proceeds for both workflows (invalid semver skips version check, does not block install). **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — both workflows have a top-level `package.json` and would otherwise trigger the post-commit auto-install pass; this test's contract is warning cardinality, not auto-install. This is the invalid-semver counterpart to T-INST-80d (unreadable files). *(Spec 3.2, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-80f**: Install-time `package.json` warning behavior for invalid JSON is "once per affected workflow." Install two workflows, each with a `package.json` containing invalid JSON (e.g., `{broken`) → each workflow's warning appears independently (two warnings, not one). Install proceeds for both workflows (invalid JSON skips version check, does not block install). **Pass `--no-install` per the section 4.10 auto-install-awareness rule** — both workflows have a top-level `package.json` (albeit malformed) and would otherwise be considered for the post-commit auto-install pass; this test's contract is warning cardinality, not auto-install behavior. (Note: SPEC 10.10 separately says malformed `package.json` skips auto-install; this test's `--no-install` is defensive and decouples warning cardinality from any auto-install evaluation that would otherwise occur.) This is the invalid-JSON counterpart to T-INST-80d (unreadable files) and T-INST-80e (invalid semver). *(Spec 3.2, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-80g**: `-y` does not override a zero-workflow source. `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains no root-level script files and no subdirectories that qualify as workflows → error, exit code 1, regardless of `-y`. *(Spec 10.3, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80h**: `-y` does not override invalid workflow or script names. `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a workflow with an invalid script name (e.g., `-bad.sh`) → error, exit code 1, regardless of `-y`. *(Spec 10.4, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80i**: `-y` does not override same-base-name collisions within a workflow. `loopx install -y <source>` where the source contains a workflow with `check.sh` and `check.ts` (same base name, different extensions) → error, exit code 1, regardless of `-y`. *(Spec 10.4, 10.7)*
- **T-INST-80j**: `-y` does not override an invalid workflow name. `loopx install -y <source>` where the derived workflow name violates `[a-zA-Z0-9_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*` (e.g., source directory named `bad.name`) → error, exit code 1, regardless of `-y`. *(Spec 10.4, 10.7)*

##### Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility (Known Gap)

SPEC 10.7 / 10.9 require the temporary staging directory itself to be removed on
staging / pre-commit failure. Current tests assert the externally visible contract
(`.loopx/` remains unchanged) but do not directly observe the implementation-private
staging directory, whose location and naming are unspecified.

- **T-INST-79a (not yet directly covered)**: Temporary staging directory is removed after a staging-phase failure. Deterministic coverage would require a test-only seam that exposes the staging-directory path.

#### Tarball Install

- **T-INST-81**: Multi-workflow tarball install with exact name derivation. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains subdirectories `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `other/` (with `index.sh`). Install the tarball. Assert installed paths are exactly `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/`, with the expected scripts present. Workflow names are derived from the source-root subdirectory names. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-82**: Wrapper-directory stripping for multi-workflow tarball. Create a `.tar.gz` with a single top-level directory `pkg/` containing `pkg/ralph/index.sh` and `pkg/other/index.sh`. After extraction, `pkg/` is stripped and the source root becomes the contents of `pkg/`. Assert installed paths are `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` (not `.loopx/pkg/`). *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-83**: No wrapper-directory stripping for multi-entry tarball. Create a `.tar.gz` with multiple top-level entries `ralph/index.sh` and `other/index.sh` (no single wrapper directory). After extraction, the entries are used directly as the source root. Assert installed paths are `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/`. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-83b**: No wrapper-directory stripping when extraction yields a single directory plus a sibling root-level file. SPEC 10.2 says "If extraction yields a **single top-level directory**, that directory's contents become the source root (wrapper-directory stripping). If extraction yields **multiple top-level entries**, the extracted contents are used directly as the source root." A tarball whose top-level entries are exactly one directory and one or more sibling files (e.g., a wrapper directory plus a sibling `README.md` at the archive root) yields multiple top-level entries — the SPEC's "single top-level directory" condition is not satisfied, so wrapper stripping must **not** occur. T-INST-82 covers the single-directory-only branch (stripping fires); T-INST-83 covers the multi-directory branch (no stripping); the **single-directory-plus-sibling-file** edge case lies between these two and is uncovered. A buggy implementation that normalized "ignore root-level files when deciding wrapper-stripping" or that stripped on "any single top-level **directory** entry" without also requiring "no other top-level entries" would wrongly fire wrapper-stripping on this fixture, producing a different installed-path layout. Create a `.tar.gz` named `archive.tar.gz` whose root contains exactly two top-level entries: `pkg/` (a directory containing `pkg/index.sh`) and `README.md` (a sibling file at the archive root). After extraction, the source root has multiple top-level entries (`pkg/` and `README.md`), so no wrapper stripping fires; the source root has no root-level files **with supported script extensions** (`README.md` is not a supported script extension), but `pkg/` qualifies as a workflow (contains a top-level script with a supported extension), so the source classifies as **multi-workflow** per SPEC 10.3. Run `loopx install <tarball-url>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) `.loopx/pkg/` is committed and contains `index.sh` (the workflow name is the **subdirectory** name `pkg`, not the **archive** name `archive` — multi-workflow tarballs derive workflow names from source-root subdirectory names per SPEC 10.2), (c) **`.loopx/archive/` does not exist** — wrapper stripping did not occur, so the archive name is not the workflow name, and (d) `.loopx/README.md` does not exist — the source-root `README.md` is a non-workflow support file and is not copied (per SPEC 10.3 multi-workflow self-containment, T-INST-83a). Together with T-INST-82 (single-directory wrapper stripping) and T-INST-83 (multi-directory no-wrapper-stripping), this closes the third extraction shape (single-dir + sibling-file). *(Spec 10.2, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-83a**: Multi-workflow tarball self-containment. Create a `.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains two workflow subdirectories `ralph/` (with `index.sh`) and `other/` (with `index.sh`), plus root-level support files `README.md` and `LICENSE`, a root-level support directory `docs/` (containing `docs/guide.md`), and a non-workflow directory `shared/` (containing only `shared/config.json` — no top-level script files). `loopx install <tarball-url>` → exit code 0. Assert: (a) `.loopx/ralph/` and `.loopx/other/` are installed and contain their respective scripts, (b) `.loopx/README.md`, `.loopx/LICENSE`, `.loopx/docs/`, and `.loopx/shared/` do NOT exist — no root support files or directories are copied. Per SPEC 10.2, classification after extraction is source-type-agnostic, so the multi-workflow self-containment rules that apply to git sources (T-INST-54 covers root support files, T-INST-54c covers root support directories, T-INST-55 covers non-workflow subdirectories skipped) must apply equally to tarball sources. Existing tarball coverage (T-INST-81–83) pins down wrapper stripping and workflow-name derivation but does not assert self-containment; this test closes that gap. *(Spec 10.2, 10.3)*
- **T-INST-84**: `.tgz` extension handled identically. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-85**: Single-workflow tarball: workflow name derived from archive name (URL last segment minus `.tar.gz`/`.tgz`, query/fragment stripped). *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-86**: Tarball URL with query string → query stripped from archive-name derivation. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-86a**: Tarball URL with fragment → fragment stripped from archive-name derivation. `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/pkg.tar.gz#v1` installs a single-workflow source. Assert the installed workflow is named `pkg` (fragment `#v1` stripped before deriving the archive name). *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-85a**: Single-workflow tarball with wrapper-directory stripping. Create a `.tar.gz` named `my-agent.tar.gz` with a single top-level wrapper directory (e.g., `wrapper/`) containing a single-workflow source at its root (e.g., `wrapper/index.sh`, `wrapper/check.sh` — root-level script files). Install the tarball. Assert: (a) the wrapper directory is stripped before classification (the source is classified as single-workflow from the unwrapped contents), (b) the installed workflow name is `my-agent` (derived from the archive name, not the wrapper directory name `wrapper`), and (c) the installed workflow at `.loopx/my-agent/` contains the expected scripts. This combines wrapper-directory stripping (tested for multi-workflow in T-INST-82) with single-workflow archive-name derivation (tested without wrapping in T-INST-85). *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-85b**: Single-workflow tarball with root-level script files plus subdirectories containing supported-extension files. Create a `.tar.gz` named `my-agent.tar.gz` whose source root (after any wrapper-directory stripping) contains root-level script files (e.g., `index.sh`, `check.sh`) plus subdirectories with supported-extension files (e.g., `lib/helpers.ts`, `src/utils.js`) and non-script root-level files (`package.json`, `README.md`). Install the tarball. Assert: (a) the source is classified as single-workflow (root-level script files take precedence unconditionally), (b) the installed workflow name is `my-agent` (derived from the archive name), (c) all root-level files and subdirectories are copied into `.loopx/my-agent/` (including `lib/`, `src/`, `package.json`, `README.md`), and (d) no sibling workflows are created from the subdirectories. This is the tarball counterpart to the git-source root-level-script precedence tests (T-INST-50–52). *(Spec 10.2, 10.3)*

#### Git Install

- **T-INST-87**: Shallow clone (`--depth 1`). Use `withFakeGit({ logFile })` so every `git` invocation loopx makes is recorded to a log file (the shim wraps the real `git` and is transparent to loopx's cloning). Run `loopx install <git-source>` against a local bare repo. Assert the log contains a `git clone` invocation (the only-one-clone cardinality is incidental and not separately asserted) whose argv includes a depth-1 specification — accept either the two-token form `--depth 1` (separate argv entries `["--depth", "1"]`) or the equals form `--depth=1` (single argv entry). Per SPEC 10.2 "The repository is cloned with `--depth 1` (shallow clone) into a temporary location." **The argv ordering of the depth flag relative to the source URL or destination path is not asserted** — SPEC 10.2 specifies only that shallow-clone is performed, not a specific argv layout, and standard `git clone` accepts options in any position before the URL/destination operands. The test's assertion is satisfied by any conforming `git clone <... --depth 1 ...> <url> [<dst>]` or `git clone <url> [<dst>] <... --depth=1 ...>` argv arrangement that preserves a valid `git clone` invocation. The `--depth 1` flag on the recorded `git clone` invocation is the black-box-visible surface for the SPEC 10.2 shallow-clone contract; SPEC 10 does not normatively define whether installed workflows retain `.git/` metadata, so this test does not assert anything about post-install `.git/` presence or absence. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-88**: Single-workflow git repo: name derived from repo URL minus `.git`. *(Spec 10.2)*
- **T-INST-89**: Multi-workflow git repo: workflow names derived from subdirectory names. *(Spec 10.2)*

#### Common Rules

- **T-INST-90**: `.loopx/` directory created if it doesn't exist. *(Spec 10)*
- **T-INST-92**: HTTP 404 during tarball download → error, exit code 1, no partial directory. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-92a**: HTTP 500 (server error) during tarball download → error, exit code 1, no partial directory. T-INST-92 covers the 4xx (client-error) branch, which is the most common failure mode. This test covers the 5xx (server-error) branch — both are HTTP error responses but a buggy implementation could special-case 4xx error handling (e.g., treating 404 as "not found" but treating 5xx as a transient retry-able condition that ends up succeeding spuriously, or surfacing differently). Use the tarball-download fault-injection mock server (the same harness mechanism used by T-INST-92, parameterized for status code) to return HTTP 500 for the tarball URL. Run `loopx install <url-returning-500>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) stderr surfaces the download failure (HTTP 500 reference is not pinned — the exact error wording is implementation-defined, since loopx may translate the HTTP status into a generic download-failure message), and (c) no partial workflow directory is left under `.loopx/` — if `.loopx/` pre-existed with unrelated workflows, those remain unmodified; otherwise `.loopx/` was not created. **Do not assert directly on the implementation-private staging directory**: the staging-path location and naming are unspecified (see known gap T-INST-79a — section 4.10 "Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility"), so a black-box test must limit itself to the externally visible SPEC 10.9 contract ("`.loopx/` itself unchanged, no destination partials"). Pinning down the implementation-private staging-path cleanup is the business of T-INST-79a, not this test. This test pins down that loopx treats both 4xx and 5xx as fatal install failures with the same externally-observable cleanup behavior — the SPEC 10.9 download-failure path does not branch on HTTP status class. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-92b**: HTTP 3xx (redirect) during tarball download is treated as a non-2xx install failure; `Location:` is not auto-followed. SPEC 10.9 says "Install failure cleanup (pre-commit failure modes): Any pre-commit install failure (download error, **HTTP non-2xx**, git clone failure, extraction failure, post-download validation failure) ... exits with code 1." T-INST-92 / T-INST-92a cover 4xx and 5xx; this test covers the 3xx branch. A buggy implementation might transparently follow `Location:` redirects (treating 3xx as a successful download path) and complete the install. Use `startLocalHTTPServer` to register two routes: a primary route at `/redirect.tar.gz` that responds with HTTP 302 and a `Location: http://localhost:PORT/real.tar.gz` header, and a secondary route at `/real.tar.gz` that serves an otherwise-valid `.tar.gz` (single-workflow source with `index.sh`). The harness records every incoming request to both routes. Run `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/redirect.tar.gz`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (the 302 is a non-2xx response and therefore an install failure under SPEC 10.9), (b) stderr surfaces a download failure (exact wording implementation-defined; the test does not pin a specific phrase, only that the failure category is download-failure / non-2xx, not extraction-failure), (c) no workflow was committed — `.loopx/` is unchanged (matches the externally visible SPEC 10.9 contract), and (d) **the secondary `/real.tar.gz` route received zero hits** — verifying via the harness's request log that loopx did not transparently follow the redirect to a tarball that would have succeeded. Assertion (d) is the key signal that distinguishes "loopx treated 302 as a failure" from "loopx silently followed the redirect and the install ended up failing for some unrelated reason"; the secondary route serves a working tarball precisely so that any redirect-following implementation would succeed (exit 0) on this fixture, making the exit-code-1 assertion unambiguous. T-INST-92 (404) and T-INST-92a (500) cover the 4xx / 5xx branches; this closes the 3xx branch. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-92c**: Tarball download transport error (connection reset before headers) is treated as a download failure. SPEC 10.9 enumerates **"download error"** as the first pre-commit failure mode, distinct from "HTTP non-2xx" — the two categories are listed separately, so a transport-level failure (TCP reset, abrupt socket close before headers, TLS handshake failure, DNS resolution failure) is a SPEC-named install-failure mode and not collapsed into the HTTP-status branch. T-INST-92 / T-INST-92a / T-INST-92b cover the HTTP-non-2xx branch (404, 500, 302); T-INST-94 / T-INST-95 cover the extraction-failure branch (corrupt archive, empty archive); T-INST-93 covers the git-clone-failure branch. The transport-error branch is not currently covered. A buggy implementation that raised an uncaught exception on a socket reset (rather than translating it into a clean SPEC 10.9 download-failure with exit 1 and `.loopx/` unchanged) would pass all the existing T-INST-92* / T-INST-94 / T-INST-95 tests but fail this case. Use `startLocalHTTPServer` to register a route at `/reset.tar.gz` whose handler **accepts the connection, then immediately destroys the underlying socket without writing any response headers or body** (e.g., `(req, res) => { req.socket.destroy(); }` for a Node `http.Server` — the socket is closed mid-request, before any HTTP response line is sent). Run `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/reset.tar.gz`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (the transport error is a SPEC 10.9 download failure), (b) stderr surfaces a download / network failure (exact wording implementation-defined; the test does not pin a specific phrase, only that the failure category is download / network failure — **not** an HTTP-status failure and **not** an extraction failure), (c) `.loopx/` is unchanged (matches the externally visible SPEC 10.9 cleanup contract — pre-commit failures leave `.loopx/` itself unchanged; if `.loopx/` pre-existed with unrelated workflows, those remain unmodified; otherwise `.loopx/` was not created), and (d) no partial workflow directory or staging artifact under `.loopx/` is left behind. **Do not assert directly on the implementation-private staging directory** for the same reason as T-INST-92 / T-INST-92a (per known gap T-INST-79a — section 4.10 "Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility"). Together with T-INST-92 / T-INST-92a / T-INST-92b (HTTP non-2xx branch) and T-INST-94 / T-INST-95 (extraction branch), this closes coverage of the SPEC 10.9 pre-commit failure-mode enumeration on the tarball-source path: download-error (this test), HTTP non-2xx (404 / 500 / 302), extraction failure (corrupt / empty archive). The git-clone-failure branch (T-INST-93) and post-download validation-failure branch (covered by the install-time validation tests T-INST-61 / T-INST-78 / etc.) round out the rest of the SPEC 10.9 list. *(Spec 10.9, 12)*
- **T-INST-93**: Git clone failure (non-existent repo) → error, exit code 1, no partial directory. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-94**: Tarball extraction failure (corrupt archive) → error, exit code 1, no partial directory. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-95**: Empty tarball (valid `.tar.gz` with no entries) → error, exit code 1. *(Spec 10.9)*
- **T-INST-96**: Successful single-workflow git install → installed workflow is runnable via `loopx run <name>`. *(Spec 10)*
- **T-INST-97a**: Pre-commit failure leaves `.loopx/` unchanged. Per Spec 10.7 / 10.9's stage-then-commit model, any pre-commit install failure (source download, extraction, preflight, staging — enumerated in Spec 10.9's "Install failure cleanup" bullet) leaves `.loopx/` itself unchanged; no destination-path cleanup is required. Create a single-workflow source (git or tarball — the failure is driven by the seam, not by source shape). Before running install, snapshot the state of `.loopx/` (either empty or containing unrelated workflows). Set `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_INSTALL_FAULT=staging-fail:<name>` (section 1.4, where `<name>` is the derived workflow name) to deterministically inject an `EACCES` during staging. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) `.loopx/` is byte-identical to the pre-install snapshot (no new workflow directory under `.loopx/<name>/`, and any pre-existing sibling workflows unmodified), and (c) stderr identifies the failing workflow and the underlying cause. This rewrites the earlier cleanup-of-partial-workflow-directory assertion to match SPEC 10.9 post-ADR-0004: under stage-then-commit, the staging directory is the only thing that could be partial, and it is removed; `.loopx/` itself never receives a partial write. Uses the staging-fail seam for the same reason as T-INST-79: SPEC 10.9 defines the outcome, not a specific filesystem-permission trigger. Runnable under any user. *(Spec 10.7, 10.9)*
- **T-INST-97a2**: Pre-commit failure (preflight) leaves `.loopx/` unchanged. Create a single-workflow source whose workflow name violates name restrictions. Snapshot `.loopx/`, run install, assert exit 1 and `.loopx/` unchanged. This is the preflight-fails-at-single-workflow variant of T-INST-97a (which covers the staging failure); together they pin down that neither pre-commit phase leaves `.loopx/` in a partial state for single-workflow sources. *(Spec 10.7, 10.9)*
- **T-INST-97b**: Successful install does not create `.loopx/package.json`. Run a successful single-workflow git install into a project. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the workflow is installed at `.loopx/<name>/`, and (c) `.loopx/package.json` does not exist. There is no `.loopx/`-level manifest — version authority lives only in the project root `package.json` and each workflow's own `package.json`. *(Spec 10.6)*

#### Auto-install of Workflow Dependencies (Spec 10.10)

All tests in this block use `withFakeNpm` (section 2.3) to control `npm`'s behavior deterministically. The helper records every invocation to a log file so tests can assert which workflows `npm install` was invoked for, in what order, and with what environment. Without the fake shim, these tests would be flaky (depend on real npm + registry) or impossible (`spawnFailure`, signal, non-zero exit cannot be driven by real npm reliably).

##### Auto-install Trigger

- **T-INST-110**: Default post-commit `npm install` runs once per committed workflow with top-level `package.json`, sequentially. Install a multi-workflow source where `alpha/` has a top-level `package.json` (and `alpha/index.sh`) and `beta/` has a top-level `package.json` (and `beta/index.sh`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, sleepSeconds: 1, logFile })`. The per-invocation `sleepSeconds: 1` is what makes the non-overlap assertion in (e) testable: with immediate-exit shims, two concurrent invocations could both record start/end timestamps within a single millisecond, and a non-overlap assertion against millisecond-resolution timestamps could pass vacuously even on a buggy implementation that ran the two `npm install` children in parallel. A 1-second per-invocation window means a parallel implementation would produce visibly overlapping intervals (the second invocation's start would precede the first invocation's end by ~1 second), and the non-overlap assertion catches that — pinning down SPEC 10.10's "sequentially in commit order" requirement. Run `loopx install <source>` (no `--no-install`). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the shim log records exactly two invocations, (c) **the set** of invocation cwds is exactly `{ .loopx/alpha/, .loopx/beta/ }` — each workflow was invoked exactly once, in either commit order (the test does **not** assume or assert a specific alpha-vs-beta order, because SPEC 10.10 says "sequentially in commit order" without defining how commit order is chosen across workflows in a source), (d) each invocation's argv is **exactly** `["install"]` — no additional flags (`--production`, `--ignore-scripts`, `--omit=dev`, etc.) are passed, per SPEC 10.10 "does not pass `--production` / `--omit=dev` / `--ignore-scripts` / any other flags", and (e) the two invocations do not overlap in wall-clock time (the log records start/end timestamps for each shim execution; assert the second invocation's start is after the first invocation's end, regardless of which workflow is which — under the 1-second per-invocation sleep, a parallel implementation would fail this assertion deterministically). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-110a**: `npm install` is skipped silently for workflows without a top-level `package.json`. Multi-workflow source where `alpha/` has `alpha/index.sh` + `alpha/package.json`, and `beta/` has only `beta/index.sh` (no `package.json`). Use `withFakeNpm`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the shim log records exactly one invocation — for `alpha/`, (c) no warning about `beta/` missing a `package.json` — the skip is silent (Spec 10.10 "Workflows without a top-level `package.json` are skipped silently"). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-110c**: Presence of `package.json` alone triggers auto-install — dependency content is not inspected. Single-workflow source with `index.sh` and a minimal `package.json` containing exactly `{ "name": "minimal-workflow", "version": "1.0.0" }` (no `dependencies`, no `devDependencies`, no `scripts`, no `loopx` field). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation for the workflow (argv `["install"]`, cwd = `.loopx/<name>/`), (c) a synthesized `.gitignore` was created in the workflow directory. Per SPEC 10.10 "The trigger is the presence of a top-level `package.json`; whether that file declares any dependencies is not inspected." A buggy implementation that skipped auto-install when the `package.json` lists zero dependencies would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-110b**: `-w <name>` scopes auto-install to the selected workflow. Same source as T-INST-110 (both `alpha/` and `beta/` have `package.json`). Run `loopx install -w alpha <source>`. Assert shim log records exactly one invocation, for `alpha/`, and none for `beta/`. Extends T-INST-57 (selective installation) to the auto-install pass. *(Spec 10.10, 10.8)*
- **T-INST-110d**: Auto-install fires on a no-`index` workflow. SPEC 2.1 allows workflows without an `index` script (they can still be targeted explicitly via `workflow:script`), and SPEC 10.10 triggers auto-install solely on the presence of a top-level `package.json` — it does not require an `index` entry. Single-workflow source with `check.sh` (non-`index` script) and a top-level `package.json` (e.g., `{ "name": "my-workflow", "version": "1.0.0" }`) and no `index.*` file. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation for the workflow (cwd = `.loopx/<name>/`, argv = `["install"]`), (c) a synthesized `.gitignore` exists at `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` containing `node_modules`, and (d) the installed workflow still does not have an `index` script (loopx did not invent one). Per SPEC 10.10 the trigger is top-level `package.json` alone; a buggy implementation that gated auto-install on the presence of an `index` entry (either by fiat or because it only walked discovered-script workflows) would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 2.1, 10.4)*
- **T-INST-110e**: Nested `package.json` alone does **not** trigger auto-install. SPEC 10.10's trigger is a `package.json` at the workflow's **top level** (`.loopx/<workflow-name>/package.json`); `package.json` files in subdirectories are not triggers. Single-workflow source with `index.sh` plus `lib/package.json` (i.e., a nested-only `package.json`) and no top-level `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the fake-npm shim log records **zero** invocations (the trigger did not fire because the top-level `package.json` is absent), (c) no `.gitignore` is synthesized at `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` (the safeguard runs under the same trigger conditions as `npm install`), and (d) the nested `lib/package.json` is preserved under `.loopx/<name>/lib/package.json` byte-for-byte. This pins down the "top-level" part of the trigger rule — an implementation that recursively scanned each installed workflow for any `package.json` and fired auto-install per-hit would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-110f**: Auto-install fires on a **selected no-`index` workflow under `-w`**. Combines T-INST-110b (`-w` scopes auto-install to the selected workflow) with T-INST-110d (no-`index` workflow still triggers auto-install when a top-level `package.json` is present). The two interactions are covered separately — `-w` × auto-install for index-bearing workflows (T-INST-110b), and no-index × auto-install for full-source installs (T-INST-110d) — but the no-index × `-w` × auto-install combination is uncovered. A buggy implementation could gate the SPEC 10.10 auto-install pass on either "workflow has an `index` entry" or "workflow was selected via the full-source path, not via `-w`" and pass both T-INST-110b and T-INST-110d while failing this combination. Multi-workflow source where `tools/` contains `tools/check.sh` (a non-`index` script) and `tools/package.json` with content `{ "name": "tools", "version": "1.0.0" }` (no `index.*`), and `other/` contains `other/index.sh` + `other/package.json` (a sibling workflow that would normally also trigger auto-install — its presence here proves the `-w` scoping is working). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install -w tools <source>` (no `--no-install`). Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation (cwd = `.loopx/tools/`, argv = `["install"]`) — the selected no-index workflow triggered auto-install despite having no `index` entry, and the unselected `other/` workflow was not invoked because of `-w`, (c) a synthesized `.gitignore` exists at `.loopx/tools/.gitignore` containing `node_modules`, (d) `.loopx/tools/check.sh` is present and `.loopx/tools/index.*` does not exist (loopx did not invent an index entry), and (e) `.loopx/other/` does not exist (selective install only writes the selected workflow). T-INST-64c proves that no-index workflows install correctly under `-w`; this test pins down that the post-commit auto-install pass also runs for them, completing the no-index × `-w` × SPEC 10.10 interaction. *(Spec 10.10, 10.8, 2.1)*
- **T-INST-110g**: Auto-install fires on a **tarball source** with a top-level `package.json` — pinning down that SPEC 10.10's post-commit auto-install pass is source-type-agnostic. SPEC 10.10 says the trigger applies "after `loopx install` commits workflow files" and is keyed solely on the presence of a top-level `package.json` — it does not condition the trigger on source type. The existing T-INST-110 / T-INST-110a / T-INST-110c / T-INST-110d / T-INST-110f positive-trigger tests are source-type-agnostic in their wording but the implementation may default to git fixtures, leaving the tarball-source path uncovered for the positive auto-install trigger surface. T-INST-113a covers the malformed-`package.json` (mode 000) failure mode on a tarball source (where a tarball is required because git does not preserve mode 000), but that is a *negative* path. A buggy implementation that wired auto-install only into the post-commit phase of the git-source code path — e.g., by calling `runAutoInstall()` inside the git-clone branch but forgetting to call it in the tarball-extraction branch — would pass T-INST-110 et al. (under git defaults) and T-INST-113a (where the tarball path correctly skips auto-install due to malformed `package.json`) while silently violating SPEC 10.10 on every successful tarball install. **Setup — naming discipline.** Per SPEC 10.2, the workflow name for a single-workflow tarball source is the **archive-name** (the URL's last path segment with archive extensions removed and query strings / fragments stripped) — **not** the wrapper directory name inside the tarball, which is stripped during the SPEC 10.2 wrapper-stripping step before classification. To eliminate any ambiguity in the assertions below, the test **intentionally makes the archive name and the wrapper directory name identical** (both `tarball-workflow`), so the unambiguous workflow name `tarball-workflow` reaches `.loopx/<name>/` whether the reader interprets `<name>` as "the wrapper directory" or "the archive-derived workflow name" — they are the same string here. **Setup — fixture.** Single-workflow tarball source served via `startLocalHTTPServer` (per the section 2.3 install-test conventions): a `.tar.gz` archive **named `tarball-workflow.tar.gz`** whose root contains a single wrapper directory **`tarball-workflow/`** with `tarball-workflow/index.sh` (a minimal valid script — e.g., emits `{"stop":true}`) and `tarball-workflow/package.json` (e.g., `{ "name": "tarball-workflow", "version": "1.0.0" }`). After SPEC 10.2 wrapper-stripping, the source root is the contents of `tarball-workflow/`; the source is classified as single-workflow (root has script files); and per SPEC 10.2 "For single-workflow tarball sources, the workflow name is the **archive-name**" the workflow name is `tarball-workflow` (derived from `tarball-workflow.tar.gz`). The source has **no** pre-existing `.gitignore`, so the `.gitignore` safeguard must synthesize one. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })` to make the `npm install` invocation deterministic and observable. Run `loopx install http://localhost:PORT/tarball-workflow.tar.gz` (no `--no-install`, no `-w`). **Assert.** (a) exit code 0, (b) the workflow is committed at `.loopx/tarball-workflow/` (the archive-derived workflow name) with `index.sh` and `package.json` from the source byte-for-byte (file-level commit succeeded on the tarball path), (c) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation with cwd = `.loopx/tarball-workflow/` and argv = `["install"]` — auto-install ran post-commit on the tarball path (no `--production`, no `--ignore-scripts`, etc., per SPEC 10.10's "does not pass ... any other flags" rule), and (d) a synthesized `.gitignore` exists at `.loopx/tarball-workflow/.gitignore` containing the single line `node_modules` (optionally with a trailing newline) — the safeguard ran on the tarball path. Together with T-INST-110 (multi-workflow source whose source type is not pinned in the test wording — typically a git fixture in current implementations) and T-INST-113a (tarball + mode-000 `package.json` → auto-install correctly skipped), this closes the "auto-install trigger fires regardless of source type" surface for the positive path. *(Spec 10.10, 10.2, 10.1)*

##### `--no-install` Opt-Out

- **T-INST-111**: `--no-install` suppresses both `npm install` and `.gitignore` synthesis. Single-workflow source with top-level `package.json` but no `.gitignore`. Use `withFakeNpm` to observe any invocation. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the shim log is empty (no `npm` invocations), (c) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` was **not** created, (d) other install-time behavior succeeded (workflow files present at `.loopx/<name>/`). Per Spec 10.10 "`--no-install` ... does not suppress any other install-time behavior; only the `npm install` step and the `.gitignore` safeguard ... are skipped." *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-111a**: `--no-install` on a workflow with a pre-existing `.gitignore` leaves that file unchanged. Source's `.gitignore` contains `dist/\n`. Use `withFakeNpm`. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) no `npm` invocation, (b) the installed `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` has the same content as the source (`dist/\n`) — loopx did not touch it. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-111b**: `--no-install` does **not** suppress preflight validation. Single-workflow source with a top-level `package.json` declaring a loopx version range not satisfied by the running version. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 with a version-mismatch error, (b) the shim log is empty (auto-install never ran), (c) no `.gitignore` was synthesized, and (d) the workflow was not committed. This proves `--no-install` suppresses only the post-commit auto-install pass, not source download / preflight validation / commit behavior. *(Spec 10.10, 10.6)*
- **T-INST-111c**: `--no-install` does **not** suppress nonfatal `package.json` parse / validation warnings. T-INST-111b covers the **fatal** preflight branch (version mismatch — exit 1, workflow not committed). This test pins down the **nonfatal** branch: a malformed `package.json` produces a SPEC 3.2 / 10.6 warning at install time but does not block the commit, and `--no-install` must not gate that warning either. Single-workflow source with `index.sh` and a top-level `package.json` containing **invalid JSON** (e.g., `{broken`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — invalid JSON is non-fatal (T-INST-113 establishes this without `--no-install`); `--no-install` suppresses only auto-install / safeguard, not commit, (b) the shim log is empty (no `npm` invocation — `--no-install` suppresses auto-install regardless of the package warning), (c) stderr contains exactly one `package.json` parse-failure warning for the workflow (the SPEC 3.2 install-time warning fires regardless of `--no-install`; `--no-install` does not gate install-time package validation warnings), (d) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` was **not** created (safeguard skipped under `--no-install`), and (e) the workflow is committed at `.loopx/<name>/` with `index.sh` and the malformed `package.json` byte-for-byte. T-INST-113 covers the same warning + auto-install-skip without `--no-install`; T-INST-111 covers `--no-install` without a malformed `package.json`. This test covers the intersection: `--no-install` suppresses only the auto-install + safeguard behaviors specified in SPEC 10.10, not install-time package-validation warnings or file-level commit. An implementation that wrongly suppressed all `package.json`-related warnings under `--no-install` (treating `--no-install` as a "skip everything package-related" knob) would fail (c). *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*

##### `.gitignore` Safeguard

- **T-INST-112**: Missing `.gitignore` is created with exactly one line: `node_modules`, **and the safeguard runs before `npm install` is spawned**. SPEC 10.10 specifies: "Immediately before spawning `npm install` in a workflow directory, loopx checks for a top-level `.gitignore` ... If none exists, loopx creates one containing the single line `node_modules`." A post-install end-state assertion alone ("`.gitignore` exists after install") cannot distinguish "safeguard ran before npm spawn" from "loopx ran npm first and created `.gitignore` afterward" — a buggy implementation could populate `node_modules/` first and then synthesize `.gitignore` retroactively, producing the same final filesystem state but violating the SPEC ordering. To pin the ordering down, the fake-npm shim must record the **state at shim process start**: extend `withFakeNpm` to record, for each invocation, whether `<cwd>/.gitignore` existed when the shim was first invoked (e.g., capture `fs.existsSync('.gitignore')` and the file's content as the very first action in the shim's preamble, before any other side effect). The recorded state is then asserted by the test. Single-workflow source with `package.json` but no `.gitignore`. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, recordGitignoreAtStart: true, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit 0, (b) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` exists at the end, (c) its end-state content is exactly `node_modules` (optionally with a single trailing newline — implementation-defined), (d) the fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation for the workflow with cwd = `.loopx/<name>/`, and (e) **the shim-start `.gitignore`-existed marker is `true`** for the recorded invocation, with the recorded content being exactly `node_modules` (matching (c)) — the safeguard had already created `.gitignore` before `npm install` was spawned, per SPEC 10.10's "Immediately before spawning `npm install`" wording. A buggy implementation that ran npm first and synthesized `.gitignore` afterward would produce a `false` shim-start marker (or a missing-content marker) and fail (e). **Implementer note for the harness.** This test requires `withFakeNpm` to expose a `recordGitignoreAtStart` option that adds a per-invocation `gitignoreAtStart: { existed: boolean, content?: string }` field to each `FakeNpmInvocation` log entry; the recording happens as the first side effect inside the shim's Bash preamble (before any of the shim's configured `stdout`/`stderr`/`createFiles` actions), guaranteeing it observes filesystem state at the moment of spawn. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112a**: Existing **regular** `.gitignore` is left unchanged (no append, no rewrite, no warning). Source's `.gitignore` is a regular file containing `dist/\n# my custom comment\n` — installed under SPEC 10.11's symlink-materialization rules as a regular file at `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore`. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0 })`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit 0, (b) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` content is byte-identical to the source's (`dist/\n# my custom comment\n`) — no `node_modules` line appended, (c) no warning on stderr about `node_modules` not being listed, (d) `npm install` still ran (the `lstat`-regular dispatch took the "regular file → leave unchanged → proceed" branch per SPEC 10.10). The non-regular branches (directory, symlink, FIFO, socket) are exercised by T-INST-112e and the symlink-known-gap entry below. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112a2**: Source `.gitignore` that is a **symlink to a regular file** is materialized as a regular file by SPEC 10.11 staging / copy and then satisfies SPEC 10.10's regular-file branch — auto-install proceeds normally and the materialized content is preserved unchanged. SPEC 10.11 says "During the staging / copy phase, loopx does **not** preserve symlink entries as symlinks in installed workflows. Any symlink that is part of a selected workflow is resolved and materialized into the destination as a regular copied file or directory" — with the explicit example "Symlinked non-script files or directories inside a selected workflow's copied content are likewise materialized as real files or directories." A workflow `.gitignore` is a non-script file inside the workflow, so the materialization rule applies; the resulting installed entry is a regular file and SPEC 10.10's `lstat` dispatch must take the regular-file branch. T-INST-112a covers the same regular-file branch when the **source** `.gitignore` was already a regular file; this test extends that coverage to the source-symlink case to pin down the **interaction** between SPEC 10.11 (source-symlink materialization) and SPEC 10.10 (`.gitignore` safeguard `lstat` dispatch). T-INST-112g (and its sub-cases) covers the post-commit symlink case via the `gitignore-replace-with-symlink:` seam — that test exists precisely because SPEC 10.11 materializes source symlinks before commit and ordinary install inputs cannot produce a committed symlink `.gitignore`. This test is the positive companion: it proves the materialization actually happens for `.gitignore` (rather than the safeguard accidentally observing the un-materialized symlink and failing). A buggy implementation that materialized source symlinks for script files but missed `.gitignore` (e.g., because the materialization pass walked only entries matching the discovered-script-extension set) would commit a symlink at `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore`, the safeguard would observe that symlink, and auto-install would surface a `.gitignore` safeguard failure for an ordinary source — a regression the safeguard tests alone cannot catch. **Setup.** Single-workflow source containing `index.sh` (minimal script — e.g., emits `{"stop":true}`), a top-level `package.json` (e.g., `{ "name": "symlink-gitignore-workflow", "version": "1.0.0" }`), a target file `gitignore-template` containing `dist/\n# project-custom\n`, and a `.gitignore -> gitignore-template` symlink at the workflow root. **Parameterize over both source types** (per the SPEC 10.2 / 10.11 source-form parity used elsewhere in this block — git and tarball must both materialize source symlinks): for the git source, commit the symlink directly into the local bare repo so a real `git clone` materializes it through the SPEC 10.11 path; for the tarball source, build a `.tar.gz` archive that records the entry as a symlink (e.g., `tar --create --file pkg.tar.gz <wf>/`) so SPEC 10.11 materialization runs through the tarball-extraction branch. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>` (no `--no-install`). **Assert** (per source-type variant). (a) exit code 0 — the materialization succeeded and the safeguard took the regular-file branch with no failure. (b) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` is a **regular file** — verified via `lstat` returning a `S_IFREG` entry type and **not** `S_IFLNK`. A buggy implementation that copied the source symlink as-is would surface `S_IFLNK` and fail this assertion. (c) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` content is byte-identical to the symlink **target's** content (`dist/\n# project-custom\n`) — SPEC 10.11 materialization resolves the symlink and copies the target's bytes; loopx neither appends `node_modules` nor mutates the materialized content (per SPEC 10.10's regular-file branch "loopx leaves it unchanged and proceeds"). (d) the fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation with cwd = `.loopx/<name>/` and argv = `["install"]` — auto-install ran, proving the safeguard saw a regular file (not a symlink) and dispatched correctly. (e) **no aggregate failure report** is emitted on stderr — the safeguard succeeded for the materialized regular `.gitignore`, so SPEC 10.10's failure-reporting machinery does not engage. (f) `.loopx/<name>/gitignore-template` is also present as a regular file containing `dist/\n# project-custom\n` (the materialization preserves the original target as well, since the target is itself a non-script workflow file selected by the workflow's contents — pinning down that SPEC 10.11 materializes the symlink to a regular file at the link's name *without* deleting or hiding the target file's separate copy). Together with T-INST-112a (regular-file source) and T-INST-112g sub-cases (symlink-via-seam post-commit failure), this completes the SPEC 10.10 × SPEC 10.11 coverage matrix at the `.gitignore` safeguard surface: source-regular-file → safeguard-regular branch (T-INST-112a), source-symlink → materialized-regular-file → safeguard-regular branch (this test), and post-commit symlink-via-seam → safeguard-symlink-failure branch (T-INST-112g). *(Spec 10.10, 10.11)*
- **T-INST-112b**: `.gitignore` synthesis is skipped when the workflow has no top-level `package.json`. Source has `index.sh` but no `package.json`. Run install. Assert `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` was not created. `.gitignore` synthesis runs only under the same trigger as `npm install`. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112c**: `.gitignore` write failure skips `npm install` for that workflow, contributes to aggregate failure, and **does not abort the auto-install pass over the remaining workflows**. Multi-workflow source with **three** workflows: `alpha/`, `beta/`, and `gamma/` — each has a normal `package.json` and no pre-existing `.gitignore` (so loopx must synthesize one for each). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run install with `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-write-fail:beta,gamma` (the fault-injection seam from section 1.4 — **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` are gated to fail). Assert: (a) exit code 1 (aggregate failure due to both `beta/` and `gamma/`'s safeguard write failures), (b) **the set** of shim-log invocation cwds is exactly `{ .loopx/alpha/ }` — `npm install` ran only for `alpha/` (its safeguard succeeded) and was skipped on both `beta/` and `gamma/` (their safeguards failed).
