The two-failing-workflow form is what makes continuation-after-failure observable regardless of commit order: SPEC 10.10 does not define commit order, so `beta/` and `gamma/` may be in any positions in the sequence — but regardless of ordering, an implementation that aborted the pass at the **first** failure would invoke `npm install` for `alpha/` only when `alpha/` is processed **before** both `beta/` and `gamma/`. Since with two failing workflows there is no commit order in which `alpha/` runs **after** the first failure without the pass having continued past at least one failure, asserting that `alpha/` ran and both `beta/` and `gamma/` are listed in the aggregate report proves the pass continued past the first failure in every possible commit order. (c) stderr contains an aggregate failure report listing **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` with `.gitignore` write failures — a pass that aborted at the first failure would list at most one of them. (d) `alpha/` is **not** listed in the aggregate report (its safeguard and `npm install` both succeeded). **No sequential-overlap assertion on shim invocations is made here** — only `alpha/` reaches the shim under this fault configuration, so a non-overlap assertion across multiple `FakeNpmInvocation` timestamps would be vacuous. The "sequentially in commit order" axis of SPEC 10.10 is exercised separately by T-INST-110 (two workflows, both reach the shim) and T-INST-114 (three workflows, all reach the shim) where multiple npm invocations actually occur. Using the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT` seam (rather than real filesystem permissions) keeps the test deterministic, CI-friendly, and runnable under any user (including `root`). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112d**: `.gitignore` write failure does **not** roll back committed workflow files. SPEC 10.10 explicitly says "Committed workflow files are not rolled back" when a `.gitignore` write fails — the failure is recorded on the same aggregate-report footing as an `npm install` spawn failure, and the user remediates manually. T-INST-112c covers the aggregate-report and continuation-after-failure clauses but does not explicitly assert the no-rollback contract on the failing workflow's directory. Same multi-workflow fixture as T-INST-112c (`alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/` each with `index.sh` + `package.json` and no pre-existing `.gitignore`), same fault: `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-write-fail:beta,gamma`. After `loopx install <source>` exits 1, assert: (a) `.loopx/alpha/index.sh`, `.loopx/alpha/package.json`, and `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` (synthesized) all exist on disk with their intended contents — `alpha/` had a successful safeguard + `npm install`, (b) `.loopx/beta/index.sh` and `.loopx/beta/package.json` exist on disk byte-for-byte from the source — file-level commit succeeded for `beta/` even though its safeguard write failed; loopx did not delete or move `beta/`'s committed files, (c) `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` does **not** exist (the safeguard write failed, so no `.gitignore` was committed for `beta/`), and (d) `.loopx/gamma/` mirrors `beta/`'s state (same shape, same no-`.gitignore` outcome — proves no-rollback applies independently to each failing workflow rather than rolling back all failures together). **Do not assert directly on the implementation-private staging-directory path**: the staging-path location and naming are unspecified (see known gap T-INST-79a — section 4.10 "Staging-directory Cleanup Visibility"), so a black-box test must limit itself to externally visible state (committed workflow files remaining in `.loopx/`). A previous revision of this test additionally asserted "no temporary staging-directory leftovers remain at the install-staging-root path"; that assertion is removed here because it contradicts the known gap and is unreachable without an implementation-specific staging-path seam. T-INST-117 covers the analogous no-rollback contract for `npm install` non-zero-exit failures; this test extends that contract to the `.gitignore`-write-failure branch, which is normatively part of the same rule but exercises a different code path. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112e**: Pre-existing `.gitignore` **directory** at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` is a safeguard failure (the non-regular branch from SPEC 10.10's `lstat` dispatch). Multi-workflow source where `alpha/` contains `alpha/index.sh`, `alpha/package.json`, and `alpha/.gitignore/` — a directory entry named `.gitignore` containing at least one placeholder file (e.g., `alpha/.gitignore/README` with arbitrary content) so the directory is non-empty and is preserved through git/tarball install — and `beta/` is a normal workflow with `beta/index.sh` + `beta/package.json` and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore`. The fixture is parameterized across git and tarball sources (per the SPEC 10.2 / 10.11 source-form parity used elsewhere in this block), since both transports must preserve a directory named `.gitignore` at the workflow root through file-level install. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, sleepSeconds: 1, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 — `alpha/`'s safeguard failure contributes to final exit `1` per SPEC 10.10's "the failure contributes to final exit code `1`" clause. (b) The fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation with cwd `.loopx/beta/` — `npm install` ran for `beta/` (its safeguard succeeded via the ENOENT-creation branch — loopx synthesized a regular `.gitignore` containing `node_modules`) and was skipped for `alpha/` (the directory at `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` failed the `lstat`-regular check). (c) The aggregate failure report on stderr lists `alpha/` as a `.gitignore` safeguard failure and does **not** list `beta/`. (d) `.loopx/alpha/index.sh` and `.loopx/alpha/package.json` exist on disk byte-for-byte from the source — no rollback of committed workflow files per SPEC 10.10's "Committed workflow files are not rolled back" clause. (e) `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` still exists as a **directory** with its placeholder file unchanged — loopx did **not** delete the directory, replace it with a regular file, mutate its contents, or otherwise touch the entry; "the entry is left unchanged" per the SPEC 10.10 non-regular branch. (f) `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` exists as a **regular file** containing exactly `node_modules` (optionally with a single trailing newline — implementation-defined, mirroring T-INST-112's content assertion), and `.loopx/beta/index.sh` + `.loopx/beta/package.json` exist on disk. The two-workflow form (one with directory `.gitignore`, one with no pre-existing `.gitignore`) proves both halves of the dispatch in a single test: the non-regular branch fails for `alpha/`, the ENOENT-creation branch succeeds for `beta/`, and the test catches a buggy implementation that aborted the entire pass on the first safeguard failure (which would also skip `beta/`, failing (b) and (f)) as well as one that wrongly proceeded with `npm install` against a directory `.gitignore` for `alpha/` (which would invoke the shim with cwd `.loopx/alpha/`, failing (b)) or one that mutated `alpha/.gitignore/` in any way (failing (e)). T-INST-112c covers continuation past `.gitignore` write failures via the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-write-fail:` seam; this test covers the externally observable directory-`.gitignore` failure path through real filesystem entries (no seam needed), pinning down the "directory" instance of SPEC 10.10's "directory, symlink, FIFO, socket, or other non-regular entry" enumeration on its concrete-fixture surface. The symlink instance is exercised separately by T-INST-112g (using the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-symlink:` seam) across three symlink-shape sub-cases (regular-file target, broken, cyclic), because SPEC 10.11 materializes source symlinks before commit and ordinary install inputs cannot produce a committed symlink `.gitignore`. *(Spec 10.10, 10.11)*
- **T-INST-112f**: `--no-install` suppresses the `.gitignore` safeguard entirely, even when the existing entry is non-regular. SPEC 10.10's safeguard bullet says "The safeguard is skipped entirely under `--no-install`" — the `lstat` dispatch is short-circuited before it runs, so a non-regular pre-existing `.gitignore` (which would otherwise produce a safeguard failure per T-INST-112e) does **not** trigger any failure under `--no-install`. Same fixture as T-INST-112e — multi-workflow source where `alpha/` contains `alpha/index.sh`, `alpha/package.json`, and `alpha/.gitignore/` (a non-empty directory), and `beta/` contains `beta/index.sh` + `beta/package.json` with no pre-existing `.gitignore`. Use `withFakeNpm({ logFile })` (any exit configuration; the shim must not be invoked under `--no-install` per SPEC 10.10). Run `loopx install --no-install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — the non-regular `.gitignore` directory does **not** trigger a safeguard failure under `--no-install` because the safeguard never ran. (b) The fake-npm shim log records **zero** invocations (no `npm install` for either workflow per SPEC 10.10's `--no-install` semantics). (c) `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` still exists as a directory with its placeholder file unchanged — loopx did not touch the entry. (d) `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` does **not** exist — the safeguard did not synthesize one for `beta/` either, per SPEC 10.10 "The safeguard is skipped entirely under `--no-install`". (e) Both workflow directories are committed at `.loopx/alpha/` and `.loopx/beta/` with their source files (file-level commit succeeded for both; only auto-install was suppressed per SPEC 10.10 `--no-install`). (f) **No aggregate failure report** on stderr — no auto-install failures occurred because the safeguard was skipped entirely, not failed. This test pins down that `--no-install` is checked **before** the `lstat` dispatch — an implementation that ran the `lstat` check first and only suppressed the npm spawn under `--no-install` would emit a safeguard-failure aggregate-report entry for `alpha/` and fail (a), (c), and (f). Together with T-INST-111 (`--no-install` with no pre-existing `.gitignore`) and T-INST-111a (`--no-install` with a regular pre-existing `.gitignore`), this completes the `--no-install` × `.gitignore`-state coverage matrix across all three concrete states observable through ordinary install inputs: {no entry, regular file, non-regular entry}. *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-112g**: Pre-existing `.gitignore` **symlink** at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` is a safeguard failure across all three symlink-shape sub-cases per SPEC 10.10's "A symlink named `.gitignore` does not satisfy the safeguard, even if it resolves to a regular file. Broken and cyclic symlinks are treated as symlink entries and therefore fail the safeguard" rule. SPEC 10.11 materializes source symlinks as regular files / directories during install staging / copy, so a committed symlink at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` cannot be produced through ordinary install inputs — deterministic coverage uses the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-symlink:<workflow>[=<kind>]` seam from section 1.4, which replaces (or creates) the path with a symlink immediately after commit and before the per-workflow safeguard `lstat`, so the dispatch observes a symlink entry. Parameterize the test over the three `<kind>` values, each in its own multi-workflow run with a normal sibling `beta/` workflow (no pre-existing `.gitignore`) to prove the safeguard pass continues past the symlink failure rather than aborting:

  - **T-INST-112g-1 (`regular-file-target`)**: Multi-workflow source where `alpha/` has `alpha/index.sh` + `alpha/package.json` (and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore` in source, so SPEC 10.11 materialization is irrelevant), and `beta/` has `beta/index.sh` + `beta/package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, sleepSeconds: 1, logFile })` and set `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-symlink:alpha=regular-file-target`. The seam, after commit and before the per-workflow safeguard `lstat` for `alpha/`, creates `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore-target` as a regular file containing `node_modules` and creates `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` as a symlink pointing to `.gitignore-target`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 — the symlink is a safeguard failure even though its target is a regular file containing the protective content. (b) The fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation with cwd `.loopx/beta/` — `npm install` ran for `beta/` (its safeguard succeeded via the ENOENT-creation branch) and was skipped for `alpha/` (the symlink failed the `lstat`-regular check). (c) The aggregate failure report on stderr lists `alpha/` as a `.gitignore` safeguard failure; `beta/` is not listed. (d) `.loopx/alpha/index.sh` and `.loopx/alpha/package.json` exist on disk byte-for-byte (no rollback). (e) `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` is still a symlink (using `lstat` to inspect the entry type, not `stat`) pointing to `.gitignore-target`, and `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore-target` still exists as a regular file containing `node_modules` — loopx did **not** delete the symlink, replace it, or follow it to overwrite the target. (f) `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` is a regular file containing `node_modules`. This sub-case is the load-bearing one: it pins the SPEC-text invariant "even if it resolves to a regular file" — an implementation that used `stat` (follows symlinks) instead of `lstat` for the safeguard check would observe a regular file at the symlink's target, take the regular-file branch, and proceed with `npm install` for `alpha/`, failing (a) and (b).
  - **T-INST-112g-2 (`broken`)**: Same fixture and shim configuration as T-INST-112g-1, with `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-symlink:alpha=broken`. The seam creates `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` as a symlink pointing to a non-existent target (e.g., `./does-not-exist`) without creating that target. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert (a)–(f) as in T-INST-112g-1, with (e) modified: `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` is still a symlink whose `readlink` target is `./does-not-exist`, and `.loopx/alpha/does-not-exist` does **not** exist. This sub-case pins the SPEC clause "Broken ... symlinks are treated as symlink entries and therefore fail the safeguard" — an implementation that fell back to `stat` after `lstat` revealed a symlink would receive `ENOENT` from the `stat` call and could mis-route to the ENOENT-creation branch (creating a regular `.gitignore` and clobbering the broken symlink). The test catches this by asserting the symlink entry is preserved and that no `npm install` ran for `alpha/`.
  - **T-INST-112g-3 (`cycle`)**: Same fixture and shim configuration as T-INST-112g-1, with `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-symlink:alpha=cycle`. The seam creates two symlinks at `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore -> .gitignore-loop` and `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore-loop -> .gitignore`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert (a)–(f) as in T-INST-112g-1, with (e) modified: both `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` and `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore-loop` remain as symlinks (verified via `lstat`), each pointing at the other. This sub-case pins the SPEC clause "cyclic symlinks are treated as symlink entries and therefore fail the safeguard" — an implementation that resolved the symlink (e.g., via `realpath` or `fs.statSync`) would encounter `ELOOP` and could produce a different failure category (e.g., a generic `lstat` failure) than the symlink branch; this test asserts the safeguard-failure category is reported uniformly across the three sub-cases.

  Together with T-INST-112e (directory), T-INST-112i (FIFO), T-INST-112j (socket), and T-INST-112c (write-failure via the `gitignore-write-fail:` seam) plus T-INST-112h (top-level `.gitignore` `lstat`-failure-other-than-`ENOENT` via the `gitignore-lstat-fail:` seam), this completes coverage of SPEC 10.10's non-regular enumeration ("directory, symlink, FIFO, socket, or other non-regular entry") and the safeguard-failure clause's `lstat`-failure branch. *(Spec 10.10, 10.11)*

- **T-INST-112h**: Top-level `.gitignore` `lstat` failure other than `ENOENT` is a safeguard failure — `npm install` is skipped for that workflow, the failure is recorded in the aggregate report, exit code is 1, committed workflow files are not rolled back, and auto-install continues to later workflows. SPEC 10.10's safeguard-failure clause enumerates the non-`ENOENT` `lstat` failure as one of two write-related branches: "`lstat` failure other than `ENOENT`, or a write failure when synthesizing `.gitignore`: loopx treats this as a `.gitignore` safeguard failure under the same aggregate-report / skip-`npm install` semantics." T-INST-112c covers the **write-failure** branch via the `gitignore-write-fail:` seam; this test covers the **`lstat`-failure** branch via the `gitignore-lstat-fail:` seam from section 1.4. The branches are observably distinct: the `lstat` failure short-circuits the dispatch before any write attempt is made, so a buggy implementation that handled write failures correctly but conflated `lstat`-failure with `ENOENT` (and proceeded to synthesize `.gitignore`) would pass T-INST-112c while violating SPEC 10.10's "`lstat` failure other than `ENOENT`" clause. Multi-workflow source with **three** workflows: `alpha/`, `beta/`, and `gamma/`, each with `index.sh` + a top-level `package.json` and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore` (so the safeguard runs in the synthesis path for the workflows whose `lstat` succeeds). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run install with `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-lstat-fail:beta,gamma` (the seam — **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` are gated to fail at `lstat`). Assert: (a) exit code 1 (aggregate failure due to both `beta/` and `gamma/`'s safeguard `lstat` failures), (b) **the set** of shim-log invocation cwds is exactly `{ .loopx/alpha/ }` — `npm install` ran only for `alpha/` (its safeguard succeeded via the `ENOENT`-creation branch) and was skipped on both `beta/` and `gamma/` (their safeguards short-circuited at the `lstat` failure before any write or spawn). The two-failing-workflow form follows the same continuation-after-failure rationale as T-INST-112c: with two failures, no commit order can mask a buggy implementation that aborted the pass at the first failure. (c) stderr contains an aggregate failure report listing **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` as `.gitignore` safeguard failures — a pass that aborted at the first failure would list at most one of them. (d) `alpha/` is **not** listed in the aggregate report (its safeguard and `npm install` both succeeded). (e) committed workflow files are **not** rolled back: `.loopx/alpha/index.sh`, `.loopx/alpha/package.json`, `.loopx/beta/index.sh`, `.loopx/beta/package.json`, `.loopx/gamma/index.sh`, and `.loopx/gamma/package.json` all exist on disk byte-for-byte from the source per SPEC 10.10's "Committed workflow files are not rolled back" clause. (f) `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` exists as a regular file containing exactly `node_modules` (synthesis succeeded for `alpha/`, mirroring T-INST-112's content assertion); `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` and `.loopx/gamma/.gitignore` do **not** exist on disk (the safeguard short-circuited at `lstat` before the write attempt — distinguishes the `lstat`-failure branch from the write-failure branch, where a write attempt may have produced a partial file). Using the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-lstat-fail:` seam (rather than real filesystem permissions) keeps the test deterministic, CI-friendly, and runnable under any user (including `root`). *(Spec 10.10)*

- **T-INST-112i**: Pre-existing `.gitignore` **FIFO** at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` is a safeguard failure (the FIFO instance of SPEC 10.10's "directory, symlink, FIFO, socket, or other non-regular entry" enumeration). Multi-workflow source where `alpha/` contains `alpha/index.sh` + `alpha/package.json` (and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore` in source, so SPEC 10.11 materialization is irrelevant), and `beta/` is a normal workflow with `beta/index.sh` + `beta/package.json` and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore` either. Ordinary install inputs cannot place a FIFO at the workflow's `.gitignore` path because git transports do not preserve FIFOs and tarball transports cannot carry FIFO entries through standard archive formats — deterministic coverage uses the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-fifo:<workflow>` seam from section 1.4, which creates a FIFO at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` immediately after commit and before the per-workflow safeguard `lstat`, so the dispatch observes a FIFO entry. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, sleepSeconds: 1, logFile })` and set `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-fifo:alpha`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 — the FIFO is a safeguard failure per SPEC 10.10's non-regular-entry enumeration; the failure contributes to final exit code `1` per SPEC 10.10's "the failure contributes to final exit code `1`" clause. (b) The fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation with cwd `.loopx/beta/` — `npm install` ran for `beta/` (its safeguard succeeded via the `ENOENT`-creation branch) and was skipped for `alpha/` (the FIFO failed the `lstat`-regular check); proves the safeguard pass continues past the FIFO failure rather than aborting. (c) The aggregate failure report on stderr lists `alpha/` as a `.gitignore` safeguard failure and does **not** list `beta/`. (d) `.loopx/alpha/index.sh` and `.loopx/alpha/package.json` exist on disk byte-for-byte (no rollback). (e) `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` is **still a FIFO** (verified via `lstat` returning a `S_IFIFO` entry type) — loopx did **not** delete the FIFO, replace it with a regular file, or otherwise touch the entry; "the entry is left unchanged" per SPEC 10.10's non-regular branch. (f) `.loopx/beta/.gitignore` is a regular file containing `node_modules` (the safeguard succeeded for `beta/` via the `ENOENT`-creation branch, mirroring T-INST-112's content assertion). The two-workflow form (one with FIFO `.gitignore`, one with no pre-existing `.gitignore`) proves both halves of the dispatch in a single test, mirroring T-INST-112e's directory pattern and T-INST-112g's symlink pattern. This sub-case pins the FIFO branch of SPEC 10.10's non-regular enumeration — an implementation that special-cased the regular/symlink/directory entry types but fell through to a generic "unrecognized" path for FIFO (e.g., proceeding with `npm install` against a FIFO `.gitignore`, or replacing the FIFO with a synthesized regular file) would fail (a), (b), or (e). *(Spec 10.10)*

- **T-INST-112j**: Pre-existing `.gitignore` **socket** at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` is a safeguard failure (the socket instance of SPEC 10.10's "directory, symlink, FIFO, socket, or other non-regular entry" enumeration). Companion to T-INST-112i (FIFO). Same fixture and shim configuration as T-INST-112i — multi-workflow source where `alpha/` has `alpha/index.sh` + `alpha/package.json` and `beta/` has `beta/index.sh` + `beta/package.json`, neither with a pre-existing `.gitignore` — and use `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-replace-with-socket:alpha`. The seam creates a Unix-domain socket at `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` immediately after commit and before the per-workflow safeguard `lstat`, so the dispatch observes a socket entry. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert (a)–(f) as in T-INST-112i, with (e) modified: `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore` is **still a socket** (verified via `lstat` returning a `S_IFSOCK` entry type). This sub-case pins the socket branch of SPEC 10.10's non-regular enumeration — an implementation that handled FIFO entries correctly but fell through to a generic path for sockets (or one that conflated FIFOs and sockets without distinguishing) would still pass T-INST-112i while failing this test. *(Spec 10.10)*

- **T-INST-112k**: Pre-existing **regular but unreadable** `.gitignore` at `.loopx/<workflow>/.gitignore` (mode `000`) — safeguard treats it as a regular file, proceeds to `npm install`, emits no safeguard failure, and leaves the file unchanged (including its mode bits). SPEC 10.10's regular-file branch normatively says: "loopx leaves it unchanged and proceeds. loopx does not read, parse, validate, append to, or **inspect the file's contents or permissions**; the workflow author is responsible for any pre-existing regular `.gitignore`." T-INST-112a covers the **content** axis of "leaves unchanged" (byte-identical content after install) but not the **permission-non-inspection** axis: a buggy implementation that read the file's content (or attempted to append to it, then bailed on `EACCES`) would still pass T-INST-112a's content assertion against a normally-readable fixture, since the read would succeed and produce no observable mutation. This test pins down the SPEC clause that loopx must **not** attempt to read or otherwise inspect a regular `.gitignore`'s permissions. Single-workflow source where the workflow has `index.sh`, a top-level `package.json`, and a regular `.gitignore` containing `dist/\n# my-content\n` (committed with normally-readable mode bits). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })` and the section 1.4 `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-make-unreadable:<workflow-name>` seam — the seam chmods the committed regular `.gitignore` to mode `000` immediately after commit and before the per-workflow safeguard `lstat`, and is a no-op when the on-disk entry is non-regular so it composes safely with the symlink / FIFO / socket replacement seams. Run `loopx install <source>` with `NODE_ENV=test` and `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-make-unreadable:<workflow-name>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — the safeguard treated the unreadable regular file as a regular file and proceeded to spawn `npm install` without inspecting its contents or permissions; (b) the fake-npm shim log records exactly one invocation with cwd `.loopx/<name>/` (auto-install ran for the workflow despite the unreadable `.gitignore`); (c) no aggregate failure report is emitted on stderr (the unreadable regular file is not a safeguard failure); (d) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` still exists as a regular file (verified via `lstat` returning a `S_IFREG` entry type — loopx did not unlink, replace, or otherwise touch the entry); and (e) the on-disk mode bits are still mode `000` from what the seam set (verified via `lstat` reading `0o000` for the user / group / other permission bits on the file) — loopx did not chmod the file readable in order to inspect its contents, did not chmod it for any other reason, and did not append a `node_modules` line that would have required re-opening it for write. **Mode-restoration division of responsibility (per the section 1.4 seam contract).** The seam contract specifies that loopx leaves the file at mode `000` when its own auto-install pass completes — loopx does **not** restore the mode bits during teardown. The test's `afterEach` (or post-assertion cleanup hook) is responsible for chmod'ing the file back to a readable mode (e.g., `0o644`) so that fixture-cleanup logic can read and remove the file; without this harness-side restoration, fixture cleanup will fail with `EACCES` under `process.getuid() !== 0`. The harness must perform the restoration **after** the post-run assertions in (d) and (e) read the on-disk mode bits, otherwise the assertion would observe the restored mode rather than the seam-set mode `000` and the test would silently pass for a buggy implementation. Earlier wording suggested that loopx's own teardown might restore the mode; that has been clarified to remove the contradiction with assertion (e), which would not survive an implementation-side restore. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** (root can read mode-`000` files, defeating the test). A buggy implementation that special-cased `.gitignore` content inspection (e.g., reading the file to check whether `node_modules` is already listed before deciding whether to append) would fail (a) with an `EACCES` error during the read, would fail (c) with a safeguard-failure aggregate report, or would fail (e) with the file's mode having been temporarily relaxed to enable the read. Together with T-INST-112a (content-non-inspection axis on a normally-readable fixture), this completes coverage of SPEC 10.10's "does not inspect ... contents or permissions" clause. *(Spec 10.10)*

##### Malformed `package.json`

- **T-INST-113**: Malformed `package.json` (invalid JSON) — warning once, auto-install skipped silently. Single-workflow source with `package.json` containing `{broken`. Use `withFakeNpm`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0 (malformed `package.json` is non-fatal per Spec 3.2/10.6), (b) the shim log is empty (`npm install` not invoked — the skip is silent per Spec 10.10 "does not add a second warning"), (c) stderr contains exactly one `package.json` parse-failure warning for the workflow (from Spec 3.2's existing warning path, not a new auto-install warning), (d) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` was not created (safeguard also skipped for the malformed workflow), (e) no aggregate failure report is emitted (skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not an auto-install failure). *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113a**: Malformed `package.json` (unreadable) — same as T-INST-113 but with mode 000. Single-workflow source from a **tarball** (git does not preserve mode 000). Use `withFakeNpm`. Run install. Assert: exit 0, no `npm` invocation, single unreadable-file warning, no `.gitignore` synthesis, no aggregate failure report. **Conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`.** *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113b**: Malformed `package.json` (invalid semver range) — same structure as T-INST-113. Source's `package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }`. Use `withFakeNpm`. Run install. Assert: exit 0, no `npm` invocation, single invalid-semver warning, no `.gitignore` synthesis, no aggregate failure report. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113g**: Malformed `package.json` (`loopx` version field is a **non-string** value) — auto-install skip per SPEC 10.10 malformed-`package.json` rules. Same structure as T-INST-113b but with a non-string range. Source's `package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": 42 } }` (JSON number, not a string). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit 0 (malformed `package.json` is non-fatal per SPEC 3.2 / 10.6), (b) the shim log is empty (`npm install` not invoked — auto-install skipped under SPEC 10.10's malformed-`package.json` rule), (c) stderr contains exactly one invalid-semver / `package.json` warning for the workflow (the SPEC 3.2 warning, not a second auto-install warning per SPEC 10.10 "does not add a second warning for the same underlying failure"), (d) `.loopx/<name>/.gitignore` was not created (safeguard skipped alongside `npm install`), (e) no aggregate failure report (skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not an auto-install failure), and (f) the workflow is still committed at `.loopx/<name>/` (file-level commit succeeded; only auto-install was skipped). T-INST-113b covers the invalid-string-range path; this test covers the non-string-type path, completing T-VER-09a / T-VER-12c at the auto-install-skip surface. A buggy implementation that gated the SPEC 10.10 skip on `typeof rangeValue === "string" && !semver.validRange(rangeValue)` (rather than "any value that is not a valid semver range, including non-strings") would wrongly proceed to `npm install` here and fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113d**: Auto-install does **not** skip for an invalid semver range in an ignored dependency field. SPEC 3.2 says workflow-level version checking reads only `dependencies.loopx` and `devDependencies.loopx`; `optionalDependencies.loopx` and `peerDependencies.loopx` are ignored (T-VER-11 / T-VER-11a / T-VER-11b pin the runtime side). SPEC 10.10's "Malformed `package.json`" auto-install skip fires only when the workflow's `package.json` is unreadable, contains invalid JSON, or has an invalid semver range **in a field that workflow-level version checking reads**. An invalid semver range in an ignored field must not trigger the auto-install skip. Single-workflow source with `package.json = { "optionalDependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }` (no `dependencies.loopx`, no `devDependencies.loopx`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stderr contains no `package.json` parse / semver / unreadable warning and no auto-install-skip indication (the ignored-field invalid range is invisible at both the version-check tier and the auto-install-skip tier), (c) the fake-npm shim log shows **exactly one** invocation for the workflow (auto-install ran), and (d) `.gitignore` safeguard was synthesized (safeguard ran alongside `npm install`). Together with T-VER-11a (runtime ignores invalid `optionalDependencies.loopx`), this pins down the symmetric rule at install time — an implementation that wrongly routed all `package.json` parse anomalies into the auto-install-skip path would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113d2**: Auto-install does **not** skip for an invalid semver range in `peerDependencies.loopx`. Companion to T-INST-113d (`optionalDependencies` invalid-range ignored at the auto-install-skip tier); this test extends the same coverage to the second ignored dependency field, `peerDependencies`. Per SPEC 3.2, workflow-level version checking reads only `dependencies.loopx` and `devDependencies.loopx`; `peerDependencies.loopx` is not one of the enumerated fields. SPEC 10.10's malformed-`package.json` auto-install-skip rule therefore must not fire for an invalid semver range in `peerDependencies.loopx`. Single-workflow source with `package.json = { "peerDependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }` (no `dependencies.loopx`, no `devDependencies.loopx`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) stderr contains no `package.json` parse / semver / unreadable warning and no auto-install-skip indication (the ignored-field invalid range is invisible at both the version-check tier and the auto-install-skip tier), (c) the fake-npm shim log shows **exactly one** invocation for the workflow (auto-install ran — `peerDependencies.loopx` is outside the SPEC 3.2 enumerated set, so its invalidity does not gate auto-install), and (d) `.gitignore` safeguard was synthesized (safeguard ran alongside `npm install`). Together with T-VER-11b2 (runtime), T-VER-13b2 (install-time version-check tier with `--no-install`), and this test (auto-install-skip tier with `withFakeNpm`), all three observation surfaces are pinned for invalid `peerDependencies.loopx`, mirroring the existing three-surface coverage for invalid `optionalDependencies.loopx` (T-VER-11a / T-VER-13a / T-INST-113d). A buggy implementation that wrongly routed all invalid semver ranges anywhere in `package.json` into the auto-install-skip path would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113e**: Auto-install does **not** skip when `dependencies.loopx` is valid-and-satisfied but `devDependencies.loopx` is an invalid semver range. Per SPEC 3.2 precedence, `dependencies` wins over `devDependencies` when present (T-VER-10b proves `devDependencies` is fully invisible when `dependencies.loopx` is valid and satisfied). Since `devDependencies.loopx` is not read, its invalidity must not surface as a warning and must not trigger the auto-install skip. Single-workflow source with `package.json = { "dependencies": { "loopx": "<running-version>" }, "devDependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }` where `<running-version>` is the actually-running loopx version (or a `*` range). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) no version mismatch / invalid-semver / parse warning on stderr, (c) exactly one `npm` invocation for the workflow (auto-install ran), and (d) `.gitignore` safeguard was synthesized. This is the install-time counterpart to T-VER-10b (runtime) and closes a subtle gap — an implementation that validates every `*.loopx` field found in `package.json` (even ones precedence-skipped) could wrongly emit an invalid-semver warning and skip auto-install here. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113f**: Auto-install does **not** skip when a non-`loopx` dependency has an invalid semver range. Companion to T-VER-11d / T-VER-13d. SPEC 3.2 restricts version validation to `dependencies.loopx` / `devDependencies.loopx`; an invalid range on any other dependency entry must not be treated as a `package.json` failure and must not trigger the SPEC 10.10 malformed-`package.json` auto-install skip. Single-workflow source with `index.sh` and a top-level `package.json` containing `{ "dependencies": { "not-loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }` (no `loopx` field anywhere). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) no `package.json`-related warnings on stderr (no invalid-semver warning, no parse warning, no auto-install-skip indication), (c) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation for the workflow (auto-install ran — invalid ranges on unrelated dependencies must not short-circuit auto-install), and (d) `.gitignore` safeguard was synthesized alongside `npm install`. An implementation that wrongly routed "any invalid semver anywhere in `package.json`" into the SPEC 10.10 skip path would fail this test. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113c**: Multi-workflow install continues past a malformed-`package.json` workflow. Per SPEC 10.10 "Malformed `package.json`": the malformed-`package.json` workflow is skipped silently for auto-install, is **not** treated as an aggregate failure, and other workflows still proceed. Create a multi-workflow source with **two** committed workflows: `alpha/` with `alpha/index.sh` and a **malformed** `alpha/package.json` (invalid JSON, e.g., `{broken`), and `beta/` with `beta/index.sh` and a **valid** `beta/package.json` (e.g., `{ "name": "beta", "version": "1.0.0" }`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 0 — not 1, because the skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not an auto-install failure per SPEC 10.10, (b) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation — for `beta/` (cwd = `.loopx/beta/`, argv = `["install"]`), and **zero** invocations for `alpha/`, (c) stderr contains exactly **one** `package.json` parse-failure warning for `alpha/` (the single existing SPEC 3.2 warning — no second auto-install warning), (d) no `.gitignore` synthesis occurred for `alpha/` (safeguard is skipped alongside `npm install` for malformed-`package.json` workflows), (e) `.gitignore` synthesis occurred for `beta/` (the safeguard ran fresh for the valid workflow), (f) **no aggregate failure report** is emitted on stderr — the skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not a failure and triggers no report, and (g) both workflow directories are committed at `.loopx/alpha/` and `.loopx/beta/`. T-INST-113 / T-INST-113a / T-INST-113b cover the malformed-`package.json` skip behavior only in single-workflow cases; this test closes the gap by exercising the SPEC 10.10 continuation rule — that other workflows still proceed when one is skipped — and the negative "no aggregate failure" assertion that the single-workflow tests cannot exercise. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*
- **T-INST-113c2**: Multi-workflow install continues past a malformed-`package.json` workflow regardless of the underlying malformation cause. Companion to T-INST-113c which covers only the **invalid-JSON** cause; this test extends the SPEC 10.10 continuation rule to the remaining SPEC 3.2 / 10.10 malformed-`package.json` causes — **unreadable `package.json`**, **invalid semver range in `dependencies.loopx`**, and **non-string `dependencies.loopx` value** — by parameterizing the same two-workflow continuation fixture over each cause variant. The single-workflow tests T-INST-113a (unreadable), T-INST-113b (invalid semver), and T-INST-113g (non-string `loopx` value) pin the per-cause auto-install-skip behavior in isolation, but they do not exercise the SPEC 10.10 "other workflows still proceed" clause for any cause other than invalid-JSON. A buggy implementation that routed the four SPEC 3.2 / 10.10 malformed-`package.json` causes through separate code paths and got the multi-workflow continuation right only for invalid-JSON would pass T-INST-113c plus the per-cause single-workflow tests yet fail this test for the un-tested causes. **Variants** (each a distinct test instance — three sub-cases): **(i) unreadable `alpha/package.json`** — same fixture shape as T-INST-113c but `alpha/package.json` is created with mode `000` after install staging (or via the tarball source variant from T-INST-113a so the mode is preserved through the install pipeline); **conditional on `process.getuid() !== 0`** (per T-INST-113a's gating); **(ii) invalid semver range in `alpha/package.json`** — `alpha/package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": "not-a-range!!!" } }` (matching T-INST-113b's range); **(iii) non-string `dependencies.loopx` value in `alpha/package.json`** — `alpha/package.json` contains `{ "dependencies": { "loopx": 42 } }` (matching T-INST-113g's non-string range). For each variant, `beta/` always has the same valid `beta/package.json` as T-INST-113c (`{ "name": "beta", "version": "1.0.0" }`). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. **Per-variant assertions** mirror T-INST-113c's seven assertions: (a) exit code 0 (skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not an auto-install failure per SPEC 10.10), (b) the fake-npm shim log records **exactly one** invocation — for `beta/` (cwd = `.loopx/beta/`, argv = `["install"]`), and **zero** invocations for `alpha/`, (c) stderr contains exactly **one** SPEC 3.2 `package.json` warning for `alpha/` matching the variant's cause (unreadable / invalid-semver / invalid-semver-non-string — same warning categories that T-INST-113a / T-INST-113b / T-INST-113g pin in single-workflow form; **no second auto-install warning** for any variant), (d) no `.gitignore` synthesis occurred for `alpha/` (safeguard is skipped alongside `npm install` for the malformed workflow regardless of cause), (e) `.gitignore` synthesis occurred for `beta/` (the safeguard ran fresh for the valid workflow regardless of `alpha/`'s cause), (f) **no aggregate failure report** is emitted on stderr (the skipped-due-to-malformed workflow is not a failure and triggers no report, regardless of cause), and (g) both workflow directories are committed at `.loopx/alpha/` and `.loopx/beta/` (file-level commit succeeds for the malformed workflow regardless of cause; only auto-install is skipped). Together with T-INST-113c (invalid-JSON cause × multi-workflow continuation) and T-INST-113a / T-INST-113b / T-INST-113g (per-cause single-workflow auto-install-skip), this closes the SPEC 10.10 "other workflows still proceed" rule across the full enumerated SPEC 3.2 malformed-`package.json` cause set. *(Spec 10.10, 3.2)*

##### `npm install` Failures

- **T-INST-114**: `npm install` non-zero exit — aggregate report, continue processing remaining workflows, final exit 1. Multi-workflow source: `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/` each with `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCodeByWorkflow: { beta: 1, gamma: 1 }, logFile })` so the shim exits non-zero when invoked in `.loopx/beta/` or `.loopx/gamma/`, regardless of their position in commit order; `alpha/` falls back to the default `exitCode` of 0. Workflow-name keying avoids assuming a commit order that SPEC 10.10 does not define (cf. T-INST-112c's rationale). The two-failing-workflow form is what makes continuation-after-failure observable regardless of commit order: with one failure only, a pass that aborted at the first failure would still invoke `npm install` for all workflows whenever the failing workflow is last in commit order, so the "all three invoked" assertion passes vacuously. With **two** failing workflows, an implementation that aborted at the first failure would invoke `npm install` for at most one of `{beta, gamma}` (whichever came first), and the three-invocations assertion below catches that. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) shim log records **three** invocations total and **the set** of invocation cwds is exactly `{ .loopx/alpha/, .loopx/beta/, .loopx/gamma/ }` — all three workflows were invoked. Because `beta/` and `gamma/`'s failures did not abort the pass, every workflow's `npm install` ran regardless of commit order; this proves SPEC 10.10's "proceeds with the next workflow" contract without relying on a specific ordering. (c) stderr contains an aggregate failure report listing **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` as `npm install` non-zero-exit failures — a pass that aborted at the first failure would list at most one of them. (d) `alpha/` is not listed, (e) all three workflow directories are committed at `.loopx/<name>/` — the non-zero npm exit does **not** roll back committed workflow files (Spec 10.10 "Dependency-install failures do not roll back the file-level install"). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-114a**: `npm` spawn failure — aggregate report, continue processing later workflows, final exit 1. Same three-workflow source. Use `withFakeNpm({ spawnFailure: true, logFile })` — the shim file is not created, so spawning `npm` fails with `ENOENT`. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) log shows zero invocations (spawn itself failed — the shim never ran), (c) stderr aggregate failure report lists all three workflows (each got its own spawn failure), (d) all three workflow directories still committed. This distinguishes spawn-failure (Spec 10.10 "`npm` is not on `PATH`") from non-zero-exit (T-INST-114). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-114c**: `npm install` spawn failure does not skip the `.gitignore` safeguard — synthesized `.gitignore` files remain on disk for each affected workflow. SPEC 10.10 specifies that the safeguard runs **immediately before** spawning `npm install`: "Immediately before spawning `npm install` in a workflow directory, loopx checks for a top-level `.gitignore` ... If none exists, loopx creates one containing the single line `node_modules`." Because the safeguard runs before the spawn attempt, a downstream spawn failure must not unwind the safeguard — the `.gitignore` was already synthesized when the spawn was attempted. T-INST-114a covers the spawn-failure / aggregate-failure-report / continue-processing branches but does not assert that the safeguard ran for each affected workflow. A buggy implementation that re-ordered the safeguard / spawn sequence (synthesizing `.gitignore` only after a successful `npm install`, or rolling back the safeguard on spawn failure) would pass T-INST-114a while violating SPEC 10.10's ordering. Same three-workflow source as T-INST-114a (`alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/`, each with `package.json`) — but ensure **none** of the workflow source trees include a pre-existing `.gitignore`, so the safeguard must synthesize one. Use `withFakeNpm({ spawnFailure: true, logFile })`. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (spawn-failure aggregate failure, same as T-INST-114a), (b) shim log records zero invocations (spawn never reached the shim), (c) the aggregate failure report on stderr lists all three workflows as spawn failures, (d) all three workflow directories committed under `.loopx/<name>/`, and (e) **for each of `.loopx/alpha/.gitignore`, `.loopx/beta/.gitignore`, `.loopx/gamma/.gitignore`**: the file exists with content exactly `node_modules` (optionally with a single trailing newline — implementation-defined, mirroring T-INST-112's content assertion). The safeguard ran before each per-workflow spawn attempt, so all three `.gitignore` files were synthesized even though every spawn failed afterward. T-INST-112 pins down the safeguard-before-spawn ordering on the success path via the `recordGitignoreAtStart` shim hook; this test pins down the ordering on the **spawn-failure** path via the externally observable `.gitignore`-on-disk surface (the shim never runs, so the in-shim hook from T-INST-112 is unavailable here). *(Spec 10.10)*
- **T-INST-114d**: Mixed auto-install failure modes in a single multi-workflow install — aggregate report distinguishes failure categories per workflow, continues processing remaining workflows, and excludes successful workflows from the report. SPEC 10.10's aggregate-failure-report contract specifies that loopx "continues processing remaining workflows" past any individual auto-install failure and that the final aggregate report identifies each failed workflow with its underlying cause; the four-workflow mixed-failure case pins down that the **failure category is preserved per workflow** rather than collapsed into a generic "auto-install failed" entry, and that all three normatively-enumerated failure categories (`.gitignore` write failure from SPEC 10.10's safeguard clause, `npm install` non-zero exit from SPEC 10.10's "non-zero exit" branch, `npm install` spawn failure from SPEC 10.10's "`npm` is not on `PATH`" branch) appear together without one displacing or aliasing another. T-INST-112c covers `.gitignore` write failure in isolation, T-INST-114 covers `npm install` non-zero exit in isolation, T-INST-114a / T-INST-114b cover spawn failure (the latter selectively per workflow), but the **simultaneous mixed-cause case** is uncovered — a buggy implementation that handled each failure category through a shared aggregate-failure code path could collapse the cause-distinction (e.g., reporting all three failures as "npm install failed for X" regardless of whether spawn or non-zero-exit or safeguard-write was the actual cause) and pass each isolated test while violating the per-workflow cause-preservation contract here. **Setup.** Multi-workflow source with **four** workflows: `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/`, and `delta/`, each with `index.sh` and a top-level `package.json` and **no** pre-existing `.gitignore` (so the safeguard must synthesize one for each). Configure faults: `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=gitignore-write-fail:alpha;npm-spawn-fail:gamma` (semicolon-separated combination per section 1.4 — `alpha/`'s safeguard write fails, `gamma/`'s spawn fails before reaching the shim). Use `withFakeNpm({ exitCodeByWorkflow: { beta: 1 }, logFile })` so the shim exits non-zero when invoked in `.loopx/beta/` and exits 0 in `.loopx/delta/`; the shim never runs for `alpha/` (skipped due to safeguard failure per SPEC 10.10) and never runs for `gamma/` (spawn-fault-injected). Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1 (aggregate failure due to three distinct failures), (b) the shim log records exactly two invocations, with cwds equal to `{ .loopx/beta/, .loopx/delta/ }` — `beta/` reached the shim and exited non-zero, `delta/` reached the shim and succeeded, `alpha/` and `gamma/` did not reach the shim, (c) the aggregate failure report on stderr lists **all three** failed workflows (`alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/`), (d) **the failure category preserved per workflow is correct**: `alpha/` is identified as a `.gitignore` write failure (matching the category from T-INST-112c), `beta/` is identified as an `npm install` non-zero-exit failure (matching the category from T-INST-114), `gamma/` is identified as an `npm install` spawn failure (matching the category from T-INST-114a) — the three categories are observably distinct in the aggregate report (even if the exact wording is implementation-defined, the test pattern-matches per workflow against either a category-specific phrase like "spawn failure" / "non-zero exit" / "`.gitignore` write failure", or a normalized error-class field), (e) `delta/` is **not** listed in the aggregate failure report (its safeguard succeeded, its `npm install` ran and exited 0; successful workflows are excluded from the report per SPEC 10.10), (f) **no rollback** of any committed workflow files: `.loopx/alpha/index.sh`, `.loopx/alpha/package.json`, `.loopx/beta/...`, `.loopx/gamma/...`, and `.loopx/delta/...` all exist on disk with their intended contents (per SPEC 10.10 / T-INST-117 / T-INST-112d), (g) `.gitignore` synthesis state per workflow: `alpha/` has no synthesized `.gitignore` (the safeguard failed before write), `beta/` / `gamma/` / `delta/` each have a synthesized `.gitignore` with content `node_modules` (the safeguard succeeded for those three, even though `gamma/`'s subsequent `npm install` spawn was fault-injected — per T-INST-114c, the safeguard runs before the spawn attempt so a downstream spawn failure does not unwind the safeguard), and (h) **continuation across mixed failures**: with three failures distributed across the run, an implementation that aborted the pass at the first failure would fail to record at least two of the three failures (missing entries in the aggregate report) and would skip `delta/` entirely (no shim invocation for `delta/`). The four-workflow mix and the three-category distinction together exercise the SPEC 10.10 "continue processing remaining workflows" contract under the worst case where each failure mode arrives at a different stage of the auto-install pipeline (safeguard-write, spawn, non-zero-exit). **No assertion on commit order is made**: SPEC 10.10 does not define commit order, so the test does not pin which workflow is processed first; the assertions are framed in terms of per-workflow outcomes (set membership on shim-log cwds, set membership and category-tag membership on the aggregate-report entries) rather than sequential observation. *(Spec 10.10, 12)*
- **T-INST-114b**: `npm install` spawn failure on one workflow does not prevent `npm install` on subsequent workflows. Multi-workflow source: `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/` each with a top-level `package.json`. Drive the per-workflow spawn-failure via the `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_FAULT=npm-spawn-fail:beta,gamma` seam (section 1.4) so loopx's spawn hook simulates `ENOENT` for **both** `beta/` and `gamma/`, while the ordinary `withFakeNpm({ exitCode: 0, logFile })` shim continues to satisfy real spawns for `alpha/`. Using two failing workflows (rather than one) makes continuation-after-failure observable regardless of commit order: SPEC 10.10 does not define commit order, so with a single failing workflow an implementation that aborted the pass at the first spawn failure could still pass if the failing workflow is processed last. With **two** failing workflows an implementation that aborted at the first failure would invoke the shim for at most one non-failing workflow, so asserting that the shim ran only for `alpha/` and that both `beta/` and `gamma/` appear in the aggregate report catches any implementation that stops at the first spawn failure. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) exit code 1, (b) the shim log records exactly one invocation — for `alpha/`, and **none** for `beta/` or `gamma/` (the seam short-circuits the spawn for both before the shim runs), (c) the aggregate failure report on stderr lists **both** `beta/` and `gamma/` as `npm install` spawn-failures — a pass that aborted at the first failure would list at most one of them. (d) `alpha/` is not listed. This test uses the seam — rather than a per-invocation shim configuration — because once a shim process starts, the spawn itself has already succeeded; a black-box fake `npm` cannot deterministically produce `ENOENT` for one invocation while handing off control to the shim for others. Together with T-INST-114a (all-invocations spawn failure via PATH control), this test closes the per-workflow spawn-failure branch of SPEC 10.10 "continue processing later workflows". *(Spec 10.10)*

##### Environment Purity

- **T-INST-115**: `npm install` inherits loopx's own `process.env` **unchanged** — positive parity for ordinary inherited vars, plus absence of injected-only vars. Per SPEC 10.10 "The `npm install` child inherits loopx's own `process.env` unchanged. `LOOPX_*` protocol variables, `RunOptions.env`, and env-file entries are not injected." Run this test with loopx's inherited environment explicitly scrubbed of any pre-existing `LOOPX_*` names so the absence assertions are meaningful. Spawn loopx with a controlled inherited environment: `MYVAR=inherited`, a base `PATH=/known/test/path:/usr/bin:/bin`, and a specific `TMPDIR=/tmp/known-loopx-tmp` (distinct from the system default — pick a real existing directory for TMPDIR so both loopx and the shim can run). Use `withFakeNpm({ logFile })` which prepends a shim directory to `PATH` for the duration of the test (so `<fake-npm-shim-dir>` becomes `PATH`'s first segment). Record the resulting effective inherited `PATH` as `${SHIM_DIR}:/known/test/path:/usr/bin:/bin` — that is the exact string loopx itself inherits. Create a global env file with `INJECTED_GLOBAL=yes` and a `PATH=/fake/env-file-path` override. The shim's log records the child's `MYVAR`, `INJECTED_GLOBAL`, `PATH`, `TMPDIR`, and `LOOPX_*` variables. Run `loopx install <source>`. Assert: (a) `MYVAR=inherited` — ordinary inherited vars reach the child, (b) **no** `INJECTED_GLOBAL` — env-file entries are not injected, (c) **no loopx-injected protocol variables** (`LOOPX_BIN`, `LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW_DIR`, `LOOPX_TMPDIR`) — script-execution-context concerns are scoped to spawned scripts, not the auto-install child, (d) `PATH` in the npm child's env equals loopx's own inherited `PATH` byte-for-byte (i.e., `${SHIM_DIR}:/known/test/path:/usr/bin:/bin`) — the env-file's `PATH=/fake/env-file-path` did **not** reach the child, loopx did **not** prepend, append, rewrite, or reorder `PATH`, and the shim-dir prefix is preserved because it was already present in loopx's inherited environment, and (e) `TMPDIR` in the npm child's env equals loopx's inherited `/tmp/known-loopx-tmp` exactly (unchanged; not rewritten to `LOOPX_TMPDIR` or any other path). The `PATH` assertion is a "passthrough byte-for-byte" check against loopx's own inherited `PATH` — including the shim-dir prefix — not a check against the pre-shim base `PATH`; either formulation of the assertion must resolve the shim prefix correctly. *(Spec 10.10, 8.3)*
- **T-INST-115a**: Inherited `LOOPX_*` protocol-variable values reach `npm install` unchanged. SPEC 10.10 says the `npm install` child "inherits loopx's own `process.env` unchanged" and that `LOOPX_*` protocol variables "are not injected." T-INST-115 pins down the "not injected" half by asserting that `LOOPX_*` names are absent from the npm child's env when loopx's own inherited env did **not** contain them. This test pins down the complementary positive case: when loopx **itself** inherits `LOOPX_*` names from the user's environment (because the user happened to set them in their shell, or because a parent loopx invocation in a delegated chain set them), those values must reach the npm child unchanged — loopx must neither scrub them nor overwrite them with its own injected protocol values (`npm install` is not a script-execution context, so the script-protocol-protected tier-1 override rule from SPEC 8.3 does not apply to the auto-install child). Single-workflow source with `index.sh` and a top-level `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ logFile })` so every npm invocation logs its observed env. Spawn `loopx install <source>` with the following values injected into loopx's **inherited** environment (in addition to the standard test-controlled `PATH` / `MYVAR` / `TMPDIR` from T-INST-115): `LOOPX_BIN=/inherited-bin/loopx`, `LOOPX_TMPDIR=/inherited-tmp`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW=/inherited-workflow`, `LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT=/inherited-project-root`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW_DIR=/inherited-workflow-dir`. The values are intentionally implausible / synthetic so a passthrough vs. overwrite is byte-distinguishable (a real run would produce derived paths under `os.tmpdir()` and the project cwd, never literal `/inherited-*` strings). Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the shim log records exactly one invocation, and (c) for each of the five `LOOPX_*` names in the inherited environment, the npm child's env contains the **inherited literal value** byte-for-byte — `LOOPX_BIN === "/inherited-bin/loopx"`, `LOOPX_TMPDIR === "/inherited-tmp"`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW === "/inherited-workflow"`, `LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT === "/inherited-project-root"`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW_DIR === "/inherited-workflow-dir"`. A buggy implementation that scrubbed `LOOPX_*` names before spawning `npm install` (e.g., to "protect" the npm child from leaked protocol context) would produce missing entries and fail (c); an implementation that overwrote them with its own derived script-execution values (e.g., the real injected `LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT` for the install command's cwd) would produce a different value than `/inherited-project-root` and also fail. Together with T-INST-115, this test distinguishes the "not injected" guarantee (loopx does not ADD protocol variables) from a hypothetical "scrubbed" or "overwritten" misreading (loopx must not REMOVE or REPLACE them either) — both halves are required by SPEC 10.10's "inherits loopx's own `process.env` unchanged" wording. *(Spec 10.10, 8.3)*
- **T-INST-115b**: Inherited `LOOPX_DELEGATED` reaches `npm install` unchanged. SPEC 13 classifies `LOOPX_DELEGATED` as a **startup-reserved-only** signal that controls loopx's delegation recursion guard (SPEC 3.2's recursion-guard clause): it is consulted at startup to decide whether to delegate, and is set on the delegated child's environment. Crucially, SPEC 13 does **not** classify `LOOPX_DELEGATED` as a script-protocol-protected variable (the protocol-protected names are `LOOPX_BIN`, `LOOPX_PROJECT_ROOT`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW`, `LOOPX_WORKFLOW_DIR`, `LOOPX_TMPDIR`); the SPEC 8.3 tier-1 override rule does not apply to it. Combined with SPEC 10.10's "the `npm install` child inherits loopx's own `process.env` unchanged" rule, an inherited `LOOPX_DELEGATED` must therefore propagate to the npm child like any ordinary inherited variable — neither scrubbed (because `npm install` is not a script-execution context where leaked protocol context matters) nor overwritten (because `LOOPX_DELEGATED` is not a script-protocol-protected name that loopx must inject). T-INST-115a covers the five script-protocol-protected names; this test closes the gap for the sixth `LOOPX_*` name, the startup-reserved-only `LOOPX_DELEGATED`. Single-workflow source with `index.sh` and a top-level `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ logFile })`. Spawn `loopx install <source>` with `LOOPX_DELEGATED=1` injected into loopx's inherited environment (alongside the standard test-controlled `PATH` / `MYVAR` / `TMPDIR` from T-INST-115). **Important fixture caveat:** `LOOPX_DELEGATED` set in loopx's inherited environment normally **prevents** delegation per SPEC 3.2 / T-DEL-04 — i.e., loopx itself does not re-delegate. That is the point: this test exercises the case where a non-delegating top-level loopx (because `LOOPX_DELEGATED` is set) still spawns the auto-install `npm install` child with the inherited `LOOPX_DELEGATED` reaching the child unchanged. Run install. Assert: (a) exit code 0, (b) the shim log records exactly one invocation, (c) the npm child's observed env contains `LOOPX_DELEGATED === "1"` (literal byte-for-byte, the inherited value), (d) the marker that would prove loopx itself re-delegated does **not** fire (no `node_modules/.bin/loopx` recursion — confirms `LOOPX_DELEGATED` recursion-guard semantics still hold for loopx itself), and (e) no parent / surrounding `LOOPX_DELEGATED` warning on stderr. A buggy implementation that scrubbed `LOOPX_DELEGATED` from the npm child's env would fail (c) — even though (d) would still pass, because the recursion guard fires before delegation; the npm-child-observation surface in (c) is independent. An implementation that overwrote `LOOPX_DELEGATED` with a different value (e.g., setting it on the npm child for some misguided "child is also delegated" reasoning) would also fail (c) byte-for-byte. Together with T-INST-115 (no protocol injection), T-INST-115a (inherited script-protocol-protected names pass through unchanged), and this test (inherited startup-reserved-only `LOOPX_DELEGATED` passes through unchanged), the SPEC 10.10 "inherits loopx's own `process.env` unchanged" rule is pinned down across all `LOOPX_*` names: protocol-protected (T-INST-115a) and the lone startup-reserved-only one (this test). *(Spec 10.10, 13, 3.2)*

##### Signal During `npm install`

- **T-INST-116**: SIGINT during `npm install` forwards to child process group and loopx exits with signal code. Use `withFakeNpm({ sleepSeconds: 30, pidFile, logFile })` (where `pidFile` is an absolute path under the test's own temp directory) — the shim writes its PID to `pidFile`, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then sleeps. Use `runCLIWithSignal` to spawn `loopx install <source>`. `waitForStderr("ready")`, read the shim PID from `pidFile`, then send SIGINT to loopx. Assert: (a) loopx exits with code 130 (128 + SIGINT), (b) the shim's PID no longer appears in the process table (was terminated — verify via `kill -0 <shim-pid>` returning non-zero). *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116a**: SIGTERM during `npm install` → exit 143, shim terminated. Same as T-INST-116 with SIGTERM. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116b**: SIGINT during `npm install` — remaining workflows are not processed (no further `npm` invocations **and** no further `.gitignore` synthesis). Multi-workflow source `alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/`, **each with `index.sh` + a top-level `package.json` and no pre-existing `.gitignore`** (so the `.gitignore` safeguard would synthesize one per workflow if the auto-install pass ran for that workflow). Use `withFakeNpm({ sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` so **every** invocation sleeps 30 s; the signal arrives during whichever workflow is processed first in commit order. The ready-protocol (shim writes `"ready"` to stderr before sleeping) lets the harness synchronize on the first invocation regardless of which workflow name it targets. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, `waitForStderr("ready")`, then send SIGINT to loopx. Assert: (a) exit 130, (b) the shim log records **exactly one** invocation — whichever workflow was first in commit order — and the other two workflow names are not present in the log, (c) **only the first-processed workflow's directory contains a synthesized `.gitignore`** (per SPEC 10.10's "Immediately before spawning `npm install`" ordering, the safeguard fired once for the workflow whose `npm install` then got interrupted), and (d) **the other two workflow directories do NOT contain a synthesized `.gitignore`** — `.gitignore` synthesis runs under the same trigger as `npm install`, so when the auto-install pass is interrupted, no further safeguard writes occur for unprocessed workflows. The test does not assume or assert which workflow name was first; SPEC 10.10's "remaining committed workflows are not processed (no further `.gitignore` synthesis or `npm install` invocations)" guarantee is pinned down by the "exactly one invocation, exactly one synthesized `.gitignore`" count, independent of commit order. A buggy implementation that continued safeguard writes for remaining workflows even after a signal — while correctly skipping their `npm install` calls — would pass (b) but fail (d). *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116b2**: SIGTERM during `npm install` — remaining workflows are not processed (SIGTERM-axis parity for T-INST-116b). SPEC 10.10's "Signals during `npm install`" clause names "SIGINT / SIGTERM" together for the "remaining committed workflows are not processed (no further `.gitignore` synthesis or `npm install` invocations)" guarantee. T-INST-116b covers the SIGINT branch via the `runCLIWithSignal` SIGINT-then-wait pattern; the SIGTERM branch is the structurally equivalent counterpart and must hold the same contract. T-INST-116a / T-INST-116c–116g cover SIGTERM termination of the active npm child (basic termination, SIGKILL escalation, process-group propagation) but do not assert that SIGTERM also blocks **remaining workflows**' processing — those tests use single-workflow or any-workflow-count fixtures and assert on child-termination behavior, not on the multi-workflow no-further-processing guarantee. A buggy implementation that correctly terminated the active npm child on SIGTERM (passing T-INST-116a / 116c / 116e) but continued processing the remaining workflows after the SIGTERM-induced exit (e.g., a code path that skipped the "remaining workflows are not processed" branch only for SIGTERM, or that fired the safeguard write loop on a SIGTERM-handler path different from SIGINT's) would pass T-INST-116b but fail this test. Use the same multi-workflow fixture and same `withFakeNpm({ sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` shim as T-INST-116b. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, `waitForStderr("ready")`, then send **SIGTERM** to loopx. Assert: (a) loopx exits with code **143** (128 + 15 for SIGTERM), (b) the shim log records **exactly one** invocation — whichever workflow was first in commit order — and the other two workflow names are not present in the log, (c) **only the first-processed workflow's directory contains a synthesized `.gitignore`** (per SPEC 10.10's "Immediately before spawning `npm install`" ordering, the safeguard fired once for the workflow whose `npm install` then got interrupted), (d) **the other two workflow directories do NOT contain a synthesized `.gitignore`** — `.gitignore` synthesis runs under the same trigger as `npm install`, so when the auto-install pass is interrupted by SIGTERM no further safeguard writes occur for unprocessed workflows, and (e) all three committed workflow directories (`alpha/`, `beta/`, `gamma/`) still exist on disk with their committed workflow files (`index.sh`, `package.json`) byte-for-byte from the source — committed workflow files are not rolled back under SPEC 10.10's signal-termination clause. As with T-INST-116b, the test does not assume or assert which workflow name was first; the order-independence is preserved by keying assertions on "exactly one invocation, exactly one synthesized `.gitignore`" rather than hard-coded ordering. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116c**: SIGKILL escalation when the `npm install` child ignores SIGTERM beyond the 5-second grace period. SPEC 10.10 "Signals during `npm install`" states: "the section 7.3 grace period and SIGKILL escalation rules apply by analogy" — i.e., after loopx forwards the signal to the npm child's process group, it waits 5 seconds for the group to exit and then sends SIGKILL. T-INST-116 / T-INST-116a cover the clean-exit path (the shim sleeps passively and is terminated by the forwarded signal); this test covers the escalation path. Configure `withFakeNpm` so the shim installs a SIGTERM trap that ignores the signal (equivalent to the `signal-trap-ignore` fixture in section 2.4), writes its PID to `pidFile`, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then sleeps indefinitely: `withFakeNpm({ sleepSeconds: 30, trapSignals: ["TERM", "INT"], pidFile, logFile })` (the `trapSignals` flag installs `trap '' TERM INT` in the shim's Bash preamble; `pidFile` is the alive-shim PID surface from the helper contract). Run `loopx install <multi-workflow source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`. Wait for `"ready"` on stderr, read the shim PID from `pidFile`, then send SIGTERM to loopx. Assert: (a) loopx waits approximately 5 seconds before the shim is terminated (the grace-period window — the test may allow a tolerance of 4–7 seconds to account for scheduling jitter), (b) after that window, the shim process is no longer running (verified via `kill -0 <shim-pid>` returning non-zero, indicating SIGKILL took effect since the shim was trapping/ignoring SIGTERM), (c) loopx exits with code 143 (128 + 15 for SIGTERM), and (d) the aggregate-failure-report branch from SPEC 10.10 is not triggered (the install was interrupted by a signal, not by a non-zero npm exit). This pins down that the SPEC 7.3 "5 seconds, then SIGKILL" escalation applies identically to the `npm install` child. `@flaky-retry(2)` due to the real-time 5-second wait. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116d**: SIGINT during `npm install` does not clean partial `node_modules/` state already produced by the interrupted install. Single-workflow source with top-level `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ createFiles: ["node_modules/partial-file"], sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` so the shim creates `node_modules/partial-file`, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then sleeps. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, wait for `"ready"`, send SIGINT, and assert: (a) loopx exits 130, and (b) `.loopx/<name>/node_modules/partial-file` still exists after loopx exits. Per SPEC 10.10, partial `node_modules/` state produced before interruption is not cleaned up by loopx. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116d2**: SIGTERM during `npm install` does not clean partial `node_modules/` state already produced by the interrupted install (SIGTERM-axis parity for T-INST-116d). SPEC 10.10's "Signals during `npm install`" clause says "Partial `node_modules/` state produced before interruption is not cleaned up by loopx" without distinguishing SIGINT and SIGTERM — the same rule applies to both signals. T-INST-116d covers the SIGINT branch; the SIGTERM branch is the structurally equivalent counterpart and must hold the same contract. A buggy implementation that left partial `node_modules/` in place on SIGINT (passing T-INST-116d) but cleaned it up on SIGTERM (e.g., a SIGTERM-handler path that mistakenly invoked an `rm -rf .loopx/<name>/node_modules` cleanup branch loopx does not own) would pass T-INST-116d and fail this test. Use the same single-workflow fixture and same `withFakeNpm({ createFiles: ["node_modules/partial-file"], sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` shim as T-INST-116d. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, wait for `"ready"`, send **SIGTERM**, and assert: (a) loopx exits with code **143** (128 + 15 for SIGTERM), and (b) `.loopx/<name>/node_modules/partial-file` still exists after loopx exits — the partial `node_modules/` state produced by the interrupted npm child is preserved on disk under SIGTERM termination just as it is under SIGINT. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116e**: Signal during `npm install` is forwarded to the npm child's **process group**, not just the npm shim process. Single-workflow source with top-level `package.json`. Use `withFakeNpm({ spawnGrandchild: true, grandchildPidFile, pidFile, sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` so the shim spawns a long-lived background child, writes the grandchild PID to `grandchildPidFile`, writes its own PID to `pidFile`, writes `"ready"` to stderr, then blocks. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, wait for `"ready"`, read the shim PID from `pidFile`, send SIGTERM to loopx, and assert: (a) loopx exits with code 143, (b) the npm shim PID is no longer running, and (c) the recorded grandchild PID is also no longer running. This is the auto-install/process-group counterpart to T-SIG-06. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116f**: SIGINT escalation when the `npm install` child traps / ignores SIGINT. SIGINT counterpart to T-INST-116c (SIGTERM escalation). SPEC 10.10 says "SIGINT / SIGTERM received while an `npm install` child is active propagates to the child's process group" and "the section 7.3 grace period and SIGKILL escalation rules apply by analogy" — both signals are subject to the 5-second grace + SIGKILL escalation. T-INST-116c covers the SIGTERM escalation branch; the SIGINT branch is uncovered, mirroring the runtime-script signal-parity gap that T-SIG-05a (runtime SIGINT escalation) closes vs. T-SIG-05 (runtime SIGTERM escalation). A buggy implementation that escalated to SIGKILL only for SIGTERM-trapping npm children — short-circuiting the grace period for SIGINT — would pass T-INST-116c but fail this test. Use the same fixture pattern as T-INST-116c with `trapSignals: ["INT", "TERM"]` and `pidFile` (already in T-INST-116c's setup) so the shim ignores **SIGINT** in addition to SIGTERM and exposes its PID via `pidFile`. Run `loopx install <multi-workflow source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, wait for `"ready"` on stderr, read the shim PID from `pidFile`, then send **SIGINT** to loopx. Assert: (a) loopx waits approximately 5 seconds before the shim is terminated (4–7 second tolerance, matching T-INST-116c), (b) after that window, the shim process is no longer running (SIGKILL took effect because the shim trapped/ignored SIGINT), (c) loopx exits with code **130** (128 + 2 for SIGINT — the originally-forwarded signal, not the SIGKILL escalation), and (d) the aggregate-failure-report branch from SPEC 10.10 is not triggered (the install was interrupted by a signal, not by a non-zero npm exit). `@flaky-retry(2)` due to the real-time 5-second wait. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*
- **T-INST-116g**: SIGINT during `npm install` is forwarded to the npm child's **process group**, not just the npm shim process. SIGINT counterpart to T-INST-116e (SIGTERM process-group). SPEC 10.10 names "SIGINT / SIGTERM" together as the signals that propagate to the npm child's process group. T-INST-116e covers the SIGTERM branch; the SIGINT branch is uncovered, mirroring the runtime-script signal-parity gap that T-SIG-06a closes vs. T-SIG-06. A buggy implementation that forwarded SIGINT only to the direct npm child (not its process group) would leave the grandchild orphaned and pass T-INST-116e (SIGTERM-only) but fail this test. Use the same fixture pattern as T-INST-116e — `withFakeNpm({ spawnGrandchild: true, grandchildPidFile, pidFile, sleepSeconds: 30, logFile })` — and deliver SIGINT instead of SIGTERM. Run `loopx install <source>` via `runCLIWithSignal`, wait for `"ready"`, read the shim PID from `pidFile`, send SIGINT to loopx, and assert: (a) loopx exits with code 130 (SIGINT), (b) the npm shim PID is no longer running, and (c) the recorded grandchild PID is also no longer running. *(Spec 10.10, 7.3)*

##### Signal During Auto-install Pass — No Active `npm` Child

SPEC 10.10's "Signals during the auto-install pass when no npm child is active" clause defines the no-active-child signal-window contract: SIGINT / SIGTERM observed during the post-commit auto-install pass, when no `npm install` child is currently running, aborts the pass and exits with `128 + signal number`; no further `.gitignore` safeguards or `npm install` children are started; committed workflow files are not rolled back; any `.gitignore` / `node_modules/` side effects completed before the signal observation remain on disk; signal termination suppresses the final auto-install aggregate failure report unless it had already been emitted. The clause enumerates four sub-windows (between sequential workflows; during `.gitignore` safeguard work before spawning npm; after one npm child exits and before the next workflow begins; after a `.gitignore` safeguard failure but before the next workflow). Each sub-window is exercised by its own test using the section 1.4 `LOOPX_TEST_AUTOINSTALL_PAUSE` seam in its **ordinal** form (`between-workflows-after-first`, `pre-spawn-first`, `post-exit-first`, `post-safeguard-failure-first`), which pauses loopx at the named position relative to the implementation's commit order long enough for the harness to deliver a signal. The ordinal form is required because SPEC 10.10 says auto-install runs "sequentially in commit order" but does not pin down what commit order is across workflows — a name-targeted pause (e.g., `between-workflows:beta`) would assume a specific name appears at a specific position and fail conformance for an implementation that processes workflows in a different deterministic order.
